The sibling one-shot ChaCha20-Poly1305 API explicitly wipes plaintext on error (`ForceZero(outPlaintext, inCiphertextLen)` at `124`). - impact: unauthenticated plaintext remains in caller memory after a failed decrypt, making AEAD misuse materially easier and allowing attacker-supplied bytes to leak or be processed despite authentication failure. - recommendation: zero `dst` on every decrypt failure after data processing, especially `MAC_CMP_FAILED_E`, and add a regression test matching the classic ChaCha20-Poly1305 decrypt semantics. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - XChaCha20-Poly1305 size_t AAD API truncates authentication to 32 bits, leaving AAD past 4 GiB unauthenticated - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/chacha20_poly1305.h:136-146`, `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:312-358,401-445` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 64-bit build; caller uses public XChaCha20-Poly1305 APIs with `ad_len > 0xffffffff` (large file/object metadata, detached headers, etc.). - evidence: public XChaCha APIs accept `size_t ad_len`, but `wc_XChaCha20Poly1305_crypt_oneshot()` forwards `ad_len` to `wc_XChaCha20Poly1305_Init(aead, ad, (word32)ad_len, ...)` (`401-403`). `wc_XChaCha20Poly1305_Init()` then authenticates AAD with `wc_Poly1305Update(&aead->poly, ad, (word32)ad_len)` and `wc_Poly1305_Pad(&aead->poly, (word32)ad_len)` (`351-357`). Data is explicitly chunked to support lengths beyond `word32`, but AAD is not. Finalization still encodes the full 64-bit `ad_len` via `wc_Poly1305_EncodeSizes64()` (`440-445`). - impact: only the low 32 bits worth of AAD bytes are MACed; AAD beyond 4 GiB can be altered without changing the tag as long as total length stays the same, breaking integrity for large-AAD users. - recommendation: stream AAD through `wc_Poly1305Update()` in `word32` chunks just like ciphertext/plaintext, or reject `ad_len > UINT32_MAX` until full-width handling exists. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Fixed-size fastmath comba-48 helpers free secret operand/result buffers without zeroization - location: `wolfcrypt/src/fp_mul_comba_48.i:35-41,431`, `wolfcrypt/src/fp_sqr_comba_48.i:38,527-531`, dispatch `wolfcrypt/src/tfm.c:423,3285` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_SMALL_STACK` build hitting `TFM_MUL48`/`TFM_SQR48`, plus later heap disclosure, allocator reuse leak, crash dump, or adjacent memory bug after private-key math. - evidence: `fp_mul_comba48()` heap-allocates `at`, copies both operands into it with `XMEMCPY(at, A->dp, ...)` / `XMEMCPY(at+48, B->dp, ...)`, then releases it with plain `XFREE(at, NULL, DYNAMIC_TYPE_TMP_BUFFER)`. `fp_sqr_comba48()` heap-allocates `b` for the full square result, copies it into `B->dp`, then frees `b` with plain `XFREE(b, NULL, DYNAMIC_TYPE_TMP_BUFFER)`. No `ForceZero` appears on these paths. - impact: RSA/DH/ECC big-int limbs and derived products remain recoverable from freed heap memory, increasing the blast radius of secondary memory-disclosure bugs. - recommendation: wipe `at`/`b` before free, or use a zero-free helper for `DYNAMIC_TYPE_TMP_BUFFER` in all fixed-size comba helpers. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Low - ASCON implementation silently builds on unsupported big-endian targets and will emit non-standard hashes/tags/ciphertext - location: `wolfcrypt/src/ascon.c:40-43,238-244,262,334,354-359,401-409,436` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the file-level TODO says `Add support for big-endian systems`, but the implementation still ships under `HAVE_ASCON` with no endian guard. It reads/writes protocol bytes directly through `state.s64` using `xorbuf(a->state.s64, ...)`, `XMEMCPY(&a->state.s64[3], nonce, ...)`, `XMEMCPY(hash, a->state.s64, ...)`, and `XMEMCPY(tag, &a->state.s64[3], ...)` without any `BIG_ENDIAN_ORDER` conversion. - impact: big-endian builds will silently produce incompatible ASCON hashes, tags, and ciphertext, causing cross-platform crypto failure that is hard to diagnose. - recommendation: fail compilation on `BIG_ENDIAN_ORDER` until support exists, or add explicit little-endian load/store conversions at every byte/word boundary. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### High - `wc_sphincs_export_private()` overruns the caller buffer by writing the public key one full buffer-length past the advertised end - Location: `wolfcrypt/src/sphincs.c:626-670,689-697`, public API `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sphincs.h:132-155` - Category: security - Reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - Severity: High - Status: candidate - Exploitability/preconditions: application calls public `wc_sphincs_export_private()` or `wc_sphincs_export_key()` with a buffer sized exactly as requested by `*outLen` / `SPHINCS_LEVELn_PRV_KEY_SIZE`. - Evidence: each branch sets `*outLen = SPHINCS_LEVELn_PRV_KEY_SIZE`, then does `XMEMCPY(out, key->k, SPHINCS_LEVELn_PRV_KEY_SIZE);` followed by `XMEMCPY(out + SPHINCS_LEVELn_PRV_KEY_SIZE, key->p, SPHINCS_LEVELn_PUB_KEY_SIZE);`. The second copy starts exactly one full advertised output length past `out`, so it deterministically writes beyond the caller buffer. `wc_sphincs_export_key()` calls this path first. - Impact: out-of-bounds write into caller memory during private-key export; practical crash/corruption and possible adjacent-data overwrite in applications that expose SPHINCS key export. - Recommendation: copy only `SPHINCS_LEVELn_KEY_SIZE` bytes from `key->k`, append `key->p` at `out + SPHINCS_LEVELn_KEY_SIZE`, and add exact-size buffer regression tests for all levels.

### High - `wc_Falcon_KeyToDer()` and `wc_Sphincs_KeyToDer()` pass secret-key lengths as `pubKeyLen`, so DER export reads past `key->p` into adjacent secret material - Location: `wolfcrypt/src/falcon.c:926-941`, `wolfcrypt/src/sphincs.c:984-1019`, sink `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:38661-38721`, struct layout `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/falcon.h:92-93`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sphincs.h:90-91`, size macros `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/falcon.h:52-60`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sphincs.h:62-72` - Category: security - Reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - Severity: High - Status: candidate - Exploitability/preconditions: application serializes Falcon or SPHINCS private keys with the public-key-including DER APIs. - Evidence: Falcon does `SetAsymKeyDer(key->k, FALCON_LEVELn_KEY_SIZE, key->p, FALCON_LEVELn_KEY_SIZE, ...)`; SPHINCS does the same with `SPHINCS_LEVELn_KEY_SIZE`. `SetAsymKeyDer()` later `XMEMCPY(output + idx, pubKey, pubKeyLen);`. The key structs place `p` immediately before `k`, so any `pubKeyLen` larger than the real public-key length causes the DER export to over-read from `key->p` into `key->k`. The repo itself distinguishes `*_PUB_KEY_SIZE` from `*_KEY_SIZE`, but these call sites ignore that distinction. - Impact: malformed DER output plus disclosure of adjacent private-key bytes into the serialized output buffer, potentially leaking secret material to disk, logs, or remote callers that can trigger key export. - Recommendation: pass `*_PUB_KEY_SIZE` to `SetAsymKeyDer()` for the public component and add tests that DER-exported public sections match `wc_*_export_public()` exactly.

### Medium - SPHINCS private-key import accepts mismatched public/private pairs because `wc_sphincs_check_key()` is a stub that always succeeds - Location: `wolfcrypt/src/sphincs.c:500-556,712-719`, parse-time use `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:9219` - Category: security - Reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - Severity: Medium - Status: candidate - Exploitability/preconditions: attacker can supply a SPHINCS private-key blob/DER containing one private key and an unrelated public key. - Evidence: `wc_sphincs_import_private_key()` imports caller-controlled `pub` via `wc_sphincs_import_public(pub, pubSz, key)` and separately copies the private bytes into `key->k` with `XMEMCPY(...)`; `wc_sphincs_check_key()` then just returns success with the comment `/* Assume everything is fine. */`. `asn.c` invokes this checker while accepting parsed SPHINCS keys. - Impact: malformed keypairs are accepted as valid, allowing identity/binding confusion where signing uses one secret key but exported or trusted public-key material is unrelated. - Recommendation: implement real private/public consistency validation (derive/compare public material, or at minimum validate embedded public bytes against the imported private key) and mirror Falcon’s explicit compare logic.

### Medium - SPHINCS level-3 public export is unreachable because `wc_sphincs_export_public()` rejects level 3 before its own level-3 branch - Location: `wolfcrypt/src/sphincs.c:317-355,689-697`, dependent API `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sphincs.h:128,134,153` - Category: complexity - Reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code` - Severity: Medium - Status: candidate - Evidence: `wc_sphincs_export_public()` returns `BAD_FUNC_ARG` when `((key->level != 1) && (key->level != 5))`, which excludes level 3 outright, but the same function later has explicit `else if (key->level == 3)` buffer and copy branches. `wc_sphincs_export_key()` and `wc_Sphincs_PublicKeyToDer()` depend on this helper. - Impact: supported SPHINCS level-3 keys cannot be exported or DER-serialized through the public API, causing production failures for one advertised parameter set. - Recommendation: fix the guard to accept `1/3/5`, then add direct tests for level-3 `export_public`, `export_key`, and `PublicKeyToDer` paths.

### High - DSA parameter import accepts attacker-chosen `g=1`/invalid subgroup values, enabling trivial signature acceptance under malicious keys - location: `wolfcrypt/src/dsa.c:425-466,1006-1117`; support: `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:12357-12379,12560-12581` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application imports untrusted DSA parameters/public keys through `wc_DsaImportParamsRawCheck()` or DER decode and then verifies signatures with that key. - evidence: `_DsaImportParamsRaw()` only primality-tests `p` when `trusted == 0`, then blindly parses `q` and `g`; `wc_DsaPublicKeyDecode()` similarly loads `p/q/g/y` with `GetInt(...)` and no subgroup/generator validation. `wc_DsaVerify_ex()` computes `v = ((g^u1 * y^u2) mod p) mod q` and accepts when `v == r`. With `g = 1` and `y = 1`, `v` is always `1`, so any signature with `r = 1` and `0 < s < q` verifies for any digest. - impact: forged DSA signatures can be accepted under attacker-supplied keys/parameters; generated keys over malicious params also collapse to degenerate `y = 1`. - recommendation: validate full DSA domain/public-key invariants on import (`p` and `q` prime, `q | p-1`, `1 < g < p`, `g^q mod p == 1`, `1 < y < p-1`, `y^q mod p == 1`) or reject attacker-controlled DSA params entirely. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### High - `wc_curve25519_import_public_ex()` accepts invalid peer points, and default `wc_curve25519_shared_secret_ex()` does not reject low-order/all-zero results - location: `wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.c:662-767,837-932`; affected internal paths: `src/pk.c:5200,5209`, `wolfcrypt/src/hpke.c:412,818,1061`, `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:38588,38634` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker controls a peer/public X25519 key passed through `wc_curve25519_import_public_ex()` or an internal caller that uses it directly; build does not enable `WOLFSSL_ECDHX_SHARED_NOT_ZERO`. - evidence: `wc_curve25519_import_public_ex()` only copies 32 input bytes into `key->p.point` and sets `pubSet = 1`; it never calls `wc_curve25519_check_public()`. `wc_curve25519_shared_secret_ex()` only rejects inputs with the sign bit set and gates the all-zero shared-secret check behind `#ifdef WOLFSSL_ECDHX_SHARED_NOT_ZERO`. The file already has `wc_curve25519_check_public()`, but these import/derive paths do not invoke it. - impact: low-order/degenerate public keys can drive a predictable X25519 shared secret through generic PK and HPKE flows, breaking contributory behavior and making downstream KDF input attacker-known on default builds. - recommendation: call `wc_curve25519_check_public()` from public-key import paths and make all-zero shared-secret rejection unconditional in `wc_curve25519_shared_secret_ex()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `wc_curve25519_delete()` frees with a zeroed/never-initialized heap hint and skips async context teardown - location: `wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.c:1114-1178`; support: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/curve25519.h:134` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `curve25519_key` has a `void *heap` field, but `wc_curve25519_init_ex()` ignores its `heap` argument (`(void)heap`) and never assigns `key->heap`. It still may allocate async state via `wolfAsync_DevCtxInit(&key->asyncDev, WOLFSSL_ASYNC_MARKER_X25519, heap, devId)`. `wc_curve25519_free()` only `ForceZero(key, sizeof(*key));` and never calls `wolfAsync_DevCtxFree(...)`. `wc_curve25519_delete()` then does `XFREE(key, key->heap, DYNAMIC_TYPE_CURVE25519);` after the zero, so object free always uses a cleared/NULL heap. - impact: custom allocator users can free `curve25519_key` objects through the wrong heap/pool, and async device contexts leak instead of closing cleanly. - recommendation: store `heap` in `wc_curve25519_init_ex()`, call `wolfAsync_DevCtxFree(&key->asyncDev, WOLFSSL_ASYNC_MARKER_X25519)` before zeroing, and preserve the original heap for the final `XFREE`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wc_SrpSetParams()` accepts arbitrary `N`/`g` with no prime/safe-group validation before `wc_SrpComputeKey()` uses them - location: `wolfcrypt/src/srp.c:341-376,701-788`; contrast fixed built-in TLS group: `src/ssl.c:8299-8382` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application exposes the generic SRP API to untrusted or tenant-configured group parameters; the built-in wolfSSL TLS SRP constants are not affected. - evidence: `wc_SrpSetParams()` only checks non-null inputs, `nSz >= gSz`, `mp_count_bits(N) >= SRP_MODULUS_MIN_BITS`, and `N > g`; it does not test that `N` is prime/safe-prime or that `g` generates the intended subgroup. `wc_SrpComputeKey()` later feeds those values directly into `mp_exptmod()` / `mp_mulmod()` on both client and server paths. - impact: weak composite or small-subgroup SRP groups can be accepted as valid, undermining SRP password-auth security and enabling attacker-controlled/weak session-key computations in API consumers. - recommendation: restrict custom groups to vetted RFC 5054 sets or validate safe-prime/subgroup invariants for `N` and `g` before accepting them. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `wolfAsync_EventWait()` and `wc_AsyncWait()` spin in a tight poll loop with no sleep/backoff - location: `wolfcrypt/src/async.c:805-860` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `wolfAsync_EventWait()` loops `while (ret == 0 && event->ret == ...WC_PENDING_E) { ret = wolfAsync_EventPoll(...); }` and `wc_AsyncWait()` calls it directly; there is no `sched_yield`, `nanosleep`, timeout, or blocking wait path. - impact: synchronous waits on slow async hardware/software jobs burn CPU continuously, causing avoidable core saturation and latency spikes under load. - recommendation: block on a backend/event primitive where available, or add bounded backoff (`wc_AsyncSleep`, `sched_yield`, timeout) instead of pure busy-polling. - reference: `grugbrain: small sharp tools`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Medium - `wc_MakeDsaKey()` allocates `tmpQ` from the default big-int heap but frees it with `dsa->heap` as a temp buffer - location: `wolfcrypt/src/dsa.c:176,235-236`; support: `wolfcrypt/src/dsa.c:72-77` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: under `WOLFSSL_SMALL_STACK`, `tmpQ` is allocated with `XMALLOC(sizeof(*tmpQ), NULL, DYNAMIC_TYPE_WOLF_BIGINT)`, but cleanup uses `XFREE(tmpQ, dsa->heap, DYNAMIC_TYPE_TMP_BUFFER)`. `wc_InitDsaKey_h()` allows callers to set a non-NULL `dsa->heap`. - impact: custom or type-aware allocators can misroute the free, causing leaks, allocator corruption, or misleading memory accounting during DSA key generation on small-stack builds. - recommendation: allocate and free `tmpQ` with the same heap/type, preferably `dsa->heap` + `DYNAMIC_TYPE_WOLF_BIGINT`, or keep both sides on the default heap consistently. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wc_AesCmacVerify[_ex]()` accepts undersized and oversized tags, weakening/ambiguating CMAC verification - location: `wolfcrypt/src/cmac.c:475-501`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/cmac.h:89-90,141-145` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller forwards attacker-controlled CMAC/tag length into public `wc_AesCmacVerify()` / `wc_AesCmacVerify_ex()` and relies on the helper to enforce library CMAC tag bounds. - evidence: `wc_AesCmacVerify_ex()` rejects only `checkSz == 0` (`cmac.c:486`) and then verifies with `ConstantCompare(check, a, (int)min(checkSz, aSz))` (`cmac.c:499`) where `aSz` is always 16. The same API family defines `WC_CMAC_TAG_MIN_SZ`/`MAX_SZ` as `4..16` in `cmac.h`, and `wc_CmacFinalNoFree()` enforces those bounds (`cmac.c:293`), but verify does not. - impact: 1-3 byte tags are accepted and authenticated as prefixes of the real MAC, reducing forgery cost to 2^8..2^24 if a peer can truncate the transmitted tag; lengths above 16 also verify only the first 16 bytes, hiding caller bugs. - recommendation: fail closed unless `WC_CMAC_TAG_MIN_SZ <= checkSz <= WC_CMAC_TAG_MAX_SZ`, and ideally require exact/agreed tag length instead of `min(checkSz, aSz)` prefix comparison. Add regression tests for `checkSz` 1..3 and `>16`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - public `wc_export_int()` can underflow the destination pointer when `keySz` is smaller than the integer, causing out-of-bounds writes - location: `wolfcrypt/src/wolfmath.c:223-252`; entry point `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/wolfmath.h:117` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application calls public `wc_export_int(..., WC_TYPE_UNSIGNED_BIN)` with attacker-influenced or mismatched `keySz`; the integer's actual unsigned size exceeds `keySz`. - evidence: the function checks only `*len < keySz`, zeroes `keySz` bytes, then writes to `buf + (keySz - (word32)mp_unsigned_bin_size(mp))` via `mp_to_unsigned_bin(...)` with no guard that `mp_unsigned_bin_size(mp) <= keySz`. When the integer is larger than `keySz`, the unsigned subtraction wraps and the write starts before `buf`. - impact: heap/stack underwrite / memory corruption in callers that trust `keySz` as a formatting width instead of a hard lower bound. - recommendation: compute `mpSz = mp_unsigned_bin_size(mp)` first and return `BUFFER_E` when `mpSz > keySz`; only zero-pad and export after the fit check succeeds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`

### Medium - `wc_bigint_free()` releases raw big-int buffers without zeroization, leaving exported private RSA/ECC/DH values in heap memory - location: `wolfcrypt/src/wolfmath.c:257-351`; sensitive producers include `wc_mp_to_bigint()` and `wc_bigint_from_unsigned_bin()`; representative private-key users `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c:5178-5192`, free paths `wolfcrypt/src/integer.c:213`, `wolfcrypt/src/sp_int.c:5203`, `wolfcrypt/src/tfm.c:4361` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker later gains heap disclosure, allocator reuse visibility, crash dump access, or a UAF/read-after-free primitive after raw key material has been exported into `WC_BIGINT` buffers. - evidence: `wc_mp_to_bigint()` and `wc_bigint_from_unsigned_bin()` copy whole multi-precision values into `dst->buf`/`a->buf`; `wc_bigint_free()` then does plain `XFREE(a->buf, a->heap, DYNAMIC_TYPE_WOLF_BIGINT);` without `ForceZero`, even though these buffers are used for raw private components such as RSA `d/p/q/dP/dQ/u` (`rsa.c:5178-5192`). - impact: freed heap can retain full private-key components and other secret math operands, materially worsening post-compromise memory disclosure. - recommendation: zero buffers in `wc_bigint_free()` (or add/use a zero-free helper) and keep `wc_bigint_zero()` as an optional early scrub, not the only safe path. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Low - liboqs-backed `wc_MlKemKey_*WithRandom()` APIs silently ignore caller-supplied randomness and use liboqs RNG instead - location: `wolfcrypt/src/ext_mlkem.c:405-423,507-521`; declarations `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/mlkem.h:324,332`; test note `wolfcrypt/test/test.c:42362` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `wc_MlKemKey_MakeKeyWithRandom()` and `wc_MlKemKey_EncapsulateWithRandom()` immediately cast `rand`/`len` to void and call `wc_MlKemKey_MakeKey(key, NULL)` / `wc_MlKemKey_Encapsulate(key, ct, ss, NULL)` with comments `OQS doesn't support external randomness.` The test suite separately notes `/* OQS does not support KATs */`. - impact: callers expecting deterministic KAT behavior, approved external entropy, or HSM-owned randomness get silently different behavior on the liboqs backend, making audits and failures hard to reason about. - recommendation: fail explicitly (`NOT_COMPILED_IN`/`BAD_FUNC_ARG`) when external randomness is requested on this backend, or document/backend-split the API so unsupported semantics are not silently ignored. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Medium - DSP ECC handle callback is a racy global; hot-swaps/cleanup can mismatch GET/DONE and drive verify through a closed handle or freed mutex - location: `wolfcrypt/src/wc_dsp.c:34-105,290-297`; call path `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:8725-8730`, lifecycle `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:445,555` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_DSP` multithreaded build; one thread runs ECC verify while another calls `wolfSSL_SetHandleCb()` or global cleanup. - evidence: `handle_function` is a process-global static; `wolfSSL_SetHandleCb()` assigns it without locking; `sp_dsp_ecc_verify_256()` reads and calls `handle_function` twice (`WOLFSSL_HANDLE_GET` before `wolfSSL_DSP_ECC_Verify_256()`, `WOLFSSL_HANDLE_DONE` after). `wolfSSL_CleanupHandle()` closes `defaultHandle` and frees `handle_mutex` but never clears `handle_function`. If the callback changes between GET and DONE, the default callback can lock `handle_mutex` on GET and never receive the matching DONE, or DONE can run through a different callback. If cleanup races verify, later callback use hits a closed handle/freed mutex. - impact: deadlock, crash, or verify requests using an invalid DSP handle; in services exposing ECC verify this is a realistic availability failure under concurrent reconfiguration/shutdown. - recommendation: snapshot the callback once per verify and use that same function for GET/DONE, serialize callback/handle mutation with a mutex, and clear/disable the callback before closing the default handle or freeing its mutex. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Fixed-size fastmath comba helpers free heap scratch containing private big-int limbs without zeroization - location: `wolfcrypt/src/fp_mul_comba_24.i:23-242`, `fp_mul_comba_20.i:22-209`, `fp_mul_comba_17.i:23-186`, `fp_mul_comba_12.i:23-146`, `fp_mul_comba_9.i:23-122`, `fp_mul_comba_8.i:23-114`, `fp_mul_comba_7.i:23-106`, `fp_mul_comba_6.i:23-98`, `fp_mul_comba_4.i:23-82`, `wolfcrypt/src/fp_sqr_comba_20.i:23-254`, `fp_sqr_comba_17.i:23-224`, `fp_sqr_comba_12.i:23-174`, `fp_sqr_comba_9.i:23-144`, `fp_sqr_comba_8.i:23-134`, `fp_sqr_comba_7.i:23-124`, `fp_sqr_comba_6.i:23-114`, `fp_sqr_comba_4.i:23-94`; dispatch `wolfcrypt/src/tfm.c:344-405,3207-3261` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `USE_FAST_MATH` + `WOLFSSL_SMALL_STACK` build, followed by heap disclosure/crash dump/allocator reuse after private-key math. - evidence: these helpers allocate temporary `at`/`b` with `XMALLOC`, copy operand limbs via `XMEMCPY(at, A->dp, ...)` / `XMEMCPY(at+N, B->dp, ...)` or build full square results in heap `b`, then return through plain `XFREE(..., DYNAMIC_TYPE_TMP_BUFFER)` with no `ForceZero`. Core `fp_mul()` / `fp_sqr()` dispatch into these routines from `tfm.c`. - impact: RSA/DH/DSA/ECC operand limbs and derived products from common fast-math paths remain recoverable from freed heap memory, widening key exposure under secondary memory-disclosure conditions. - recommendation: scrub `at`/`b` before every free, or centralize a zero-free helper for `DYNAMIC_TYPE_TMP_BUFFER` in fast-math code so all comba variants stay aligned. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - `wc_DeCompressDynamic()` grows the output buffer linearly and recopies the whole buffer on each refill, making large decompressions quadratic - location: `wolfcrypt/src/compress.c:203-289`; exposed caller `wolfcrypt/src/pkcs7.c:15772` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: when `inflate(..., Z_BLOCK)` returns `Z_BUF_ERROR`, the loop does `i++`, `newSz = tmpSz + inSz`, allocates a fresh buffer, `XMEMCPY(newTmp, tmp, tmpSz)`, frees the old buffer, and retries. There is no geometric growth or `XREALLOC`, so outputs that require many expansions pay repeated full-buffer copies. - impact: large or high-expansion compressed inputs incur O(n^2) copy/allocator churn on top of decompression, causing avoidable latency spikes and memory pressure; this compounds the existing compressed-data DoS surface. - recommendation: grow geometrically (for example 1.5x/2x), use checked realloc when possible, and enforce absolute output caps while streaming instead of repeated full copies. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: small sharp tools`

### Medium - Public zlib wrapper breaks its documented raw-deflate mode by OR-ing negative `windowBits` with `15` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/compress.c:84-107,122-173`; API `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/compress.h:45-51` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the comments/documentation say `windowBits` in `-1..-15` should select raw deflate, but `wc_Compress_ex()` passes `DEFLATE_DEFAULT_WINDOWBITS | windowBits` to `deflateInit2()` and `wc_DeCompress_ex()` passes `DEFLATE_DEFAULT_WINDOWBITS | windowBits` to `inflateInit2()`. For example `-15 | 15 == -1`, not `-15`, so the requested raw-deflate window is destroyed before it reaches zlib. - impact: callers cannot reliably use the advertised raw-deflate mode; integrations expecting raw/zlib/gzip parity will fail in non-obvious ways and are harder to reason about or test. - recommendation: preserve negative raw values verbatim and only apply the default/base window to non-negative modes; add regression tests for `windowBits = -15`, `0`, and gzip autodetect values. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - ARMv8-32 X25519/Ed25519 asm leaves secret scalar and nonce intermediates on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-curve25519.S:3315-3681,3689-3979,5227-6660,6670-8590`; callers `wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.c:197,744,770`, `wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.c:459-469,512-518` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash-dump/core access, or another read primitive over prior stack contents on 32-bit ARM builds using this asm backend. - evidence: the secret-bearing asm helpers allocate large local frames (`curve25519`: `sub sp, sp, #0xbc` / `#0xc0`; `sc_reduce`: `#56`; `sc_muladd`: `#0x50`) and return via plain `add sp, sp, ...; pop {..,pc}` without any scrub. `curve25519.c` calls this backend for public-key generation and shared secret (`curve25519(pub, priv, ...)`, `curve25519(o.point, private_key->k, ...)`), and `ed25519.c` feeds secret `nonce`/`az` into `sc_reduce()`/`sc_muladd()`. Searches in the generated backend find no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero_*`; callers only wipe their own top-level buffers such as `az`, `nonce`, and `o`. - impact: private-scalar, nonce, and derived ladder/reduction state remain resident on thread stacks after return, increasing key-recovery exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics. - recommendation: add a generated/compiler-safe zeroization epilogue for secret-bearing asm helpers before restoring `sp`/returning, or move these paths behind wrappers that scrub the temporary frame. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Thumb2 AES assembly uses secret-dependent table lookups for key schedule and round transforms, reintroducing cache-timing leakage across ECB/CBC/CTR/GCM - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-aes-asm.S:705-748,930-1118,1160,1940,2740,3558-3765,4880-6990,6992-8074`; dispatch from public AES wrappers in `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:6489,6710,7150,9898,10631` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_ARMASM && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2` build on a cacheful/shared ARM target where an attacker can observe timing/cache state or power while victim AES operations run. - evidence: the asm fast path extracts secret state/key bytes with `UBFX/LSR` and immediately indexes S-box/T-tables via `LDR/LDRB [table, idx, LSL #2]` in `AES_set_encrypt_key` (`705+`), `AES_encrypt_block` (`930+`), and `AES_decrypt_block` (`3558+`). Mode entry points `AES_ECB_encrypt`, `AES_CBC_encrypt`, `AES_CTR_encrypt`, `AES_GCM_encrypt`, `AES_ECB_decrypt`, and `AES_CBC_decrypt` all funnel through those table lookups. Unlike the generic C T-table path, which has explicit `WOLFSSL_AES_TOUCH_LINES` / `GetTable()` mitigation hooks (`wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:1879-1950,2203`), this Thumb2 asm file contains no equivalent constant-time or line-touching defense. - impact: AES key material and intermediate state become observable through cache-timing/power side channels; on affected devices this can enable cross-process/co-resident key recovery or plaintext inference for TLS/session traffic using the software AES path. - recommendation: do not use this Thumb2 T-table backend on shared-cache targets; prefer hardware AES/ARM crypto extensions or a constant-time bitsliced/fixsliced implementation. If the asm path must remain, gate it behind an explicit unsafe opt-in or add an equivalent constant-time/table-touch mitigation with tests/benchmarks proving the behavior. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - ARMv8-32 non-NEON SHA512 asm leaves key-derived message blocks and digest state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-sha512-asm.S:204-7618`; call sites `wolfcrypt/src/sha512.c:797-800,842-847`; keyed use `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:1185` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 32-bit ARM build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM` and `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_NO_NEON`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory; impact is strongest for keyed SHA512 users such as HMAC. - evidence: `Transform_Sha512_Len_base` allocates a `0xc0` stack frame (`sub sp, sp, #0xc0`), writes the current block schedule into `sp[0..124]`, and copies the incoming digest/state into `sp[128..188]`, then returns via plain `add sp, sp, #0xc0; pop {..., pc}` with no scrub. `sha512.c` selects this backend as `Transform_Sha512_Len_base`, and HMAC drives secret key-mixed `opad`/`ipad` data through `wc_Sha512Update()`. - impact: prior HMAC/keyed-SHA512 blocks and intermediate hash state remain resident on thread stacks after the transform returns, increasing secret exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics on affected ARM builds. - recommendation: zero the full temporary frame before restoring `sp`/returning, or wrap the generated asm with a compiler-safe scrub epilogue; add a regression test or codegen rule so secret-bearing hash asm paths stay wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - `wc_curve448_import_public_ex()` accepts unvalidated peer public keys, and `wc_curve448_shared_secret_ex()` only rejects all-zero output behind an opt-in macro - location: `wolfcrypt/src/curve448.c:139-191,298-323`; affected consumers: `src/pk.c:5654,5663`, `src/sniffer.c:3020,3110` (contrast guarded TLS path `src/tls.c:9456,9463`) - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can supply a chosen X448 public key to code using the raw/API import+shared-secret path; build does not enable `WOLFSSL_ECDHX_SHARED_NOT_ZERO` and caller does not separately invoke `wc_curve448_check_public()`. - evidence: `wc_curve448_import_public_ex()` only checks length, copies bytes into `key->p`, and sets `key->pubSet = 1`; `wc_curve448_shared_secret_ex()` then computes `curve448(o, private_key->k, public_key->p)` and only performs the RFC-style zero-shared-secret rejection inside `#ifdef WOLFSSL_ECDHX_SHARED_NOT_ZERO`. The repo's TLS path explicitly calls `wc_curve448_check_public()` before import, but `src/pk.c` and `src/sniffer.c` import then derive the secret without that guard. - impact: a malicious low-order / otherwise invalid X448 public key can drive a predictable all-zero or non-contributory shared secret through generic ECDH consumers, breaking contributory behavior and risking predictable session/key-agreement output. - recommendation: fail closed in `wc_curve448_import_public_ex()` or `wc_curve448_shared_secret_ex()` by always validating peer keys or, at minimum, always rejecting all-zero shared secrets for X448. Keep raw callers from having to remember a separate validation step. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### High - `fp_mul_comba32()` leaks its heap scratch buffer on three fast-path returns in `WOLFSSL_SMALL_STACK` builds - location: `wolfcrypt/src/fp_mul_comba_32.i:36-38,204,240,276,291`; dispatch: `wolfcrypt/src/tfm.c:417` - category: performance - severity: high - status: candidate - evidence: the small-stack path allocates `at = XMALLOC(sizeof(fp_digit) * 64, ...)` at entry, but the early exits for `out_size <= 40`, `<= 48`, and `<= 56` return immediately after `COMBA_FINI` without reaching the only `XFREE(at, ...)` at the function end. `fp_mul()` dispatches into this helper from core fast-math multiply. - impact: every affected multiply leaks one 64-digit temporary buffer; repeated RSA/ECC/DSA operations on common sub-1024-bit to ~1792-bit operand sizes can steadily exhaust heap memory and crash long-lived processes or enable remote handshake/verification DoS on small-stack builds. - recommendation: route all returns through one cleanup label (or free `at` before each early return) and add a regression test that repeatedly exercises the `out_size <= 40/48/56` paths under `WOLFSSL_SMALL_STACK`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wc_RipeMdFinal()` computes the high 32 bits of the bit-length after already shifting away the carry bits from `loLen` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/ripemd.c:341-342`; correct pattern in sibling code: `wolfcrypt/src/sha.c:939-940` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application hashes inputs larger than `2^29` bytes (~512 MiB) with RIPEMD. - evidence: RIPEMD finalization does `ripemd->loLen = ripemd->loLen << 3;` and only then `ripemd->hiLen = (ripemd->loLen >> (8*sizeof(ripemd->loLen) - 3)) + (ripemd->hiLen << 3);`. Once `loLen` has already been shifted, the carry into `hiLen` is lost for large inputs. The SHA implementation in the same repo performs the operations in the opposite order (`hiLen` first, then `loLen`). - impact: RIPEMD digests become wrong for large messages, causing silent integrity mismatches, bad signature/hash verification, and cross-implementation incompatibility once inputs cross 512 MiB. - recommendation: compute `hiLen` from the pre-shift low length, then shift `loLen`, matching the SHA/MD5/MD4 finalization pattern. Add a large-input regression test around the `2^29`-byte boundary. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-11 Cryptography`

### Medium - `ED25519_SMALL` low-memory scalar multiply leaves large secret point/scalar intermediates on the stack after signing or public-key derivation - location: `wolfcrypt/src/ge_low_mem.c:409-429,432-434,437-460`; secret call paths: `wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.c:286,469` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `HAVE_ED25519 && ED25519_SMALL`; attacker later obtains stack contents via crash dump, stack disclosure, or adjacent memory bug after Ed25519 signing or public-key generation. - evidence: `ed25519_smult()` allocates `ge_p3 r` plus per-iteration `ge_p3 s` and returns without any scrub; `ge_tobytes()` and related helpers keep field elements `x/y/z1` on the stack and also return unsanitized. These paths are reached from `ge_scalarmult_base()` during `wc_ed25519_make_public()` and signing, where the scalar input is derived from the private key (`az`) or per-signature nonce. - impact: scalar-dependent ladder state and derived point coordinates remain in stack memory after use, materially increasing private-key / nonce exposure under secondary memory-disclosure conditions. - recommendation: zero secret-bearing local `ge_p3`/field-element buffers on all exits in the `ED25519_SMALL` path with compiler-resistant zeroization helpers. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Generic AArch64 CPUID fallback enables AES/PMULL/SHA flags unconditionally on unsupported OS targets, turning feature detection into a dangerous default - location: `wolfcrypt/src/cpuid.c:339-359`; consumers: `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:821,823,824`, `wolfcrypt/src/sha3.c:679`, `wolfcrypt/src/sha512.c:833` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_CPUID_AARCH64` build on an AArch64 target that is not Linux/Android/Apple/*BSD and not using `WOLFSSL_AARCH64_PRIVILEGE_MODE`; runtime CPU/VM lacks one or more advertised crypto extensions. - evidence: the fallback `cpuid_set_flags()` branch sets `CPUID_AES`, `CPUID_PMULL`, `CPUID_SHA256`, and optional SHA512/RDM/SHA3/SM3/SM4 bits solely from build macros, without reading hardware state. Downstream crypto code consumes those flags to enable hardware/asm paths (`aes->use_aes_hw_crypto = IS_AARCH64_AES(cpuid_flags)`, etc.). - impact: binaries can select unsupported instructions and crash with `SIGILL`/illegal-instruction faults at runtime on weaker cores or constrained VMs, creating hard-to-debug production outages on non-mainstream AArch64 platforms. - recommendation: default unknown AArch64 platforms to zero feature bits unless runtime detection succeeds or the application explicitly opts in to forced features. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-13 Configuration`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Medium - Thumb2 SHA512 asm leaves key-mixed message blocks and digest state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-sha512-asm.S:206-3664`; call sites `wolfcrypt/src/sha512.c:800,847`; keyed use `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:1185,1192` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 32-bit ARM Thumb2 build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_NO_NEON`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `Transform_Sha512_Len_base` allocates a `0xc0` stack frame (`thumb2-sha512-asm.S:208`), stores copied digest state at `sp+128..184` (`215-222`) and the current message schedule at `sp+0..124` (`246+`), then returns with plain `ADD sp, sp, #0xc0; POP {..., pc}` (`3663-3664`) and no scrub. `sha512.c` selects/calls this backend directly (`800,847`). HMAC feeds key-mixed `opad` and `innerHash` blocks through `wc_Sha512Update()` (`hmac.c:1185,1192`), so secret-derived blocks hit this frame on keyed SHA512 use. - impact: prior HMAC blocks and intermediate SHA512 state remain on thread stacks after the transform returns, increasing secret exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics on affected ARM builds. - recommendation: zero the full temporary frame before restoring `sp`/returning, or wrap the asm backend in a compiler-resistant scrub epilogue; add a regression/codegen check so secret-bearing hash asm paths stay wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - ARMv8-32 non-NEON SHA256 asm leaves HMAC/key-mixed blocks and digest state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-sha256-asm.S:108-2567`; call sites `wolfcrypt/src/sha256.c:1127-1144`; keyed use `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:370,668,958,1144-1151` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection` - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 32-bit ARM build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_NO_NEON && !NO_SHA256`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `Transform_Sha256_Len_base` allocates a `0xc0` frame (`sub sp, sp, #0xc0`), stores the message schedule at `sp+0..60` and a copied digest/state at `sp+64..92`, then returns via `add sp, sp, #0xc0; pop {r4, r5, r6, r7, r8, r9, r10, r11, pc}` with no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero` anywhere in the file. `sha256.c` dispatches `wc_Sha256Update()` to this backend, and HMAC feeds key-mixed `pad`, `opad`, and `innerHash` through `wc_Sha256Update()`. - impact: prior HMAC blocks and intermediate SHA256 state remain resident on thread stacks after the transform returns, increasing secret exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics on affected ARM builds. - recommendation: zero the temporary frame before restoring `sp`/returning, or add a compiler-safe scrub wrapper/codegen rule so secret-bearing SHA256 asm paths are wiped.

### Medium - Thumb2 X25519/Ed25519 asm leaves secret ladder, scalar, and nonce intermediates on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-curve25519.S:2531-2937,2946-3269,3280-3463,3931-4114,4543-5257,5268-6469`; callers `wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.c:197,364,744`, `wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.c:465,513,517-518` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Thumb2 ARM build using this asm backend; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash dump/core access, or another read primitive over prior stack contents. - evidence: secret-bearing helpers allocate large local frames (`curve25519`: `SUB sp, sp, #0xbc` and `#0xc0`; `fe_invert`: `#0x88`; `fe_pow22523`: `#0x68`; `sc_reduce`: `#0x38`; `sc_muladd`: `#0x50`) and return via matching `ADD sp, sp, ...; POP {..,pc}` with no scrub. These paths handle X25519 private-scalar operations (`curve25519(pub, priv, ...)`, `curve25519(o.point, private_key->k, ...)`) and Ed25519 secret data (`sc_reduce(nonce)`, `sc_muladd(..., hram, az, nonce)`). Callers wipe top-level buffers like `az`/`nonce`, but the asm frames themselves are never zeroed. - impact: private-scalar, nonce, reduction, and ladder state remains resident on thread stacks after return, increasing key-recovery exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics. - recommendation: add a compiler-safe zeroization epilogue for secret-bearing asm helpers before restoring `sp`/returning, or route them through wrappers that scrub the temporary frame. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Low - `WC_NO_CACHE_RESISTANT` Thumb2 Ed25519 fallback reintroduces secret-dependent table fetches in basepoint scalar multiplication - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-curve25519.S:1412-1478` (`fe_cmov_table` non-cache-resistant branch); call chain `wolfcrypt/src/ge_operations.c:9073-9094,9106-9166`, sign/make-key users `wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.c:286,469` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build defines `WC_NO_CACHE_RESISTANT` on a cacheful/shared Thumb2 target where an attacker can observe timing/cache state while Ed25519 make-key or sign runs. - evidence: the fallback `fe_cmov_table` computes the selected table slot from secret-derived `b` with `CLZ lr, r2`, `MUL r2, r2, #0x60`, `ADD r1, r1, r2`, then `LDM r1!` loads only that precomputed entry. `ge_select()` passes `b` from `ge_scalarmult_base()` digits `e[i]`, and Ed25519 uses `ge_scalarmult_base(&A, az)` and `ge_scalarmult_base(&R, nonce)` on secret material. The cache-resistant branch exists immediately above under `#ifndef WC_NO_CACHE_RESISTANT`; this issue is the explicit fallback when that protection is compiled out. - impact: per-signature secret digits can influence memory addresses and cache footprint, reopening timing/cache side channels against Ed25519 private-key operations on affected builds. - recommendation: do not ship Thumb2 Ed25519 with `WC_NO_CACHE_RESISTANT` on shared/cacheful targets; prefer the constant-time branch or gate the fallback behind an explicit unsafe opt-in/documented build failure. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - Thumb2 SHA256 asm leaves HMAC/key-mixed blocks and digest state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-sha256-asm.S:72-2557`; call sites `wolfcrypt/src/sha256.c:1126-1140`; keyed use `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:370,668,958,1144-1151` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection` - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 32-bit ARM Thumb2 build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_NO_NEON && !NO_SHA256`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `Transform_Sha256_Len_base` allocates a `0xc0` frame (`SUB sp, sp, #0xc0`), stores the message schedule at `sp+0..60` and copied digest state at `sp+64..88`, then returns with plain `ADD sp, sp, #0xc0; POP {r4, r5, r6, r7, r8, r9, r10, r11, pc}`. Repo search finds no `ForceZero`, `wc_MemZero`, or `memset` in this asm file. `sha256.c` dispatches to this backend under `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2`, and HMAC sends key-mixed `pad`, message/key blocks, `opad`, and `innerHash` through `wc_Sha256Update()`. - impact: prior HMAC blocks and intermediate SHA256 state remain resident on thread stacks after the transform returns, increasing secret exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics on affected Thumb2 ARM builds. - recommendation: zero the temporary frame before restoring `sp`/returning, or wrap the asm backend in a compiler-resistant scrub epilogue/codegen rule so secret-bearing SHA256 asm paths are wiped.

### Low - Thumb2 ML-KEM asm object ships five apparently unused exported routines alongside the one helper actually selected - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-mlkem-asm.S:171-3893`, consumer `wolfcrypt/src/wc_mlkem_poly.c:4819` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: this single generated object exports `mlkem_thumb2_ntt`, `mlkem_thumb2_invntt`, `mlkem_thumb2_basemul_mont`, `mlkem_thumb2_basemul_mont_add`, `mlkem_thumb2_csubq`, and `mlkem_thumb2_rej_uniform`, but repo-wide search outside the Thumb2 asm sources finds only one consumer: `#define mlkem_csubq_c mlkem_thumb2_csubq` in `wc_mlkem_poly.c:4819`. No other in-repo call sites reference the other five exports. - impact: Thumb2 ML-KEM builds that pull this object for `mlkem_thumb2_csubq` also carry several hundred extra lines of unreachable generated asm, increasing binary size and audit surface on embedded targets and making dead code drift harder to notice. - recommendation: split the generated helpers into per-symbol objects/sections or only generate/compile the helpers that are actually selected; otherwise wire and test the extra exports or drop them. - reference: `grugbrain: too much abstraction`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-sha256-asm.S` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-sha256-asm.S`, callers `wolfcrypt/src/sha256.c:1129-1144` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: unlike the already-flagged 32-bit ARM/Thumb2 SHA256 backends, this AArch64 file keeps the SHA256 message schedule and working state in GPR/SIMD registers (`v0-v25`, `w4-w24`) and uses only small prologue spill frames for callee-saved regs (`stp x29, x30, ...`, `stp d8..d15`). Repo search found direct call sites only in `sha256.c` (`Transform_Sha256_Len_neon/crypto` at `1129-1144`), with HMAC feeding this path through `wc_Sha256Update()` (`hmac.c:370,668,958,1144-1151`). No concrete trust-boundary bug, secret-on-stack copy, or material perf issue was found in this chunk beyond existing 32-bit findings.

### Medium - ARMv8-32 SHA3 asm leaves SHA3/SHAKE/HMAC/PQ intermediate state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-sha3-asm.S:94-128,350,415-419,2377`; dispatch/use: `wolfcrypt/src/sha3.c:648,666,769,805,890,902,1866,2107`, secret-bearing callers `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:393,403,751,791,981,991,1227,1234,1267,1274`, `wolfcrypt/src/ed448.c:75,123,129`, `wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.c:403,429,457,465-472,589-600,646-665,817-824`, `wolfcrypt/src/wc_mlkem_poly.c:2511,2529,2714,2862,2892-2912,3007,3086,3101-3104,3202-3215,4408,4474`, `wolfcrypt/src/wc_slhdsa.c:411-422,464,482,516-531,576,596,757,791-835` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 32-bit ARM build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM`, `WOLFSSL_SHA3`, and not `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack contents. - evidence: the NEON `BlockSha3` path allocates `sub sp, sp, #16`, stores temporary lanes with `vst1.8 {d25, d26}, [r3]`, and returns via `add sp, sp, #16; ... bx lr`; the non-NEON path allocates `sub sp, sp, #0xcc`, spills round/state words throughout that frame (for example `str r2, [sp, #200]` plus many `str/strd` to `sp+...`), then returns via `add sp, sp, #0xcc; pop ...` with no wipe. Repo search finds no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero` in this asm file. `sha3.c` selects this backend via `SHA3_BLOCK = BlockSha3` and uses it for SHA3 and SHAKE operations; those paths feed secret-derived data from HMAC-SHA3 and SHAKE-based Ed448/ML-KEM/Dilithium/SLH-DSA code. - impact: key-mixed HMAC blocks, SHAKE sponge state, and PQ/Ed448 secret-derived intermediates remain resident on thread stacks after permutation rounds, increasing post-compromise key/material exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics. - recommendation: add a compiler-safe zeroization epilogue for both asm variants before restoring `sp`/returning, or wrap the asm backend so the temporary frame is scrubbed on every exit; keep `WC_SHA3_NO_ASM` as a safe fallback until the asm path is wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Thumb2 SHA3 asm leaves the full Keccak state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-sha3-asm.S:93-1164`; call paths `wolfcrypt/src/sha3.c:769-805,890-902,1866,2107`, secret use `wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.c:847-875,3516,7721,8158,8288,8444,8568` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 32-bit ARM Thumb2 build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && WOLFSSL_SHA3`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `BlockSha3` allocates a `0xcc` frame (`SUB sp, sp, #0xcc`), spills nearly the entire 25-lane Keccak state and round temporaries to `[sp+0..200]` via many `STR/STRD`, then returns with `ADD sp, sp, #0xcc; POP {r4, r5, r6, r7, r8, r9, r10, r11, pc}` and no scrub. `sha3.c` dispatches SHA3/SHAKE through this backend, and Dilithium calls `BlockSha3(state)` in `dilithium_squeeze256()` / related paths that absorb `priv_seed` and `priv_rand_seed` during keygen/signing. - impact: stale thread stacks retain secret SHA3/SHAKE state, including Dilithium private-seed and per-signature randomness state, increasing key/nonce exposure under secondary memory disclosure. - recommendation: zero the full `0xcc` frame before restoring `sp`, or wrap the asm backend in a compiler-resistant scrub epilogue and add a regression/codegen check so secret-bearing SHA3 asm paths stay wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - ARMv8-32 Poly1305 asm leaves key- and message-derived multiplication intermediates on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-poly1305-asm.S:38-247,352-561,813-1005`; call paths `wolfcrypt/src/poly1305.c:289,292,504,552,606-663`, AEAD users `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:180,216,256,301,346,350,426,448,452` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 32-bit ARM build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM && !__aarch64__ && !WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && HAVE_POLY1305`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: the scalar block helpers allocate stack scratch (`sub sp, sp, #28`, `sub sp, sp, #12`), save parameters and key/message-derived partial products with `stm lr, {r0, r1, r2, r3}`, `stm sp, {r10, r11, r12}` and `stm sp, {r5, r6, r12}`, then return via plain `add sp, sp, #28; pop ...` / `pop ...` with no scrub. `poly1305.c` routes `wc_Poly1305SetKey/Update/Final` through `poly1305_arm32_blocks[_16]` on this backend, and ChaCha20-Poly1305/XChaCha20-Poly1305 feed all tag computation through those APIs. - impact: stale stacks retain Poly1305 accumulator state and multiplication intermediates derived from the one-time authenticator key and message blocks, widening AEAD integrity/confidentiality exposure under secondary memory disclosure. - recommendation: zero stack scratch on all block-helper return paths before restoring `sp`; keep the context zeroization in `poly1305_final()` as a second layer, not the only scrub. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-chacha-asm.S` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-chacha-asm.S`, consumers `wolfcrypt/src/chacha.c:138,215,374,383`, layout `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/chacha.h:83,88,91` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `wc_chacha_crypt_bytes` uses its `160`-byte stack frame only for saved callee-saved registers (`stp x29,x30 ...`, `stp d8..d15 ...`); the ChaCha state/keystream stays in GPR/SIMD regs and is not copied to stack. The partial-block path intentionally writes leftover keystream to `ctx->over` via `add x4, x0, #0x44` and `str w5, [x0, #64]`, matching `ChaCha` layout (`left` at offset 64, `over` after it) and `chacha.c`’s `ctx->left/ctx->over` consumption. `wc_chacha_setiv` stores the counter/nonce into `X[12..15]` at offsets `48/52/60`, and `wc_chacha_setkey` retains the expected `BIG_ENDIAN_ORDER` swaps. No concrete trust-boundary bug, memory-safety issue, or material perf problem was found in this chunk.

### Low - ARMv8-32 ML-KEM asm backend silently assumes little-endian word layout but is selected on any 32-bit `WOLFSSL_ARMASM` build - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-mlkem-asm.S`; selection `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/wc_mlkem.h:340-345` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the asm treats two `sword16` coefficients as the low/high halves of a 32-bit `ldr` word throughout `mlkem_arm32_ntt` / `mlkem_arm32_invntt` / `mlkem_arm32_basemul_mont` / `mlkem_arm32_csubq` using `smulbb`/`smulbt`/`pkhtb`/`bfi`, and `mlkem_arm32_rej_uniform` extracts 12-bit samples from 32-bit loads with `ubfx`/`bfi`. The file has no `BIG_ENDIAN_ORDER` handling, while `wc_mlkem.h` maps `mlkem_ntt`, `mlkem_invntt`, `mlkem_basemul_mont`, `mlkem_basemul_mont_add`, and `mlkem_rej_uniform_c` directly to this arm32 backend under `#elif defined(WOLFSSL_ARMASM)` with no endian guard. The generic ML-KEM C code does carry explicit `BIG_ENDIAN_ORDER` branches in `wolfcrypt/src/wc_mlkem_poly.c` for endian-sensitive byte interpretation. - impact: big-endian 32-bit ARM builds can silently generate non-standard ML-KEM polynomials/ciphertexts/shared secrets, causing keygen/encap/decap interoperability failures that are hard to diagnose. - recommendation: gate this backend to little-endian ARM only (`!BIG_ENDIAN_ORDER`) or add explicit byte-order conversions plus regression tests on big-endian coefficient/rejection paths. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - ARMv8-32 ChaCha asm leaves recent keystream and ChaCha20-Poly1305 one-time-key material on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-chacha-asm.S:109-448,521-1152`; call path `wolfcrypt/src/chacha.c:383`; keyed use `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:174,194,339,362` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 32-bit ARM build using this asm backend; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: both `wc_chacha_crypt_bytes` variants allocate local frames (`sub sp, sp, #52` and `sub sp, sp, #44`), spill secret-derived round/keystream state onto them (`stm sp, {r8, r9, r10, r11, r12}`; `str lr/[r8]/[r9]/[r11], [sp, ...]`), then return via plain `add sp, sp, ...; pop` with no scrub; repo search finds no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero` in this file. `wc_Chacha_Process()` dispatches into this asm path, and ChaCha20-Poly1305 derives the Poly1305 `authKey` by calling `wc_Chacha_Process(&aead->chacha, authKey, authKey, 32)` before wiping only the caller-side `authKey` buffer. - impact: stale stack contents can expose recent ChaCha keystream bytes and, during AEAD setup, the Poly1305 one-time key for the active nonce/message, enabling plaintext recovery for those bytes and tag forgery for that message once leaked. - recommendation: zero the temporary stack frame before restoring `sp`/returning (or wrap the asm with a compiler-resistant scrub epilogue) and add a regression/codegen check so secret-bearing ChaCha asm paths stay wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Inline Thumb2 X25519/Ed25519 backend leaves secret ladder, scalar, and nonce intermediates on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-curve25519_c.c:2773,3271,3679,4414,5173,5608,5921,6718`; callers `wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.c:197,364,744`, `wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.c:465,513` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Thumb2 ARM build uses the inline-asm backend (`WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE` selects `thumb2-curve25519_c.c` in `cmake/functions.cmake:956,959`); attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: secret-bearing helpers in this file allocate large local frames (`curve25519`: `SUB sp, sp, #0xbc` / `#0xc0`; `fe_invert`: `#0x88`; `fe_pow22523`: `#0x68`; `sc_reduce`: `#0x38`; `sc_muladd`: `#0x50`). Repo search finds no `ForceZero`, `wc_MemZero`, or `memset` anywhere in `thumb2-curve25519_c.c`, so these temporaries are returned intact. Those helpers process X25519 private scalars and Ed25519 nonce/hash/private-scalar material via the listed callers. - impact: private scalar, ladder, reduction, and nonce state persists on thread stacks after use, materially widening key-recovery exposure under secondary memory-disclosure or crash-forensics conditions. - recommendation: add compiler-safe zeroization epilogues before each `ADD sp, sp, ...` return path, or route secret-bearing routines through wrappers that scrub the temporary frame. Keep the non-inline constant-time C path as fallback until the Thumb2 inline backend is wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Low - `WC_NO_CACHE_RESISTANT` reintroduces secret-dependent Ed25519 table fetches in the inline Thumb2 backend - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-curve25519_c.c:1577-1641`; selection path `cmake/functions.cmake:956,959` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables both `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE` and `WC_NO_CACHE_RESISTANT` on a cacheful/shared Thumb2 target where an attacker can observe timing/cache state during Ed25519 make-key or signing. - evidence: the non-cache-resistant `fe_cmov_table()` branch computes a secret-derived table offset with `CLZ lr, %[b]`, multiplies it into `%[base]`, then fetches only that chosen slot via `LDM %[base]! ...`; the guarded constant-time branch exists immediately above under `#ifndef WC_NO_CACHE_RESISTANT`. `b` comes from `ge_select()` / `ge_scalarmult_base()` digits used by Ed25519 signing/public-key generation (`wolfcrypt/src/ge_operations.c:9073-9094,9106-9166`, `wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.c:286,469`). - impact: secret digits influence memory addresses and cache footprint, reopening timing/cache side channels against Ed25519 private-key operations on builds that take the inline backend. - recommendation: do not ship `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE` Thumb2 Ed25519 with `WC_NO_CACHE_RESISTANT` on shared/cacheful targets; prefer the constant-time branch or fail the build for this combination. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - Thumb2 IAR ML-KEM rejection sampler returns the destination pointer instead of the accepted-sample count - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-mlkem-asm_c.c:38,3508-3855`; consumers `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/wc_mlkem.h:329`, `wolfcrypt/src/wc_mlkem_poly.c:3489-3499,3691-3699` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Thumb2 inline-asm build under IAR (`__IAR_SYSTEMS_ICC__` forces `WOLFSSL_NO_VAR_ASSIGN_REG`). - evidence: this file defines `WOLFSSL_NO_VAR_ASSIGN_REG` for IAR (`thumb2-mlkem-asm_c.c:38`). In `mlkem_thumb2_rej_uniform()`, the asm computes the result only with hardcoded `LSR r0, r9, #1`, but `r0` is not tied to an output/clobber; the C epilogue then does `return (word32)(size_t)p;` (`thumb2-mlkem-asm_c.c:3855`). `wc_mlkem.h` maps `mlkem_rej_uniform_c` to this backend on Thumb2 (`wc_mlkem.h:329`), and matrix generation uses the return as a coefficient count via `ctr = mlkem_rej_uniform_c(...); while (ctr < MLKEM_N) { ctr += mlkem_rej_uniform_c(...); }` (`wc_mlkem_poly.c:3489-3499,3691-3699`). - impact: on IAR Thumb2 builds the helper can return the advanced destination pointer instead of the number of accepted coefficients, so refill loops can terminate early and proceed with partially generated ML-KEM polynomials, causing invalid/non-deterministic keygen, encapsulation, and decapsulation behavior. - recommendation: return the accepted count through a real C output operand (or compute it from `len`/`r9` into a named output variable) instead of an undeclared hardcoded `r0`, and add an IAR Thumb2 regression test that checks `mlkem_rej_uniform_c()` and matrix generation counts. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Inline ARMv8-32 SHA3 backend leaves SHA3/SHAKE/HMAC/PQ intermediate state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-sha3-asm_c.c:86,121,343,393,398,2356`; selection `cmake/functions.cmake:481,740` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE` on 32-bit ARM with `WOLFSSL_SHA3`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: the inline-asm `BlockSha3()` variants allocate stack scratch (`sub sp, sp, #16` in the NEON path; `sub sp, sp, #0xcc` in the scalar path), spill round/state words to that frame (`vst1.8 {d25-d26}, [r3]`, `str r2, [sp, #200]`, many `str/strd` to `sp+...`), and return via plain `add sp, sp, ...` with no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero` in the file. `cmake/functions.cmake` selects this `_c.c` backend for inline ARM asm builds. - impact: stale stacks retain key-mixed HMAC-SHA3 state and SHAKE state used by Ed448/ML-KEM/Dilithium/SLH-DSA code, increasing secret-material exposure under secondary memory disclosure. - recommendation: add a compiler-safe zeroization epilogue for both inline asm variants before restoring `sp`, or disable this backend until the temporary frame is scrubbed. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Thumb2 ChaCha asm leaves keystream and ChaCha20-Poly1305 one-time-key material on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-chacha-asm.S:116-125,255,378-385,467-468`; call path `wolfcrypt/src/chacha.c:315,336,374,383`; AEAD key use `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:174,194,339-340,362` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Thumb2 ARM build using this asm backend; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `wc_chacha_crypt_bytes` allocates a `0x34` frame, spills `x[12..15]` with `STRD ... [sp,#16/#24]`, later writes the generated block back to the frame with `STM sp, {r8, r9, r10, r11, r12}`, and returns via `ADD sp, sp, #0x34; POP ...` without any wipe. The short-block path copies leftover keystream into `ctx->over` (`ADD r12, lr, #0x44`, `STR r12, [lr, #64]`). `wc_Chacha_Process()` uses this backend, and ChaCha20-Poly1305 derives its Poly1305 `authKey` by calling `wc_Chacha_Process(&aead->chacha, authKey, authKey, 32)` before wiping only the caller-side `authKey` buffer. - impact: prior stack contents can expose recent ChaCha keystream and, during AEAD setup, the Poly1305 one-time authenticator key for the active nonce/message, enabling plaintext recovery for affected bytes and tag forgery for that message once leaked. - recommendation: zero the temporary frame before restoring `sp`/returning, or wrap the Thumb2 asm backend in a compiler-resistant scrub epilogue and add regression coverage for AEAD auth-key derivation paths. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - AArch64 `poly1305_final` incompletely zeroizes the precomputed `r` schedule, leaving secret limbs in the context after MAC finalization - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-poly1305-asm.S:485,499-501,539-541,589-591,639-641,690-704`; layout `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/poly1305.h:91-99`; callers `wolfcrypt/src/poly1305.c:552,663` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_ARMASM && __aarch64__` build; attacker can read or reuse a `Poly1305` context after `wc_Poly1305Final()`. - evidence: `poly1305_set_key` stores precomputed key material at `[x0,#16..31]`, `[x0,#32..47]`, `[x0,#48..63]`, `[x0,#64..79]`, plus top limbs at `[x0,#80]`, `#84`, `#88`, `#92`. `poly1305_final` then zeros `[x0,#96..119]`, `[x0,#0..15]`, `[x0,#16..31]`, `[x0,#48..63]`, `[x0,#64..71]`, `[x0,#32..39]`, and `[x0,#120..135]`, but never touches `[x0,#40..47]`, `[x0,#72..79]`, or `[x0,#80..95]`. The file even comments `# Zero out r.`, `# Zero out r_2.`, `# Zero out r_4.` while skipping those slots. - impact: finalized contexts retain secret Poly1305 key-schedule material across reuse/free, worsening post-compromise memory disclosure and defeating intended key scrubbing on AArch64 builds. - recommendation: zero the full AArch64 precompute region (`r4/r3/r2/r1/r4321`) or clear the whole `Poly1305` context, including `buffer/leftover/finished`, after finalization. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - AArch64 fallback SHA3 asm spills secret-derived Keccak intermediates to stack and returns without scrubbing - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-sha3-asm.S:258-448` (`BlockSha3_base`); dispatch/use `wolfcrypt/src/sha3.c:679-686,767,803,888,900,1864,2105`; secret-bearing callers include `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c` SHA3 cases and SHAKE users in `wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.c:468,592,596,649,653,657,661,820,884,2416,3492`, `wolfcrypt/src/wc_mlkem_poly.c:2894,3101,3202,3209,3212`, `wolfcrypt/src/ed448.c:123` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: AArch64 build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM && WOLFSSL_SHA3` that takes the non-crypto fallback (`!IS_AARCH64_SHA3(cpuid_flags)` or no `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_CRYPTO_SHA3`); attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `BlockSha3_base` allocates a `160`-byte frame (`stp x29, x30, [sp, #-160]!`), then stores state-derived column/parity words to stack each round (`str x0, [x29, #32]`, `str x28, [x29, #24]`) and exits with `ldp x29, x30, [sp], #0xa0; ret` without any wipe. `sha3.c` selects `BlockSha3_base` as `SHA3_BLOCK` on AArch64 when the SHA3 extension is unavailable, and routes both SHA3 and SHAKE absorbs/squeezes through it. - impact: stale thread stacks retain 128 bits of secret-derived Keccak state from HMAC-SHA3 and SHAKE-based Ed448/PQ operations, increasing secret-material exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics on affected AArch64 systems. - recommendation: keep those temporaries in registers or zero the temporary frame before return; if that is not practical, prefer the crypto-extension path or the C fallback for secret-bearing SHA3/SHAKE uses until the asm fallback is scrubbed. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Inline ARMv8-32 SHA256 backend leaves HMAC/key-mixed blocks and digest state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-sha256-asm_c.c:97-2572`; selection `cmake/functions.cmake:425,585`; call sites `wolfcrypt/src/sha256.c:1127-1144`; keyed use `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:370,668,958,1144-1151` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE` on 32-bit ARM with the non-NEON SHA256 path (`WOLFSSL_ARMASM && !__aarch64__ && !WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_NO_NEON && !NO_SHA256`); attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: the inline `Transform_Sha256_Len_base()` allocates a `0xc0` stack frame (`armv8-32-sha256-asm_c.c:97`), writes the message schedule at `sp+0..60` and copied digest/state words at `sp+64..92` (`127`, `145`, many `str/strd` to `[sp,...]`), then returns via plain `add sp, sp, #0xc0` (`2572`) with no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero`/`memset` anywhere in the file. `sha256.c` dispatches SHA256 updates to this backend, and HMAC feeds key-mixed `pad`, `opad`, and `innerHash` through `wc_Sha256Update()`. - impact: prior HMAC blocks and intermediate SHA256 state remain resident on thread stacks after the transform returns, increasing secret exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics on affected inline-asm ARM builds. - recommendation: add a compiler-resistant zeroization epilogue before restoring `sp`, or wrap the inline asm backend so the temporary frame is scrubbed on every exit; keep the NEON/crypto or plain C path as fallback until the base inline backend is wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Inline Thumb2 ChaCha backend leaves keystream and ChaCha20-Poly1305 one-time-key material on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-chacha-asm_c.c:169-585`; selection `cmake/functions.cmake:896`; call path `wolfcrypt/src/chacha.c:383`; AEAD key use `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:152-194` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && HAVE_CHACHA`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `wc_chacha_crypt_bytes()` allocates a `0x34` stack frame (`thumb2-chacha-asm_c.c:169`), spills `x[12..15]` to `[sp,#16/#24]` (`182-183`), later writes generated block words back to the frame with `STM sp, {r8, r9, r10, r11, r12}` (`320`), and returns via plain `ADD sp, sp, #0x34` (`585`). Repo search finds no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero`/`memset` in this file. `wc_Chacha_Process()` dispatches to this backend (`chacha.c:383`), and ChaCha20-Poly1305 derives the Poly1305 `authKey` by calling `wc_Chacha_Process(&aead->chacha, authKey, authKey, 32)` before wiping only the caller-side `authKey` buffer (`chacha20_poly1305.c:152-194`). - impact: stale stack contents can expose recent ChaCha keystream and, during AEAD setup, the Poly1305 one-time authenticator key for the active nonce/message, enabling plaintext recovery for affected bytes and tag forgery for that message once leaked. - recommendation: add a compiler-resistant zeroization epilogue before restoring `sp`, or disable this inline backend in favor of the C/non-inline path until the temporary frame is scrubbed. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Inline Thumb2 SHA256 backend leaves HMAC/key-mixed blocks and digest state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-sha256-asm_c.c:91-2470`; selection `cmake/functions.cmake:435,595`; call sites `wolfcrypt/src/sha256.c:1126-1140`; keyed use `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:370,668,958,1144-1151` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_NO_NEON && !NO_SHA256`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `Transform_Sha256_Len_base()` allocates a `0xc0` frame (`thumb2-sha256-asm_c.c:91`), stores copied digest/state words at `sp+64..88` (`98-101`) and the message schedule throughout `sp+0..60`, then returns with plain `ADD sp, sp, #0xc0` (`2470`). Repo search finds no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero`/`memset` in this file. `sha256.c` dispatches updates to this backend under Thumb2 (`1126-1140`), and HMAC feeds `pad`, `opad`, and `innerHash` through `wc_Sha256Update()` (`hmac.c:370,668,958,1144-1151`). - impact: prior HMAC blocks and intermediate SHA256 state remain resident on thread stacks after the transform returns, increasing secret exposure under secondary memory disclosure or crash forensics on affected inline-asm Thumb2 builds. - recommendation: zero the temporary frame before restoring `sp`, or route the inline backend through a compiler-safe scrub wrapper/codegen rule; keep the plain C or non-inline backend as fallback until this path is wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Thumb2 Poly1305 asm leaves key- and message-derived multiplication intermediates on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-poly1305-asm.S:38-256`; dispatch/use `wolfcrypt/src/poly1305.c:285,501,552,663`, AEAD users `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:180,216,256,301,346,350,426,448,452` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Thumb2 ARM build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && !WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE && HAVE_POLY1305`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `poly1305_blocks_thumb2_16` allocates a `0x1c` frame (`SUB sp, sp, #0x1c`), spills parameters and block/multiply state with `STM lr, {r0, r1, r2, r3}` and `STM sp, {r10, r11, r12}`, then returns via plain `ADD sp, sp, #0x1c; POP {..., pc}`. Repo search finds no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero` in this asm file. `wc_Poly1305Update/Final` route Thumb2 blocks through this helper. - impact: stale thread stacks retain Poly1305 accumulator/multiplication state derived from the one-time key and message blocks, widening ChaCha20-Poly1305 authenticator-state exposure under secondary memory disclosure. - recommendation: zero the temporary frame on all return paths before restoring `sp`, or wrap the Thumb2 helper in a compiler-resistant scrub epilogue; keep context zeroization in `poly1305_final()` as a second layer, not the only wipe. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`
