### Medium - Inline ARMv8-32 ChaCha backend leaves recent keystream and ChaCha20-Poly1305 one-time-key material on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-chacha-asm_c.c:161-523,625-1286`; selection `cmake/functions.cmake:886`; call path `wolfcrypt/src/chacha.c:383`; keyed use `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:174,194,339,362` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: 32-bit ARM inline-asm build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE && WOLFSSL_ARMASM && !__aarch64__ && !WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: both inline `wc_chacha_crypt_bytes` variants allocate stack frames (`sub sp, sp, #52` and `sub sp, sp, #44`), spill round/keystream state into them (`stm sp, {r8, r9, r10, r11, r12}`, `str r11, [sp, #20]`, `str r8, [sp, #12]`, `vstm r5, {q0-q3}` for the short-block path), and return via plain `add sp, sp, ...` with no scrub; repo search finds no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero` in this file. `wc_Chacha_Process()` dispatches here, and ChaCha20-Poly1305 derives its Poly1305 `authKey` by calling `wc_Chacha_Process(&aead->chacha, authKey, authKey, 32)` before wiping only the caller-side buffer. - impact: stale stacks can expose recent ChaCha keystream bytes and, during AEAD setup, the Poly1305 one-time authenticator key for the active nonce/message, enabling plaintext recovery for affected bytes and tag forgery for that message once leaked. - recommendation: zero the temporary stack frame before every return (or wrap the inline asm with a compiler-resistant scrub epilogue) and add regression/codegen checks so secret-bearing ChaCha asm paths stay wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Inline Thumb2 SHA512 backend leaves HMAC/key-mixed blocks and digest state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-sha512-asm_c.c:115-3584`; selection `cmake/functions.cmake:462,720`; call sites `wolfcrypt/src/sha512.c:800,847`; keyed use `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:381,1185,1192` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_NO_NEON && (WOLFSSL_SHA512 || WOLFSSL_SHA384)`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `Transform_Sha512_Len_base()` allocates a `0xc0` frame (`thumb2-sha512-asm_c.c:115`), copies digest state to `sp+128..184` (`122-129`) and the message schedule to `sp+0..124` (`158+`), then exits with only `EOR r0, r0, r0; ADD sp, sp, #0xc0` (`3583-3584`). Repo search finds no `ForceZero`, `wc_MemZero`, or `memset` in this file. `sha512.c` dispatches SHA512 updates to this backend, and HMAC feeds key-mixed `pad`, `opad`, and `innerHash` blocks through `wc_Sha512Update()`. - impact: stale thread stacks retain prior SHA512 block data and intermediate state, including HMAC key-derived blocks, increasing secret-material exposure under secondary memory disclosure on affected Thumb2 inline-asm builds. - recommendation: zero the full temporary frame before restoring `sp`, or wrap the inline asm backend in a compiler-resistant scrub epilogue; keep the plain C or non-inline backend as fallback until this path is wiped. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Inline Thumb2 Poly1305 backend leaves key- and message-derived multiplication intermediates on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-poly1305-asm_c.c:64-291`; selection `cmake/functions.cmake:818`; dispatch/use `wolfcrypt/src/poly1305.c:285,501,552,663`; AEAD users `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:180,216,256,301,346,350,426,448,452` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Thumb2 ARM inline-asm build with `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && HAVE_POLY1305`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: `poly1305_blocks_thumb2_16()` allocates a `0x1c` frame (`SUB sp, sp, #0x1c`), spills state/arguments to that frame (`STM lr, {%[ctx], %[m], %[len], %[notLast]}` at `sp+0xc`, plus `STM sp, {r10, r11, r12}` for multiplication temporaries), and returns via plain `ADD sp, sp, #0x1c` with no `ForceZero`/`wc_MemZero`/`memset` anywhere in `thumb2-poly1305-asm_c.c`. `poly1305.c` routes Thumb2 block processing through this helper, and ChaCha20-Poly1305 drives tag computation through those APIs. - impact: stale thread stacks retain Poly1305 accumulator/multiplication state derived from the one-time key and message blocks, widening AEAD authenticator-state exposure under secondary memory disclosure. - recommendation: zero the temporary frame on every return path before restoring `sp`, or wrap the inline Thumb2 helper in a compiler-resistant scrub epilogue; keep `poly1305_final()` context zeroization as a second layer, not the only wipe. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Inline Thumb2 SHA3 backend leaves the full Keccak state on the stack after return - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/thumb2-sha3-asm_c.c:80-1155`; selection `cmake/functions.cmake:490,749`; call paths `wolfcrypt/src/sha3.c:769-805,890-902,1866,2107` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE && WOLFSSL_ARMASM_THUMB2 && WOLFSSL_SHA3`; attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure, crash/core access, or another read primitive over stale stack memory. - evidence: inline `BlockSha3()` allocates a `0xcc` frame (`SUB sp, sp, #0xcc`), spills nearly the whole 25-lane Keccak state and round temporaries throughout `[sp+0..200]`, then returns with plain `ADD sp, sp, #0xcc`. Repo search finds no `ForceZero`, `wc_MemZero`, or `memset` in this file. - impact: stale thread stacks retain secret SHA3/SHAKE state, including HMAC-SHA3 state and PQ seed/randomness state processed through SHAKE users, increasing key/nonce exposure under secondary memory disclosure. - recommendation: zero the full `0xcc` frame before restoring `sp`, or disable this inline backend until the temporary frame is scrubbed. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Inline AArch64 fallback SHA3 backend spills secret-derived Keccak intermediates to stack and returns without scrubbing - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-sha3-asm_c.c:183-358`; selection `cmake/functions.cmake:409,480,739`; dispatch `wolfcrypt/src/sha3.c:680,686` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE && __aarch64__ && WOLFSSL_SHA3`; runtime takes `BlockSha3_base` instead of the SHA3-extension path, and attacker later gains same-process stack disclosure or crash/core access. - evidence: `BlockSha3_base()` allocates a `64`-byte frame (`stp x29, x30, [sp, #-64]!`), stores the state pointer and state-derived parity/column words to `[x29,#24/#32/#40/#48]`, then exits with `ldp x29, x30, [sp], #0x40` and no wipe. Repo search found no `ForceZero`, `wc_MemZero`, or `memset` in this file. - impact: stale stacks retain secret-derived Keccak intermediates from HMAC-SHA3 and SHAKE-based Ed448/ML-KEM/Dilithium/SLH-DSA operations, increasing post-compromise secret-material exposure on affected AArch64 builds. - recommendation: keep those temporaries in registers or zero the 64-byte frame before return; prefer the crypto-extension path or plain C fallback for secret-bearing SHA3/SHAKE uses until fixed. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Inline AArch64 `poly1305_final()` incompletely zeroizes the precomputed `r` schedule, leaving secret limbs in the context after MAC finalization - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-poly1305-asm_c.c:447-457,495-497,545-547,595-597,612-652`; layout `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/poly1305.h:91-102`; callers `wolfcrypt/src/poly1305.c:552,663` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE && __aarch64__` build; attacker can read or reuse a `Poly1305` context after `wc_Poly1305Final()`. - evidence: `poly1305_set_key()` stores `r64` at `[ctx]`, then precomputed limbs at `#64/#92`, `#48/#88`, `#32/#84`, and `#16/#80`. `poly1305_final()` zeros `[ctx]`, `#16..31`, `#48..63`, `#64..71`, `#32..39`, `#96..112`, and `#120..135`, but leaves `#40..47`, `#72..79`, and `#80..95` untouched; it even duplicates the `#16` clear instead of covering the remaining slots. - impact: finalized contexts retain secret Poly1305 key-schedule material across reuse/free, weakening intended key scrubbing and increasing post-compromise forgery exposure if the context memory is later disclosed. - recommendation: zero the entire AArch64 precompute region or clear the whole `Poly1305` context, including `buffer`, `leftover`, and `finished`, after finalization. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-chacha-asm_c.c` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-chacha-asm_c.c`, consumers `wolfcrypt/src/chacha.c:138,215,374,383`, layout `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/chacha.h:82,88` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this inline AArch64 ChaCha path does not allocate a stack scratch frame; state and keystream stay in GPR/SIMD registers. The partial-block path intentionally stores leftover keystream to the context with `add x4, ctx, #0x44` and `str rol8, [ctx,#64]`, matching `ChaCha`’s `left` field and `ctx->over` consumption in `chacha.c:332-374`. `wc_chacha_setiv()` stores counter/nonce at offsets `48/52/60`, matching `chacha.c:138`. No concrete trust-boundary bug, stack disclosure issue, or material performance problem was found in this chunk.

### Low - CryptoCell SHA256 backend leaves the hardware hash context in memory because `wc_Sha256Free()` is a no-op - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/cryptoCellHash.c:121-125`, selected via `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:4231-4232`, state field `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sha256.h:227` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_CRYPTOCELL`; caller relies on `wc_Sha256Free()` to dispose a `wc_Sha256` object; attacker later obtains the object memory through reuse, heap disclosure, or crash dump. - evidence: this backend's `wc_Sha256Free()` only null-checks and returns, while the CryptoCell-flavored `wc_Sha256` struct embeds `CRYS_HASHUserContext_t ctx;`. The generic software SHA256 path does scrub the context (`ForceZero(sha256, sizeof(*sha256))` at `wolfcrypt/src/sha256.c:2494`). `wc_HmacFree()` mitigates its own wrapper by later zeroing the whole `Hmac` (`wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:1452,1532`), so the exposure is raw/embedded `wc_Sha256` users that depend on the free hook itself. - impact: in-progress SHA256 state, including buffered message bytes and internal digest state, can survive after API-level free on CryptoCell builds, increasing post-compromise disclosure of hashed secret material or partial plaintext under later memory disclosure. - recommendation: make the CryptoCell backend scrub the entire `wc_Sha256` object (or at least `ctx` and digest buffers) in `wc_Sha256Free()` to match the generic backend's cleanup behavior. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-11 Cryptography`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-sha256-asm_c.c` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-sha256-asm_c.c` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: both inline AArch64 SHA256 entry points (`Transform_Sha256_Len_neon` and `_crypto`) operate on message schedule and working state in GPR/SIMD registers (`w4-w24`, `v0-v25`) and the inline asm contains no `sp` adjustment or stack scratch frame. The file only loads constants/input blocks and stores the final digest back to `sha256`. No concrete trust-boundary bug, stale-stack copy, or material perf issue was found in this chunk beyond already-recorded 32-bit ARM SHA256 findings.

### Medium - Inline ARMv8-32 Poly1305 backend leaves precomputed one-time key schedules in `Poly1305` after `wc_Poly1305Final()` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-32-poly1305-asm_c.c:1297-1354,1423-1435`; layout `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/poly1305.h:109-113`; call paths `wolfcrypt/src/poly1305.c:552,663`, AEAD users `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:180,301,346,448,452` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: inline ARM32 NEON Poly1305 build (`WOLFSSL_ARMASM_INLINE`, not `WOLFSSL_ARMASM_NO_NEON`); attacker can read a finalized/reused `Poly1305` context from heap/stack after `wc_Poly1305Final()`. - evidence: `poly1305_set_key()` stores extra precomputes at `ctx+0x7c` (`vstm.32 lr, {d0-d4}`) and `ctx+0xa4` (`vstm.32 lr, {d10-d14}`), which align with the `r_21[10]` / `r_43[10]` arrays in the 32-bit `Poly1305` layout. `poly1305_final()` then zeroes only `h` (`ctx+16`), base `r` (`ctx+0`), and padding (`ctx+40`); it never clears those precompute regions. - impact: finalized contexts retain secret Poly1305 key schedule material for the active key/nonce, defeating intended scrubbing and widening post-compromise tag-forgery/material-disclosure risk for ChaCha20-Poly1305 and raw Poly1305 users. - recommendation: zero the full ARM32 NEON key schedule (`r`, `pad`, `r_21`, `r_43`) and preferably `leftover`/`buffer`/`finished`, or clear the whole context after finalization. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### High - CryptoCell backend becomes permanently one-way after first cleanup because `cc310_Init()`'s static `initialized` flag is never reset - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/cryptoCell.c:69-94,97-108`; lifecycle callers `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:300,539` - category: complexity - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_CRYPTOCELL` build; process calls `wolfCrypt_Init()` again after a full `wolfCrypt_Cleanup()` cycle. - evidence: `cc310_Init()` uses a function-local `static int initialized = 0`; once set, later calls skip `cc310_enable()`, `SaSi_LibInit()`, and `CRYS_RndInit()`. `cc310_Free()` calls `SaSi_LibFini()`, `CRYS_RND_UnInstantiation(&wc_rndState)`, and `cc310_disable()`, but cannot clear that static flag. `wc_port.c` still routes subsequent library init/cleanup cycles through `cc310_Init()` / `cc310_Free()`. - impact: after one successful init+cleanup cycle, later re-init returns success without re-enabling the HW or re-instantiating RNG state; subsequent CryptoCell-backed RNG/ECC/RSA operations run against torn-down state and can fail unpredictably for the rest of the process. - recommendation: move `initialized` to file scope and clear it in `cc310_Free()`, or replace it with a single refcounted state machine that fully reinitializes on the first post-cleanup init. Add a regression test for init/cleanup/re-init. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - CryptoCell init failure leaks an enable reference and leaves CC310 IRQ/HW enabled - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/cryptoCell.c:48-60,69-90`; entry via `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:300` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_CRYPTOCELL` build; `SaSi_LibInit()` or `CRYS_RndInit()` fails even transiently. - evidence: `cc310_enable()` increments `cc310_enableCount` and enables `NRF_CRYPTOCELL`/IRQ before any fallible init. On either `SaSi_LibInit()` or `CRYS_RndInit()` failure, `cc310_Init()` returns immediately without `cc310_disable()` and without decrementing the count. - impact: failed init attempts leave the hardware block and interrupt enabled and the reference count inflated; later success+cleanup cycles may never drive the count back to zero, causing persistent power/IRQ leakage and hard-to-debug low-power/interrupt behavior on embedded targets. - recommendation: only increment/enable after successful library/RNG init, or add a rollback path that calls `cc310_disable()` on every init failure before returning. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-sha512-asm_c.c` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/arm/armv8-sha512-asm_c.c`, callers `wolfcrypt/src/sha512.c:788,794` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: repo search found no `sp`/stack-frame use or zeroization gaps in this inline AArch64 file; both `Transform_Sha512_Len_neon()` and `Transform_Sha512_Len_crypto()` operate in GPR/SIMD registers and store only the final digest back to `sha512`. Unlike the already-flagged 32-bit SHA512 backends, no concrete secret-on-stack copy, trust-boundary bug, or material performance issue was found in this chunk.

### High - Shared sniffer state is used after free under concurrent decode/remove/cleanup, and `HAVE_C___ATOMIC` disables the only list locks - location: `src/sniffer.c:568-578,645-651,1543-1568,1601-1643,5165-5209,5243-5338,6840-6950,7754-7850` - category: complexity - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: shared sniffer state used from multiple threads (for example packet decode racing `ssl_RemoveSession()`/`ssl_FreeSniffer()` or async polling); on common GCC/Clang builds `HAVE_C___ATOMIC` makes `LOCK_SESSION()` / `LOCK_SERVER_LIST()` no-ops. - evidence: `ServerList`, `SessionTable`, and `SessionCount` are plain globals, but under `HAVE_C___ATOMIC` the lock macros expand to `WC_DO_NOTHING`. `GetSnifferServer()` and `GetSnifferSession()` return raw pointers after unlock; callers then dereference them long after lookup (`CreateSession()` uses `session->context->ctx`, `ssl_DecodePacketInternal()` keeps using `session` across sequence checks and `ProcessMessage()`). The same objects are freed concurrently by `RemoveSession()`, `ssl_RemoveSession()`, and `ssl_FreeSniffer()`. - impact: concurrent control-plane or cleanup activity can free `SnifferServer` / `SnifferSession` / embedded `WOLFSSL` objects while decode still uses them, causing crashes, heap corruption, or stuck async state in production sniffers. - recommendation: do not replace these list/object locks with bare `__atomic_fetch_add` support; keep real mutexes around shared state or add explicit refcounted ownership so lookup never returns an unprotected node. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - IPv6 extension-header parsing reads beyond the packet before any bounds check - location: `src/sniffer.c:2119-2153` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can feed a truncated IPv6 packet with `next_header != TCP_PROTOCOL` into `ssl_DecodePacket*()`. - evidence: `CheckIp6Hdr()` only checks `length < IP6_HDR_SZ`, then when `iphdr->next_header != TCP_PROTOCOL` it sets `exthdr = (Ip6ExtHdr*)((byte*)iphdr + IP6_HDR_SZ)` and immediately reads `exthdr->length` to compute `hdrsz` before verifying that even 1 extension-header byte exists beyond the fixed 40-byte IPv6 header. - impact: malformed/truncated IPv6 frames can trigger out-of-bounds reads during sniffer packet classification, leading to crashes or undefined parser behavior from unauthenticated network input. - recommendation: require at least `sizeof(Ip6ExtHdr)` bytes before every extension-header dereference and track the current `next_header` separately instead of reading fields from unchecked memory. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V5 File Handling`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Client/ServerHello session-id parsing always copies 32 bytes, enabling out-of-bounds reads on short IDs and corrupting resumption state - location: `src/sniffer.c:3731-3737,4095-4109` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can supply malformed Hello messages to the sniffer; the server-hello path is reachable from passive packet input alone. - evidence: after reading the encoded session-id length (`b` / `bLen`), both `ProcessServerHello()` and `ProcessClientHello()` unconditionally `XMEMCPY(..., input, ID_LEN)` and set `sessionIDSz = ID_LEN` instead of the advertised length. `ProcessServerHello()` only validates `b + SUITE_LEN + ENUM_LEN <= *sslBytes`, so a short session-id in a minimally sized ServerHello can make the fixed 32-byte copy read past the packet buffer; both paths also mix following fields into the cached session ID when the real length is `< ID_LEN`. - impact: malicious traffic can crash the sniffer on truncated Hello packets and can poison session-resumption matching by caching non-session-id bytes as the session ID. - recommendation: reject `b`/`bLen > ID_LEN`, require `b`/`bLen <= *sslBytes`, copy only the advertised length, and store the real `sessionIDSz` (zero the remainder if fixed-size storage is needed). - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - AES-GCM/CCM sniffer path intentionally skips AEAD tag verification and forwards unauthenticated plaintext to callers - location: `src/sniffer.c:4821-4907,6437-6542` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can inject or tamper with monitored TLS records that reach the sniffer and fit TCP sequence tracking. - evidence: `DecryptDo()` documents `/* bypass the auth tag checking */` and, for `wolfssl_aes_gcm` / `wolfssl_aes_ccm`, calls `wc_AesGcmEncrypt` / `wc_AesCcmEncrypt` with `NULL, 0` tag output instead of authenticated decrypt/verify. `ProcessMessage()` then treats `errCode == 0` as success, runs `DoApplicationData()`, and copies the resulting bytes to `*data` or `StoreDataCb`. - impact: forged or modified AEAD records are accepted as if genuine plaintext, so an on-path packet injector can corrupt sniffer output, downstream IDS/DLP feeds, or forensic logs without possessing TLS keys. - recommendation: verify AEAD tags by default and make any best-effort/no-auth decode mode an explicit opt-in diagnostic flag with conspicuous API naming. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Keylog secret buckets remain dangling after cleanup, so reloading keylog secrets can walk freed `SecretNode` pointers - location: `src/sniffer.c:865-903,7416-7458,7469-7489,7572-7587,7737-7745` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_SNIFFER_KEYLOGFILE` build; process calls `ssl_FreeSniffer()` and later reloads/uses keylog secrets in the same process. - evidence: `ssl_FreeSniffer()` calls `freeSecretList()`. `freeSecretList()` frees every `SecretNode` in each bucket but never writes `secretHashTable[i] = NULL`. Later `ssl_LoadSecretsFromKeyLogFile()` -> `parseKeyLogFile()` -> `addSecretNode()` / `findSecret()` start from `secretHashTable[idx]` and traverse whatever stale pointer remains in that bucket. - impact: cleanup/re-init with keylog support can dereference freed secret-table nodes, causing crashes or undefined behavior during later secret loads or secret callbacks. - recommendation: null each bucket as it is drained or clear the whole table after free; add a regression test for init/load/free/reload. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `namedKeysMutex` is locked and freed but never initialized on new `SnifferServer` objects - location: `src/sniffer.c:442,801-804,1851-1923,4027-4055` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `SnifferServer` embeds `wolfSSL_Mutex namedKeysMutex`; SNI paths call `wc_LockMutex(&sniffer->namedKeysMutex)` when inserting named keys and `wc_LockMutex(&session->context->namedKeysMutex)` when selecting them, and `FreeSnifferServer()` later calls `wc_FreeMutex(&srv->namedKeysMutex)`. Repo search found no `wc_InitMutex(&sniffer->namedKeysMutex)` anywhere in `src/sniffer.c` when a new `SnifferServer` is allocated/zeroed. - impact: HAVE_SNI builds can hit undefined behavior or immediate crashes on first named-key add/use/cleanup because the mutex object was never initialized. - recommendation: initialize `namedKeysMutex` when creating a new `SnifferServer` and unwind it on allocation/setup failure paths. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Medium - `DSA_set0_*` leaves `dsa->internal` stale, so later sign/export operations can use the previous key instead of the newly assigned one - location: `src/pk.c:1111-1161,1337-1388,1600-1652,1755-1761,2063-2144` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller reuses a `WOLFSSL_DSA` object after it was already synchronized/generated/loaded (`inSet == 1`), then replaces external key material with `wolfSSL_DSA_set0_pqg()` and/or `wolfSSL_DSA_set0_key()`. - evidence: `SetDsaInternal()` is only called when `dsa->inSet == 0` and then sets `dsa->inSet = 1`. Both `wolfSSL_DSA_set0_pqg()` and `wolfSSL_DSA_set0_key()` free/replace the external BIGNUM pointers but never clear `dsa->inSet` or refresh `dsa->internal`. Later paths such as `dsa_do_sign()` and `wolfSSL_PEM_write_mem_DSAPrivateKey()` explicitly skip `SetDsaInternal()` when `inSet` is still 1, so `wc_DsaSign_ex()` / `wc_DsaKeyToDer()` keep using the old internal `DsaKey` while getters/bit-counts observe the new external values. - impact: key/state desynchronization can produce signatures, verification results, and PEM exports for the wrong key material, breaking integrity and making key handling audit-hostile. - recommendation: invalidate and rebuild internal state on every successful `DSA_set0_*` mutation (`dsa->inSet = 0`, `dsa->exSet = 1`), or update the internal `DsaKey` immediately and fail atomically if synchronization fails. Add a regression test that mutates a previously initialized DSA object and proves sign/export use the new values. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_DH_check()` does not validate generator suitability or safe-prime properties, so attacker-chosen weak DH parameters can pass as “valid” - location: `src/pk.c:4288-4414,4542-4583,4731-4839,4845-5028`; test expectations `tests/api/test_ossl_dh.c:496-553` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application accepts DH parameters from an untrusted file/BIO/DER source via `wolfSSL_PEM_read_*DHparams()` / `wolfSSL_DH_LoadDer()` and relies on `wolfSSL_DH_check()` as the gate before keygen/agreement. - evidence: `wolfSSL_DH_check()` only flags missing `g` and non-prime `p`; it never checks whether `g` is actually suitable, whether `p` is a safe prime, or whether subgroup/order constraints hold. The OpenSSL compatibility constants include `DH_CHECK_P_NOT_SAFE_PRIME`, but this function never sets it; the tests only expect `DH_NOT_SUITABLE_GENERATOR` when `g` is missing and `DH_CHECK_P_NOT_PRIME` when `p` is composite. Downstream code then proceeds into `wolfSSL_DH_generate_key()` / `wolfSSL_DH_compute_key[_padded]()` using those parameters. - impact: malicious DH parameters such as `g = 1` or weak-subgroup groups can clear the advertised validation step, enabling predictable/shared-secret confinement or trivially weak handshakes in applications that trust this API for parameter vetting. - recommendation: either implement full parameter validation (safe-prime/order/generator checks, preferably reusing wolfCrypt helpers) or fail closed/return unsupported when only partial validation is available; do not report success for checks that were not performed. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - legacy PEM private-key encryption still derives encryption keys with MD5/SHA-1, making password-protected key files cheap to crack offline - location: `src/pk.c:332-426,475-548,2063-2120,6057-6092,6899-6923`; callers in `src/pk_ec.c:4094`, `src/pk_rsa.c:1312` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller exports a private key with a password/cipher through the legacy PEM APIs and an attacker later obtains the encrypted PEM blob. - evidence: `der_to_enc_pem_alloc()` defaults `hashType` to `WC_MD5` (or `WC_SHA` when MD5 is compiled out) and passes it to `EncryptDerKey()`, which in turn calls `wc_BufferKeyEncrypt()` for traditional PEM encryption. The DSA-specific `wolfSSL_PEM_write_mem_DSAPrivateKey()` hardcodes `EncryptDerKey(..., WC_MD5)`, and `wolfSSL_PEM_do_header()` decrypts legacy PEM with `wc_BufferKeyDecrypt(..., WC_MD5)`. `wolfSSL_PEM_write_bio_PrivateKey()` still routes DSA private-key export through that legacy path. - impact: encrypted PEM files use legacy EVP_BytesToKey-style derivation with MD5/SHA-1 rather than a modern password-hardening KDF, materially lowering offline cracking cost for stolen private-key files. - recommendation: default private-key export to PKCS#8 PBES2/PBKDF2 (or stronger password KDFs where available), and gate or deprecate legacy PEM encryption behind an explicit compatibility option instead of using it as a normal password-protected export path. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - Multiple public buffer-loading APIs accept negative lengths and then cast them to unsigned parse sizes, enabling out-of-bounds PEM reads - location: `src/ssl_load.c:2356-2411,3969,4084,4110,4485,4516,3606,3636,3727,5287`; contrast guard at `src/ssl_load.c:4041` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller passes a negative signed length from untrusted input or integer underflow into buffer-based cert/key loading APIs; 64-bit PEM paths are the clearest case. - evidence: `ProcessBuffer()` takes `long sz` and passes it to `DataToDerBuffer(buff, (word32)sz, ...)`; `DataToDerBuffer()` forwards PEM input to `PemToDer(buff, (long)len, ...)`. Public entry points such as `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer_ex()`, `wolfSSL_CTX_use_certificate_buffer()`, `wolfSSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey_buffer()`, `wolfSSL_use_certificate_buffer()`, and `wolfSSL_use_PrivateKey_buffer()` pass their signed `sz` straight through, while only `wolfSSL_CTX_trust_peer_buffer()` rejects `sz < 0`. - impact: a negative size can wrap to a multi-GB parse length and drive PEM parsing past the caller’s buffer, causing out-of-bounds reads and process crashes from a bad API input boundary. - recommendation: reject `sz < 0`/`derSz < 0` in every public buffer API and in `ProcessBuffer()` before any unsigned cast; keep lengths signed until validated. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_CTX_set_default_verify_paths()` trusts attacker-controlled environment variables via plain `getenv`, allowing CA store override in privileged callers - location: `src/ssl_load.c:5409-5426`; env macro `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/types.h:1216-1219` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `OPENSSL_EXTRA` build, `NO_GETENV` not set, and a privileged or security-sensitive process calls `wolfSSL_CTX_set_default_verify_paths()` with attacker-controlled environment variables. - evidence: the function reads `SSL_CERT_DIR` and `SSL_CERT_FILE` with `XGETENV(...)`, duplicates them, then calls `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations_ex(ctx, certFile, certDir, flags)`. `XGETENV` maps to plain `getenv` in `types.h`, not a `secure_getenv`/`issetugid`-aware wrapper. - impact: a local attacker can redirect trust to an attacker-chosen CA file or directory and MITM outbound TLS from privileged helpers or services that inherit environment variables. - recommendation: ignore these environment overrides in privileged contexts, or gate them behind `secure_getenv`/`issetugid` checks and an explicit opt-in compatibility flag. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - Legacy macOS system-CA loader imports every trust-settings certificate as a TLS root without checking per-cert trust results or policy scope - location: `src/ssl_load.c:3352-3389` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: macOS build takes the manual loader path (`HAVE_SECURITY_SECTRUSTSETTINGS_H && !WOLFSSL_APPLE_NATIVE_CERT_VALIDATION`); the user/admin/system trust settings contain certificates that are explicitly distrusted or trusted only for non-TLS policies. - evidence: `LoadSystemCaCertsMac()` calls `SecTrustSettingsCopyCertificates()` for user/admin/system domains and feeds every returned certificate directly into `ProcessBuffer(..., CA_TYPE, ...)`. Repo search in this path finds no `SecTrustSettingsCopyTrustSettings`, `kSecTrustSettingsResult`, or SSL policy filtering before import. - impact: wolfSSL’s CA set can diverge from macOS trust policy and re-trust certificates that native validation would deny or scope away, enabling TLS acceptance of chains anchored in those certificates. - recommendation: consult each certificate’s trust settings and SSL policy constraints before importing, or retire this path in favor of Apple-native certificate validation. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Failed cert/key reloads overwrite the last known-good credential before validation, turning parse errors into persistent handshake outages - location: `src/ssl_load.c:1248-1298,1316-1368,2184-2217,2261-2264` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `ProcessBufferPrivKeyHandleDer()` frees the existing ctx/ssl key and stores the new DER before `ProcessBufferTryDecode()` validates it. `ProcessBufferCertHandleDer()` likewise frees/stores the certificate before `ProcessBufferCert()` parses it. On later decode failure, `ProcessBuffer()` returns an error but does not restore the previous credential or remove the newly stored invalid one. - impact: a malformed cert/key during hot reload or config rollout can poison a live `WOLFSSL_CTX`/`WOLFSSL` and make subsequent handshakes fail until another successful reload, instead of preserving the previous working identity. - recommendation: decode and validate into temporary objects first, then swap into ctx/ssl only on success; otherwise keep the old credential unchanged. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `SHOW_SECRETS` / debug key logging in `src/keys.c` prints live TLS secrets and traffic keys to logs/stdout - location: `src/keys.c:3515-3554,4035-4041,4117-4123`; sinks in `wolfcrypt/src/logging.c:508,533,570,1724` and `stdio` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `SHOW_SECRETS`, or enables `WOLFSSL_DEBUG_TLS` together with active debug logging (`WOLFSSL_BUFFER` implementation). Anyone who can read process stdout/stderr, log callbacks, serial consoles, or collected logs gets the secrets. - evidence: `SetKeysSide()` dumps `keys->client_write_key` / `keys->server_write_key` with `WOLFSSL_BUFFER(...)`; `MakeSslMasterSecret()` prints the pre-master secret and final master secret with `printf("%02x", ...)`. `WOLFSSL_BUFFER` is a real logging API (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/logging.h:363`) and `wolfcrypt/src/logging.c` hex-renders bytes (`"%02x "`) and forwards them to `wolfssl_log(...)`; only the non-debug macro path turns it into `WC_DO_NOTHING` (`logging.h:393`). The repo already carries sample configs enabling `WOLFSSL_DEBUG_TLS` (`IDE/MPLABX16/user_settings.h:283`, `IDE/iotsafe/user_settings.h:96`). - impact: complete compromise of TLS confidentiality/integrity for affected sessions: leaked pre-master/master secrets and record keys allow passive decryption and active forgery of captured traffic. - recommendation: hard-disable these secret dumps in production-capable builds; at minimum gate them behind a stronger opt-in that is excluded from release/sample configs, and redact traffic secrets instead of printing raw key bytes. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V16 Security Logging and Error Handling`

### Medium - `SetKeys()` returns on partial failure without rollback, leaking cipher contexts and leaving half-initialized SSL state - location: `src/keys.c:2401-3418` (`SetKeys`) - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: multiple branches assign directly into `ssl` cipher pointers, then early-return on later allocation/init failure with no cleanup. Example ARC4: allocate `enc->arc4`, then if `dec->arc4` allocation fails return `MEMORY_E`; if `wc_Arc4Init(enc->arc4)` succeeds but `wc_Arc4Init(dec->arc4)` fails, return `ASYNC_INIT_E` with `enc->arc4` still live. The same pattern repeats in the ChaCha, DES3, AES, AES-GCM, AES-CCM, ARIA, Camellia, and SM4 branches. - impact: under low-memory conditions or transient device/hardware-init failures, repeated handshake/renegotiation attempts leak heap objects / device-backed cipher state and leave `ssl->{encrypt,decrypt}` partially populated, making recovery paths harder to reason about and increasing outage risk. - recommendation: stage allocations/initialization in locals and publish to `enc/dec` only after both sides succeed, or centralize all error exits through a rollback block that frees/zeros any partially created contexts. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - SSLv3/TLS 1.0/1.1 master-secret derivation paths free raw hash structs without calling backend cleanup hooks - location: `src/keys.c:3891-3983` (`DeriveKeys`), `src/keys.c:4011-4142` (`MakeSslMasterSecret`) - category: performance - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: both functions call `wc_InitMd5(md5)` / `wc_InitSha(sha)` and then release the buffers with `WC_FREE_VAR_EX(md5, ...)` / `WC_FREE_VAR_EX(sha, ...)` but never call `wc_Md5Free()` / `wc_ShaFree()`. Those cleanup hooks do exist (`wolfcrypt/src/md5.c:504`, `wolfcrypt/src/sha.c:1048`) and other handshake helpers call them explicitly after temporary use (`src/internal.c:21223,21238`). - impact: on alternative/hash-accelerated ports where `wc_*Free()` releases backend state, repeated old-TLS handshakes or error paths can leak hash resources or device handles until later process teardown. - recommendation: call `wc_Md5Free()` / `wc_ShaFree()` on every initialized temporary before `WC_FREE_VAR_EX(...)`, including failure paths, even when the backing memory itself is heap-allocated. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Certificate-supplied OCSP/CRL responder URLs are fetched as arbitrary outbound HTTP destinations, enabling SSRF from certificate verification - location: `src/wolfio.c:2195-2225,2297-2322`; sources `src/ocsp.c:541-559`, `src/crl.c:589-590`; default wiring `src/ssl_certman.c:1743,2170,2262` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_OCSP` and/or `HAVE_CRL_IO` enabled with the default network callbacks; verifier processes a peer certificate whose AIA/CRL URL (or override URL) points at attacker-chosen host:port. - evidence: `CheckCertOCSP` passes `ocspRequest->url`/`urlSz` into `cm->ocspIOCb(...)`, and CRL handling passes certificate `extCrlInfo` into `crl->crlIOCb(...)`; the default callbacks `EmbedOcspLookup()` / `EmbedCrlLookup()` call `wolfIO_DecodeUrl()` then `wolfIO_TcpConnect(&sfd, domainName, port, io_timeout_sec)` and issue raw HTTP over that socket. - impact: a malicious certificate can make the verifier open outbound TCP connections to internal or link-local services during handshake/revocation checking, turning certificate validation into an SSRF primitive and causing handshake stalls against unreachable targets. - recommendation: do not auto-fetch certificate-provided URLs by default; require an allowlist/validation callback for responder hosts and schemes, and block loopback, link-local, RFC1918, and arbitrary ports unless explicitly permitted. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V4 API and Web Service`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - OCSP/CRL URL bytes flow into the HTTP request line and `Host:` header without CR/LF filtering, widening the revocation-fetch path into header/request injection - location: `src/wolfio.c:1664-1713,2086-2093`; sources `src/ocsp.c:541-559`, `src/crl.c:589-590` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: revocation fetching is enabled and the supplied OCSP/CRL URL contains control characters or other illegal HTTP bytes; attacker controls certificate AIA/CRL URI or configured override URL. - evidence: `wolfIO_DecodeUrl()` copies raw URL bytes into `outName`/`outPath` until `:`, `/`, `]`, or `\0`, but does not reject `\r`, `\n`, tabs, or spaces; `wolfIO_HttpBuildRequest_ex()` then concatenates `path` directly into `"<METHOD> <path> HTTP/1.1"` and `domainName` into `"\r\nHost: "` using `XSTRNCPY`. - impact: attacker-controlled revocation URLs can shape extra HTTP headers or malformed request lines to the chosen responder/internal target, increasing SSRF impact and making the network side effect harder to reason about. - recommendation: parse URLs with a strict URI parser and reject control characters/whitespace in host and path before building the HTTP request; preferably use a hardened HTTP client instead of raw string concatenation. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V4 API and Web Service`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Chunked HTTP response assembly is quadratic-copy and attacker-controlled up to 32 MiB, enabling revocation-fetch CPU/memory DoS - location: `src/wolfio.c:1730-1819,1825-1981` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_HTTP_CLIENT` path used for OCSP/CRL fetch; remote responder or on-path attacker can return `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` with many small chunks. - evidence: `wolfIO_HttpProcessResponseGenericIO()` recognizes `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` and calls `wolfIO_HttpProcessResponseBuf()` for each chunk; that helper allocates a fresh `newRecvSz = old + chunkSz`, copies the entire old buffer into the new allocation, frees the old buffer, then appends the new chunk. The aggregate body limit is still `WOLFIO_HTTP_MAX_BODY` = 32 MiB. - impact: a responder streaming many tiny chunks can force O(n^2) copying and allocator churn in the certificate-validation path, materially increasing handshake latency and enabling CPU/memory exhaustion before the OCSP/CRL object is even parsed. - recommendation: switch to amortized/geometric buffer growth or a streaming parser, and add a chunk-count/overhead limit for revocation fetches. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: small sharp tools`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in basic DTLS/TCP socket wrappers beyond the issues above - location: `src/wolfio.c` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `EmbedReceive/Send`, `wolfIO_Recv/Send`, and the basic socket wrappers mostly translate platform errno values and fail closed on send/recv/connect errors; no additional memory-safety bug or secret-exposure path stood out in this chunk beyond the revocation HTTP client and URL handling.

### High - Persistent session-cache restore raw-loads live pointers, so cache import can later dereference/free stale or attacker-controlled addresses - location: `src/ssl_sess.c:487-524,634-683,203-204,3910,3982,4092-4108`; pointer-bearing layout `wolfssl/internal.h:4690,4723,4731,4733` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `PERSIST_SESSION_CACHE` build; process restores cache from a file/memory buffer after restart or from a not-fully-trusted source; restored sessions include non-static ticket/nonce/peer pointers. - evidence: `wolfSSL_memrestore_session_cache()` / `wolfSSL_restore_session_cache()` blindly `XMEMCPY` / `XFREAD` raw `WOLFSSL_SESSION` bytes back into `SessionCache`. `WOLFSSL_SESSION` embeds owning pointers (`peer`, `ticket`, `ticketNonce`). Later code dereferences them during resume (`wolfSSL_DupSessionEx()` copies from `input->ticket` and `input->ticketNonce.data`) and frees them during eviction/free (`EvictSessionFromCache()` / `wolfSSL_FreeSession()`). - impact: stale cross-process pointers or crafted pointer values can turn cache restore into crash, heap corruption, or arbitrary-free/read on the next resume/flush path. - recommendation: do not persist raw `WOLFSSL_SESSION` structs; serialize only POD fields and rebuild pointers on restore, or reuse explicit session serialization and reject pointer-bearing session features in persistent cache. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Persistent session cache serializes only one session per row because `SIZEOF_SESSION_ROW` ignores `SESSIONS_PER_ROW` - location: `src/ssl_sess.c:93-109,462,524,596,683` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `SessionRow` stores `WOLFSSL_SESSION Sessions[SESSIONS_PER_ROW]`, but `SIZEOF_SESSION_ROW` is only `sizeof(WOLFSSL_SESSION) + (sizeof(int) * 2)`. All mem/file save+restore paths use that truncated size for each row. - impact: save/restore keeps only the first session in each row (default cache drops 2/3 of entries; TITAN drops 30/31), crushing post-restart resume hit rate and leaving trailing slots stale on in-process restore. - recommendation: serialize the full row payload (all `SESSIONS_PER_ROW` sessions plus row metadata, excluding lock members explicitly) and add a regression test with multiple populated slots per row. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_GetSessionClient()` returns an unlocked cache pointer, enabling TOCTOU/UAF with concurrent flush or remove - location: `src/ssl_sess.c:334-366,935-1016,818-846,3221-3275` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: multithreaded client process shares the global session cache; another thread flushes/removes/overwrites the same cache row between lookup and `wolfSSL_SetSession()`. Risk is highest with `SESSION_CACHE_DYNAMIC_MEM`. - evidence: `wolfSSL_GetSessionClient()` locks `clisession_mutex` and the row, sets `ret = current`, then unlocks both before returning. `wolfSSL_SetServerID()` immediately passes that raw pointer to `wolfSSL_SetSession()`. Concurrent flush/remove paths free dynamic cache entries with `XFREE(..., DYNAMIC_TYPE_SESSION)`. - impact: callers can dereference freed cache memory or duplicate an overwritten slot, causing crashes or resuming the wrong session under load. - recommendation: duplicate or refcount the session while the row is locked, or return a stable handle that `wolfSSL_SetSession()` resolves and validates under the same lock. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_GetSessionAtIndex()` shallow-copies cache-owned pointer fields into caller objects - location: `src/ssl_sess.c:2173-2202,4092-4108`; public API `wolfssl/ssl.h:1533`; pointer fields `wolfssl/internal.h:4690,4723,4731,4733` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `SESSION_INDEX` build and application uses `wolfSSL_GetSessionAtIndex()` with a caller-owned `WOLFSSL_SESSION`, then frees or mutates that copy. - evidence: the API does raw `XMEMCPY(session, cacheSession, sizeof(WOLFSSL_SESSION))` instead of `wolfSSL_DupSession()`. `WOLFSSL_SESSION` contains owning pointers (`peer`, `ticket`, `ticketNonce.data`), and `wolfSSL_FreeSession()` later frees those members. - impact: the copied object aliases cache-owned buffers; freeing or mutating it can double-free cache memory, corrupt live cache state, or leave dangling pointers in the active session cache. - recommendation: replace the raw struct copy with `wolfSSL_DupSession()` into a safe destination, or scrub/normalize all pointer members and take proper refs before returning cache data. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RSA `set0_*` mutations leave stale/partially updated internal key state active after a failed update - location: `src/pk_rsa.c:1800-1884,2006-2208,1936,3123,3498,3606,3691,3774` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application reuses an already-initialized `WOLFSSL_RSA` and a later `wolfSSL_RSA_set0_key()` / `_set0_factors()` / `_set0_crt_params()` call fails partway (for example BN conversion/allocation failure or malformed replacement values). - evidence: each `set0_*` frees/replaces external BIGNUMs, calls `SetRsaInternal()`, and on failure only nulls some external pointers; none of these paths clear `rsa->inSet`. `SetRsaInternal()` is the gate that refreshes `rsa->internal`, but later operations such as `wolfSSL_RSA_size()`, `wolfSSL_RSA_To_Der_ex()`, `wolfSSL_RSA_sign_mgf()`, and the RSA public/private encrypt/decrypt wrappers all skip re-sync when `rsa->inSet` is still 1. - impact: after a rejected key update, subsequent sign/decrypt/export operations can keep using the previous or partially updated internal `RsaKey` while getters expose the new/NULL external fields, causing wrong-key signatures/decryption and audit-hostile key state. - recommendation: make `set0_*` transactional: clear `inSet`/`exSet` before mutation, build a temporary `RsaKey`, and only swap/mark synchronized on full success; on failure, restore the previous external state instead of leaving stale internal state live. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - RSA private-key PEM export leaks the DER buffer on every call - location: `src/pk_rsa.c:1277-1315,1333-1374,1395-1433` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `wolfSSL_PEM_write_mem_RSAPrivateKey()` allocates `derBuf` through `wolfSSL_RSA_To_Der(rsa, &derBuf, 0, rsa->heap)` and then returns after `der_to_enc_pem_alloc(...)` without any `XFREE(derBuf, ...)`. The BIO and file helpers delegate to this function and free only the returned PEM buffer. - impact: every RSA private-key export leaks one DER-sized allocation (roughly the private-key size plus ASN.1 overhead), so repeated export/generation paths can accumulate unbounded heap growth and eventual OOM in long-lived services. - recommendation: free `derBuf` on all exit paths in `wolfSSL_PEM_write_mem_RSAPrivateKey()` after `der_to_enc_pem_alloc()` returns, including failure cases. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `WOLFSSL_CRL.currentEntry` is a shared scratch pointer with no synchronization, so concurrent CRL load/free can clobber or free the in-progress entry - location: `src/crl.c:73,338-339,865-892`; entry paths `src/ssl_api_crl_ocsp.c:34-55`, `src/ssl_certman.c:1804-1826` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: same `WOLFSSL_CRL` object is used concurrently for CRL reload and another reload or teardown (`FreeCRL`) from another thread, e.g. hot-reload/admin APIs in a multithreaded process. - evidence: `BufferLoadCRL()` stores the in-progress entry in the object field `crl->currentEntry`, parses into it, then passes that shared pointer to `AddCRL()` / `CRL_Entry_free()` with no lock around the field. `FreeCRL()` separately does a raw `XFREE(crl->currentEntry, ...)` and nulls it, also without taking `crlLock` or calling `CRL_Entry_free()`. - impact: concurrent reload/cleanup can overwrite or free the scratch `CRL_Entry` while another thread is still parsing or adding it, causing crashes, leaked mutex/resources, or heap corruption during live CRL management. - recommendation: keep the in-progress entry local to `BufferLoadCRL()` instead of storing it in `WOLFSSL_CRL`, or guard the field with the same lock used for `crlList` and always destroy it via `CRL_Entry_free()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `BIO_set_conn_hostname()` reads past the old hostname buffer on `WOLFSSL_NO_REALLOC` builds when growing the host string - location: `src/bio.c:2791,2821-2830`; build flag `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/settings.h:4664` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_NO_REALLOC`; caller reuses a connect/socket BIO and sets a longer hostname than the existing `b->ip`. - evidence: in the `#ifdef WOLFSSL_NO_REALLOC` branch, `tmp = b->ip; b->ip = XMALLOC(newLen+1, ...)` is followed by `XMEMCPY(b->ip, tmp, newLen)` before the new hostname is copied in. `currLen` is only used to decide whether to reallocate, so when `newLen > currLen + 1` the copy reads past the old allocation. - impact: hostname updates can trigger out-of-bounds reads and process crashes on no-realloc builds. - recommendation: remove the pointless pre-copy or cap it to `currLen + 1`, and preserve/free the old buffer safely on allocation failure instead of assigning `b->ip` before success. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Negative `len`/`num` inputs reach `size_t` copy/file sinks and corrupt BIO ring indices instead of failing closed - location: `src/bio.c:132,142,262-387,625,673-806,1546-1592,1601-1670` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller passes a negative signed length into public BIO APIs such as `wolfSSL_BIO_read()`, `wolfSSL_BIO_write()`, `wolfSSL_BIO_nread()`, or `wolfSSL_BIO_nwrite()`, for example after unchecked cast/underflow from untrusted size fields. - evidence: these entry points do not reject negative lengths. `wolfSSL_BIO_MEMORY_read()` truncates `sz = len` when `len < 0` and then does `XMEMCPY(buf, ..., (size_t)sz)`; file BIOs pass `(size_t)len` to `XFREAD`/`XFWRITE`; base64 write casts `len` to `word32`; and the BIO-pair helpers clamp `sz = num` then mutate indices with `bio->pair->rdIdx += sz` / `bio->wrIdx += sz`, moving them backwards on negative input. - impact: negative lengths can become huge copy/read/write sizes or corrupt ring-buffer state, causing same-process OOB access, crashes, or persistent BIO corruption instead of a clean error return. - recommendation: reject `len < 0` / `num < 0` at all BIO read/write entry points before any cast or index arithmetic, and add regression tests for `-1`/underflowed sizes on MEMORY/BIO/FILE paths. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `ASN1_item_d2i()` SEQUENCE decoding ignores the declared outer length and will parse members from trailing bytes past the SEQUENCE - location: `src/ssl_asn1.c:597-621`; public wrappers `wolfssl/openssl/asn1.h:187-196,284-301` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses `ASN1_SEQUENCE(...)/IMPLEMENT_ASN1_FUNCTIONS(...)` decoders on untrusted DER/BER. - evidence: `d2i_ASN_SEQUENCE()` reads `slen` with `GetSequence()`, but then discards it: after `s += idx; len -= idx`, every member is parsed with `d2i_generic(mem, &s, &len)` against the remaining full buffer, and there is no `slen` decrement or final `consumed == slen` check. The generated `d2i_*` wrappers in `asn1.h` route straight into `wolfSSL_ASN1_item_d2i()`. - impact: a malformed SEQUENCE can advertise a short body but satisfy later fields from attacker-controlled bytes outside the declared SEQUENCE, causing parser boundary bypass / non-canonical DER acceptance and potential field smuggling across length checks. - recommendation: parse members against a local `seqLen = slen`, fail if any field exceeds `seqLen`, and require `seqLen == 0` before advancing `*src`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_a2i_ASN1_INTEGER()` has an allocation-failure buffer overflow when extending an already-dynamic INTEGER - location: `src/ssl_asn1.c:1041-1076,1455-1464`; inline buffer size `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/asn_public.h:295-299` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker controls large hex input to `wolfSSL_a2i_ASN1_INTEGER()` via BIO and can trigger memory-pressure / allocation failure during growth. - evidence: `wolfssl_asn1_integer_require_len()` moves a dynamic INTEGER back to `a->intData` before allocating a larger buffer. If that `XMALLOC(len)` fails and `keepOldData` is true, it still executes `XMEMCPY(a->data, oldData, (size_t)a->length)` while `a->data == a->intData`; that inline buffer is only `WOLFSSL_ASN1_INTEGER_MAX` (20 bytes). `wolfSSL_a2i_ASN1_INTEGER()` is the in-file caller that passes `keepOldData = (outLen != 0)` while appending attacker-supplied lines. - impact: instead of failing cleanly under low-memory conditions, parsing a large multi-line ASN.1 INTEGER can overflow the 20-byte inline buffer and corrupt memory. - recommendation: do not switch `a->data` back to `intData` until a replacement allocation succeeds; on allocation failure preserve the old dynamic buffer/state and return failure. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_i2d_ASN1_TYPE()` encodes `GENERALIZEDTIME` values with the `GENERALSTRING` tag - location: `src/ssl_asn1.c:4643-4664`; value union `wolfssl/ssl.h:569-572` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the `WOLFSSL_V_ASN1_GENERALIZEDTIME` case calls `wolfSSL_i2d_ASN1_GENERALSTRING(at->value.utf8string, pp)` instead of a GeneralizedTime encoder/tag. Because `generalizedtime` and `utf8string` share the same union slot, the payload is serialized with the `ASN_GENERALSTRING` tag rather than `ASN_GENERALIZEDTIME`. - impact: any emitted `ASN1_TYPE` carrying a GeneralizedTime is DER-invalid / mis-tagged, breaking interoperability and signatures over structures built through this compatibility API. - recommendation: add a proper `i2d_ASN1_GENERALIZEDTIME` path (tag 24) and regression-test `ASN1_TYPE_set(..., V_ASN1_GENERALIZEDTIME, ...)` round-trips. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `wolfSSL_ASN1_item_free()` leaks unset CHOICE containers - location: `src/ssl_asn1.c:103-104,175-184` - category: performance - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `asn1_item_init()` initializes CHOICE selectors to `-1`, but the `WOLFSSL_ASN1_CHOICE` free path does `if (asn1Type(obj, item->toffset) < 0) break;` before the only `XFREE(obj, ...)` in that branch. Freeing a freshly created CHOICE with no selected arm skips member cleanup and also skips freeing the container itself. - impact: repeated create/free of unselected CHOICE objects leaks heap memory. - recommendation: always free the CHOICE container; only skip arm-specific cleanup when the selector is unset. - reference: `grugbrain: boring code`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### High - `wolfSSL_EC_POINT_hex2point()` can overflow its stack buffer on long compressed-point strings - location: `src/pk_ec.c:1308-1320,1336-1340,1379-1386`; buffer bound `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ecc.h:145,148` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application passes attacker-controlled hex into the OpenSSL-compat `EC_POINT_hex2point()` API with a compressed prefix (`02`/`03`). - evidence: the parser reuses `char strGx[MAX_ECC_BYTES * 2 + 1]` as a byte buffer (`octGx = (byte*)strGx`), sets `sz = XSTRLEN(hex + 2) / 2`, and calls `hex_to_bytes(hex + 2, octGx + 1, sz)`. `hex_to_bytes()` then writes `output[i]` for every `i < sz` with no bound check. There is no cap tying `sz` to `key_sz` or `MAX_ECC_BYTES`, so oversized compressed strings overrun the fixed stack buffer before `wolfSSL_ECPoint_d2i()` is called. - impact: callers that expose this parser to untrusted input can be crashed and potentially hit stack memory corruption/code-execution primitives from a single oversized EC point string. - recommendation: reject compressed hex lengths other than exactly `2 * key_sz`, cap `sz` against both `key_sz` and `MAX_ECC_BYTES`, and parse into a dedicated bounded `key_sz + 1` buffer. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `EC_POINT_hex2point()` misparses every compressed `02...` point as odd-parity `03...` - location: `src/pk_ec.c:1379-1385`; test coverage gap `tests/api/test_ossl_ec.c:340,619-627` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller imports a valid even-parity compressed EC point through `EC_POINT_hex2point()`. - evidence: the compressed branch accepts both `hex[1] == '2'` and `hex[1] == '3'` but unconditionally writes `octGx[0] = ECC_POINT_COMP_ODD` before decode, so even-parity `02...` inputs are always converted into the opposite point. Existing tests only exercise a `03...` compressed sample and therefore do not catch the bug. - impact: valid compressed public keys can be silently rewritten to a different point, breaking public-key matching and causing verification/import failures that are hard to reason about. - recommendation: derive the first octet from the caller input (`02` -> `ECC_POINT_COMP_EVEN`, `03` -> `ECC_POINT_COMP_ODD`) and add an even-parity regression test. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `EC_KEY_set_group()` updates only metadata and leaves existing internal key/domain state on the old curve - location: `src/pk_ec.c:4343-4411,4481-4504`; stale-state consumers `src/pk_ec.c:4809-4833,5247-5288,5424-5499` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller reuses a populated `WOLFSSL_EC_KEY` after load/generation (`inSet == 1`), then calls `EC_KEY_set_group()` before validation, verification, or ECDH. - evidence: `SetECKeyInternal()` is the only path that copies `group->curve_idx` into `((ecc_key*)key->internal)->idx/dp` and marks `eckey->inSet = 1`. `wolfSSL_EC_KEY_set_group()` frees/replaces `key->group` but never clears `key->inSet`/`key->exSet` or rebuilds `key->internal`. Later paths such as `wolfSSL_EC_KEY_check_key()`, `wolfSSL_ECDSA_do_verify()`, and `wolfSSL_ECDH_compute_key()` only resync when `inSet == 0`, so after a group swap they continue operating on stale internal curve/key material while the object reports the new group. - impact: one `EC_KEY` object can report one curve while crypto operations still run on another, creating silent policy/integrity failures and audit-hostile key-handling state. - recommendation: invalidate or rebuild internal/external state on successful `EC_KEY_set_group()` (`key->inSet = 0`, `key->exSet = 0`, or immediate full resync) and add regression coverage for mutating the group of a populated key. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_PKCS12_verify_mac()` accepts negative password lengths and casts them to `word32`, enabling oversized password walks and crashy OOB reads - location: `src/ssl_p7p12.c:2072-2081`, downstream `wolfcrypt/src/pkcs12.c:527-552,649-655` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller passes a negative `pswLen` from untrusted input, integer underflow, or sentinel-style API use into the public OpenSSL-compat wrapper. - evidence: `wolfSSL_PKCS12_verify_mac()` takes `int pswLen` and forwards it as `wc_PKCS12_verify_ex(pkcs12, (const byte*)psw, (word32)pswLen)` with no `pswLen < 0` guard. The downstream PKCS#12 password path uses `pswSz` in `if ((pswSz * 2 + 2) > sizeof(unicodePasswd))` and then `for (i = 0; i < pswSz; i++)`, so `pswLen == -1` wraps to `0xffffffff`, the size check overflows, and the loop can read far past `psw`. - impact: malformed API input can turn MAC verification into an out-of-bounds read / process crash instead of a clean parameter error. - recommendation: reject `pswLen < 0` before casting, use checked signed-to-unsigned conversion in the wrapper, and add a regression test for `pswLen == -1`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### High - `wolfSSL_X509_verify_cert()` mutates shared `X509_STORE` and shared temp CA state without per-verification isolation, so concurrent validations can corrupt each other's chain-building - location: `src/x509_str.c:575-589,617-663,724-736`, store exposure `src/ssl_api_cert.c:1503,1533-1538`, embedded-store init `src/internal.c:2687-2718` - category: complexity - severity: high - status: candidate - evidence: verification aliases `certs = ctx->store->certs`, appends `ctx->ctxIntermediates` directly into that shared stack via `addAllButSelfSigned(certs, ...)`, adds issuers into the shared cert manager with `X509StoreAddCa(..., WOLFSSL_TEMP_CA)`, then later pops `numInterAdd` entries from `ctx->store->certs` and calls `wolfSSL_CertManagerUnloadTempIntermediateCerts(ctx->store->cm)`. Repo search in this file shows refcounting and a lookup-dir mutex, but no lock around this verify path or around `store->certs` mutation. `SSL_CTX_get_cert_store()` returns the shared store object for a context. - impact: concurrent handshakes or cert-verification threads using the same store can see each other's temporary intermediates, pop the wrong appended certificates, or unload another thread's temp CA mid-verify, causing nondeterministic validation failures and hard-to-reproduce outages. - recommendation: keep per-verification untrusted/intermediate state local to the `X509_STORE_CTX` instead of mutating `store->certs` / shared CM temp CA state, or add explicit store-level synchronization around the whole chain-build lifecycle. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Medium - caller-supplied intermediates are silently dropped when `X509_STORE` has no `certs` stack because `certsToUse` is populated and then never used - location: `src/x509_str.c:582-584,617,739-740`, affected store shape `src/ssl_api_cert.c:1503`, `src/internal.c:2687-2718` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: when `ctx->store->certs == NULL` and `ctx->ctxIntermediates` is non-empty, `wolfSSL_X509_verify_cert()` allocates `certsToUse` and copies intermediates into it, but the issuer lookup still calls `X509StoreGetIssuerEx(&issuer, certs, ctx->current_cert)` using `certs` (still NULL). `certsToUse` is only freed at exit. The embedded `SSL_CTX` store returned by `SSL_CTX_get_cert_store()` initializes `cm`, `param`, and lookup state but not `certs`/`trusted` stacks. - impact: OpenSSL-compatible callers that supply an untrusted intermediate stack to `X509_STORE_CTX_init(..., sk)` against an `SSL_CTX`-managed store can fail certificate validation even though the needed intermediate was provided, causing unexpected handshake/verification outages. - recommendation: actually use `certsToUse` for issuer lookup when `store->certs` is NULL, or always build an explicit local untrusted stack before entering the chain loop. Add a regression test with `SSL_CTX_get_cert_store()` plus a separately supplied intermediate stack. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_PKCS7_encode_certs()` steals ownership of the caller's cert stack before any fallible work, so failure paths can later double-free or invalidate caller-owned certificates - location: `src/ssl_p7p12.c:870-928`, free sink `src/ssl_p7p12.c:88-97` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `wolfSSL_PKCS7_encode_certs()` does `p7->certs = certs` immediately, and the file's own comment notes `TODO: takes ownership even on failure below`. Multiple later checks can still fail (`pkcs7->certList` already set, missing `derCert`, `wc_PKCS7_InitWithCert`, `wc_PKCS7_AddCertificate`, `wc_PKCS7_SetSignerIdentifierType`, `wolfSSL_i2d_PKCS7_bio`), but none roll back `p7->certs`. `wolfSSL_PKCS7_free()` later unconditionally `wolfSSL_sk_pop_free(p7->certs, NULL)`. - impact: callers that free or reuse their cert stack after a failed encode can hit double-free/use-after-free patterns later when the PKCS7 object is cleaned up, and callers that do not free it leak ownership unexpectedly. - recommendation: transfer ownership only after all validation/encoding steps succeed, or document and enforce ownership transfer on entry with a distinct API; on failure, restore `p7->certs = NULL`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### High - DTLS 1.3 duplicate-ACK tracking returns while holding `dtls13Rtx.mutex`, so a retransmitted handshake packet can wedge the connection - location: `src/dtls13.c:723-757`, entry `src/internal.c:19327 -> src/dtls13.c:1537-1551` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_RW_THREADED` DTLS 1.3 build; peer retransmits/duplicates a handshake record before the seen-ACK list is flushed. - evidence: `Dtls13RecordRecvd()` adds every received handshake record number via `Dtls13RtxAddAck()`. In `Dtls13RtxAddAck()`, the duplicate branch (`/* already in list. no duplicates. */ return 0;`) and the `Dtls13NewRecordNumber()==NULL` branch return from inside the `wc_LockMutex(&ssl->dtls13Rtx.mutex)` block; `wc_UnLockMutex(&ssl->dtls13Rtx.mutex)` runs only on the insertion path. - impact: one duplicate/retransmitted handshake record can leave `ssl->dtls13Rtx.mutex` permanently locked, after which ACK flush and scheduled retransmit paths (`SendDtls13Ack()`, `Dtls13DoScheduledWork()`, `Dtls13RtxFlushAcks()`) stall or fail, causing connection-level DoS. - recommendation: use a single unlock epilogue (`goto out`) so every path after `wc_LockMutex()` releases the mutex before returning. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### High - DTLS 1.3 retransmit queue is mutated from read/write paths without consistent locking, enabling racy unlink/free during retransmission - location: `wolfssl/internal.h:5878-5893`, `src/dtls13.c:649-675,1596-1643,2659-2683,2945-2976`, callers `src/internal.c:23579`, `src/internal.c:26209,26635`, `src/ssl.c:6027`, `src/tls13.c:13661,14790` - category: complexity - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: concurrent read/write use of one DTLS 1.3 connection, especially `WOLFSSL_RW_THREADED`; peer can send ACKs or post-handshake certificate traffic while retransmit work is running. - evidence: `Dtls13Rtx` has a mutex, but `Dtls13RtxSendBuffered()` walks `ssl->dtls13Rtx.rtxRecords` without it, while `DoDtls13Ack()` -> `Dtls13RtxRemoveRecord()` and `Dtls13RtxProcessingCertificate()` unlink/free nodes from the same list without that mutex. Only the tail-pointer update in `Dtls13RtxRecordUnlink()` is partially locked. - impact: the send path can race a read-side free/relink of the current retransmit node, leading to UAF/crashes, lost retransmissions, or corrupt queue state that is extremely hard to reason about under load. - recommendation: protect all `rtxRecords` traversal and mutation with one lock or explicit refcounted ownership; partial tail-pointer locking is not enough. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - OCSP-enabled verification fails open when the certificate omits an OCSP responder URL - location: `src/ocsp.c:534-547`, enable/verify path `src/ssl_certman.c:2111-2166,2397-2413` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application enables OCSP and trusts wolfSSL to enforce revocation, but accepts a chain whose leaf/intermediate lacks an AIA OCSP URL. - evidence: `wolfSSL_CertManagerEnableOCSP()` sets `cm->ocspEnabled = 1`; certificate verification later calls `CheckCertOCSP(cm->ocsp, cert)`. In `CheckOcspRequest()`, when no override URL is configured and `ocspRequest->urlSz == 0`, the code logs `"Cert has no OCSP URL, assuming CERT_GOOD"` and returns `0`. - impact: revocation checking is silently bypassed for otherwise trusted certificates that omit OCSP responder metadata, defeating caller expectations that enabling OCSP enforces revocation. - recommendation: fail closed by default (`OCSP_NEED_URL` / lookup failure) when OCSP is enabled, or make this fail-open behavior an explicit opt-in compatibility flag. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_OCSP_CERTID_dup()` shallow-copies ownership pointers, so freeing both IDs double-frees shared `status` state - location: `wolfssl/ocsp.h:44`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/asn.h:2775-2778`, `src/ocsp.c:137-163,810-817,877,1399-1408,1539` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: OpenSSL-compat caller duplicates a `WOLFSSL_OCSP_CERTID` from `wolfSSL_OCSP_cert_to_id()` or `wolfSSL_d2i_OCSP_CERTID()` and later frees both objects. - evidence: `WOLFSSL_OCSP_CERTID` is `typedef struct OcspEntry`. Creation paths allocate `status` and set `ownStatus = 1`. `wolfSSL_OCSP_CERTID_free()` calls `FreeOcspEntry(certId, NULL)`, which frees `entry->status` (and nested `serialInt`) when `ownStatus` is set. But `wolfSSL_OCSP_CERTID_dup()` only does `XMEMCPY(certId, id, sizeof(WOLFSSL_OCSP_CERTID))`, so the duplicate and original share the same `status` pointer and ownership bit. - impact: normal OpenSSL-style dup/free lifecycles can corrupt the heap or crash consumers of the compatibility API. - recommendation: implement a deep copy of nested OCSP CertID state, or clear ownership in duplicates and document aliasing explicitly if deep copy is not supported. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `tests/api/test_mlkem.c` segment 1/2 - location: `tests/api/test_mlkem.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is ML-KEM KAT test data plus straightforward test harness calls (`wc_MlKemKey_*`, `XMEMCMP`) over fixed compile-time vectors; no production entry point, trust-boundary crossing, or attacker-controlled sink appears here beyond test-only allocation/assert scaffolding.

### Medium - Byte-aligned `BN_rand()` / `BN_pseudo_rand()` lose a full byte of entropy, and `BN_rand_range()` inherits the bias - location: `src/ssl_bn.c:2053-2163,2179-2206`; repo example `tests/api.c:23557` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses the exported OpenSSL-compat BN RNG APIs for secrets, nonces, serials, or range sampling with bit lengths or upper bounds whose bit length is a multiple of 8. - evidence: `wolfSSL_BN_rand()` computes `len = (bits + 7) / 8`, fills `len` random bytes, then always calls `mp_rshb((mp_int*)bn->internal, 8 - (bits % 8))`; when `bits % 8 == 0` this right-shifts by 8, so 8/16/64/...-bit requests drop a whole byte. `wolfSSL_BN_pseudo_rand()` is just `wolfSSL_BN_rand()`, and `wolfSSL_BN_rand_range()` repeatedly calls it with `n = wolfSSL_BN_num_bits(range)`. Repo code already uses this path for a 64-bit X.509 serial (`tests/api.c:23557`), which therefore only gets 56 bits of randomness. - impact: byte-aligned outputs are biased and 8 bits weaker than requested; for byte-aligned `BN_rand_range()` bounds, the top 255/256 of the intended range are unreachable, weakening scalar/token generation and increasing collision or guessability of generated values. - recommendation: only truncate when `bits % 8 != 0` (or mask the unused high bits instead of unconditional right-shift), and add regression tests for 8/16/64-bit `BN_rand*()` plus byte-aligned `BN_rand_range()` coverage. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`

### High - DTLS 1.3 stateless cookie replies still decrypt tickets / run PSK lookup logic on unverified ClientHello packets - location: `src/dtls.c:519-588,605-795,937-1006` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: DTLS 1.3 server build with `WOLFSSL_DTLS13` and (`HAVE_SESSION_TICKET` or `!NO_PSK`); attacker can send spoofed UDP ClientHello packets carrying `pre_shared_key` identities before cookie validation. - evidence: `DoClientHelloStateless()` processes the first unverified ClientHello and, when no cookie is present, calls `SendStatelessReplyDtls13()`. That path parses `TLSX_PRE_SHARED_KEY` from the unverified packet (`TLSX_PreSharedKey_Parse_ClientHello(...)`), refines suites, then `FindPskSuiteFromExt()` iterates suites × identities and may call `DoClientTicket_ex()` (ticket decrypt) or `FindPskSuite()` (application PSK lookup) before any successful `CheckDtlsCookie()`. - impact: spoofable pre-auth UDP traffic can force expensive ticket decryption and/or user PSK callback work before address validation, defeating the intended cheap stateless cookie gate and making CPU-exhaustion floods practical. - recommendation: keep pre-cookie processing constant and cheap: do not decrypt tickets or invoke PSK lookup callbacks before a verified cookie, or hard-cap/skip PSK identity processing on the first ClientHello and fall back to a generic cookie reply. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V2 Validation and Business Logic`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - `BN_ucmp()` preserves negative signs instead of comparing absolute values - location: `src/ssl_bn.c:40-68,1155-1196`; export `wolfssl/openssl/bn.h:135` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses the exported OpenSSL-compat `BN_ucmp()` API to enforce absolute-value bounds on attacker-controlled negative BIGNUMs. - evidence: `wolfSSL_BN_ucmp()` duplicates both operands, then for negative values calls `wolfssl_bn_set_neg(abs_a, 1)` / `wolfssl_bn_set_neg(abs_b, 1)`. But `wolfssl_bn_set_neg(..., 1)` calls `mp_setneg()`; only `0` clears the sign. The final `wolfSSL_BN_cmp()` therefore still performs a signed compare, so negative operands are treated as negative rather than compared by magnitude. - impact: callers using `BN_ucmp()` for magnitude checks can accept out-of-range negative values because any negative input compares as smaller than a positive bound regardless of absolute size. - recommendation: clear the sign (`neg = 0`) or normalize both operands to absolute values before comparing, and add regression tests covering negative inputs. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V2 Validation and Business Logic`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`
