### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in DTLS CID helpers beyond the pre-cookie PSK/ticket work above - location: `src/dtls.c:1009-1435` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the CID set/get/parse helpers consistently cap configured CID length against `DTLS_CID_MAX_SIZE`, validate parsed CID length against the extension length before copying, and bound `wolfSSL_dtls_cid_parse()` against the relevant record-header sizes. No additional concrete trust-boundary or memory-safety bug stood out in this sub-block.

### Medium - CRL buffer-loading APIs accept negative lengths and pass them into PEM/DER parsers as large unsigned sizes - location: `src/ssl_api_crl_ocsp.c:34-55`, `src/ssl_certman.c:1804-1826`, `src/crl.c:827-874,1153-1201` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller passes a negative signed `long sz` into `wolfSSL_CTX_LoadCRLBuffer()` or `wolfSSL_LoadCRLBuffer()`, e.g. from unchecked length underflow or foreign-function binding misuse. - evidence: the public wrappers forward `sz` unchanged to `wolfSSL_CertManagerLoadCRLBuffer()`. Repo search found no `sz < 0` guard in `src/ssl_certman.c`; it calls `BufferLoadCRL(cm->crl, buff, sz, type, VERIFY)`. `BufferLoadCRL()` then feeds PEM input to `PemToDer(buff, sz, CRL_TYPE, ...)` and DER input to `ParseCRL(..., (word32)sz, ...)`. - impact: negative sizes can wrap into multi-GB parse lengths, driving out-of-bounds reads and process crashes at the CRL API boundary instead of failing closed. - recommendation: reject `sz < 0` in the public CRL buffer APIs and in `wolfSSL_CertManagerLoadCRLBuffer()` / `BufferLoadCRL()` before any cast or parser call. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - OCSP stapling response setter adopts caller memory and later frees it as internal state, creating invalid-free / UAF hazards - location: `src/ssl_api_crl_ocsp.c:588-613`; later use/free `src/tls.c:3387-3388`, `src/internal.c:8919,9259` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application calls `wolfSSL_set_tlsext_status_ocsp_resp[_multi]()` with stack/static/foreign-allocator memory, or frees/mutates `resp` after the call. - evidence: `wolfSSL_set_tlsext_status_ocsp_resp_multi()` only validates null/len, `XFREE`s the old buffer, then stores the caller pointer directly into `ssl->ocspCsrResp[idx].buffer`. Handshake code later reads that pointer in `tls.c`, and connection teardown frees every `ssl->ocspCsrResp[i].buffer` unconditionally in `internal.c`. - impact: ownership confusion at this public API can turn normal cleanup or stapled-response use into invalid frees, double frees, or corrupted OCSP responses. - recommendation: copy `resp` into wolfSSL-owned memory (or add an explicit take-ownership API and keep this one copy-in), and expose getters as borrowed `const` pointers only. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_lh_retrieve()` returns the first hash match without any key equality check, so config DB lookups can return the wrong entry on collisions - location: `src/ssl_sk.c:1276-1307`; config users `src/conf.c:291,410,1012,320-395` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses the OpenSSL-style CONF database with attacker-controlled or collision-prone section/name strings; worst case is builds where CONF support remains enabled but no SHA hash is available. - evidence: `wolfSSL_lh_retrieve()` computes a hash and returns the first node whose cached `stack->hash` equals it; it never compares the looked-up key with the candidate entry. The CONF path sets `ret->hash_fn = (wolf_sk_hash_cb)wolfSSL_CONF_VALUE_hash;`, and `wolfSSL_CONF_get_section()` / `wolfSSL_NCONF_get_string()` retrieve through this hash-only lookup. `wolfSSL_LH_strhash()` also has a `"No SHA available for wolfSSL_LH_strhash"` fallback in `conf.c:395`, which collapses hashes instead of disabling lookup. - impact: colliding entries can shadow the intended section/value, and no-SHA builds can degenerate into returning the first matching-bucket entry for unrelated config lookups, making configuration enforcement audit-hostile and unreliable. - recommendation: after a hash hit, compare the actual key fields before returning; if no deterministic hash implementation is available, fail closed for CONF/lhash support instead of returning collision-prone lookups. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `wolfSSL_CTX_SetEchConfigs()` destroys the last known-good ECH config set before validating the replacement - location: `src/ssl_ech.c:204-219` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the ctx setter does `FreeEchConfigs(ctx->echConfigs, ctx->heap); ctx->echConfigs = NULL;` before calling `SetEchConfigsEx(...)`. On parse/validation failure it returns the error with the previous config list already gone. - impact: a malformed or partially rolled-out ECH update can disable ECH for the whole context until another successful load, causing avoidable privacy downgrade / rollout outage instead of preserving the previous working config. - recommendation: parse into a temporary `WOLFSSL_EchConfig*` and swap only on success; keep the old list untouched on failure. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `tests/api/test_tls13.c` - location: `tests/api/test_tls13.c`, adjacent `src/tls13.c`, `src/internal.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is primarily TLS 1.3 regression coverage. Adjacent production checks did not surface a new concrete exploit beyond issues already captured elsewhere: middlebox-compat now disables CCS when ClientHello has an empty legacy_session_id (`src/tls13.c:7083-7085`), the TLS 1.3 parser already rejects missing/duplicate extensions and bad key shares (`src/tls13.c:5729,5749,7253,7257,12459-12905`), and CertificateRequest generation explicitly honors `wolfSSL_set1_sigalgs_list()` (`src/tls13.c:7958`). The opt-in plaintext-alert compatibility path (`src/internal.c:22063-22096`) is notable but did not show a stronger repo-specific exploit path than bounded connection teardown in this pass.

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `tests/api/test_certman.c` - location: `tests/api/test_certman.c`, adjacent `src/ssl_certman.c`, `src/crl.c`, `src/ocsp.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mostly regression and API-argument coverage around cert-manager, CRL, OCSP, name constraints, and pathLen handling. Adjacent production lookups did not surface a stronger new issue than ones already captured: the CRL static revoked-list regression exercised at `tests/api/test_certman.c:1810-1915` is already fixed in current code by sorting before ownership transfer (`src/crl.c:170-175`, `SortCRL_CertList` at `src/crl.c:119`); duplicate-CRL-extension and reason-extension tests are parser cleanup coverage; and the negative-size CRL buffer/API behaviors exercised here are already represented by the existing CRL length finding. Noted but not promoted in this pass: invalid CRL file-type values are still treated as non-PEM in path/file loaders (`tests/api/test_certman.c:1712-1720`, `src/crl.c:2163,2216`), which is a low-signal fail-open config-validation quirk rather than a material exploit path.

### Medium - PKCS7 round-trip tests use oversized buffer lengths instead of actual encoded sizes, masking trailing-data parser regressions - location: `tests/api/test_pkcs7.c:738-744,785-795,1014-1018,1799,1952,2000,2142,2216,2267,2891-2895,3188-3192` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the suite often encodes into a fixed `output` buffer and then verifies/decodes with the full buffer capacity rather than the returned encoded length, e.g. `wc_PKCS7_EncodeSignedData(pkcs7, output, outputSz)` followed by `wc_PKCS7_VerifySignedData(pkcs7, output, outputSz)` at `:738-744`, and `wc_PKCS7_EncodeEnvelopedData(pkcs7, output, sizeof(output))` followed by `wc_PKCS7_DecodeEnvelopedData(pkcs7, output, sizeof(output), ...)` at `:2891-2895` and `:3188-3192`. The same file also has paths that do track the exact encoded size (`signedSz` at `:785`, `outLen` at `:1014`), showing the API expects callers to use the returned length. - impact: the tests bless success with padded input and will not catch regressions where PKCS7 verify/decode starts accepting trailing attacker-controlled bytes after a valid CMS object, weakening boundary coverage around signed/enveloped-data parsing. - recommendation: always round-trip with the exact returned length, and add explicit negative tests for non-zero trailing junk after valid SignedData/EnvelopedData blobs. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`

### Low - `test_wc_PKCS7_SetAESKeyWrapUnwrapCb()` filesystem branch uses undeclared locals, so non-buffer ECC test builds lose AES key-wrap callback coverage - location: `tests/api/test_pkcs7.c:3108-3207`, especially `:3146-3163` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: in `test_wc_PKCS7_SetAESKeyWrapUnwrapCb()`, the `#else /* File system. */` branch uses `certFile`, `keyFile`, `eccClientCert`, and `eccClientKey`, but this function declares none of them. Repo search finds those names only in a different function (`tests/api/test_pkcs7.c:2616-2617,2625-2626`), not at file scope. - impact: builds without `USE_CERT_BUFFERS_256` hit a config-specific test compile failure or must disable this test path, leaving the custom AES key wrap/unwrap callback path untested on filesystem-based ECC builds. - recommendation: declare the needed file handles/path strings in this function or refactor both callers to a shared cert/key loader helper so the callback test builds in all supported configs. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `ExpectTure` typo breaks the RSA filesystem branch of `test_wc_PKCS7_EncodeSignedData_ex()` - location: `tests/api/test_pkcs7.c:1171` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the file uses `ExpectTure((fp = XFOPEN("./certs/1024/client-cert.der", "rb")) != XBADFILE);`; repo search found no other `ExpectTure` symbol or macro definition. - impact: the `!USE_CERT_BUFFERS_1024 && !USE_CERT_BUFFERS_2048` RSA test path does not compile cleanly, shrinking coverage for the `_ex` SignedData path on filesystem-based builds. - recommendation: fix the typo to `ExpectTrue` and add CI coverage for no-cert-buffer PKCS7 test configs so these branches compile and run. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Reviewed - no direct production-code PKCS7 exploit was established from this test chunk beyond the build/coverage issues above - location: `tests/api/test_pkcs7.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: chunk review focused on PKCS7 signed/enveloped/compressed-data tests plus adjacent `wolfcrypt/src/pkcs7.c`/`compress.c` entry points; the concrete repo-specific issues found here were test harness blind spots and config-specific compile failures rather than a fully evidenced new runtime vulnerability in the library path from this chunk alone.

### Low - `WOLFSSL_SMALL_STACK` CryptoCB AES tests use `sizeof(pointer)` for key and message lengths, so embedded/small-stack builds exercise 4/8-byte buffers instead of real AES sizes - location: `tests/api/test_aes.c:5416-5515,5549,5779-5909` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: under `#ifdef WOLFSSL_SMALL_STACK`, `key`, `plain`/`plaintext`, `cipher`/`ciphertext`, `decrypted`, and `authTag` are heap pointers, but the tests call `wc_AesGcmSetKey(aes, key, sizeof(key))`, `wc_AesGcmEncrypt(..., sizeof(plain), ..., sizeof(authTag), ...)`, `wc_AesGcmDecrypt(..., sizeof(cipher), ..., sizeof(authTag), ...)`, and assert `mockSeKey.keySz == (int)sizeof(key)` / `aes->keylen == (int)sizeof(key)`. On 32/64-bit builds those are pointer widths, not the allocated AES/key/message lengths. - impact: the CryptoCB secure-element/offload coverage for `WOLFSSL_SMALL_STACK` silently validates 4/8-byte keys and only 4/8 bytes of payload, so regressions in real AES-128 key import or full-buffer GCM offload can slip through on embedded-targeted builds. - recommendation: carry explicit `keySz`/`plainSz`/`tagSz` variables (for example `AES_128_KEY_SIZE`, `16`, `32`, `AES_BLOCK_SIZE`) instead of `sizeof(pointer)`, and assert against those explicit lengths. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`

### Low - AES-EAX Wycheproof vector loop never feeds invalid tags into decrypt, so the advertised negative vectors do not test decrypt-side authentication failure - location: `tests/api/test_aes.c:4943-4964` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `test_wc_AesEaxVectors()` computes `authtag` with `wc_AesEaxEncryptAuth(...)`, compares it to `vectors[i].tag`, but then always calls `wc_AesEaxDecryptAuth(..., authtag, len, ...)` instead of `vectors[i].tag`. For vectors marked `valid = 0`, decrypt therefore uses the freshly generated valid tag and is still expected to return `0`. - impact: the invalid-vector half of this AES-EAX test cannot catch regressions where decrypt accepts a forged/mutated vector tag, weakening coverage on an authentication boundary. - recommendation: for decrypt, pass `vectors[i].tag`; expect success only for `valid = 1` and `AES_EAX_AUTH_E` for invalid cases. Keep the existing standalone garbage-tag test as an additional smoke check. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`

### High - `X509_STORE_CTX_cleanup()` / re-init leave `trusted_stack` override live, so reused verify contexts can trust stale or freed anchors from a previous verification - location: `src/x509_str.c:253-269,577-578,691-697`; struct field `wolfssl/ssl.h:693`; reuse pattern in tests `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_str.c:595,724,817-822` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application reuses one `X509_STORE_CTX` across verifications and ever called `X509_STORE_CTX_trusted_stack(ctx, trusted)` on it; caller later cleans up/re-inits the ctx and may free or replace the old trusted stack. - evidence: `wolfSSL_X509_STORE_CTX_trusted_stack()` stores a borrowed pointer in `ctx->setTrustedSk`; repo search shows no reset path except assignment there. `wolfSSL_X509_STORE_CTX_cleanup()` just calls `wolfSSL_X509_STORE_CTX_init(ctx, NULL, NULL, NULL)`, and `wolfSSL_X509_STORE_CTX_init()` resets `store/current_cert/ctxIntermediates` but does not clear `setTrustedSk`. The verifier then still prefers `ctx->setTrustedSk` over `ctx->store->certs` (`certs = ctx->setTrustedSk`) and later resolves issuers from that override. - impact: a prior request's custom trust anchors can silently bleed into later verifications, bypassing expected store trust policy; if the old stack was freed after cleanup, the next verify walks a dangling pointer and can crash/UAF. - recommendation: clear `ctx->setTrustedSk` in both `X509_STORE_CTX_cleanup()` and `X509_STORE_CTX_init()`, and add a regression test that sets a trusted stack, cleans up/re-inits the ctx, frees the old stack, and verifies that the next chain build uses only the new store state. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V8 Authorization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - ECC raw/X9.63 import APIs accept invalid curve points by default unless an opt-in validation macro is enabled - location: `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:41,10945-10947,11734-11736`; public entry points `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ecc.h:862,884,890`; proof-of-behaviour `tests/api/test_ecc.c:995,1005` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application imports attacker-controlled EC public keys through `wc_ecc_import_x963()`, `wc_ecc_import_raw()`, or `wc_ecc_import_unsigned()` on the default build and then uses the resulting key object for ECDH/ECIES or other trust-bearing operations. - evidence: the ECC source advertises `WOLFSSL_VALIDATE_ECC_IMPORT` as "Validate ECC key on import      default: off". The only import-time `wc_ecc_check_key(key)` calls in this area are gated behind that macro (`ecc.c:10945-10947`, `11734-11736`). The API tests explicitly expect malformed coordinates to import successfully when the macro is absent: `wc_ecc_import_raw(&key, "0", qy, d, curveName)` and `wc_ecc_import_raw(&key, qx, "0", d, curveName)` both return `0`. - impact: invalid/off-curve or small-subgroup points can be admitted into live ECC key objects on default builds, pushing key validation onto every caller and creating a realistic invalid-curve/small-subgroup footgun for applications that use these public import APIs on untrusted input. - recommendation: validate imported public points and private/public key consistency unconditionally in the public ECC import APIs, or at minimum make validation default-on with an explicit opt-out for trusted internal callers only. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete X509-extension issue promoted from `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_ext.c` beyond existing X509 parser/state findings - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_ext.c`, adjacent `src/x509.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is primarily OpenSSL-compat regression coverage for extension getters/setters, Name Constraints accessors, and X509V3 printing. Adjacent spot-checks in `src/x509.c` did not surface a stronger new exploit path than issues already captured elsewhere; most notable observations here were test-coverage gaps and compatibility semantics rather than a clearer memory-safety or trust-boundary break.

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `tests/api/test_ossl_ec.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_bio.c` - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_ec.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_bio.c`; adjacent checks `src/pk_ec.c`, `src/bio.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mostly OpenSSL-compat regression coverage over fixed vectors and local BIO/EC harnesses. Adjacent spot checks only reaffirm already-filed production issues in these areas (notably `EC_POINT_hex2point()` compressed handling in `src/pk_ec.c`, stale populated-key `EC_KEY_set_group()` state in `src/pk_ec.c`, and BIO negative-length / `WOLFSSL_NO_REALLOC` hostname paths in `src/bio.c`). One suspicious test path, `test_wolfSSL_BIO_up_ref()`, matches real refcount semantics rather than a UAF: `wolfSSL_BIO_up_ref()` uses `wolfSSL_RefInc` (`src/bio.c:947-951`) and `wolfSSL_BIO_free()` only releases storage when `wolfSSL_RefDec` drops the count to zero (`src/bio.c:3024-3036`). No additional repo-specific exploit path or material performance failure was established from this chunk beyond those existing findings.

### Medium - `X509_check_host` wildcard-policy flags are advertised but not implemented, and even exact SAN matches fail when callers enable them - location: `src/x509.c:15490-15516,15579`; evidence from `tests/api/test_ossl_x509.c:397-422` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses the OpenSSL-compat hostname verifier with `WOLFSSL_NO_WILDCARDS`, `WOLFSSL_NO_PARTIAL_WILDCARDS`, or `WOLFSSL_MULTI_LABEL_WILDCARDS` to harden TLS name checks. - evidence: `wolfSSL_X509_check_host()` logs `X509_CHECK_FLAG_* not yet implemented` for these flags in `src/x509.c`; the shipped test cert has SAN `example.com`, yet `tests/api/test_ossl_x509.c` asserts `X509_check_host(x509, "example.com", ..., WOLFSSL_NO_WILDCARDS/NO_PARTIAL_WILDCARDS/MULTI_LABEL_WILDCARDS, NULL)` returns failure instead of succeeding on the exact non-wildcard SAN. - impact: OpenSSL-ported clients that enable stricter wildcard policy flags can break hostname verification for otherwise valid certificates, causing production TLS outages and making the advertised hardening knobs unusable. - recommendation: implement the flag semantics, or reject unsupported flags explicitly/document them; at minimum preserve exact-SAN matching when only wildcard-restricting flags are set. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - EC private-key PEM export leaks the DER buffer on every call - location: `src/pk_ec.c:4046-4096,4008-4010,4148-4150` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `wolfSSL_PEM_write_mem_ECPrivateKey()` computes `der_max_len`, allocates `derBuf` (`XMALLOC` at `:4075`), serializes with `wc_EccKeyToDer(..., derBuf, der_max_len)` at `:4085`, and passes that buffer into `der_to_enc_pem_alloc(...)` at `:4094`. Repo search in this function found the only `XFREE(derBuf, ...)` on the `wc_EccKeyToDer` failure branch at `:4088`; there is no corresponding free after successful PEM conversion. The file/BIO wrappers delegate to this helper at `:4008` and `:4148`, so they inherit the leak. - impact: every EC private-key PEM export leaks one DER-sized heap allocation, so repeated export/serialization in long-lived services accumulates heap growth and eventual OOM. - recommendation: free `derBuf` on all exit paths after `der_to_enc_pem_alloc()` returns, including failure paths. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Reviewed - no new material security finding promoted from `tests/api/test_slhdsa.c` - location: `tests/api/test_slhdsa.c`, adjacent `wolfcrypt/src/wc_slhdsa.c:6990-7022,6214-6215,7049-7050` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the SLH-DSA chunk is mostly direct NULL/size/sign/verify/import coverage. Adjacent code review did confirm an order-dependent API footgun (`wc_SlhDsaKey_ImportPublic()` sets `key->flags = WC_SLHDSA_FLAG_PUBLIC`, matching the test comment at `tests/api/test_slhdsa.c:1143`), but downstream sign/check paths reject missing-private-key state instead of using stale secret material, so this pass did not promote it as a material exploit path.

### Medium - `wolfSSL_PEM_write_bio_PKCS7()` wraps PKCS7 DER in CERTIFICATE PEM headers, producing mislabeled PEM output - location: `src/ssl_p7p12.c:945,1020,1033`; header types `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:27130,27171,27289`; weak coverage `tests/api/test_ossl_p7p12.c:586-589` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the PKCS7 writer serializes with `wolfSSL_i2d_PKCS7()` and then calls `wc_DerToPemEx(output, outputSz, ..., CERT_TYPE)`. The ASN layer has a distinct `PKCS7_TYPE` and `-----BEGIN PKCS7-----` header support, so this path emits a certificate-labeled PEM block for PKCS7 data. The test only checks return success and never asserts the PEM label. - impact: applications exporting PKCS7 for OpenSSL/tooling interop can emit mislabeled PEM that downstream consumers reject or mis-handle as an X.509 certificate instead of a PKCS7 object. - recommendation: switch the PEM conversion to `PKCS7_TYPE` and add a regression test that asserts `BEGIN/END PKCS7` wrappers, not just non-empty BIO output. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing`

### Low - `test_wolfSSL_PEM_write_bio_PKCS7()` truncates the 1024-bit cert buffer to `sizeof(int)`, so that config path never exercises real PKCS7 PEM generation - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_p7p12.c:488-495,561` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the `USE_CERT_BUFFERS_1024` branch declares `byte cert[sizeof(sizeof_client_cert_der_1024)]`, then sets `certSz = sizeof(cert)` and copies only `certSz` bytes from `client_cert_der_1024` before passing the truncated buffer to `wc_PKCS7_InitWithCert()`. - impact: `USE_CERT_BUFFERS_1024` builds get a broken/meaningless coverage path for PKCS7 PEM writing, so regressions in that configuration can slip past CI. - recommendation: size the buffer with `sizeof_client_cert_der_1024`, keep explicit certificate/key length variables, and assert the emitted PEM wrapper/content on this branch. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `tests/api/test_ocsp.c` beyond already logged OCSP ownership/fail-open issues - location: `tests/api/test_ocsp.c`, adjacent `src/ocsp.c`, `src/ssl_api_crl_ocsp.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk exercises OCSP response parsing, `OCSP_basic_verify`, stapling callbacks, dynamic-cert callback stapling, CERT_UNKNOWN-to-CRL fallback, and OCSP responder generation. Adjacent production lookups reinforced already logged issues (`wolfSSL_set_tlsext_status_ocsp_resp_multi()` caller-memory ownership, `wolfSSL_OCSP_CERTID_dup()` shallow copy, and fail-open OCSP when no responder URL exists) but did not expose a stronger new exploit path from this segment alone.

### Medium - ARIA-GCM EVP update still has a signed length-overflow path on buffered input, despite equivalent AES-GCM/CCM guards - location: `wolfcrypt/src/evp.c:998-1027` (contrast `wolfcrypt/src/evp.c:797-815,923-941`) - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_ARIA` builds using the OpenSSL-compat `EVP_aria_*_gcm()` streaming API; attacker can drive very large total plaintext/ciphertext through repeated `EVP_CipherUpdate`/`EVP_EncryptUpdate`/`EVP_DecryptUpdate` calls. Risk is highest on 32-bit builds. - evidence: the ARIA-GCM path computes `int size = ctx->authBufferLen + inl;` (`src/evp.c:1009`) and then appends with `XMEMCPY(tmp + ctx->authBufferLen, in, (size_t)inl); ctx->authBufferLen += inl;` (`:1026-1027`) without the checked-add guard used by AES-GCM and CCM (`if (inl > INT_MAX - ctx->authBufferLen)` at `:797` and `:923`). - impact: once buffered input approaches `INT_MAX`, the signed sum can wrap; on 32-bit this can shrink the allocation and turn the subsequent append into heap corruption, while on 64-bit it can trigger huge allocations/OOM and connection or process-level DoS. - recommendation: apply the same overflow check/helper used in the AES-GCM/CCM paths before any `authBufferLen + inl` arithmetic in ARIA-GCM, promote the size calculation to checked `size_t`, and add a regression test mirroring the new AES-GCM overflow test. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no additional repo-specific finding in `tests/api/test_evp_cipher.c` / `tests/api/test_ossl_asn1.c` beyond production-code issues already captured - location: `tests/api/test_evp_cipher.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_asn1.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is primarily regression/compatibility coverage. The notable security signal was that tests now exercise existing production parser/EVP edge cases (e.g. ASN.1 sequence parsing, ASN.1 INTEGER growth, AEAD overflow handling), but no separate test-only trust-boundary bug stood out.

### Low - `test_wc_RsaPublicEncryptDecrypt_ex()` does not compile on `WOLFSSL_RSA_PUBLIC_ONLY` builds - location: `tests/api/test_rsa.c:862-905` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `plain` is declared only inside `#ifndef WOLFSSL_RSA_PUBLIC_ONLY`, but `WC_ALLOC_VAR(plain, ...)`, `ExpectNotNull(plain)`, and `WC_FREE_VAR(plain, NULL)` remain outside that guard. The function's outer `#if` does not exclude `WOLFSSL_RSA_PUBLIC_ONLY`. - impact: public-only RSA builds lose this OAEP compatibility test to a config-specific compile failure, shrinking regression coverage for a supported build shape. - recommendation: guard all `plain` allocation/use/free with the same `#ifndef WOLFSSL_RSA_PUBLIC_ONLY`, or split the encrypt-only and decrypt-only assertions into separate tests. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `test_ossl_dh` encodes inconsistent `d2i_DHparams()` pointer-consumption expectations, weakening parser-boundary coverage - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_dh.c:70,954,984`; parser consumption site `src/pk.c:3626,3636` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the suite asserts `pt == buf` after successful `d2i_DHparams(NULL, &pt, len)` but later expects `pt != buf` when calling `d2i_DHparams(&dh, &pt, len)`. The parser implementation has an explicit `*pp += idx` success path. - impact: this mismatch can hide or ossify a `d2i_*` consumption regression on one call form, breaking callers that parse concatenated ASN.1 objects or rely on consumed-length accounting. - recommendation: make both success forms assert pointer advancement and add a trailing-data / concatenated-object regression around `*pp` consumption. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`

### Reviewed - no additional direct production-code issue promoted from `tests/api/test_ossl_bn.c` / `tests/api/test_rsa.c` beyond already captured BN/RSA findings - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_bn.c`, `tests/api/test_rsa.c`; adjacent `src/ssl_bn.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass rechecked BN compat math/rand coverage and RSA raw import/export, PSS, OAEP, and bounds-check paths. New concrete evidence from this chunk was configuration-specific test breakage and parser-boundary coverage mismatch rather than a stronger new runtime exploit path beyond existing inbox findings.

### Medium - `EVP_PBE_scrypt()` ignores its `maxmem` safety bound, so callers cannot cap attacker-driven scrypt memory usage - location: `wolfcrypt/src/evp.c:5163-5202`; allocation sinks `wolfcrypt/src/pwdbased.c:789,802,809`; API surface `wolfssl/openssl/evp.h:1047-1050`; chunk trigger `tests/api/test_evp_pkey.c:559-632` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses the OpenSSL-compat `EVP_PBE_scrypt()` API on untrusted or policy-controlled scrypt parameters and relies on `maxmem` to prevent resource exhaustion. - evidence: the public API advertises `maxmem` (`evp.h`) and its in-file doc says it is the "maximum size of buffer used for calculation", but `wolfSSL_EVP_PBE_scrypt()` immediately does `(void)maxmem;` and then calls `wc_scrypt(...)` anyway. `wc_scrypt()` allocates buffers directly from the chosen work factors: `blocks = XMALLOC((size_t)blocksSz, ...)`, `v = XMALLOC(((size_t)1 << cost) * (size_t)bSz, ...)`, and `y = XMALLOC((size_t)(blockSize * 128), ...)`. - impact: a service that thought it had a hard memory ceiling can still be driven into large allocations / OOM by oversized scrypt cost parameters, turning password-hash or key-import paths into memory-DoS. - recommendation: enforce `maxmem` before calling the KDF (or plumb it into `wc_scrypt_ex()`), reject parameter sets whose computed workspace exceeds the bound, and add a regression test that `EVP_PBE_scrypt(..., maxmem=small, ...)` fails before allocating. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V2 Validation and Business Logic`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete production issue surfaced from `tests/api/test_dtls.c` beyond the DTLS findings already captured - location: `tests/api/test_dtls.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mainly regression/memio coverage for CID handling, stateless accept, retransmit timing, replay, and malformed-record rejection. The only production-relevant problem reinforced here is the already-recorded DTLS 1.3 duplicate-ACK/deadlock path (`Dtls13RtxAddAck()` duplicate calls are exercised at `tests/api/test_dtls.c:910-919`).

### Reviewed - no further repo-specific issue beyond the ignored `EVP_PBE_scrypt()` bound surfaced from the rest of `tests/api/test_evp_pkey.c` - location: `tests/api/test_evp_pkey.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: aside from the scrypt API behavior above, this chunk is compatibility/KAT coverage for EVP wrappers, key assignment, sign/verify, derive, and print helpers; no additional trust-boundary-to-sink bug was substantiated from the test code itself in this pass.

### Low - `DIGEST_COUNT_COPY_TEST` frees the source XOF state mid-test and then keeps using it, making SHAKE copy regression coverage use-after-free prone and unreliable - location: `tests/api/test_digest.h:550-584`; affected uses `tests/api/test_sha3.c:812,1249` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the macro's first subtest does `wc_##name##_Copy(&src, &dst)`, finalizes both, then calls `wc_##name##_Free(&src);` instead of freeing `dst`. The macro immediately reuses `src` in later `Update`/`Copy`/`Final` checks and only frees `dst` near the end. Repo search shows this macro is instantiated for SHAKE128 and SHAKE256 copy tests. - impact: SHAKE copy tests can operate on freed state or pass spuriously depending on allocator/free semantics, so sanitizer builds may trip on the test and real copy-regression bugs can slip past because the harness is no longer exercising a clean live source/destination pair. - recommendation: free `dst` after the first subtest, keep `src` alive until all subtests finish (or reinitialize it before reuse), and add an explicit regression test that each subcase starts from a fresh initialized source state. - reference: `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no additional production-code finding promoted from `tests/api/test_ossl_cipher.c`, `tests/api/test_hash.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_crypto.c`, `tests/api/test_tls.c` - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_cipher.c`, `tests/api/test_hash.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_crypto.c`, `tests/api/test_tls.c`; adjacent spot checks `src/ssl.c:16941-16954`, `src/internal.c:38099-38144`, `src/x509.c:14516-14526`, `src/ssl_crypto.c:3205-3254` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is primarily unit/regression coverage. Adjacent production lookups only confirmed already-sane or already-known behaviors in the areas exercised here (curve-name fallback for `set1_curves_list`, TLS 1.2 null-compression rejection, X509 private-key/public-key consistency check plumbing, and AES CFB wrapper entry handling); no new stronger trust-boundary break, memory-safety issue, or material performance failure was established from this segment alone.

### Low - `TEST_OSSL_X509_ACERT_DECLS` registers `test_wolfSSL_X509_ACERT_new_and_sign` twice and never registers `test_wolfSSL_X509_ACERT_asn` - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_acert.h:33-38`, implementations `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_acert.c:370,415`, aggregate include `tests/api.c:34054` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the decl macro ends with two identical `TEST_DECL_GROUP("ossl_x509_acert", test_wolfSSL_X509_ACERT_new_and_sign)` entries; repo search finds `test_wolfSSL_X509_ACERT_asn()` only as a prototype/definition, not in any test-registration list. - impact: normal aggregated API test runs skip the ACERT ASN coverage entirely while double-running the sign path, making ASN parser/serializer regressions in this compatibility surface easier to miss. - recommendation: replace the duplicate final entry with `test_wolfSSL_X509_ACERT_asn`, and add a CI sanity check that each declared test symbol is registered exactly once. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `TEST_OSSL_X509_LOOKUP_DECLS` duplicates hash-dir coverage and omits `test_wolfSSL_X509_load_crl_file` - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_lu.h:33-38`, implementations `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_lu.c:219,297`, aggregate include `tests/api.c:34068` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the macro lists `TEST_DECL_GROUP("ossl_x509_lu", test_wolfSSL_X509_LOOKUP_ctrl_hash_dir)` twice and never includes `test_wolfSSL_X509_load_crl_file`; repo search finds the omitted symbol only as a prototype/definition. - impact: the shared test harness under-covers CRL file-loading behavior and overstates lookup-dir coverage, so regressions in `X509_load_crl_file()` can slip through routine runs. - recommendation: swap the duplicate entry to `test_wolfSSL_X509_load_crl_file` and add a registry-completeness check over `tests/api/*.h` vs `tests/api/*.c`. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `TEST_DES3_DECLS` runs `test_wc_Des3_CbcEncryptDecrypt` twice and never runs `test_wc_Des3_EcbEncrypt` in the aggregate suite - location: `tests/api/test_des3.h:32-36`, implementations `tests/api/test_des3.c:120,177`, aggregate include `tests/api.c:33846` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the final two entries in `TEST_DES3_DECLS` are both `test_wc_Des3_CbcEncryptDecrypt`; repo search finds `test_wc_Des3_EcbEncrypt()` only as a prototype/definition, not in the registration macro consumed by `tests/api.c`. - impact: ECB-specific DES3 regression coverage is absent from the main API runner despite an implemented test, reducing signal for config-specific cipher-path breakage. - recommendation: replace the duplicated final decl with `test_wc_Des3_EcbEncrypt` and add an automated duplicate/omission check for `TEST_*_DECLS` macros. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `TEST_SIGNATURE_DECLS` registers the ECC size test twice and never runs the RSA size test in the aggregate API suite - location: `tests/api/test_signature.h:30-32`, implementations `tests/api/test_signature.c:39,80`, aggregate include `tests/api.c:33905` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `TEST_SIGNATURE_DECLS` expands to `test_wc_SignatureGetSize_ecc` twice; repo search shows `test_wc_SignatureGetSize_rsa()` is implemented in `tests/api/test_signature.c:80` but not referenced by the macro that `tests/api.c` includes. - impact: normal API test runs skip RSA signature-size coverage entirely while double-running the ECC path, making RSA regression/compatibility bugs in `wc_SignatureGetSize(WC_SIGNATURE_TYPE_RSA, ...)` easier to miss. - recommendation: replace the second macro entry with `test_wc_SignatureGetSize_rsa` and add a duplicate/omission sanity check for `TEST_*_DECLS` registration macros. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

Repo search shows every declared TLS-extension test is manually registered once in `tests/api.c:34425-34430`, and the `TEST_WC_ENCRYPT_DECLS` / `TEST_POLY1305_DECLS` macros are each included once in `tests/api.c:33872` / `:33850` with matching implementations in `tests/api/test_wc_encrypt.c:39` and `tests/api/test_poly1305.c:39`. No duplicate/omitted registration, production trust boundary, or adjacent stronger exploit path surfaced from this chunk alone.

### High - `wolfSSL_X509_set_serialNumber()` copies attacker-sized ASN.1 INTEGERs into a 32-byte fixed serial buffer - location: `src/x509.c:16095-16109`; fixed storage `wolfssl/internal.h:5401-5402,5425-5543` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller passes an attacker-controlled or foreign-parsed `WOLFSSL_ASN1_INTEGER` longer than 31 bytes into the public OpenSSL-compat `X509_set_serialNumber()` / `wolfSSL_X509_set_serialNumber()` API. - evidence: the setter only checks `s->length >= 3`, `s->data[0] == ASN_INTEGER`, and `s->data[1] == s->length - 2`, then does `XMEMCPY(x509->serial, s->data + 2, s->length - 2); x509->serialSz = s->length - 2; x509->serial[x509->serialSz] = 0;`. `x509->serial` is a fixed `byte serial[EXTERNAL_SERIAL_SIZE]`, and `EXTERNAL_SERIAL_SIZE` is `32`; there is no upper-bound check before the copy or terminator write. - impact: oversized serials corrupt adjacent `WOLFSSL_X509` fields during cert construction/rewriting, yielding crashable memory corruption and potential code-execution primitives in consumers that expose this compatibility API across a trust boundary. - recommendation: reject serial payloads larger than the fixed buffer budget before copying (accounting for the extra terminator write), or move serial storage to checked dynamic allocation; add a regression test for 32+ byte serials. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - OpenSSL RAND output stays duplicated across `fork()` when `WOLFSSL_NO_GETPID` disables child detection - location: `src/ssl.c:19022-19045,19514-19532`; proof-of-behaviour `tests/api/test_ossl_rand.c:238-241` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build defines `WOLFSSL_NO_GETPID` on a fork-capable system and the application uses the OpenSSL-compat global RAND APIs (`RAND_seed`/`RAND_bytes`) in parent and child processes. - evidence: `currentRandPid = getpid()` during RNG init and the `if (p != currentRandPid)` reseed path in `wolfSSL_RAND_bytes()` are both compiled only under `#if defined(HAVE_GETPID) && !defined(WOLFSSL_NO_GETPID)`. In the disabled-pid build, the test suite explicitly expects identical post-fork output with `ExpectBufEQ(randbuf, childrand, sizeof(randbuf))`. - impact: prefork worker models can reuse the same DRBG state in parent and child, producing duplicate/predictable `RAND_bytes()` output for tokens, IVs, nonces, or other application secrets. - recommendation: fail closed on fork-capable `WOLFSSL_NO_GETPID` builds, or force an unconditional child/process-change reseed path (for example via `pthread_atfork`/first-use reseed from OS entropy) instead of codifying identical output as expected behavior. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete production issue promoted from `tests/api/test_sha512.c`, `tests/api/test_blake2.c`, `tests/api/test_chacha.c`, `tests/api/test_curve448.c`, `tests/api/test_sm3.c`, `tests/api/test_chacha20_poly1305.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_acert.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_pk.c` - location: listed test files plus adjacent `src/x509.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/curve448.c`, `src/ssl.c` spot checks - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mostly fixed-vector/hash/cipher/Curve448/OpenSSL-compat API coverage. Adjacent spot checks did not surface a stronger new trust-boundary bug than the serial-buffer overflow and fork-RNG issue above; the remaining code here is largely straightforward test scaffolding over compile-time vectors and direct API assertions.

Repo search found 80 `tests/api/test_*.c` source files and the manifest lists the same 80 under `tests_unit_test_SOURCES`; it also ships the matching test headers plus the generated OCSP blob header/script (`test_ocsp_test_blobs.h`, `create_ocsp_test_blobs.py`) in `EXTRA_DIST`. The concrete test-coverage issues found elsewhere in this area stem from registration macros and test code, not from omitted build/distribution entries in this manifest.

### Low - `test_wc_Des3_CbcEncryptDecryptWithKey()` encodes the wrong NULL-IV contract for DES3 CBC helpers - location: `tests/api/test_wc_encrypt.c:77,86`; implementation `wolfcrypt/src/wc_encrypt.c:145,168`, `wolfcrypt/src/des3.c:1204-1216` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the test asserts `wc_Des3_CbcEncryptWithKey(..., key, NULL)` and `wc_Des3_CbcDecryptWithKey(..., key, NULL)` return `0`, but the wrappers pass `iv` straight into `wc_Des3_SetKey(...)` and the DES3 implementation rejects `iv == NULL` with `BAD_FUNC_ARG`. - impact: the regression suite blesses an impossible/fail-open API contract on a crypto boundary, creating false failures and pressure to weaken IV validation rather than catching real regressions. - recommendation: make the NULL-IV cases expect `BAD_FUNC_ARG` and keep the helper docs/tests aligned that CBC helpers require an 8-byte IV. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`

### Low - `TEST_OSSL_PKCS7_DECLS` mis-registers `test_wolfSSL_RAND_poll`, so the harness runs the RAND test twice and attributes one run to PKCS7 - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_p7p12.h:37-44`, duplicate registration `tests/api/test_ossl_rand.h:36`, manifest use `tests/api.c:34097,34110`, macro semantics `tests/api/api_decl.h:35` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the PKCS7 decl macro ends with `TEST_DECL_GROUP("ossl_p7", test_wolfSSL_RAND_poll)` even though `test_wolfSSL_RAND_poll` belongs to `TEST_OSSL_RAND_DECLS`; `TEST_DECL_GROUP` is a plain initializer with no dedupe, and `tests/api.c` includes both macro lists. - impact: test output and filtering misattribute a RAND API check to the PKCS7 suite and execute it twice, which hides real suite composition and can mask missing PKCS7 coverage behind a passing unrelated test. - recommendation: remove the stray RAND registration from `TEST_OSSL_PKCS7_DECLS` or replace it with the intended PKCS7 test, and add a duplicate-test check when assembling the manifest. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no additional repo-specific production issue promoted from `tests/api/test_md2.c`, `tests/api/test_md4.c`, `tests/api/test_arc4.c`, `tests/api/test_poly1305.c`, `tests/api/test_dh.c`, and the declaration-only headers in this chunk - location: `tests/api/test_md2.c`, `tests/api/test_md4.c`, `tests/api/test_arc4.c`, `tests/api/test_poly1305.c`, `tests/api/test_dh.c`, `tests/api/*.h` listed in this chunk - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: these files are mostly fixed-vector KATs, direct bad-arg tests, and test-manifest declarations; spot checks around adjacent production entry points did not surface a stronger new trust-boundary or memory-safety issue than the existing inbox findings already recorded elsewhere.

### Low - `certext_test` is gated on nonexistent `WOLFSSL_GEN_CERT`, leaving the certificate-extension test effectively dead in normal cert-gen builds - location: `wolfcrypt/test/test.h:336-337`; corroborating call/definition guards `wolfcrypt/test/test.c:827,2739,22256` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the header declares `certext_test()` only under `defined(WOLFSSL_GEN_CERT)`, while repo search found no real `#define WOLFSSL_GEN_CERT` anywhere in code/configs; actual build knobs and surrounding cert-generation code use `WOLFSSL_CERT_GEN` (`cmake/options.h.in:290`, `examples/configs/user_settings_all.h:110`, many others). The same typo guard also wraps the test invocation and definition in `wolfcrypt/test/test.c`, so enabling cert generation with the documented macro does not enable this test. - impact: certificate-extension generation/regression coverage is silently skipped across normal `WOLFSSL_CERT_GEN` builds, making cert-ext bugs easier to ship and harder to notice. - recommendation: rename the guard to `WOLFSSL_CERT_GEN` consistently in `test.h` and `test.c`, and add a CI sanity check for test guards that reference undefined feature macros. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - VS2022 test solution advertises ARM64 and DLL configurations but silently builds the x64/Win32 non-DLL test project instead - location: `wolfcrypt/test/test-VS2022.sln:18-50`, `wolfcrypt/test/test-VS2022.vcxproj:2-17,59-155` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the solution exposes `Debug|ARM64`, `DLL Debug|ARM64`, `DLL Release|ARM64`, and `Release|ARM64`, but the test project `{D04BDF66-664A-4D59-BEAC-8AB2D5809C21}` maps those entries to `Debug|x64` or `Release|x64` (`.ActiveCfg`/`.Build.0 = ...|x64`). The project file itself defines only `Debug|Win32`, `Debug|x64`, `Release|Win32`, and `Release|x64`; there are no ARM64 or DLL-specific project configurations, and every test config carries the same `WOLFSSL_LIB` preprocessor define. - impact: Windows users/CI can select an `ARM64` or `DLL` solution configuration and believe they validated native ARM64 or DLL-consumer behavior, while the test binary actually exercises the x64/Win32 non-DLL path. That hides platform-specific alignment/codegen bugs and DLL import/export/runtime mismatches until production. - recommendation: add real ARM64 and DLL configurations to `test-VS2022.vcxproj` (with DLL-consumer settings that do not reuse the static-style `WOLFSSL_LIB` build assumptions), or remove the misleading solution entries so unsupported matrices fail fast instead of aliasing silently. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - filesystem-only `test_DSA_do_sign_verify()` branch in `tests/api/test_ossl_dsa.c` does not compile due to a malformed `XFOPEN` assertion - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_dsa.c:71` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the branch uses `ExpectTrue((fp = XFOPEN("./certs/dsa2048.der", "rb") != XBADFILE);`, which is missing the `)` after `XFOPEN(...)`. Nearby equivalents use the correct form (`tests/api/test_dsa.c:90`, `tests/api/test_evp_pkey.c:779`: `ExpectTrue((fp = XFOPEN(...)) != XBADFILE);`). This breaks the `#else` path when neither `USE_CERT_BUFFERS_1024` nor `USE_CERT_BUFFERS_2048` is set. - impact: filesystem-only OpenSSL DSA compatibility builds lose this sign/verify regression to a config-specific compile failure, shrinking coverage on a supported build shape. - recommendation: fix the call to `ExpectTrue((fp = XFOPEN("./certs/dsa2048.der", "rb")) != XBADFILE);` and add a no-cert-buffer CI leg. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no new material production finding promoted from the rest of this chunk (`test_ossl_x509_vp`, `test_ossl_x509_info`, `test_pkcs12`, `test_ossl_x509_io`, digest/cipher/math/signature tests) - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_vp.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_info.c`, `tests/api/test_pkcs12.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_io.c`, `tests/api/test_cmac.c`, `tests/api/test_sha256.c`, `tests/api/test_camellia.c`, `tests/api/test_des3.c`, `tests/api/test_ripemd.c`, `tests/api/test_ascon.c`, `tests/api/test_rc2.c`, `tests/api/test_wolfmath.c`, `tests/api/test_signature.c`, `tests/api/test_sha.c`, headers in this chunk - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mostly fixed-vector/API regression code. Adjacent production spot-checks did not surface a stronger new exploit path: `wolfSSL_X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set1_host()` clamps to `WOLFSSL_HOST_NAME_MAX-1` before copy (`src/x509.c:10487-10498`, bound `wolfssl/ssl.h:864,881`), `wolfSSL_X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set1_ip_asc()` uses bounded copy into `ipasc` (`src/x509.c:10552-10557`, bound `wolfssl/ssl.h:873,883`), and the PKCS#12 bad-MAC-salt regression exercised here matches an existing cleanup path (`wolfcrypt/src/pkcs12.c:470-471,512`). No additional concrete trust-boundary, memory-safety, or material performance bug was established from this segment alone.

### Low - wolfcrypt cert-generation tests write predictable filenames into the build tree with plain `fopen("wb")`, enabling local symlink clobber in shared workspaces - location: `wolfcrypt/test/test_paths.h.in:31-32`, adjacent `wolfcrypt/test/test.c:21867-21904,3222,3229,3275` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can pre-create or replace files in the build directory (for example shared CI workspace, reused checkout, or symlink planted before a privileged/local test run). - evidence: the generated test path header defaults `CERT_WRITE_TEMP_DIR` to `@abs_top_builddir@/`; the test harness then hard-codes names such as `cert.der`, `cert.pem`, `key.der`, `key.pem`, `othercert.der`, and `certreq.pem` under that directory, and `_SaveDerAndPem()` opens them with `XFOPEN(fileDer, "wb")` / `XFOPEN(filePem, "wb")` rather than a unique temp-file API. - impact: running `testwolfcrypt` can overwrite attacker-chosen paths via symlink following, clobbering files writable by the test user and creating avoidable CI/dev-host integrity risk. - recommendation: write generated artifacts via `mkstemp`/`O_CREAT|O_EXCL` in a private temp directory, or disable temp-file output by default and require an explicit safe output dir for cert-generation tests. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V5 File Handling`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - legacy `wolfcrypt/test/test.sln` advertises x64 and DLL test builds that the paired `test.vcproj` cannot satisfy, leaving stale/broken Windows test configurations - location: `wolfcrypt/test/test.sln:14-20,25-38`, `wolfcrypt/test/test.vcproj:9-18,94-103` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the solution exposes `Debug|x64`, `Release|x64`, `DLL Debug|x64`, `DLL Release|x64` and maps the `test` project to `Debug|x64`/`Release|x64` active configs, but the project file declares only `<Platform Name="Win32"/>` and only `Debug|Win32` / `Release|Win32` configurations. - impact: Windows users relying on the shipped legacy solution get a misleading test/build matrix: the advertised x64 and DLL test configs are not real project definitions, so those paths are effectively untested or fail at build time, making config-specific regressions easier to ship. - recommendation: either add matching x64/DLL configurations to `test.vcproj` or delete the stale legacy solution/project pair from distribution in favor of the maintained VS2022 project files. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Low - OpenSSL-compat `HMAC_Update()` / `CMAC_Update()` treat `NULL` + non-zero input as a successful no-op instead of failing closed - location: `src/ssl_crypto.c:1898-1917,2252-2264`; inconsistent low-level behavior/tests `tests/api/test_hmac.c:317,358,399,440,481`, `tests/api/test_ossl_mac.c:324-331,454-456` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller reaches the incremental MAC wrappers with `data == NULL` and `len > 0` (e.g. parser/FFI bug, optional-buffer misuse, stale pointer cleared before MACing). - evidence: the OpenSSL HMAC wrapper only calls `wc_HmacUpdate` when `(data != NULL) && (len > 0)` and otherwise returns success, so `HMAC_Update(hmac, NULL, n)` silently omits that chunk; the shipped compat test explicitly expects success for `HMAC_Update(hmac, NULL, sizeof(key))`. Low-level HMAC tests in `tests/api/test_hmac.c` expect `wc_HmacUpdate(&hmac, NULL, nonzero)` to fail with `BAD_FUNC_ARG`, proving the wrapper changed fail-closed semantics. The CMAC wrapper has the same pattern: it only calls `wc_CmacUpdate(...)` when `data != NULL`, and `tests/api/test_ossl_mac.c` asserts `CMAC_Update(cmacCtx, NULL, AES_128_KEY_SIZE) == 1`. - impact: MAC state can silently exclude intended bytes while the API reports success, hiding caller bugs and potentially authenticating/trusting truncated data instead of aborting on invalid input. - recommendation: reject `data == NULL && len > 0` in the OpenSSL-compat update wrappers, align incremental semantics with one-shot `HMAC(...)` and low-level `wc_HmacUpdate`, and add regression tests that require failure for non-zero lengths with `NULL` input. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `tests/api/test_sm2.c`, `tests/api/test_ed25519.c`, `tests/api/test_evp_digest.c`, and most of `tests/api/test_tls_ext.c` beyond the wrapper/API issue above - location: `tests/api/test_sm2.c`, `tests/api/test_ed25519.c`, `tests/api/test_evp_digest.c`, `tests/api/test_tls_ext.c`; adjacent checks `src/tls.c`, `src/ssl.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass did not find a stronger new runtime bug in the covered SM2/Ed25519/EVP-digest paths beyond argument-validation and known compatibility surfaces. `test_tls_hmac_size_overflow()` matches a current fix already visible in `src/tls.c:1310-1312` (`WC_SAFE_SUM_WORD32` guards), `test_TLSX_TCA_Find()` now matches current code that checks both type and id (`src/tls.c:2926-2929`), and `test_TLSX_SNI_GetSize_overflow()` appears to exercise overflow-hardening rather than expose a new live bug.

### Medium - Name-constraints matching accepts malformed leading-dot DNS constraints instead of rejecting the certificate - location: `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:19759-19831,19921-19961`; behavior locked in by `tests/api/test_asn.c:667-670` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can present a cert chain containing a critical `nameConstraints` DNS subtree encoded with a leading dot (for example `.domain.com`), or an enterprise/intermediate CA in the trust path already uses this invalid encoding. - evidence: the matcher comments `RFC 5280 does not allow leading dot in constraint` (`asn.c:19829`) but still treats `.domain.com` as a valid suffix constraint; the tests explicitly keep this non-RFC behavior "for backwards compatibility" and assert matches for `sub.domain.com` and `a.b.domain.com`. The same matcher is used during name-constraints confirmation (`asn.c:19921,19961`). - impact: wolfSSL can accept chains with malformed critical DNS name constraints that stricter validators reject, creating cross-validator trust-policy mismatches and allowing invalidly constrained chains to validate instead of failing closed. - recommendation: reject leading-dot DNS constraints during parse/verification by default; if compatibility is required, hide it behind an explicit opt-in flag and add a negative regression test that malformed DNS constraints fail validation.

### Medium - RSA-PSS parameter decoding parses but does not enforce `trailerField == 1`, accepting invalid AlgorithmIdentifiers on certificate/PKCS7 trust boundaries - location: `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:7564-7793`, especially `:7760-7763`; affected callers `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:8377-8382,14394-14405`, `wolfcrypt/src/pkcs7.c:5460`; test gap `tests/api/test_asn.c:796-918` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: attacker controls RSA-PSS `AlgorithmIdentifier` parameters in an X.509/PKCS7 object processed by wolfSSL. - evidence: `DecodeRsaPssParams()` initializes and validates hash/MGF/salt, then reaches `/* [3] trailerField */` with the inline comment `/* Unused - trailerField. */` (`asn.c:7760-7763`), so the field is parsed but not checked for the required default value. Certificate and PKCS7 parsing both call this decoder, while the tests cover hash/MGF/salt/null cases only and never a non-default trailer field. - impact: malformed RSA-PSS encodings with non-default trailer fields are accepted instead of rejected, causing non-canonical/invalid signature-algorithm parameters to validate in wolfSSL when stricter implementations fail closed. - recommendation: reject any parsed `trailerField` other than `1`, and add negative tests for non-default trailer values on certificate and PKCS7 parsing paths.

### Low - OpenSSL-compat one-shot digest helpers return shared static buffers when output is `NULL`, making MD5/SHA/SHA2 results race-prone across threads - location: `src/ssl_crypto.c:176-185,1050-1057,1117-1124,1184-1191,1251-1258,1318-1325`; exercised by `tests/api/test_ossl_dgst.c:121,213,219,252,279,307,432` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - status: candidate - severity: low - exploitability/preconditions: multithreaded process uses `MD5()/SHA()/SHA224()/SHA256()/SHA384()/SHA512()` with `md == NULL` / `hash == NULL` and consumes the returned pointer after concurrent calls. - evidence: each helper declares a function-static digest buffer (`static ... dgst[...]`) and immediately aliases it when `hash == NULL`; the tests intentionally rely on the `NULL`-output shortcut returning a valid pointer. - impact: concurrent callers can overwrite each other’s returned digest bytes, leading to wrong fingerprint comparisons, cache keys, or signature-related decisions in applications that use the compatibility shortcut. - recommendation: prefer caller-supplied output buffers; at minimum document the `NULL`-output path as thread-unsafe, or replace the static buffer with thread-local storage / an explicit opt-in compatibility mode.

### Reviewed - no additional chunk-specific issue promoted from `tests/api/test_sm4.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_name.c`, and `tests/api/test_sha3.c` beyond the findings above - location: `tests/api/test_sm4.c`, `tests/api/test_ossl_x509_name.c`, `tests/api/test_sha3.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: these files are primarily fixed-vector/KAT and API-argument regression coverage over local buffers and compatibility wrappers. The concrete repo-specific issues surfaced in this pass came from adjacent production code touched by the tests: malformed name-constraint acceptance, RSA-PSS trailer-field laxness, and static-buffer digest compatibility behavior.

### Low - dead OCSP blob helper builds invalid `CertID` values by hashing the certificate serial into `issuerKeyHash` - location: `tests/api/create_ocsp_test_blobs.py:227-235,266-269` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `single_response_from_cert()` does `serialHash = sha1(serial.asOctets()).digest()` and then `cert_id_from_hash(issuerHash, serialHash, serial)`, so the generated `issuerKeyHash` is the SHA-1 of the serial number, not the issuer public key hash required by RFC 6960. wolfSSL’s OCSP matcher compares both `issuerHash` and `issuerKeyHash` (`src/ocsp.c:752-753`). The branch is currently dead in this generator because `create_response()` checks `entry.get('certificate')`, but the shipped `response_definitions` never populate a `certificate` key. - impact: future tests that start using this helper will generate OCSP responses whose `CertID` cannot match the intended certificate, creating misleading revocation regressions/false negatives while the dead branch remains unaudited. - recommendation: either remove the unused helper path or compute `issuerKeyHash` from the issuer SPKI like `cert_id()` does, then add a regression that generated `certificate`-based responses are actually accepted by the OCSP verification path. - reference: `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `test_OBJ_NAME_do_all()` debug callback passes an `OBJ_NAME*` to `%s`, making verbose test runs crash-prone and misleading - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_obj.c:36-50` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `obj_name_t(const OBJ_NAME* nm, void* arg)` receives a struct pointer, but under `DEBUG_WOLFSSL_VERBOSE` it does `BIO_printf(bio, "%s\n", nm);`. `%s` treats `nm` as a NUL-terminated character buffer, not an `OBJ_NAME` object. - impact: verbose/debug test runs can print garbage or segfault in the callback instead of exercising `OBJ_NAME_do_all()`, reducing coverage exactly when developers enable extra diagnostics. - recommendation: print a real string field such as `nm->name` (or remove the debug print entirely) and keep the callback type-safe. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Medium - suite harness disables DH key/prime validation on almost every integration run, so default TLS/DTLS coverage will not catch DH parameter-validation regressions - location: `tests/suites.c:74,942-945,1591-1592`; effect in examples at `examples/server/server.c:1749,2311,3601-3602` and `examples/client/client.c:2253,2887,4115-4116,4712-4713` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the harness defines `disableDHPrimeTest` as `"-2"` and appends it to both client and server for every test unless the extra arg contains `doDH` (`tests/suites.c:942-945` comment: "disable DH prime check is added to all tests by default"). The example client/server parse `-2` into `doDhKeyCheck = 0` and then call `wolfSSL_SetEnableDhKeyTest(ssl, 0)` on live connections. Only the dedicated `tests/test-dhprime.conf` path is invoked with `doDH` (`tests/suites.c:1591-1592`). - impact: malformed/weak DH peer keys or parameter-validation regressions in the default example/client-server path can ship without the main suite noticing, because nearly all negotiated-DH cases are exercised with validation explicitly turned off. - recommendation: invert the default so DH key checks stay enabled for normal suite runs; keep `-2` only for narrowly scoped legacy/interoperability cases, and add at least one negative test that proves invalid DH inputs fail with checks enabled. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`

### Medium - all SRP unit tests are compiled out unless SHA-512 is enabled, even for SHA-1/SHA-256 SRP builds - location: `tests/srp.c:27,851-863`; affected test bodies use non-SHA512 types at `tests/srp.c:51-55,123-159,208-450` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the entire file and `SrpTest()` body are wrapped in `#if defined(WOLFCRYPT_HAVE_SRP) && defined(WOLFSSL_SHA512)`, but most tests exercise `SRP_TYPE_TEST_DEFAULT` (`SRP_TYPE_SHA256` when `NO_SHA`, else `SRP_TYPE_SHA`) or explicit `SRP_TYPE_SHA`; only `test_SrpKeyGenFunc_cb()` is SHA-512-specific. - impact: SRP-enabled builds without `WOLFSSL_SHA512` get zero SRP regression coverage, so authentication-state, parameter-order, and proof/key-generation regressions in SHA/SHA256 SRP configurations can slip through CI unnoticed. - recommendation: gate the base SRP tests on `WOLFCRYPT_HAVE_SRP` alone, and keep only the SHA-512 callback/vector test behind `WOLFSSL_SHA512`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V6 Authentication`, `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `grugbrain: testing`

### Low - SRP tests cover only happy-path proofs and NULL/size errors, not malicious peer public values or tampered proofs, despite dedicated rejection paths in production code - location: `tests/srp.c:321-450,827-841`; missing expectations contrasted with `wolfcrypt/src/srp.c:815,825,829,854,858,868,874,1001` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: repo search finds no `SRP_BAD_KEY_E` checks in `tests/srp.c`; the tests call `wc_SrpComputeKey()` and `wc_SrpVerifyPeersProof()` only for call-order/NULL/BUFFER_E cases and valid vectors. Production `wc_SrpComputeKey()` has several explicit bad-peer-value checks that return `SRP_BAD_KEY_E`, and `wc_SrpVerifyPeersProof()` relies on a final `ConstantCompare(proof, digest, size)` check. - impact: regressions that start accepting zero/out-of-range `A`/`B` values or forged SRP proofs would not be caught by this unit suite, weakening coverage on an authentication boundary. - recommendation: add negative vectors for `A=0`, `B=0`, `A/B >= N`, and mutated client/server proofs, asserting nonzero failure (`SRP_BAD_KEY_E` or verify failure as appropriate). - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V6 Authentication`, `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`

### Reviewed - no direct new production-code exploit was promoted from `tests/suites.c` or `tests/srp.c` beyond the suite/coverage issues above - location: `tests/suites.c`, `tests/srp.c`; adjacent checks `examples/client/client.c`, `examples/server/server.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/srp.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk mainly exposes test harness behavior and SRP vector coverage. Adjacent spot checks showed the SRP implementation does contain bad-key and proof-compare checks (`SRP_BAD_KEY_E` branches and `ConstantCompare`), so the concrete issues here are that the tests/suite defaults undercut those checks rather than a stronger new runtime flaw from this chunk alone.

### Low - DTLS suite comments and logs overstate protocol coverage: many cases labeled `DTLSv1.1` or `TLSv1.2` actually run DTLSv1/DTLSv1.2 only - location: `tests/test-dtls-reneg-server.conf`, `tests/test-dtls-resume.conf`, `tests/test-dtls-group.conf`; runner `tests/suites.c:913-915`; option semantics `examples/server/server.c:953-961,1999,2613`, `examples/client/client.c:1253-1262,2549,3195` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: these configs use `-u` or `-G` (DTLS/SCTP-DTLS) with only `-v 2` and `-v 3`, while the example help text documents those values as `DTLSv1` and `DTLSv1.2` only. Representative mislabeled entries include `tests/test-dtls-reneg-server.conf:217-247` (`# server/client DTLSv1.1 ...` with `-u -v 2`), `:301-343` (`# TLSv1/TLSv1.2 ...` with `-u` still forcing DTLS), with the same pattern repeated in `test-dtls-resume.conf` and `test-dtls-group.conf`. `tests/suites.c` prints the comment text before executing the argv, so CI/log output reports the wrong protocol/version under test. - impact: maintainers and downstream CI can believe DTLSv1.1 or stream-TLS paths are covered when these suites only exercise DTLSv1/DTLSv1.2; that hides real coverage gaps and misdirects failure triage. - recommendation: fix the comments to match the actual argv, or add real version-specific selectors/separate TLS configs before claiming TLS/DTLSv1.1 coverage. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - Main DTLS/SCTP positive suites still require insecure legacy profiles (`RC4`, `3DES`, NULL-encryption, anonymous DH) to keep passing - location: `tests/test-sctp.conf:103-141,293-329,734-744,1003-1025`, `tests/test-dtls-reneg-server.conf:121-143,301-343,724-736,1037-1063`, `tests/test-dtls-resume.conf:121-143,301-343,724-736,1037-1063`, `tests/test-dtls-group.conf:121-143,301-343,724-736,1037-1063`; suite runner `tests/suites.c:1261-1288,1448`; example surfaces `examples/server/server.c:960,2121,3020`, `examples/client/client.c:1261,2773,3511` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build leaves legacy suites enabled and an operator copies shipped example/test settings or otherwise enables the same cipher strings/anonymous mode in a real deployment. - evidence: these standard success-path DTLS configs include `RC4-SHA`, `DES-CBC3-SHA`, `ECDHE-ECDSA-NULL-SHA`, `ECDHE-PSK-NULL-SHA256`, and `ADH-AES128-SHA` as normal server/client handshakes rather than isolated legacy/negative tests. `tests/suites.c` loads all four files as ordinary suites, and the example client/server still expose SCTP/DTLS plus anonymous `-a` mode and cipher-string selection. - impact: CI treats weak/unauthenticated/cleartext DTLS profiles as required functionality, which makes retirement harder and increases the chance example-driven deployments expose traffic to passive sniffing (`NULL`), active MITM (`ADH`), or deprecated cipher risk (`RC4`/`3DES`). - recommendation: move deprecated/unsafe cipher coverage into an explicit opt-in legacy suite, keep the default DTLS success suites limited to authenticated non-deprecated ciphers, and document `-a`/NULL-cipher use as test-only. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Medium - `test_memio_setup_ex()` frees caller-owned `WOLFSSL_CTX*` on later SSL-allocation failures, corrupting shared-context malloc-fail tests - location: `tests/utils.c:231-372`; reuse call sites `tests/api.c:25193-25212,28519-28528`; failure-injection trigger `tests/unit.c:76-80` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the helper only creates a context when `*ctx_c == NULL` / `*ctx_s == NULL`, so it supports borrowed/reused contexts, but its later error paths unconditionally call `wolfSSL_CTX_free(*ctx_c)` / `wolfSSL_CTX_free(*ctx_s)` after `wolfSSL_new(*ctx_c)` / `wolfSSL_new(*ctx_s)` failures. The API tests reuse an already-populated `ctx_c` to create a second client SSL (`ssl_c2`), so an injected `wolfSSL_new()` failure frees a shared context still referenced by existing SSL objects and later freed again by the caller. `unit_test()` explicitly enables malloc-fail injection through `WOLFSSL_FORCE_MALLOC_FAIL_TEST`. - impact: low-memory and fault-injection runs can hit UAF/double-free on the test harness instead of exercising the intended library cleanup paths, reducing confidence in malloc-failure coverage and destabilizing sanitizer/CI runs. - recommendation: track ownership per call and free only contexts created inside this invocation; leave borrowed/reused `ctx_*` pointers to the caller on SSL-object creation failures. Splitting context setup from SSL creation would simplify reasoning. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Low - `WOLFSSL_DUMP_MEMIO_STREAM` uses `sprintf` into a 64-byte stack buffer, and existing test names are long enough to overflow it deterministically - location: `tests/utils.c:82-85`; name source `tests/api/api_decl.h:34-35`, `tests/api.c:34081,34858` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: test build enables `WOLFSSL_DUMP_MEMIO_STREAM` and runs the normal API suite. - evidence: `test_memio_write_cb()` builds `char dump_file_name[64]; sprintf(dump_file_name, "%s/%s.dump", tmpDirName, currentTestName);`. `currentTestName` is populated from the registered test-case name string, and the repo already ships `test_wolfSSL_X509_CRL_add_revoked_oversized_revocation_date` (59 characters). `"/" + ".dump" + NUL` alone pushes that past 64 bytes even before any non-empty `tmpDirName`, so the dump-path formatting can smash the stack in this debug path. - impact: debug/CI runs that enable memio stream dumping can crash or corrupt stack state while writing trace files, turning a troubleshooting feature into a deterministic memory-safety fault. - recommendation: replace `sprintf` with a bounded formatter (`snprintf`/`XSNPRINTF`) and reject or dynamically allocate paths that exceed the destination buffer. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Reviewed - no additional material issue promoted from `tests/unit.h`, `tests/unit.c`, or `tests/w64wrapper.c` beyond the `tests/utils.c` harness bugs above - location: `tests/unit.h`, `tests/unit.c`, `tests/w64wrapper.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mainly assertion macros, unit-test CLI plumbing, and a straightforward `w64wrapper` self-test. The concrete repo-specific issues found here were in the memio helper code (`tests/utils.c`), not a new production-library trust-boundary break in the other files from this chunk.

### Low - QUIC alert callback test helper stores the TLS record-type constant instead of the callback's alert byte - location: `tests/quic.c:672-682`; corroborating constant `wolfssl/internal.h:6561` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `ctx_send_alert(WOLFSSL *ssl, WOLFSSL_ENCRYPTION_LEVEL level, uint8_t err)` records `ctx->alert_level = (int)level;` and then `ctx->alert = alert;` instead of `err`. Repo search finds `alert` here only as the `QuicTestContext` field and as the internal enum constant `alert = 21`, so the helper latches a fixed record-type value rather than the emitted alert description. - impact: any QUIC alert-path assertions built on this harness will validate a constant instead of the real alert byte, masking wrong-alert regressions in handshake/error handling. - recommendation: store `err` (`ctx->alert = err;`) and add a regression that forces a specific QUIC alert and checks both level and alert code. - reference: `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - QUIC early-data test harness ignores `wolfSSL_read_early_data()`'s byte-count out parameter and uses the status return as a length - location: `tests/quic.c:1154-1166`; API contract `doc/dox_comments/header_files/ssl.h:14485`; implementation writes `*outSz` in `src/tls13.c:15305,15330` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `QuicConversation_step()` calls `wolfSSL_read_early_data(..., &written)` but then updates `conv->early_data_len += (size_t)n;` and never uses `written`. The public docs describe `outSz` as the number of early-data bytes read, and the TLS 1.3 implementation explicitly zeroes and updates `*outSz`. - impact: the QUIC 0-RTT regression can mis-account payload on partial-read or status-driven paths, weakening coverage for truncation/short-read bugs in early-data handling. - recommendation: use `written` for length accounting, and assert status and byte count separately across WANT_READ/partial-read cases. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`

### Reviewed - no new concrete production QUIC vulnerability promoted from `tests/quic.c` / adjacent `src/quic.c` beyond the harness issues above - location: `tests/quic.c`, adjacent `src/quic.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is primarily QUIC regression scaffolding. Adjacent spot checks in `src/quic.c` did not surface a stronger repo-specific exploit path in this pass: QUIC transport params are copied before swap (`src/quic.c:402,409-410`), missing local transport params fail closed at handshake setup (`src/quic.c:473-474`), and buffered QUIC CRYPTO records are capped by `WOLFSSL_QUIC_MAX_RECORD_CAPACITY` (`src/quic.c:82,135`, `wolfssl/quic.h:301`).

### Low - `WOLFSSL_DUMP_MEMIO_STREAM` test dumps build file paths with `sprintf` into a 64-byte stack buffer, so long registered test names overflow it - location: `tests/utils.c:82-85`, duplicate path `tests/api.c:4555-4557,34858`; declarations `tests/utils.h:30-32` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: test build enables `WOLFSSL_DUMP_MEMIO_STREAM` and runs the API suite or memio tests. - evidence: both dump helpers allocate `char dump_file_name[64];` and then do `sprintf(dump_file_name, "%s/%s.dump", tmpDirName, currentTestName);`. `currentTestName` is assigned from `testCases[i].name` (`tests/api.c:34858`). The registered test `test_wolfSSL_X509_CRL_add_revoked_oversized_revocation_date` is present in the suite (`tests/api.c:21898,34081`) and is long enough that `tmpDirName + '/' + currentTestName + ".dump"` exceeds 64 bytes. - impact: debug memio-dump runs can smash stack state and crash or corrupt CI/test processes on ordinary long-named tests, making this diagnostic path unsafe and unreliable. - recommendation: replace `sprintf` with checked `snprintf` or dynamically size the path from `strlen(tmpDirName) + strlen(currentTestName) + sizeof("/.dump")`, and add a regression test with a long test name. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing`
