### Medium - default DTLS 1.3 passing suite normalizes disabling HelloRetryRequest cookie exchange, which the docs flag as DoS/amplification-prone - location: `tests/test-dtls13.conf:263-267`, `examples/server/server.c:3295`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files/ssl.h:13746-13748`, `src/ssl.c:12102` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: operator runs the shipped example server with the tested `-J n` path or copies the passing suite config into automation/deploy tooling. - evidence: the DTLS 1.3 suite contains a normal passing case `# server DTLSv1.3 no HelloRetryRequest cookie` with `-J n`; the example server maps that mode to `wolfSSL_disable_hrr_cookie(ssl)`. The public docs for this path warn that when DTLS v1.3 omits cookie exchange, it "may make server vulnerable to DoS/amplification". `src/ssl.c` notes cookie exchange is on by default for DTLS, so this test is explicitly disabling the protection. - impact: the repo’s default green-path DTLS 1.3 coverage blesses an example/server mode that drops DTLS’s stateless anti-amplification defense, increasing the chance operators ship a spoofable handshake path that burns CPU/bandwidth before peer address validation. - recommendation: move `-J n` coverage behind an explicitly unsafe/negative test, or require conspicuous opt-in naming/documentation instead of keeping it in the normal passing DTLS 1.3 suite. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Low - `test-fails.conf` comments out the server version flag for the “ECC no signer error” case, so expected-fail coverage can fail for the wrong reason - location: `tests/test-fails.conf:71-80`, `tests/suites.c:1603-1604`, `examples/server/server.c:1634`, `wolfssl/test.h:464-474` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the server half of `# server ECC no signer error` has `#-v 3` commented out, while the client half still forces `-v 3`. The suite runner marks the whole file `expFail`, so this block is accepted as long as it fails somehow. The server uses `SERVER_DEFAULT_VERSION`, which is build-defaulted in `wolfssl/test.h`, so TLS-1.3-enabled builds can run the server side at the default version instead of the intended TLS 1.2 case. - impact: this expected-fail test can pass via protocol-version mismatch or other generic handshake failure rather than exercising the intended “missing signer for ECC chain” path, weakening regression signal on certificate-validation failures. - recommendation: restore an explicit server `-v 3` for this block (or split out a deliberate version-mismatch case) so the expected failure is tied to the intended cert-validation condition. - reference: `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - several config suites contain exact duplicate handshake blocks, adding CI/runtime cost without adding coverage - location: `tests/test-sha2.conf:245-323`, `tests/test-sm2.conf:1-25`, `tests/test-dtls13.conf:81-118,171-192` - category: performance - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `test-sha2.conf` repeats the same `DHE-PSK-AES256-CBC-SHA384` and `DHE-PSK-NULL-SHA256` client/server pairs multiple times; `test-sm2.conf` repeats the same initial `ECDHE-ECDSA-SM4-CBC-SM3` server/client pair twice; `test-dtls13.conf` duplicates the EarlyData and KeyUpdate client/server scenarios. - impact: routine suite runs spend time on identical handshakes while making the config files harder to review for real coverage gaps or missing negative cases. - recommendation: dedupe identical blocks and add a simple config-lint in the suite harness that flags repeated adjacent server/client tuples. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Reviewed - chunk mostly extends already-logged “positive suites still exercise insecure legacy profiles” coverage rather than exposing a stronger new production bug - location: `tests/test-dtls.conf`, `tests/test-sha2.conf`, `tests/test-dtls-srtp.conf`; supporting suite ids `src/internal.c:27771,28121,28133,28137`, SRTP profiles `src/ssl.c:1490-1492` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk adds more passing-suite references to already-known insecure/legacy options (`ADH-AES128-SHA`, `ECDHE-ECDSA-NULL-SHA`, `NULL-SHA256`, `ECDHE-PSK-NULL-SHA256`, `SRTP_NULL_SHA1_32`, `SRTP_NULL_SHA1_80`) but did not substantiate a stronger new trust-boundary break beyond the new DTLS 1.3 no-cookie normalization above.

### Medium - FIPS builds auto-disable strict ECDSA DER length checks, accepting signatures with trailing garbage on TLS/X.509/PKCS7 trust boundaries - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/settings.h:4383-4386`; strict/lax decoders `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:36584-36589,36521,36602,36624,36659`; verify path `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:8593,8596`; affected callers `src/internal.c:5680`, `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:19391`, `wolfcrypt/src/pkcs7.c:4632`; regression proof `tests/api/test_ecc.c:446-451` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build uses FIPS v2+ settings and verifies attacker-controlled ECDSA signatures from TLS handshakes, certificate chains, or PKCS7/CMS objects. - evidence: `settings.h` unconditionally does `#define NO_STRICT_ECDSA_LEN` for `FIPS_VERSION_GE(2,0)` with the comment `ECDSA length checks off by default for CAVP testing`. The strict decoder path checks full consumption (`sigLen != idx + len`, `idx != sigLen`) in `asn.c`; `DecodeECC_DSA_Sig()` calls `DecodeECC_DSA_Sig_Ex(..., 1)`, while `wc_ecc_verify_hash()` has an alternate `DecodeECC_DSA_Sig_Ex(..., 0)` path. The shipped test explicitly flips from reject to accept for `siglen+1` when `NO_STRICT_ECDSA_LEN` is set. - impact: wolfSSL can accept malformed non-canonical ECDSA signatures with appended bytes that stricter validators reject, creating parser differential bugs and allowing malformed signed TLS/cert/PKCS7 data to validate instead of failing closed. - recommendation: stop auto-enabling `NO_STRICT_ECDSA_LEN` for all FIPS builds; scope the lax mode to explicit CAVP/self-test builds only, keep strict full-consumption checks in production, and add negative regressions for trailing-byte ECDSA signatures on TLS, certificate, and PKCS7 verification paths. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - shipped `test-tls-downgrade.conf` and `test-psk-no-id-sha2.conf` are never queued by `tests/suites.c`, so downgrade and SHA2 no-hint PSK coverage silently rots - location: `tests/include.am:44,80`, `tests/suites.c:1497-1509`, `tests/test-psk-no-id-sha2.conf:67`, parser `tests/suites.c:910,926-928` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: repo search found `test-tls-downgrade.conf` and `test-psk-no-id-sha2.conf` only in `tests/include.am`; unlike nearby suites, `tests/suites.c` never enqueues either file, while it does enqueue `tests/test-psk.conf` (`:1497`) and `tests/test-tls13-psk.conf` (`:1509`). The dead PSK SHA2 file has already drifted: line 67 starts with ` No Hint server...` instead of `#`, and the suite parser only treats `#` as comment (`:910`) otherwise tokenizing the text into argv (`:926-928`). - impact: stream-TLS downgrade tests and SHA2 PSK/no-identity cases are distributed but not exercised in the standard harness, so regressions in these transport/authentication boundaries can ship unnoticed and the configs bit-rot. - recommendation: either wire both configs into `tests/suites.c` with explicit expected-fail handling where needed, or remove them from `EXTRA_DIST`; add a manifest-vs-runner sanity check so shipped `.conf` cases cannot go unexecuted. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V6 Authentication`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`

### Medium - Ed25519/Ed448/P-521 mTLS configs disable CRL checks while their comments read like revocation coverage - location: `tests/test-ed25519.conf:33-34,57-65`, `tests/test-ed448.conf:20-21,44-52`, `tests/test-p521.conf:20-21,44-52`; option semantics `examples/server/server.c:986-987,1926-1928`, `examples/client/client.c:1324-1325,2445-2447` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the client-auth cases for these cert types pass `-V` on the server and `-C` on the client; the example help/arg parsing defines `-V` as `Disable CRL` on server and `-C` as `Disable CRL` on client. The same blocks are annotated `Remove -V when CRL for ... certificates available` / `Enable when CRL ... available`, confirming the exercised path is handshake success with revocation explicitly turned off. - impact: routine suites do not actually exercise revocation enforcement for Ed25519/Ed448/P-521 certificate flows, so CRL-handling regressions on those trust paths can slip through CI while the config names/comments suggest broader coverage than exists. - recommendation: relabel the current cases as no-CRL interoperability tests, keep CRL disabled only where fixtures are missing, and add explicit CRL-enabled positive/negative cases once revocation material exists for each cert family. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no additional direct production-code exploit promoted from the remaining config/doc files in this chunk beyond test-harness coverage drift - location: `tests/test-dtls13-pq-hybrid-frag.conf`, `tests/test-tls13-ecc.conf`, `tests/test-rsapss.conf`, `tests/test-tls13-psk.conf`, `tests/TXT_DB.txt`, `tests/include.am`, `tests/test-tls13-down.conf`, `tests/test-ed448.conf`, `tests/test-p521.conf`, `tests/test-tls13-pq.conf`, `tests/test-sctp-sha2.conf`, `tests/test-dtls13-cid.conf`, `tests/test-dtls-sha2.conf`, `tests/test-dtls13-pq-hybrid.conf`, `tests/test-dtls13-psk.conf`, `tests/test-dtls13-pq-frag.conf`, `tests/test-dtls13-downgrade.conf`, `tests/test-enckeys.conf`, `tests/test-dhprime.conf`, `tests/test-dtls13-pq.conf`, `tests/test-dtls-srtp-fails.conf`, `tests/test-dtls-downgrade.conf`, `tests/NCONF_test.cnf`, `tests/test-dtls13-downgrade-fails.conf`, `tests/test-dtls-fails.conf`, `tests/test-psk.conf`, `tests/test-dtls-fails-cipher.conf`, `tests/CONF_FILES_README.md`, `tests/README` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is predominantly suite configuration and test-data/docs. The concrete repo-specific risk surfaced here was missing/misleading execution of security-boundary tests rather than a stronger new trust-boundary-to-sink flaw in production code from these files alone.

### Reviewed - no new candidate finding in `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/mpi_class.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/oid_sum.h` - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/mpi_class.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/oid_sum.h`; adjacent checks `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/settings.h:327-328,4672-4675`, `examples/configs/user_settings_arduino.h:644-647`, `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:6839,7115,7119,7170-7194`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/integer.h:53` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `mpi_class.h` is the generated LibTomMath dependency-expansion header pulled in by `integer.h`; its `LTM1/LTM2/LTM3` self-include scheme is compile-time machinery, but this chunk alone did not expose a runtime trust-boundary sink or material production failure. `oid_sum.h` explicitly warns about reduced-width OID sums on sub-32-bit targets, but repo defaults already force the old/smaller OID path when `WC_16BIT_CPU` is enabled (`settings.h`, Arduino settings), and ASN.1 decoding contains explicit OID collision/sanity handling (`asn.c` special-value comment plus round-trip `OidFromId` collision check) rather than a clear misparse-to-accept path from this chunk alone.

### Medium - hardware-crypto mutexes have a documented first-use race that can double-initialize the same static lock under concurrent requests - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/wc_port.h:807-814`, adjacent implementation `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:1777-1801,1838-1904`; affected callers include `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:164`, `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:3560`, `wolfcrypt/src/des3.c:126`, `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:563`, `wolfcrypt/src/md5.c:70` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_CRYPT_HW_MUTEX` (auto-enabled for STM32/MMCAU/PIC32/MAX3266X, etc.) and two threads hit a hardware-backed crypto path before explicit init. Remote traffic can trigger this in threaded services on cold start. - evidence: the header comment warns `wolfSSL_CryptHwMutexInit` is called on first lock and recommends calling it early to avoid a `race condition` if two `wolfSSL_CryptHwMutexLock` calls happen simultaneously. The implementation uses unsynchronized plain ints (`static int wcCryptHwMutexInit = 0;`, plus per-algo `wcCryptHw{Rng,Aes,Hash,Pk}MutexInit = 0;`) and checks `if (... == 0) { wc_InitMutex(...); ... = 1; }` before `wc_LockMutex(...)`, with no atomic/once primitive around the initialization gate. - impact: concurrent first use can run `wc_InitMutex()` twice on the same static mutex or race into lock/unlock on partially initialized hardware-lock state, leading to crash/DoS and potentially unsynchronized access to shared crypto peripherals, which can corrupt RNG/AES/hash operations. - recommendation: make hardware-mutex initialization one-time and thread-safe (`pthread_once`/`InitOnceExecuteOnce`/atomic compare-exchange), or eagerly initialize all hardware mutexes during `wolfCrypt_Init()` before worker threads start. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete issue promoted from `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/pkcs11.h` or `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sp_int.h` in this pass - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/pkcs11.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sp_int.h` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is primarily ABI/type/macro surface. `pkcs11.h` is a direct constant/struct/function-table declaration header, and `sp_int.h` mostly exposes type sizing, compile-time feature selection, and declarations for SP math already implemented elsewhere. This pass did not substantiate a stronger trust-boundary break, memory-safety bug, or material performance failure from these headers alone beyond the hardware-mutex race reached through `wc_port.h`/`wc_port.c`.

### Medium - `WC_RSA_NONBLOCK` disables RSA ciphertext/signature range checks, so verify/decrypt paths can accept non-canonical inputs `>= n` instead of failing closed - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/rsa.h:46-58`, enforcement helper `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c:3120-3153`, gated call sites `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c:3220-3235`, affected wrappers `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c:3807-3878,3901-4070`, trust-boundary callers `src/pk_rsa.c:3398,3712`, `wolfcrypt/src/pkcs7.c:4363` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WC_RSA_NONBLOCK` and processes attacker-controlled RSA ciphertexts or signatures through `wc_RsaPrivateDecrypt*()`, `wc_RsaSSL_Verify*()`, or `wc_RsaPSS_Verify*()` (for example certificate/PKCS7 verification or RSA decrypt APIs exposed across a network or file boundary). - evidence: the public header explicitly says "RSA bounds check is not supported with RSA non-blocking mode" and forcibly `#define`s `NO_RSA_BOUNDS_CHECK` under `WC_RSA_NONBLOCK`. The skipped helper `RsaFunctionCheckIn()` parses the input integer and rejects values `<= 1` for private decrypt (`rsa.c:3143`) and any value not strictly less than the modulus (`rsa.c:3153`). `wc_RsaFunction()` only invokes that helper on `RSA_PRIVATE_DECRYPT` / `RSA_PUBLIC_DECRYPT` when `NO_RSA_BOUNDS_CHECK` is not defined (`rsa.c:3220-3235`). The normal verify/decrypt wrappers route untrusted inputs into those decrypt modes (`rsa.c:3807-3878,3901-4070`), and the existing modulus-bounds test is compiled out exactly when `NO_RSA_BOUNDS_CHECK` is set (`tests/api/test_rsa.c:1081,1094`). - impact: malformed RSA ciphertexts/signatures with representative value `>= n` are reduced modulo `n` and processed instead of rejected, creating non-canonical signature acceptance / validator-differential behavior and weakening decrypt-side invariants on public trust boundaries. - recommendation: keep the `c < n` / `c > 1` precheck in non-blocking builds by doing it before the async state machine starts or by adding an equivalent non-blocking-safe range check; keep the modulus-bounds regression enabled for `WC_RSA_NONBLOCK` builds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no additional chunk-specific issue promoted from `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/cryptocb.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/mem_track.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/tfm.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/pkcs7.h`, and `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/memory.h` beyond the RSA non-blocking bounds-check issue above - location: listed headers plus spot checks in `wolfcrypt/src/cryptocb.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/pkcs7.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/memory.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is largely ABI/config/header surface. Adjacent searches did not establish a stronger new repo-specific trust-boundary bug than the `WC_RSA_NONBLOCK` range-check bypass: crypto-callback registry races are already logged elsewhere, PKCS7 certificate/key-size limits here read as compatibility constraints rather than a fresh exploit path, and the memory/stack tracking helpers are mostly diagnostic code with local cleanup/robustness concerns rather than a higher-severity production vulnerability from this chunk alone.

### Medium - X25519 peer-key validation only rejects `0`/`1`, so low-order public keys can pass into shared-secret derivation - location: `wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.c:879,882,899,922,670,744,767`; network entry points `src/internal.c:32433-32449,6167` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker controls a peer X25519 public key (raw API import or TLS `key_share` on the wire). - evidence: `wc_curve25519_check_public()` documents and implements checks for size mismatch and public keys equal to `zero or one` only (`curve25519.c:879,899,922`). Adjacent repo search did not find any low-order-point blacklist or all-zero shared-secret rejection in `curve25519.c`. The TLS parser feeds untrusted network key shares through `wc_curve25519_check_public(...); wc_curve25519_import_public_ex(...)` at `src/internal.c:32433-32449`, and later `X25519SharedSecret()` derives the secret with `wc_curve25519_shared_secret_ex(...)` at `src/internal.c:6167`. - impact: malformed low-order X25519 public keys that are not literal `0`/`1` can be accepted and drive the derived shared secret to a predictable value (typically all-zero), creating invalid-key acceptance and cross-implementation mismatch; raw API consumers that use the returned secret directly can lose secrecy/integrity on that channel. - recommendation: reject the RFC 7748 low-order public-key set and/or fail closed when scalar multiplication yields an all-zero shared secret; add negative tests for low-order peer keys in `tests/api/test_curve25519.c` and TLS key-share parsing. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete production issue promoted from `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/error-crypt.h`, `sha256.h`, `wc_mlkem.h`, `random.h`, `wc_lms.h`, `hash.h`, `mlkem.h`, `wc_xmss.h`, `sha3.h`, `ed25519.h`, `hmac.h` beyond the X25519 validation issue above - location: listed headers plus adjacent spot checks in `wolfcrypt/src/{random.c,hash.c,hmac.c,ed25519.c,wc_mlkem.c,wc_mlkem_poly.c}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mostly API/struct/macro surface area. Adjacent code checks did not establish a stronger new exploit path: `wc_MlKemKey_Decapsulate()` enforces exact ciphertext length and re-encodes/compares ciphertext (`wolfcrypt/src/wc_mlkem.c:1524,1557`), private-key decode verifies the embedded public-key hash (`:1763`), generic hash wrappers reject bad args / undersized outputs (`wolfcrypt/src/hash.c:541,825,935`), and HMAC enforces FIPS minimum key length unless explicitly bypassed for HKDF internals (`wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:524-527,1581-1582,1655-1656`). `random.h` itself exposes per-`WC_RNG` PID tracking, and adjacent `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:914-915,1405-1407` already reseeds per-fork for the low-level RNG path, so this chunk did not add a stronger RNG issue than the previously logged OpenSSL-compat RAND/fork finding.

### Medium - `USE_INTEGER_HEAP_MATH` rebrands variable-time big-int helpers as `*_ct` and uses them in private-key paths, reintroducing timing side channels when the option is enabled - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/integer.h:33,319,350,373`; enable knobs `configure.ac:1125-1132`, `examples/configs/user_settings_template.h:96`; affected private-op path `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c:2532-2770` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: build opts into heap math (`--enable-heapmath` or shipped user settings defining `USE_INTEGER_HEAP_MATH`) and exposes attacker-triggerable private-key work such as RSA sign/decrypt. - evidence: the heap-math header explicitly warns it "is not timing resistant" and then aliases constant-time-named helpers to variable-time ones: `mp_to_unsigned_bin_len_ct -> mp_to_unsigned_bin_len`, `mp_cmp_ct -> mp_cmp`, `mp_montgomery_reduce_ct -> mp_montgomery_reduce`. The RSA private implementation `RsaFunctionPrivate()` still calls `mp_montgomery_reduce_ct(tmp, &key->n, mp)` and later `mp_to_unsigned_bin_len_ct(tmp, out, (int)*outLen)`, so heap-math builds silently lose the constant-time behavior those call sites imply. - impact: deployments built with heap math can reintroduce measurable timing differences on private RSA operations (and related secret-key big-int work), weakening key confidentiality and making remote/local side-channel attacks more feasible than operators expect from the `*_ct` call sites. - recommendation: do not allow `USE_INTEGER_HEAP_MATH` in production secret-key builds; fail compilation when heap math is combined with private RSA/ECC/DH/TLS, or provide real constant-time implementations instead of aliasing `*_ct` names to variable-time primitives.

### Reviewed - no additional concrete issue promoted from `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/async.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sp.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/logging.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sha512.h`, and `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/wc_slhdsa.h` beyond the heap-math side-channel exposure above - location: listed headers plus adjacent `wolfcrypt/src/async.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/logging.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/sha512.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/wc_slhdsa.c` spot checks - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mostly API/type declarations and feature/config glue. Adjacent searches did not establish a stronger new trust-boundary bug than the heap-math constant-time aliasing issue; the remaining hits here were expected platform-specific wrappers, bounded SLH-DSA size checks, and debug/async declarations without a clearer exploit path from this segment alone.

### Medium - Public `Aes` API exposes a contradictory init/free contract, making config-dependent uninitialized-state bugs and resource leaks easy for callers - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:277-506,536,767-774`; docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/aes.h:3,41,50`; implementation `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:5065-5067,13511-13694` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `struct Aes` is a public, backend-heavy object with mutable dispatch flags/resources (`use_aesni`, `use_aes_hw_crypto`, AFALG `alFd`/`rdFd`, kcapi handle, SE050/Xilinx/PSA state), and the public `wc_AesSetKey()` surface is declared directly on that struct. The shipped docs still describe `wc_AesSetKey()` as a function that “initializes an AES structure by setting the key and [IV]”, but the implementation comment says `wc_AesInit()` before `wc_AesSetKey()` “is imperative, to avoid a read of uninitialized data in aes->use_aesni”. `wc_AesFree()` later closes backend handles/Fds and `ForceZero(aes, sizeof(Aes))`, and `WC_AESFREE_IS_MANDATORY` is enabled for many backend builds. - impact: consumers can reasonably skip `wc_AesInit()` / `wc_AesFree()` based on the public API/docs, then hit config-specific UB on AESNI/HW builds or leak backend resources on AFALG/kcapi/SE050/Xilinx/PSA paths. The lifetime rules are hard to infer locally and easy to misuse outside the test suite. - recommendation: make `Aes` opaque or make `wc_AesSetKey()` fail closed/auto-init on first use; update the public header/examples so `wc_AesInit()`/`wc_AesFree()` are consistently mandatory and remove the conflicting “initializes an AES structure” wording. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Medium - `WOLFSSL_DILITHIUM_ASSIGN_KEY` turns Dilithium imports into borrowed aliases, so later key use reads caller memory and safe wrappers become lifetime-unsafe - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/dilithium.h:737-747`; aliasing imports `wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.c:11492-11495,11580-11583`; cleanup `wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.c:10891-10930`; affected safe wrapper surface `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.rs:600-603,643-646,768,861,1305-1311` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_DILITHIUM_ASSIGN_KEY` and a caller imports key bytes from a buffer whose lifetime or contents are not guaranteed for the full `dilithium_key` lifetime (especially FFI/safe-language wrappers). - evidence: with that macro the key struct stores `const byte* p; const byte* k;` instead of owned arrays; `wc_dilithium_import_public()` assigns `key->p = in` and `wc_dilithium_import_private()` assigns `key->k = priv`; `wc_dilithium_free()` only `ForceZero(key, sizeof(*key))`, so borrowed private bytes are neither owned nor scrubbed. The Rust wrapper exposes safe `import_public`/`import_private` from `&[u8]`, later `export_private`/`sign_msg`, and `Drop`, but has no lifetime tie to the source slice. - impact: later sign/export/verify paths can read freed or attacker-mutated memory, and imported private-key bytes remain in caller buffers after `free`, creating integrity failures and potential memory-safety bugs across wrappers. - recommendation: keep import APIs copy-owning; if alias mode must exist, split it into explicitly borrowed APIs with documented lifetime rules, disable safe wrappers when the macro is on, and avoid silently changing import ownership semantics at compile time. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - PKCS#8 encryption surfaces still expose RC4, 40-bit RC2, MD5+DES, SHA1+DES, and DES/3DES as normal options for private-key wrapping - location: public enums `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/asn_public.h:91-103`; helper entry points `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/asn_public.h:865-871`; implementation cases `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:9336-9393,10020,10210-10216`; example CLI `examples/pem/pem.c:428-440,493-494` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application or operator chooses one of these public PKCS#8/PBE identifiers or uses the shipped PEM example with the advertised legacy mode names. - evidence: the public enum exports `ENC_PKCS8_PBE_SHA1_RC4_128`, `ENC_PKCS8_PBE_SHA1_40RC2_CBC`, `ENC_PKCS8_PBES1_MD5_DES`, `ENC_PKCS8_PBES1_SHA1_DES`, `ENC_PKCS8_ALG_DES`, and `ENC_PKCS8_ALG_DES3`; `asn.c` contains live `PBE_SHA1_RC4_128`, `PBE_SHA1_40RC2_CBC`, `PBES1_MD5_DES`, and `PBES1_SHA1_DES` cases that flow into `wc_CryptKey`; `examples/pem` advertises the same weak names as ordinary CLI choices. - impact: developers can generate “encrypted” private-key files with materially weak legacy crypto, reducing private-key-at-rest protection and making offline password attacks cheaper than PBES2+AES defaults. - recommendation: legacy-gate or remove these modes from normal builds/examples, mark them unsafe if retained for interoperability, and steer callers to PBES2 + AES-256 + strong PBKDF2/HMAC defaults. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete production issue promoted from the rest of this header-heavy chunk (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ecc.h`, remaining `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/asn_public.h` declarations) beyond the Dilithium ownership toggle and PKCS#8 legacy-encryption exposure above - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ecc.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/asn_public.h`; adjacent checks `wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c`, `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.rs`, `examples/pem/pem.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mostly public type/constant/API declarations. Spot checks around the few trust-boundary-sensitive declarations surfaced the two concrete issues above; the remaining ECC/ASN declarations here did not show a stronger memory-safety, authz, or material performance defect on their own.

### Medium - Falcon PKCS#8 export uses secret-key length for the embedded public key, producing malformed DER and copying adjacent private-key bytes into the `publicKey` field - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/falcon.h:52-60,76,92-93`, `wolfcrypt/src/falcon.c:926,933-939`, serializer copy in `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:38721` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `falcon.h` defines distinct `FALCON_LEVEL{1,5}_KEY_SIZE` and `FALCON_LEVEL{1,5}_PUB_KEY_SIZE`, and `struct falcon_key` stores `p[FALCON_MAX_PUB_KEY_SIZE]` separately from `k[FALCON_MAX_PRV_KEY_SIZE]`. But `wc_Falcon_KeyToDer()` passes `FALCON_LEVEL{1,5}_KEY_SIZE` as both the private and public length to `SetAsymKeyDer(...)`; `SetAsymKeyDer` then copies `pubKeyLen` bytes into the DER. For Falcon-512/1024 that makes the encoded `publicKey` BIT STRING longer than the real public key and pulls bytes past `p` into adjacent `k` storage. - impact: exported Falcon private-key DER is non-canonical/malformed and duplicates secret-buffer bytes inside the nominal public-key field, creating avoidable interop failures and brittle key-serialization behavior. - recommendation: pass `FALCON_LEVEL{1,5}_PUB_KEY_SIZE` for the public-key length in `wc_Falcon_KeyToDer()`, and add a round-trip regression that asserts the PKCS#8 `publicKey` BIT STRING length matches the Falcon public key size. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - incremental CMAC state uses a 32-bit byte counter as a chaining sentinel, so streams past 4 GiB can wrap and miscompute the MAC - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/cmac.h:56-74`, `wolfcrypt/src/cmac.c:241,246` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller MACs a message or stream larger than 4 GiB through repeated `wc_CmacUpdate()` calls (directly or through a higher-level mode reusing this CMAC core). - evidence: `struct Cmac` stores `word32 totalSz`; `wc_CmacUpdate()` uses `if (cmac->totalSz != 0)` to decide whether to XOR in the previous digest before encrypting the next full block, then advances state with `cmac->totalSz += WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE`. Once `totalSz` wraps at 2^32 bytes, the next full block is treated like the first block and chaining is skipped. - impact: CMAC tags on very large inputs become non-standard/wrong, causing integrity failures and cross-implementation interoperability breaks on large-file or long-stream authentication paths. - recommendation: track first-block state separately from the processed-byte count, or widen the counter to `word64`, and add a regression that crosses the 4 GiB boundary with at least one additional full block. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete issue promoted from `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sha.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/dh.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/misc.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ed448.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/poly1305.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/fe_operations.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/kdf.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/lms.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sakke.h`, and `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/wolfmath.h` beyond the Falcon and CMAC findings above - location: listed headers plus adjacent `wolfcrypt/src/dh.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/ed448.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/kdf.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/sakke.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/wc_lms.c` spot checks - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is mostly public API/feature-gating declarations. Adjacent spot checks did not establish a stronger new trust-boundary bug than the Falcon DER length mix-up and the CMAC large-stream counter wrap; the remaining areas reviewed here showed expected argument bounds/checks (for example Ed448 trusted/untrusted import branching, SAKKE identity-size checks, HKDF label-size bounds, and DH public-key validation entry points) rather than a new concrete exploit path.

### Medium - `WC_RNG_BANK_SUPPORT` loses its promised thread safety under `WOLFSSL_NO_ATOMICS`, so concurrent callers can check out the same DRBG instance - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/rng_bank.h:25-32,59-65`; implementation `wolfcrypt/src/rng_bank.c:250,338-351,458-460`; no-atomics fallback `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/wc_port.h:582-585,655-663` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WC_RNG_BANK_SUPPORT` with `WOLFSSL_NO_ATOMICS` in a multithreaded environment (the API is documented for thread-safe/kernel use). - evidence: `rng_bank.h` advertises a bank of persistent RNGs with “thread safety” but downgrades each instance lock to plain `int` when `WOLFSSL_NO_ATOMICS` is set. `wc_rng_bank_checkout()` relies on `wolfSSL_Atomic_Int_CompareExchange()` for exclusive ownership, yet the no-atomics fallback is just `if (*c == *expected_i) { *c = new_i; return 1; }` with plain `WOLFSSL_ATOMIC_LOAD/STORE`, and `wc_rng_bank_checkin()` clears the lock the same way. - impact: two threads can simultaneously “win” checkout of the same `struct wc_rng_bank_inst`, racing on one shared `WC_RNG` state and turning a randomness boundary into corrupted/duplicate DRBG output or request-triggered crashes. - recommendation: refuse `WC_RNG_BANK_SUPPORT` on multithreaded `WOLFSSL_NO_ATOMICS` builds, or replace the fake compare-exchange path with a real mutex-backed lock when atomics are unavailable. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - BLAKE2 init reads packed parameter blocks through unaligned word casts, crashing strict-alignment little-endian builds - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/blake2-int.h:64-100`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/blake2-impl.h:41-58`, `wolfcrypt/src/blake2b.c:106-115,121-149`, `wolfcrypt/src/blake2s.c:102-111,118-145` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables BLAKE2 on a strict-alignment little-endian target; any caller reaches `wc_InitBlake2b()`/`wc_InitBlake2s()` or keyed init. - evidence: the BLAKE2 parameter structs are packed with `#pragma pack(push, 1)`. `blake2b_init()` / `blake2s_init()` instantiate `blake2[b|s]_param P[1]` and pass it to `blake2[b|s]_init_param()`, which folds the packed buffer into state with `S->h[i] ^= load64(...)` / `load32(...)`. On little-endian builds `load64()` and `load32()` do raw `*(word64*)(src)` / `*(word32*)(src)` dereferences; unlike `store64()`, the read side has no `WOLFSSL_GENERAL_ALIGNMENT` guard. - impact: normal BLAKE2 initialization can fault with SIGBUS/unaligned-access traps, yielding attacker-triggerable DoS in services that hash request data with BLAKE2 on strict-alignment targets. - recommendation: stop dereferencing packed BLAKE2 params via raw word casts; use alignment-safe byte/memcpy loads for `load32/load64` on possibly packed addresses, or remove packing from the local init path. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - OpenSSL-compat security-level API is a silent no-op, so ports that rely on `SSL_CTX_set_security_level()` keep negotiating weaker TLS than intended - location: `wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h:397-398`, `wolfssl/ssl.h:3379-3381,6104-6105,2845`, `src/ssl.c:5242-5244,18555-18557`, `tests/api.c:25839,25852,25859,1713-1740,6908` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application is ported from OpenSSL, uses `SSL_CTX_set_security_level()` / `SSL_set_security_level()` as its main TLS hardening control, and does not separately pin protocol versions, key sizes, and cipher suites. - evidence: the OpenSSL shim explicitly states `wolfSSL does not support security levels` and still aliases `SSL_CTX_set_security_level` to `wolfSSL_CTX_set_security_level` (`wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h`). Public `ssl.h` still advertises set/get APIs. The implementation comments say setting the level is unsupported and only a system crypto-policy can do it (`src/ssl.c`). The shipped test `test_wolfSSL_security_level()` sets level 1 then expects `SSL_CTX_get_security_level(ctx) == 0`, proving the setter is ignored. In the same compat surface, the default cipher list is `"" /* default all */`, and the API tests exercise broad/legacy suites including `RC4-SHA`, `DES-CBC3-SHA`, and `NULL-SHA`, showing weak suites are still part of normal cipher-list handling unless the application restricts them separately. - impact: an OpenSSL-compatible application can believe it enforced a stronger security policy while wolfSSL actually leaves weaker cipher/protocol/key-size choices enabled, creating downgrade/misconfiguration exposure on production TLS endpoints after migration. - recommendation: fail closed when the unsupported security-level setters are called (return an error rather than silently ignoring), or implement real OpenSSL-style enforcement over protocol minimums, cipher filtering, and min key sizes; document the migration hazard prominently and add a regression that `set_security_level()` either changes behavior or fails explicitly. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - default RSA/DH acceptance floor stays at 1024 bits unless hardening mode is enabled, so stock builds accept sub-112-bit peer keys on TLS/X.509 trust boundaries - location: `wolfssl/internal.h:1225-1237,1923-1935`, init/enforcement `src/internal.c:2558-2562,7102-7106,32173`, `src/ssl_certman.c:136,3063-3069` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build uses default policy (`!WOLFSSL_HARDEN_TLS`, `!WOLFSSL_MAX_STRENGTH`); attacker controls the peer certificate key size or DH parameters presented during handshake. - evidence: `internal.h` defaults both `WOLFSSL_MIN_DHKEY_BITS` and `WOLFSSL_MIN_RSA_BITS` to `1024` in the non-hardened path. Context init copies those values into `ctx->minDhKeySz` / `ctx->minRsaKeySz`, and runtime verification only rejects below those minima (`length < ssl->options.minDhKeySz`; `cert->pubKeySize < cm->minRsaKeySz`). - impact: wolfSSL clients/servers can silently accept 1024-bit DH groups and 1024-bit RSA certs/keys by default, leaving downgrade/legacy-crypto exposure that modern 2048-bit-baseline deployments expect to fail closed. - recommendation: raise the default RSA/DH minima to 2048 bits outside an explicit legacy-compat mode, and require an opt-in override for 1024-bit interoperability. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`

### Medium - `--enable-nullcipher` / `--enable-anon` compatibility builds automatically advertise cleartext or unauthenticated suites in the default suite list - location: build flags `configure.ac:4506-4513,5675-5691`; auto-enable logic `wolfssl/internal.h:452,649`; default-suite insertion `src/internal.c:3785-3788,4196-4199`; exposed names `src/internal.c:28120-28133` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: operator compiles wolfSSL with `--enable-nullcipher` or `--enable-anon` (or equivalent macros) and does not replace the default cipher list; a peer offers these suites as the best mutual match. - evidence: `configure.ac` maps those options to `HAVE_NULL_CIPHER` / `HAVE_ANON`. `internal.h` then defines `BUILD_TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_NULL_SHA` and `BUILD_TLS_DH_anon_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA`, and `InitSuites()` appends them to `suites->suites` by default. The shipped cipher table publishes them as `ECDHE-ECDSA-NULL-SHA` and `ADH-AES128-SHA`. - impact: a build that merely enables compatibility support can end up negotiating no encryption (`NULL`) or no peer authentication (`anon DH`) unless the application remembers to lock the cipher list down, creating passive-sniffing and active-MITM risk. - recommendation: keep NULL/anonymous suites compile-available but out of the default negotiation set; require an explicit cipher-list opt-in or dedicated `ALLOW_LEGACY_SUITES` gate before advertising them. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - OpenSSL-compat/session-cert cache shape can turn large session caches into massive static memory footprints - location: `wolfssl/internal.h:1966-1972,4591-4598,4737`, auto-enable block `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/settings.h:2502-2503`, cache sizing/storage `src/ssl_sess.c:69-70,93-111` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: each `WOLFSSL_SESSION` embeds `WOLFSSL_X509_CHAIN chain`, which embeds `MAX_CHAIN_DEPTH` (`9`) `x509_buffer`s, each with `byte buffer[MAX_X509_SIZE]` (`2048`). The settings bundle at `settings.h:2502-2503` auto-defines `SESSION_CERTS`, and `HUGE_SESSION_CACHE` sizes the cache to `SESSIONS_PER_ROW 11` and `SESSION_ROWS 5981` (65,791 slots). When `NO_SESSION_CACHE_REF` is not set, `SessionRow` stores `WOLFSSL_SESSION Sessions[SESSIONS_PER_ROW]` inline and `SessionCache[SESSION_ROWS]` is static. - impact: compatibility builds that combine session cert caching with a large cache can consume enormous BSS/RAM and cache-serialization bandwidth before any traffic arrives, making embedded/server deployments fragile and raising startup/OOM risk. - recommendation: decouple peer-chain storage from every cached session, default large caches to reference mode, and make `SESSION_CERTS` an explicit opt-in with tighter per-session size accounting. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - QUIC input buffering has only a per-record cap, no per-connection queue limit, so a peer can drive unbounded heap growth through `wolfSSL_provide_quic_data()` - location: `wolfssl/quic.h:187-193,299-301`, `src/quic.c:73,82,91,142,713,735-741`, `wolfssl/internal.h:6424-6426`, compat alias `wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h:1930` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: QUIC-enabled application forwards attacker-controlled CRYPTO data into `wolfSSL_provide_quic_data()` / `SSL_provide_quic_data()`. - evidence: the QUIC input path allocates each record with `XMALLOC` and may `XREALLOC` it up to `WOLFSSL_QUIC_MAX_RECORD_CAPACITY` (1 MiB). Completed records are linked onto `ssl->quic.input_head/input_tail`; the only tracked state is `input_head`, `input_tail`, and `scratch`, and repo search found no total buffered-byte or record-count limit. The enqueue path rejects only lower encryption levels (`level < ssl->quic.input_tail->level`), so many same-level records can accumulate. - impact: a malicious peer can send many syntactically acceptable CRYPTO records/fragments faster than the app drains handshake/post-handshake input, causing per-connection heap growth and remote memory DoS well beyond normal socket-buffer backpressure. - recommendation: enforce a per-connection buffered-byte/record cap in `wolfSSL_provide_quic_data()`, fail/close on overflow, and document the required drain cadence for callers. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no additional material issue promoted from `wolfssl/sniffer_error.h`, `wolfssl/sniffer_error.rc`, `wolfssl/callbacks.h`, `wolfssl/crl.h`, `wolfssl/version.h`, `wolfssl/include.am`, `wolfssl/options.h.in`, or `wolfssl/version.h.in` beyond already-tracked CRL loader issues and low-signal maintenance risks - location: listed files; adjacent checks `src/sniffer.c:239,376-380`, `src/internal.c:29907-30029`, `src/tls13.c:5800-5801,7883-7885,8031-8033,9258-9260,10100-10102,11518,11715-11717`, `src/crl.c:560,827-908,1152,2146-2299` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the sniffer error ids/resource strings/message table appear synchronized through code 99 (`KEYLOG_FILE_INVALID`); the callback packet arrays are bounds-checked and only expose lossy debug bookkeeping rather than a stronger trust-boundary break in this pass; `crl.h` here is an internal declaration surface for CRL paths already captured elsewhere; the version/options/include files are generated/build metadata without a clearer runtime exploit or material performance failure from this chunk alone.

### Medium - enabling built-in OCSP/CRL lookups turns certificate revocation URLs into outbound raw HTTP connects (SSRF from peer cert metadata) - location: `wolfssl/wolfio.h:710-758`; runtime `src/ssl_certman.c:1743,2170-2171,2262-2263`, `src/ocsp.c:534-559`, `src/crl.c:588-589`, `src/wolfio.c:1656,1706,2195-2211,2297-2311` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application enables `wolfSSL_CertManagerEnableOCSP()` / `wolfSSL_CertManagerEnableCRL()` with default callbacks and verifies attacker-influenced CA-signed certificates or chains (common in private PKI / mTLS / enterprise trust deployments). - evidence: enabling CRL/OCSP wires `cm->crl->crlIOCb = EmbedCrlLookup` and `cm->ocspIOCb = EmbedOcspLookup`. `CheckOcspRequest()` takes the URL from `ocspRequest->url` unless an override is configured, and `CheckCertCRL_ex()` passes `extCrlInfo` from the certificate into `crlIOCb`. The embedded fetchers accept only `http://` (`wolfIO_DecodeUrl()`), default port `80`, then call `wolfIO_TcpConnect(&sfd, domainName, port, ...)` and send raw HTTP requests built from the certificate URL/path. - impact: peer certificate metadata crosses directly into outbound network access, enabling blind SSRF/egress to internal HTTP services or metadata endpoints and leaking validation traffic over plaintext HTTP. - recommendation: do not auto-install network fetchers on revocation enablement; require an explicit caller-supplied allowlisted callback, or at minimum restrict host/scheme/port against policy and prefer stapled/local revocation data over live URL chasing. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - documented DTLS cookie callback API appears unwired: `wolfSSL_CTX_SetGenCookie()` stores `CBIOCookie`, but DTLS cookie generation uses internal helpers and repo-wide reads of `CBIOCookie` are absent - location: `wolfssl/wolfio.h:973-979`, `src/wolfio.c:2447-2450`, live DTLS cookie path `src/dtls.c:211,287,900,905`, storage fields `wolfssl/internal.h:4033,6141` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `wolfSSL_CTX_SetGenCookie()` only does `ctx->CBIOCookie = cb`. Repo-wide search found no read/call of `CBIOCookie` beyond that assignment, while DTLS 1.2 cookie handling generates/sends cookies through `CreateDtls12Cookie()` and `SendHelloVerifyRequest()`. The only shipped test around this API sets the callback and cookie ctx but does not prove callback execution (`tests/api.c:15326-15330`). - impact: applications can believe they installed custom DTLS anti-DoS cookie logic, but the library appears to ignore it; that creates security-posture/configuration drift and leaves a dead, misleading API surface. - recommendation: either wire `CBIOCookie` into DTLS cookie generation/verification and add an execution test, or deprecate/remove the API/docs so cookie behavior is locally understandable. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Reviewed - no additional material issue promoted from `wolfssl/wolfio.h` macro/portability scaffolding beyond the revocation-fetch SSRF and dead cookie-callback concerns above - location: `wolfssl/wolfio.h` and adjacent `src/wolfio.c`, `src/ocsp.c`, `src/crl.c`, `src/dtls.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the rest of this chunk is mostly platform socket typedef/error-code glue and callback declarations. Adjacent checks did not surface a stronger new memory-safety bug or higher-severity exploit path in this pass.

### Medium - sniffer public APIs can overflow caller-owned error buffers because they accept only `char* error` but always write up to 80 bytes - location: `wolfssl/sniffer.h:70-151,184-188,339-349`; sink `src/sniffer.c:147,385-391,1440-1443`; network entry `src/sniffer.c:6900-6911` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: embedding application passes a smaller-than-80-byte `error` buffer; a remote malformed packet or a bad key/keylog input triggers a sniffer error path. - evidence: the public sniffer APIs expose `char* error` with no accompanying length. Internally, `SetError()` calls `GetError(idx, error); TraceError(idx, error);`, and `GetError()` unconditionally copies `MAX_ERROR_LEN-1` bytes with `MAX_ERROR_LEN = 80`. The decode/parser path reachable from `ssl_DecodePacketWithSessionInfo()` / `ssl_DecodePacket()` raises `SetError(...)` on malformed packet/header cases (`src/sniffer.c:2143,2153,2204,3446`). - impact: attacker-controlled malformed traffic can turn a too-small host-side error buffer into a stack/heap overwrite in programs embedding the sniffer API. - recommendation: add an explicit error-buffer length parameter to all `char* error` APIs, or return const/dynamically allocated error strings instead of copying into caller memory; at minimum, enforce and document an 80-byte minimum in one public macro/API contract. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - sniffer callback registration is process-global and unsynchronized, so concurrent reconfiguration can call the wrong callback with the wrong context - location: `wolfssl/sniffer.h:190-197,267-270`; globals `src/sniffer.c:591-613`; setters `src/sniffer.c:7083-7093,7152-7158,7252-7263`; use sites `src/sniffer.c:2318-2323,6523-6532` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: multithreaded application changes sniffer callbacks or contexts after packet processing has started. - evidence: `ConnectionCb`, `ConnectionCbCtx`, `KeyCb`, `WatchCb`, and `StoreDataCb` are static `WC_THREADSHARED` globals. The setter APIs do plain assignments (`ConnectionCb = cb; ConnectionCbCtx = ctx; StoreDataCb = cb; KeyCb = cb; WatchCb = cb;`) with no mutex/atomic pairing, while decode-time code later dereferences the callback and separate context (`ConnectionCb((const void*)session, &info, ConnectionCbCtx)`) and invokes `StoreDataCb` on decrypted plaintext. - impact: hot-swapping callbacks can race with live packet handling, causing stale fn/context pairings, crashes/UAF, or leaking decrypted data/session metadata to the wrong consumer. - recommendation: store callback+ctx as an atomic snapshot or protect setter/use with a lock; better, make these callbacks per-sniffer instance and document setter APIs as init-time-only if runtime changes are unsupported. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Low - `error-ssl.h` gives `DTLS_TOO_MANY_FRAGMENTS_E` and `DTLS_PARTIAL_RECORD_READ` the same value (`-455`), collapsing fatal and retryable DTLS outcomes - location: `wolfssl/error-ssl.h:202,224`; return/use sites `src/internal.c:9787,9800,11971,12067,19561,22384`; string mapping `src/internal.c:27538,27647-27650` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the enum assigns both `DTLS_TOO_MANY_FRAGMENTS_E` and `DTLS_PARTIAL_RECORD_READ` to `-455`. Runtime returns `DTLS_PARTIAL_RECORD_READ` for ignorable partial datagrams (`src/internal.c:11971,12067`) and treats that code specially in the receive path (`src/internal.c:22384`), while fragment-limit exhaustion returns `DTLS_TOO_MANY_FRAGMENTS_E` (`src/internal.c:9787,9800,19561`). `SetErrorString()` can only represent one meaning for `-455`; repo search finds a `case DTLS_TOO_MANY_FRAGMENTS_E:` but no separate `DTLS_PARTIAL_RECORD_READ` case. - impact: callers using `wolfSSL_get_error()` / error-string translation cannot distinguish a benign partial-datagram retry condition from a fragment-flood hard failure, weakening DTLS retry, telemetry, and DoS triage logic. - recommendation: assign `DTLS_PARTIAL_RECORD_READ` a unique code and add regression coverage that checks both numeric returns and human-readable error strings for the two paths. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no stronger repo-specific vulnerability was promoted from `wolfssl/ocsp.h` / adjacent `src/ocsp.c` in this pass - location: `wolfssl/ocsp.h`, adjacent `src/ocsp.c:203-224,490-559,1682-1955` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this header mostly exposes OCSP object aliases and OpenSSL-compat request-context helpers. Adjacent checks showed wolfSSL’s internal cert-validation path uses `CheckCertOCSP_ex()` -> `CheckOcspRequest()` with callback-driven I/O/URL handling, while the `WOLFSSL_OCSP_REQ_CTX_*` HTTP/BIO helpers appear to mirror caller-supplied path/header/BIO data rather than exposing a clearer repo-specific trust-boundary break from this chunk alone.

### High - linuxkm direct-RSA decrypt advertises a full-size output to `wc_RsaDirect()` while only allocating `req->dst_len`, enabling kernel heap overwrite on short destination buffers - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_rsa_glue.c:765-836`; API contract `doc/dox_comments/header_files/rsa.h:151-152` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: wolfSSL linuxkm is built with `WC_RSA_NO_PADDING`/`LINUXKM_DIRECT_RSA` and a local caller reaches the kernel crypto `rsa` decrypt path with `0 < req->dst_len < key_len` (for example via AF_ALG or another in-kernel crypto consumer). - evidence: `km_direct_rsa_dec()` accepts any `req->dst_len` up to `ctx->key_len`, allocates `dec = malloc(req->dst_len)`, but sets `out_len = ctx->key_len` and calls `wc_RsaDirect(enc, ctx->key_len, dec, &out_len, ctx->key, RSA_PRIVATE_DECRYPT, &ctx->rng)`. The wolfCrypt API docs say `outSz` must be passed in as the length of the `out` buffer. Because `wc_RsaDirect()` only sees the pointer plus `out_len == key_len`, it is told the undersized heap buffer is `key_len` bytes long. - impact: a short-buffer direct-RSA decrypt request can write a full modulus-sized plaintext into a smaller kernel heap allocation before the later `if (out_len > req->dst_len)` check runs, yielding kernel memory corruption / local DoS and possible privilege escalation. - recommendation: fail closed before decrypt unless `req->dst_len >= ctx->key_len` (and set `req->dst_len = ctx->key_len` like the encrypt path), or pass the real allocation size to `wc_RsaDirect()` and never call it with a smaller-than-modulus destination for direct RSA. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`

### Medium - `ShowX509Chain()` ignores `wolfSSL_get_chain_cert_pem()` failure/size output and can write past a 3072-byte stack buffer - location: `wolfssl/test.h:1272-1286`; supporting callee behavior `src/ssl.c:13729,13734` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `SHOW_CERTS` + `SESSION_CERTS` + (`OPENSSL_EXTRA` or `OPENSSL_EXTRA_X509_SMALL`), and a peer can present a certificate chain containing a PEM-encoded cert larger than the fixed local buffer (or otherwise trigger `wolfSSL_get_chain_cert_pem()` failure). - evidence: `ShowX509Chain()` allocates `unsigned char certPem[3072];`, calls `wolfSSL_get_chain_cert_pem(chain, i, certPem, sizeof(certPem), &length);`, ignores the return value, then unconditionally does `certPem[length] = 0;` and prints `%s`. In `src/ssl.c`, `wolfSSL_get_chain_cert_pem()` stores the required output length in `*outLen` before checking capacity (`:13729`) and returns `BUFFER_E` when `inLen` is too small (`:13734`), so an oversized peer cert leaves `length > sizeof(certPem)` and the caller performs a deterministic stack OOB write/read. - impact: remote peer certificates can crash or corrupt stack memory in cert-display builds, turning a diagnostic path into a memory-safety bug. - recommendation: check the return code, treat `BUFFER_E` as a size query, allocate `length + 1` dynamically (or skip printing), and only NUL-terminate when `0 <= length < sizeof(certPem)`.

### Low - example session-ticket callback prints a 16-byte binary `key_name` with `%s`, causing out-of-bounds reads on unknown tickets - location: `wolfssl/test.h:4726-4809` (printf at `:4756`); wired into example servers at `examples/server/server.c:2797-2799`, `examples/echoserver/echoserver.c:172-174`; ticket-name size `wolfssl/ssl.h:4902` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - status: candidate - severity: low - exploitability/preconditions: example/server build enables the custom ticket callback (`wolfSSL_CTX_set_TicketEncCb(ctx, myTicketEncCb)`) and an unauthenticated client presents a resumption ticket with an unknown `key_name` lacking a NUL byte. - evidence: on decrypt, `myTicketEncCb()` compares the client-supplied `byte key_name[WOLFSSL_TICKET_NAME_SZ]` against `myKey_ctx.name` and on mismatch logs `printf("client presented unknown ticket key name %s\n", key_name);`. `WOLFSSL_TICKET_NAME_SZ` is 16 bytes, so `key_name` is arbitrary binary data, not a guaranteed C string. - impact: a remote client can make the example server read past the 16-byte ticket-name buffer into adjacent memory for logging, leaking stack bytes to logs or crashing the process in this example path. - recommendation: log the ticket name as bounded hex/bytes, or copy into a NUL-terminated scratch buffer before any `%s` formatting.

### Medium - Zephyr non-POSIX fallback `inet_addr()` never advances past `.` separators, so numeric IPv4 addresses are parsed incorrectly - location: `wolfssl/test.h:220-236,1439` - category: complexity - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `grugbrain: boring code` - status: candidate - severity: medium - evidence: the Zephyr fallback parser loops `for (i=0, j=0; i<4; i++)` and accumulates digits while `cp[j] != '.' && cp[j] != '\0'`, but never increments `j` after stopping on `'.'`. After the first octet, `j` stays on the dot, later octets remain zero, and `build_addr()` feeds numeric peers directly through this helper with `addr->sin_addr.s_addr = inet_addr(peer);`. - impact: non-POSIX Zephyr examples/tests misroute or fail numeric IPv4 connections, including the default `127.0.0.1` path, making network failures look like TLS bugs and breaking example interoperability. - recommendation: delete the custom parser and use `getaddrinfo`/`inet_pton` for numeric peers too; if kept, advance past separators and validate octet bounds.

### Medium - LKCAPI AES request cloning shallow-copies live `Aes` objects, so per-request cleanup can close/free backend state still owned by the session context - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_aes_glue.c:396-458,438,632,698,804,876,1273,1480,1975,2049,2163,2237`; shared state declared in `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:367-377,420-425`; teardown in `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:13636-13648,13669-13670`; backend population in `wolfcrypt/src/port/af_alg/afalg_aes.c:54,61` and `wolfcrypt/src/port/kcapi/kcapi_aes.c:64-65` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables an AES backend that stores owned resources inside `Aes` (for example AF_ALG sockets, KCAPI handles, or heap-backed `streamData`), and a kernel consumer uses this LKCAPI driver for CBC/CFB/GCM/CTR/OFB requests. - evidence: `km_AesGet()` allocates `malloc(sizeof(Aes))` and does `XMEMCPY(aes_copy, ret, sizeof(Aes))`; CTR/OFB repeat the same full-struct copy manually. The clone is then destroyed with `km_AesFree() -> wc_AesFree(*aes); free(*aes);`. `Aes` is not plain key material: it contains owned fields like `alFd`, `rdFd`, `struct kcapi_handle* handle`, and `byte* streamData`. `wc_AesFree()` closes/destroys/frees those resources. The glue only nulls `aes_copy->streamData` for one small-stack GCM build, leaving the other owned members aliased. - impact: one request can tear down resources still referenced by `ctx->aes_encrypt`/`ctx->aes_decrypt`, leading to stale-handle use, double-close/double-destroy on later requests, and request-triggered kernel crypto DoS/integrity failures. - recommendation: do not `memcpy` live `Aes` objects and then call `wc_AesFree()` on the clone. Add an explicit clone path that copies only mutable IV/counter/register state, or keep per-request scratch separate from backend-owned `Aes` resources. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - CBC/CFB/GCM/CTR/OFB data paths do a heap allocate/free of a full `Aes` struct for every request - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_aes_glue.c:396-447,632,698,804,876,1273,1480,1970-1975,2044-2049,2158-2163,2232-2237` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `km_AesGet(..., copy_p=1)` allocates `malloc(sizeof(Aes))`, copies the whole object, and frees it after each operation; CBC/CFB/GCM use that path, and CTR/OFB open-code the same `malloc` + `XMEMCPY` + `km_AesFree` sequence. - impact: high-rate kernel crypto users pay allocator churn in the hot path and can start failing requests with `-ENOMEM` under load or memory pressure even after successful key setup, which is especially costly for packet and block-storage callers. - recommendation: move mutable per-request state into request-local scratch/preallocated context, or copy only the small mutable fields needed for IV/counter isolation instead of heap-cloning the entire `Aes` object on every request. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `linuxkm_test_aescfb()` starts with the OFB self-test and logs failures as AES-GCM, so CFB triage points at the wrong primitive - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_aes_glue.c:2698-2701` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the CFB self-test function begins with `ret = aesofb_test();` and, on failure, emits `wc_test_render_error_message("aesgcm_test failed: ", ret);`. - impact: OFB regressions can fail the CFB test path for the wrong reason and the emitted error text misdirects maintainers toward AES-GCM, wasting debugging time and obscuring real kernel-module coverage. - recommendation: remove the stray OFB preflight or replace it with the intended CFB-specific check, and fix the failure label to match the code path. - reference: `grugbrain: testing`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - FIPS LKCAPI registration flips the global kernel `fips_enabled` flag on and does not restore it on normal unload/error paths - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_glue.c:274-279,355-363`; callers `linuxkm/module_hooks.c:1000-1015,1083-1086` - category: security/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `LINUXKM_LKCAPI_REGISTER` + `HAVE_FIPS` + `CONFIG_CRYPTO_MANAGER` build; admin loads the module or triggers `/sys/module/libwolfssl/install_algs`. - evidence: `linuxkm_lkcapi_register()` logs `changing fips_enabled from 0 to 1 for FIPS module` then does `enabled_fips = fips_enabled = 1`. The only reset is inside `deinstall_algs_handler()`. The normal module-init failure path and module exit path call `linuxkm_lkcapi_unregister()` / `linuxkm_lkcapi_sysfs_deinstall()` directly and never undo `fips_enabled`. - impact: one wolfSSL module registration attempt can leave unrelated kernel crypto users stuck in unexpected global FIPS mode until reboot/manual repair, changing algorithm availability/behavior and disabling the crypto manager's non-FIPS tests/fuzzing outside this module. - recommendation: do not mutate the global `fips_enabled` bit for module-local registration; if a temporary override is unavoidable, restore it in every success, failure, and unload path inside `linuxkm_lkcapi_register()`/`linuxkm_lkcapi_unregister()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - LKCAPI registration can fail open and keep an algorithm marked loaded after its self-test fails - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_glue.c:366-391,410-443`; entry points `linuxkm/module_hooks.c:1000-1015`, sysfs control `linuxkm/lkcapi_glue.c:235-251,287-288` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `LINUXKM_LKCAPI_REGISTER` build; a wolfCrypt-backed AES/SHA/ECDSA/ECDH/RSA/DH tester returns failure and `crypto_unregister_*()` does not immediately set `CRYPTO_ALG_DEAD` (a state the code explicitly anticipates). - evidence: both `REGISTER_ALG` macros register the algorithm first, run `tester()`, then on failure call `crypto_unregister_*()`. If `alg.base.cra_flags` is still not `CRYPTO_ALG_DEAD`, the code logs `marking as loaded despite test failure`, sets `alg_loaded = 1`, and increments `linuxkm_lkcapi_n_registered`. - impact: the kernel crypto API can retain and select a wolfCrypt implementation that just failed its own correctness/self-test, and registration rollback becomes partial/inconsistent instead of fail-closed. - recommendation: never set `alg_loaded` after a failed self-test; treat inability to kill/unregister the algorithm as a hard registration failure, roll back earlier registrations, and abort install until the failing algorithm is definitely unavailable. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - truncated KPP secret blobs bypass length validation and drive out-of-bounds reads in kernel DH/FFDHE import - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_dh_glue.c:316-366,479-539,584-667` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can reach the registered `dh` / `ffdhe*` `crypto_kpp_set_secret()` path (for example through an in-kernel caller or AF_ALG exposure on systems exporting these KPP algorithms). - evidence: `km_dh_decode_secret()` only checks `len < DH_KPP_SECRET_MIN_SIZE` and then compares the header’s `secret.len` against `expected_len` computed from attacker-controlled `key_size`, `p_size`, and `g_size`; it never verifies the actual callback argument `len` matches or even covers `expected_len`. A blob whose real `len` is only the minimum header size but whose embedded length fields self-consistently claim a larger payload will pass decode, after which `km_dh_set_secret()` feeds `params.p/g/key` into `wc_DhSetKey()` / `wc_DhImportKeyPair()` and `km_ffdhe_set_secret()` feeds `params.key` into `wc_DhImportKeyPair()`, reading beyond the supplied buffer. - impact: malformed DH/FFDHE secret imports can turn this kernel crypto registration into an out-of-bounds read / crash primitive and can contaminate imported DH state with adjacent kernel memory instead of failing closed. - recommendation: reject unless the real `len` matches the computed payload size, use checked addition for `expected_len`, and derive `params.{key,p,g}` only after validating the real buffer length. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no second chunk-specific issue promoted from `linuxkm/lkcapi_dh_glue.c` beyond the KPP secret length-validation flaw above - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_dh_glue.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining chunk content is mostly static KPP algorithm registration and self-test vector tables. Spot checks did not surface a stronger independent trust-boundary break than the shared `km_dh_decode_secret()` parser bug; the other notable rough edges here were lower-signal API/cleanup concerns rather than a clearer exploit or production incident path in this pass.

### Medium - VS2022 benchmark `ARM64` solution configs are fake and remap the app project to `x64`, making `ARM64` benchmark builds non-functional or mislabeled - location: `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark-VS2022.sln:32-33,38-39,44-45,50-51`; adjacent project config list `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark-VS2022.vcxproj:4-17` - category: performance/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the solution advertises `Debug|ARM64`, `DLL Debug|ARM64`, `DLL Release|ARM64`, and `Release|ARM64`, but for the benchmark project each `*.ARM64.ActiveCfg/Build.0` is mapped to `Debug|x64` or `Release|x64`. Adjacent `benchmark-VS2022.vcxproj` defines only `Win32` and `x64` project configurations, while the same solution maps `wolfssl-VS2022` to real `ARM64` configs. - impact: selecting `ARM64` in Visual Studio can build an x64 benchmark app against an ARM64 library config or otherwise produce non-ARM64 artifacts/results under ARM64 labels, breaking CI/package assumptions and invalidating benchmark numbers on the target architecture. - recommendation: add real ARM64 project configurations for the benchmark app, or remove the ARM64 solution configurations until they are actually supported. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Low - legacy `benchmark.vcxproj` is non-portable and x64-build brittle: repo-local header is referenced through a developer-specific path and x64 configs leave `ConfigurationType` implicit - location: `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.vcxproj:33-38,162` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the file declares `Debug|x64` and `Release|x64` configs but those property groups set only `<PlatformToolset>v143</PlatformToolset>`; unlike the Win32 groups they omit `<ConfigurationType>Application</ConfigurationType>`. The same project also lists `benchmark.h` via `..\..\..\..\..\github\wolfssl\wolfcrypt\benchmark\benchmark.h` even though the repo contains `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.h`. - impact: Visual Studio x64 benchmark builds depend on undeclared defaults, and checkouts outside a `...\github\wolfssl\...` path lose the project header item/IntelliSense context. That makes Windows benchmark maintenance and reproduction unnecessarily fragile. - recommendation: make x64 configs explicit `Application` builds and change the header entry to a repo-relative path such as `benchmark.h` or `..\..\wolfcrypt\benchmark\benchmark.h`. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Reviewed - `wolfcrypt/benchmark/README.md` mainly contains usage/docs drift (for example still pointing users at `benchmark.sln` / `wolfssl.vcxproj` rather than the VS2022 files) but this chunk did not establish a stronger security issue beyond the concrete Visual Studio project/config problems above - location: `wolfcrypt/benchmark/README.md:119-123` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the actionable issues in this chunk are the project-file misconfigurations with direct build/benchmark impact; the remaining README mismatches are documentation drift without a clearer trust-boundary break from this segment alone.

### Low - Windows benchmark projects hard-wire the full `IDE/WIN/user_settings.h` feature set into a wolfCrypt benchmark build, inflating build/binary surface and making benchmark results depend on unrelated TLS/X.509 features - location: `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark-VS2022.vcxproj:81-82,100-101,117-118,135-136`, `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.vcproj:41-42,116-117`; adjacent config `IDE/WIN/user_settings.h:11,23,40-45,65` - category: performance/complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: both Visual Studio benchmark projects add `../../IDE/WIN` to include paths and force `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS`; the referenced Windows user settings in turn enable unrelated features like `WOLFSSL_TLS13`, `OPENSSL_EXTRA`, `HAVE_CRL`, `HAVE_CRL_MONITOR`, `HAVE_OCSP`, `HAVE_OCSP_RESPONDER`, `WOLFSSL_CERT_GEN`, and `HAVE_ECC`. This is a benchmark target (`benchmark.c`) but its Windows build is coupled to a broad TLS/X.509 config rather than a minimal benchmark-specific one. - impact: Windows benchmark builds compile/link and ship more code than the benchmark itself needs, increasing build time/binary size and making published performance numbers/config behavior depend on non-benchmark feature toggles. - recommendation: give the benchmark projects a minimal dedicated `user_settings.h` (or stop forcing `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` there) so the benchmark measures wolfCrypt code without unrelated TLS/X.509/revocation features. - reference: `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete security issue promoted from `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.sln`, `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark-VS2022.vcxproj.user`, or the legacy VC project metadata beyond the Windows benchmark config-coupling concern above - location: `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.sln`, `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark-VS2022.vcxproj.user`, `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.vcproj` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is build/distribution metadata. Spot checks found stale/legacy IDE settings and debug-working-directory scaffolding, but no stronger trust-boundary bug, secret exposure, or runtime memory-safety issue from these files alone.

### Medium - x86 vector-register slot repair leaks `struct pid` references on live-slot collisions, allowing request-driven kernel memory growth in linuxkm builds - location: `linuxkm/x86_vector_register_glue.c:165-181`, collision callers `linuxkm/x86_vector_register_glue.c:345-352,438-449`, feature gate `linuxkm/linuxkm_wc_port.h:648-675`, init/cleanup `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:250,558` - category: security/performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: x86 linuxkm build with `WOLFSSL_USE_SAVE_VECTOR_REGISTERS`; repeated crypto requests hit `wc_save_vector_registers_x86()` while the current CPU slot is already owned by another live PID (the code explicitly treats migration/stale-slot collisions as expected enough to repair). - evidence: the occupied-slot path does `if (find_get_pid(slot_pid) == NULL) { ... reclaim ... }` and otherwise warns/returns `NULL`, but there is no `put_pid()` on the non-NULL path; repo-wide search found no matching `put_pid` in linuxkm. Each live collision therefore takes a pid reference and leaks it before `wc_save_vector_registers_x86()` unwinds with `BAD_STATE_E`/`WC_ACCEL_INHIBIT_E`. - impact: repeated collision/retry paths can pin PID objects and grow kernel memory until local DoS, while also making an already fragile per-CPU FPU-state recovery path harder to recover cleanly from. - recommendation: use a non-refcounting liveness probe (`find_vpid`/`pid_task`) or immediately `put_pid()` after successful `find_get_pid()`, and add a stress test for occupied-slot collision/migration that checks for refcount stability. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete issue promoted from `linuxkm/linuxkm-fips-hash.c`, `linuxkm/linuxkm_memory.h`, `linuxkm/pie_redirect_table.c`, `linuxkm/linuxkm-fips-hash-wrapper.sh`, `linuxkm/get_thread_size.c`, `linuxkm/Kbuild`, `linuxkm/module_exports.c.template`, `linuxkm/README.md`, `linuxkm/include.am`, and `linuxkm/wolfcrypt.lds` in this pass - location: listed files plus adjacent checks in `linuxkm/linuxkm_memory.c`, `linuxkm/Makefile`, `linuxkm/module_hooks.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk was dominated by linuxkm build glue, FIPS hash tooling, and linker/export metadata. Adjacent checks confirmed `wc_fips_generate_hash()` does range-check the FIPS fenceposts before hashing, and the remaining build-script hardening tradeoffs (`-fno-stack-protector`, optional objtool disable, broad symbol export generation) did not by themselves expose a stronger new trust-boundary break than the concrete x86 vector-register pid-ref leak above.

### Reviewed - no additional concrete issue promoted from `examples/asn1/dumpasn1.cfg` or its adjacent generator/consumer - location: `examples/asn1/dumpasn1.cfg`, `examples/asn1/gen_oid_names.rb`, `examples/asn1/oid_names.h`, `examples/asn1/asn1.c` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is an example OID-name data file, not a runtime parser boundary in the library. Adjacent code shows it is only consumed by the offline Ruby generator (`gen_oid_names.rb:123-136` reads `dumpasn1.cfg` and emits `oid_names.h`), while the example ASN.1 printer includes the generated table and does a simple linear lookup over `asn1App_oid_names_len` (`asn1.c:37,59-61`). The generator only imports `OID` and `Description`, escapes quotes in names, and does not execute config content. The remaining rough edges here are example-only data/lookup limitations (large static table, duplicate/legacy OID labels) without a clearer exploit path or material production impact from this chunk alone.

### Reviewed - no additional concrete issue promoted from `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.h` / adjacent benchmark harness code - location: `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.h`, `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.c:3690-3700,4847-4927,16354-16359,16558-16869`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/types.h:1177` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this header is only the benchmark/test entry surface (`benchmark_test`, `wolfcrypt_benchmark_main`, per-algorithm `bench_*` prototypes). Adjacent code shows a standalone harness rather than a production trust boundary: `benchmark_test()` just brackets `benchmark_init()` / `benchmark_free()` (`benchmark.c:4847-4927`), `wolfcrypt_benchmark_main()` parses operator-supplied CLI knobs with `XATOI(argv[1])` (`:16621,16675,16693,16714,16829`; `XATOI -> atoi` in `types.h:1177`), `benchmark_configure()` only updates local benchmark globals (`:16354-16359`), and `benchmark_init()` allocates benchmark buffers from `bench_size` (`:3690-3700`). That can self-inflict local benchmark misconfiguration/OOM, but this chunk did not expose a stronger repo-wide security, complexity, or material performance defect beyond benchmark-tool behavior.
