### Reviewed - no additional concrete issue promoted from generated example OID table `examples/asn1/oid_names.h` - location: `examples/asn1/oid_names.h`, adjacent consumer `examples/asn1/asn1.c:55-62` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is a generated data table (`oid_names.h:22,34,5571`) that is only included by the example ASN.1 dumper (`examples/asn1/asn1.c:37`). The consumer does a straightforward linear lookup over 2767 static OID/name pairs (`asn1App_OidToName()` loop at `:55-62`). That is example-local and not a stronger repo-wide trust-boundary, memory-safety, or material production performance issue on its own in this pass.

### Low - example ASN.1 printer accepts negative `--offset` / `--length` values that wrap to huge `word32` parse bounds and drive out-of-bounds reads - location: CLI parsing `examples/asn1/asn1.c:415,436-437`; option storage/use `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:44291,44295,45052,45055,45059`; example build scope `examples/asn1/include.am:7-8` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: the non-installed `examples/asn1/asn1` tool is built/run and an attacker or wrapper passes a negative or oversized `-o/--offset` or `-l/--length` argument. - evidence: `asn1.c` parses both options with `atoi(argv[0])` and casts directly to `word32`; `wc_Asn1PrintOptions_Set()` stores the value verbatim; `wc_Asn1_PrintAll()` then does `asn1->data = data + opts->offset`, and sets `asn1->max` to either `opts->length` or `len - opts->offset`, with no visible `offset <= len`/`length <= len` guard. Inputs like `-o -1` or `-l -1` therefore become ~4 GiB bounds against a much smaller heap buffer. - impact: crafted arguments can make the example parser walk past the loaded file buffer and crash while inspecting ASN.1, and any service/script that shells out to this helper inherits the same local memory-safety bug. - recommendation: replace `atoi` with checked `strtoul`/`strtol`, reject negatives/non-numeric input, and clamp `offset`/`length` to the loaded buffer before calling `wc_Asn1_PrintAll()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - PEM delimiter detection uses `strncmp()` on a raw file buffer without remaining-length checks, so truncated trailing hyphens trigger out-of-bounds reads - location: raw read buffer `examples/asn1/asn1.c:90-113`; PEM scan `examples/asn1/asn1.c:210-242`; example build scope `examples/asn1/include.am:7-8` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: the example ASN.1 printer is run in PEM mode on attacker-controlled input containing a short/truncated `-`, `-----`, or `-----EN` suffix near EOF. - evidence: `asn1App_ReadFile()` returns the file bytes plus length only; it does not append a NUL terminator. `FindPem()` iterates while `i < len` / `j < len` and, when the current byte is `'-'`, calls `strncmp((char*)data + i, "-----BEGIN", 10)`, `strncmp(..., "-----", 5)`, and `strncmp(..., "-----END", 8)` without checking that 10/5/8 bytes remain in-bounds. - impact: malformed PEM files can make the helper read past the heap allocation while searching for headers/footers, causing local crashes or adjacent-memory disclosure in debug tooling. - recommendation: check `i + 10 <= len`, `i + 5 <= len`, and `j + 8 <= len` before comparing, or switch to length-guarded `memcmp()` on the remaining slice instead of `strncmp()` on non-string data. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - `--base64` handling is internally inconsistent: help advertises `-B`, parser expects `-b64`, and explicit base64 mode is still overwritten by filename sniffing - location: help text `examples/asn1/asn1.c:317`; parser `examples/asn1/asn1.c:378-380`; `format_set` handling `examples/asn1/asn1.c:391,449,487-495` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: usage prints `-B, --base64`, but the option parser accepts `-b64`/`--base64`. Unlike `--der` and `--pem`, the base64 branch never sets `format_set = 1`, so a later `.pem` or `.der` filename suffix can silently override an explicit `--base64` request. - impact: operators and scripts can believe they forced base64 decoding while the tool takes a different parse path based on the filename, creating unnecessary triage friction and non-local behavior. - recommendation: accept the documented `-B` alias, set `format_set = 1` in the base64 branch, and add a regression covering `--base64 file.pem`. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Reviewed - no additional material issue promoted from `examples/asn1/gen_oid_names.rb` or `examples/asn1/include.am` beyond the example-tool parsing flaws above - location: `examples/asn1/gen_oid_names.rb`, `examples/asn1/include.am` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `include.am` only wires the example as a `noinst_PROGRAMS` target plus static assets, and the Ruby generator is an offline codegen helper for `oid_names.h`; this pass did not surface a stronger trust-boundary break there than the concrete CLI/PEM parsing issues in `examples/asn1/asn1.c`.

### Medium - RISC-V Poly1305 finalization leaves key-derived state resident after `wc_Poly1305Final()` - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/poly1305.h:122-130`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/riscv/riscv-64-poly1305.c:446-563,575-656`, AEAD callers `wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:301,448,452` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: RISC-V ASM Poly1305 build; attacker can later read or reuse a finalized `Poly1305` object (memory disclosure, pool reuse, crash dump, debug surface). - evidence: `wc_Poly1305SetKey()` stores the one-time pad in `ctx->pad[0..1]` and, on vector builds, precomputes `ctx->r2[]`. `wc_Poly1305Final()` zeroes `ctx->h[0..2]` and `ctx->r[0..1]`, but the `/* Zero out pad. */` block only reloads `ctx->pad[0..1]` into temporaries (`ld t3, %[ctx_pad_0]`; `ld t4, %[ctx_pad_1]`) and never stores zeros; this backend also has no `ctx->r2[]` wipe. - impact: finished ChaCha20-Poly1305/Poly1305 contexts retain one-time MAC key material and vector precomputes in memory, enlarging the blast radius of later context disclosure. - recommendation: explicitly zero `ctx->pad[0..1]` and `ctx->r2[]` before return (and preferably scrub leftover buffered bytes too); add a regression that inspects a finalized RISC-V Poly1305 context for cleared key state. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - RISC-V vector SHA-256 `wc_Sha256HashBlock()` returns digest words in the wrong order, breaking LMS interoperability - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/riscv/riscv-64-sha256.c:76-97,1044-1060,1137-1189`, LMS consumers `wolfcrypt/src/wc_lms_impl.c:222-390,470-573` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_RISCV_VECTOR_CRYPTO_ASM`, `WOLFSSL_HAVE_LMS`, and `!WOLFSSL_LMS_FULL_HASH`. - evidence: the vector backend stores SHA-256 state in non-standard order (`InitSha256()` comment/data: `f, e, b, a, h, g, d, c`), and `wc_Sha256FinalRaw()` has a dedicated reorder path using digest indices `3,2,7,6,1,0,5,4`. But `wc_Sha256HashBlock()` on the same backend just `ByteReverseWord32()`s `sha256->digest[0..7]` directly after `Sha256Transform(sha256, data, 1)`, then resets the vector-ordered state. `wc_lms_impl.c` uses `wc_Sha256HashBlock()` as the LMS fast path when full-hash mode is off. - impact: affected RISC-V builds compute a non-standard block hash for LMS, causing valid external LMS signatures/keys to fail verification and locally generated signatures to fail on conforming implementations. - recommendation: apply the same vector digest reordering in `wc_Sha256HashBlock()` that `wc_Sha256FinalRaw()` already uses, and add an LMS known-answer test on RISC-V vector builds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete issue promoted from `wolfcrypt/src/port/riscv/riscv-64-sha3.c` beyond the SHA-256/LMS and Poly1305 findings above - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/riscv/riscv-64-sha3.c` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this chunk is a direct RISC-V Keccak permutation implementation. This pass did not establish a stronger trust-boundary bug or material performance failure there than the two backend-specific correctness/secret-retention issues above.

### Medium - linuxkm ECDSA verify allocates attacker-sized kernel buffers with no curve-based cap, enabling local memory-DoS through the kernel crypto API - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_ecdsa_glue.c:148,162,175,189,394-427`, `linuxkm/README.md:17,55`, contrast `linuxkm/lkcapi_rsa_glue.c:695-700,789-794` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: wolfSSL linuxkm is built with LKCAPI ECDSA registration and a local caller can reach the registered `ecdsa-nist-p{256,384,521}` verifier via AF_ALG or another kernel crypto consumer. - evidence: the module registers `.verify = km_ecdsa_verify` for the ECDSA drivers, and `km_ecdsa_verify()` trusts request-controlled lengths (`sig_len = req->src_len; hash_len = req->dst_len;`), then does `malloc(sig_len + hash_len)`, `scatterwalk_map_and_copy(..., sig_len + hash_len, ...)`, and `wc_ecc_verify_hash(...)` with no upper bound tied to `ctx->curve_len`. Nearby RSA glue explicitly rejects oversize request lengths before allocating/copying. - impact: a local requester can submit oversized verify jobs that force large kernel allocations/copies on each request, causing memory pressure, CPU burn, and likely kernel-side DoS on systems exposing the crypto API. - recommendation: fail closed before allocation unless `sig_len` and `hash_len` fit sane curve-derived maxima (for example ASN.1 ECDSA signature upper bound plus allowed digest sizes), and reject requests whose total exceeds a small fixed bound for the selected curve. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - ARM/AArch64 linuxkm PIE FIPS hashing intentionally zeroes relocation targets, so call/jump retargeting within the same segment can evade the integrity hash - location: `linuxkm/linuxkm_memory.c:383-413,423-447,757,782-793`, `linuxkm/module_hooks.c:1731,1760-1762`, enablement `configure.ac:755-767` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: FIPS linuxkm PIE build (`WC_SYM_RELOC_TABLES`/dynamic hash path) on ARM or AArch64, plus an attacker who can tamper module text or a shipped module before integrity verification. - evidence: for `WC_R_ARM_*` and `WC_R_AARCH64_*` relocations, `wc_reloc_normalize_text()` does not reconstruct destination addresses and instead sets `reloc_buf = 0`; it then preserves only `dest_seg` and `reloc_type` via XOR labels before writing the canonicalized bytes. `wc_fips_generate_hash()` HMACs this normalized text, and `updateFipsHash()` installs the resulting `verifyCore`. Unlike the x86_64 path, the actual destination offset is discarded for these relocation classes. - impact: retargeting an ARM/AArch64 relocated branch/data reference to a different symbol in the same destination segment can leave the canonicalized bytes unchanged, creating an integrity-check blind spot in the FIPS hash for those builds. - recommendation: include normalized destination offsets for ARM/AArch64 relocations the same way x86_64 does, or disable/fail the dynamic FIPS hash path on relocation types that cannot be reconstructed without losing target identity. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-11.x`

### Low - stray `@echo` in a `.ONESHELL` recipe makes the incremental linuxkm FIPS user-build path brittle - location: `linuxkm/Makefile:394-400` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the `libwolfssl-user-build` rule uses `.ONESHELL` and its success branch contains `@    	@echo ' already matches (no update needed).'`; the second `@echo` is part of the shell script, not make syntax. When `wolfcrypt/test/testwolfcrypt` succeeds and the `else` branch runs, the shell can execute `@echo` instead of `echo`, turning the no-op/incremental path into a command-not-found failure. - impact: repeat `linuxkm-fips-hash` / `module-with-matching-fips-hash*` runs can fail in the already-up-to-date branch, making the FIPS release workflow flaky and harder to reason about. - recommendation: remove the extra `@` and keep the branch to plain shell syntax; add a regression or CI smoke test that runs the target twice and expects the second invocation to succeed. - reference: `grugbrain: boring code`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no additional concrete issue promoted from `wolfcrypt/src/port/ppc32/ppc32-sha256-asm.S` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/ppc32/ppc32-sha256-asm.S`, adjacent call sites `wolfcrypt/src/sha256.c:721-722,1089,1485-1492` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the chunk is generated PPC32 SHA-256 compression assembly with no new external trust boundary. The main sharp edge spotted here is that `Transform_Sha256_Len` shifts `len` by 6 and falls straight into the block loop, so a zero-block call would misbehave, but repo-wide callers in `sha256.c` only invoke it with one full block (`Transform_Sha256_Len(..., WC_SHA256_BLOCK_SIZE)`) or through `XTRANSFORM_LEN` under `if (len >= WC_SHA256_BLOCK_SIZE)` with block-aligned `blocksLen`. No stronger chunk-local exploit path, data exposure, or material production performance failure was established from this file alone beyond that internal precondition hazard.

### Medium - RV64 asm ChaCha/SHA-512 fast paths impose hidden alignment requirements on public `byte*` APIs, causing misaligned-load crashes or severe fixup slowdowns on strict-alignment targets - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/riscv/riscv-64-chacha.c:765,799,2158,2231,2339`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/riscv/riscv-64-sha512.c:557,592,753,800-827,1036` - category: security/performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_RISCV_ASM` build on RV64 systems where unaligned `ld/sd` or vector loads are trapped/emulated slowly; caller passes unaligned input/output/data buffers (common with zero-copy slices, framed packet offsets, mmap/file views, or packed structs). - evidence: `wc_Chacha_Process()` only validates NULLs, then the asm paths load/store caller buffers directly with alignment-sensitive ops (`VL4RE32_V(..., %[m])`, `VS4R_V(..., %[c])`, scalar `ld 0(%[m])`, `sd 0(%[c])`). `wc_Sha512Update()` likewise forwards caller `data` straight into `Sha512Transform()` when full blocks are available, and the bitmanip/vector-crypto transforms read blocks with raw `ld` / `VL8RE64_V` from `%[data]` instead of staging through an aligned buffer. - impact: legal but unaligned byte buffers can SIGBUS/illegal-instruction-fault the process or hit kernel misaligned-access emulation, turning normal hash/encrypt requests into request-triggered DoS or large throughput cliffs on RV64 deployments. - recommendation: gate these asm paths on proven alignment support, or add an unaligned fallback that copies each block into an aligned local buffer / uses byte-safe loads before entering the fast path; document any remaining alignment precondition explicitly if the API cannot tolerate arbitrary `byte*` inputs. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### High - QNX CAAM resource manager exposes secure-partition read/write/free to any local user and never enforces partition ownership - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/caam_qnx.c:1502-1509,1741-1742,1363-1387,1422-1435,1461-1477,1566-1575`; consumer wrappers `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_init.c:772,789,812,835` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: QNX CAAM daemon is running; attacker has local access to `/dev/wolfCrypt` (created as mode `0666`). - evidence: the device is attached as `/dev/wolfCrypt` with `iofunc_attr_init(&ioattr, S_IFCHR | 0666, ...)`, and `iofunc_devctl_verify()` checks only that the caller opened the device for read/write. `doGET_PART()` records an owner in `sm_ownerId[partNumber]`, but `doREAD_PART()` and `doWRITE_PART()` only call `sanityCheckPartitionAddress()` and then map/copy the requested CAAM secure-memory address; `WC_CAAM_FREE_PART` calls `caamFreePart(args[0])` and clears `sm_ownerId[args[0]]` without comparing `ocb`/owner. - impact: any local user can read, overwrite, or free another process's CAAM secure partition, exposing or corrupting black-key material and causing cross-process crypto DoS/integrity loss. - recommendation: make the device root- or group-restricted by default, and replace raw partition-number/address devctls with per-open capability/handle checks that enforce `sm_ownerId` on every read/write/free path. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-8.x`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-11.x`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`

### High - QNX secure-partition owner table is undersized and written with unchecked partition indexes, enabling local memory corruption in the CAAM daemon - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/caam_qnx.c:66-68,953,1386,1573`; partition producers `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/caam_driver.c:276,296,1268-1320` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: local caller can reach QNX `WC_CAAM_GET_PART` / `WC_CAAM_ECDSA_KEYPAIR` devctls (same world-writable `/dev/wolfCrypt` default). - evidence: QNX allocates `CAAM_ADDRESS sm_ownerId[MAX_PART]` with `#define MAX_PART 7`, but the CAAM driver scans/uses partitions `0..14` (`for (i = 0; i < 15U; i++)` in `caamFindUnusedPartition()` / `caamFreeAllPart()`). The QNX paths then store owners with no bounds check: `sm_ownerId[partNumber] = (CAAM_ADDRESS)ocb;`, `sm_ownerId[args[2]] = (CAAM_ADDRESS)ocb;`, and `sm_ownerId[args[0]] = 0;`. A request for partition `7` or higher writes past the 7-entry array; keygen can also hit the same path when lower partitions are occupied and `args[2]` is set from the returned partition number. - impact: an unprivileged local caller can corrupt adjacent daemon globals/heap state, yielding crashable root-service memory corruption and possible code-execution primitives. - recommendation: size the owner table to the real hardware partition range (or query it dynamically), reject out-of-range partition IDs before any array access, and fail closed before storing/freeing owner metadata. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-1.x`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`

### High - SECO AES-GCM backend drops HSM authentication/decrypt failures and always reports success - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_seco.c:994-1078,1395-1396`; caller entrypoints `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_aes.c:235-249` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build uses `WOLFSSL_SECO_CAAM` and routes AES-GCM through the SECO backend. - evidence: `wc_CAAM_AesGcmDecrypt()` / encrypt dispatch `CAAM_AESGCM` requests into `SynchronousSendRequest()`, which calls `wc_SECO_AESGCM()`. That function sets `auth_args.flags` for encrypt/decrypt, calls `err = hsm_auth_enc(cipher_hdl, &auth_args);`, but then ends with unconditional `return HSM_NO_ERROR;`. Any `hsm_auth_enc()` failure, including bad-tag/authentication failure on decrypt, is discarded before `wc_TranslateHSMError()` sees it. - impact: tampered AES-GCM ciphertext/tag pairs can be reported as successful decrypt operations, breaking AEAD authenticity/integrity guarantees and letting callers consume unauthenticated output. - recommendation: return the real `err` from `wc_SECO_AESGCM()`, zero/ignore output on failure, and add regression tests that a bad GCM tag is rejected on SECO builds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-11.x`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`

### Reviewed - no additional issue from `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/caam_integrity.c` was promoted above the QNX authorization/memory-corruption and SECO AES-GCM findings in this pass - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/caam_integrity.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this file is an older INTEGRITY-only CAAM transport with similar descriptor/ring logic and some reliability rough edges, but this chunk did not show a stronger independent exploit path or production-impacting failure mode than the concrete QNX devctl boundary breaks and SECO auth-failure masking above.

### Medium - HMAC finup/digest error path leaks a heap-allocated `struct Hmac`, enabling local kernel-heap DoS through failing `hmac(sha*)` requests - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_sha_glue.c:764-876,895-910`; supporting state init `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:1326-1334`; exposed algo name example `linuxkm/module_hooks.c:1684` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: HMAC LKCAPI registration enabled; local AF_ALG or in-kernel shash caller can issue a failing HMAC request (for example omit `setkey`, leaving the copied context unkeyed). - evidence: `km_hmac_init()` does `t_ctx->wc_hmac = malloc(sizeof *t_ctx->wc_hmac)` and copies the per-tfm template. `km_hmac_finup()` calls `wc_HmacUpdate(ctx->wc_hmac, data, len)` and on nonzero return immediately `return -EINVAL;` without `km_hmac_free_tstate(ctx)`, unlike `km_hmac_update()`/`km_hmac_final()`. The template is created by `km_hmac_init_tfm()` via `wc_HmacInit()`, and `wc_HmacInit()` sets `hmac->macType = WC_HASH_TYPE_NONE` until `setkey` runs. The file itself notes `sizeof(struct Hmac) is 832`. - impact: repeated failing HMAC digest/finup operations can strand ~832-byte kernel allocations per request, letting an unprivileged local caller consume kernel heap and eventually degrade or crash systems exposing the hash API. - recommendation: on the `wc_HmacUpdate()` failure path in `km_hmac_finup()`, call `km_hmac_free_tstate(ctx)` before returning; audit the analogous SHA3 finup/init-failure paths for the same pattern and add an AF_ALG regression that issues an unkeyed HMAC request and checks for no leak. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - FIPS linuxkm HMAC registration intentionally bypasses the 112-bit minimum HMAC key floor on older-kernel test paths, so undersized keys are accepted instead of failing closed - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_sha_glue.c:773-780`; key-floor enforcement `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:524-525,874-878`; minimum definition `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/hmac.h:69` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_FIPS` build on the affected branch (`!FIPS_VERSION3_LT(6,0,0)`, tests enabled, kernel < 6.0) and a caller sets a short key through the registered `hmac(sha*)` algorithms. - evidence: `linuxkm_hmac_setkey_common()` explicitly comments `kernel 5.10.x crypto manager expects FIPS-undersized keys to succeed` and then calls `wc_HmacSetKey_ex(..., 1 /* allowFlag */)`. In wolfCrypt, `wc_HmacSetKey()` defaults `allowFlag = 0` for FIPS, and `wc_HmacSetKey_ex()` only rejects `length < HMAC_FIPS_MIN_KEY` when `!allowFlag`; `HMAC_FIPS_MIN_KEY` is `14` bytes (`112 bit`). - impact: a FIPS-branded kernel crypto provider can silently accept 1-13 byte HMAC keys that callers may expect to be rejected, weakening MAC strength/policy enforcement and creating validator/policy drift across deployments. - recommendation: keep the FIPS minimum-key check in production and isolate any crypto-manager compatibility exception to self-test-only codepaths/builds; if interoperability must remain, fail registration loudly instead of silently weakening runtime policy. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - patched `mix_pool_bytes` callback does O(input_bytes × rng_instances) byte-by-byte DRBG mutation in the kernel random fast path, creating easy local CPU amplification on large writes to `/dev/urandom`/entropy sources - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_sha_glue.c:1052-1055,1462-1494,1526-1534`; callback hook example `linuxkm/patches/6.12/WOLFSSL_LINUXKM_HAVE_GET_RANDOM_CALLBACKS-6v12.patch:364-369` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `LINUXKM_DRBG_GET_RANDOM_BYTES` with the callback patchset; local code can feed large buffers into kernel `mix_pool_bytes()` inputs (for example writes to `/dev/urandom`). - evidence: startup sizes the RNG bank to `nr_cpu_ids + 4`. `random_bytes_handlers.mix_pool_bytes = wc_mix_pool_bytes`, and the shipped callback patches insert `(void)call_mix_pool_bytes_cb(buf, len);` into kernel `mix_pool_bytes()`. `wc_mix_pool_bytes()` then iterates every bank instance and every input byte, directly updating `DRBG_internal->V` one byte at a time before checkin/resched. - impact: attacker-controlled large entropy writes are amplified by CPU count inside the kernel RNG path, causing avoidable system-wide CPU burn and latency spikes in a sensitive subsystem. - recommendation: do not walk every byte of every bank instance in the callback; hash/compress the incoming buffer once, reseed only a bounded subset of instances, and cap per-callback work so random-pool writes cannot scale with `len * (nr_cpu_ids+4)`. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no stronger new trust-boundary break promoted from the remaining SHA/DRBG registration macros and linuxkm portability scaffolding in this chunk beyond the HMAC leak, FIPS short-key bypass, and random-pool CPU amplification above - location: `linuxkm/lkcapi_sha_glue.c`, `linuxkm/linuxkm_wc_port.h` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: most remaining content here is compile-time registration gating, ABI shims, and kernel portability macros. Spot checks of SHA/SHA3 registration, default-RNG replacement, and custom memory/mutex wrappers did not yield a more concrete exploit path than the three issues appended above from this segment alone.

### Medium - IMX6 CAAM CBC/ECB helpers silently floor non-block-aligned lengths and return success, unlike the generic AES backend that rejects them with `BAD_LENGTH_E` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/caam_aes.c:118-148,171-201,220-248,265-293`; contrast `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:6403-6424` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses the IMX6 CAAM AES CBC/ECB APIs with attacker-influenced or unchecked lengths that are not multiples of 16. - evidence: each helper computes `blocks = sz / WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE`, sends only `blocks * WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE` to hardware, and then returns `0` even when `sz % WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE != 0`; there is no remainder check in this port. Repo search shows the generic `wc_AesCbcEncrypt()` path in `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c` returns `BAD_LENGTH_E` instead. - impact: callers can believe all `sz` bytes were processed when the CAAM path actually leaves a trailing partial block untouched, causing plaintext/stale-tail disclosure, truncated decrypts, or integrity failures in hardware-backed builds only. - recommendation: fail closed with `BAD_LENGTH_E` on any CBC/ECB encrypt/decrypt length not divisible by `WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE`, matching the generic backend contract. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - IMX6 CAAM AES port calls `wc_caamAddAndWait()` with the wrong arity at eight sites, even though the visible declaration and definition require `(buf, sz, arg, type)` - location: bad call sites `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/caam_aes.c:201,248,293,365,428,469,557,642`; correct 4-arg example in same file `:148`; declaration `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/caam/wolfcaam.h:68`; definition `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_init.c:477` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the shared helper is declared/defined with four parameters including an explicit buffer-count `int sz`, but most `caam_aes.c` call sites pass only three arguments (`buf, arg, CAAM_*`). One nearby call in the same file (`:148`) does pass four arguments, showing the inconsistency is local rather than API-wide. - impact: the IMX6 CAAM AES backend is build-fragile/broken on its hardware-specific path, and any future attempt to paper over this mismatch risks dispatching the wrong buffer-count into the CAAM request marshalling layer. - recommendation: fix every call to pass the explicit buffer count expected by the helper and add CAAM IMX6 build coverage so this port cannot regress silently. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing`

### Medium - IMXRT1170 CAAM initializes four job rings but hard-codes every hash/AES/ECC operation onto `kCAAM_JobRing0`, leaving the extra rings idle - location: ring setup `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_fsl_nxp.c:56-83`; hard-coded ring selection `:127,264,331,415,545,652,746,804` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: init populates `jobRingInterface[0..3] = &jr0..&jr3`, but every helper later does `hndl.jobRing = kCAAM_JobRing0;`. The file also initializes/frees `caamMutex` (`:54,63,89`) without using it around requests, so the apparent multi-ring concurrency scaffolding is dead. - impact: multithreaded IMXRT1170 deployments bottleneck all CAAM work on a single hardware ring, increasing latency and wasting configured accelerator capacity under load. - recommendation: shard work across job rings (for example per-thread or round-robin) or remove the dead multi-ring scaffolding if the backend intentionally supports only one ring. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - CAAM ECC private-key consistency callback ignores the supplied public key and returns success for any black P-256 key, turning a key/cert match check into an unconditional pass - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_ecdsa.c:760-767`; callback packaging `wolfcrypt/src/cryptocb.c:740,755-758`; caller `src/ssl_api_pk.c:111` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `wc_CryptoCb_EccCheckPrivKey()` passes `key`, `pubKey`, and `pubKeySz` through `WC_PK_TYPE_EC_CHECK_PRIV_KEY`, but the CAAM implementation immediately `(void)`s `pubKey`/`pubKeySz` and returns `0` whenever `key->dp->id == ECC_SECP256R1 && key->blackKey > 0`. - impact: hardware-backed P-256 deployments can accept mismatched certificate/public-key and private-key pairs during configuration, deferring the failure to later handshake/signing paths instead of detecting the bad pairing when loaded. - recommendation: derive/export the public point from the CAAM-backed private key (or sign/verify a test digest) and compare it to the provided public key, or otherwise return `CRYPTOCB_UNAVAILABLE` so callers do not mistake this stub for a real consistency check. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Medium - Renesas TSIP/FSPSM ECDSA verification paths parse attacker-controlled DER signatures with fixed offsets and no `sigSz` bounds checks, enabling remote out-of-bounds reads during TLS certificate/server-key verification - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_util.c:2077-2132,3490-3554`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_util.c:314-372,994-1059`, dispatch from `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_common.c:603-625,1161-1180,1246-1265` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Renesas TSIP or FSPSM TLS client build; attacker controls a server `ServerKeyExchange`/cert signature or a certificate-chain signature reaching these callbacks. - evidence: `wc_tsip_EccVerify()`, `wc_fspsm_EccVerifyTLS()`, `wc_tsip_tls_CertVerify()`, and `wc_fspsm_tls_CertVerify()` all branch on `sig[offset]` / `signature[offset]` at hard-coded offsets `0x3`, `0x25`, `0x26` and copy 32-byte `r`/`s` chunks, but do not validate that `sigSz` covers those reads; the FSPSM paths explicitly mark `sigSz` unused. `renesas_common.c` wires these functions into live TLS signature-verification callbacks and certificate verification. - impact: a truncated/malformed ECDSA signature from the network can make the client read past the supplied signature buffer, crashing verification or mixing adjacent memory into the hardware verify request instead of failing closed. - recommendation: replace fixed-offset DER parsing with a length-checked ASN.1 decoder (or at minimum reject unless `sigSz` covers every inspected/copied byte before reading), and add regression tests for short/truncated ECDSA signatures on the Renesas verify paths. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Renesas session-key setup leaks the global hardware mutex on allocation failure, turning one low-memory handshake into a persistent accelerator deadlock/DoS - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_util.c:3147-3289` (`return MEMORY_E` at `:3224,:3237` before `tsip_hw_unlock()`), `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_util.c:726-870` (`return MEMORY_E` at `:762,:794,:801,:811,:819` before `wc_fspsm_hw_unlock()`) - category: security/performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Renesas TSIP/FSPSM TLS build; handshake reaches session-key generation while allocator pressure makes one of the `XMALLOC(sizeof(Aes)...)` / wrapped-key allocations fail. - evidence: both `wc_tsip_generateSessionKey()` and `wc_fspsm_generateSessionKey()` acquire the process-global hardware lock first, then perform heap allocations for `enc->aes`, `dec->aes`, and FSPSM wrapped-key storage. Several failure branches `return MEMORY_E;` immediately from inside the locked region; the corresponding `*_hw_unlock()` happens only later on the normal path. - impact: once a single handshake hits one of these `MEMORY_E` branches, the global Renesas accelerator mutex stays locked and subsequent crypto/TLS operations using that backend can hang or fail until process restart. On constrained devices this is a realistic request-driven DoS under load. - recommendation: centralize cleanup with a single `goto out`/defer block that always releases the hardware mutex, and free any partially allocated AES state before returning. Add a fault-injection test that forces allocation failure after lock acquisition and verifies later handshakes still progress. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wc_fspsm_GenerateRandBlock()` cannot compile as written because it uses `sizeof(buffer)` on a non-existent identifier, breaking configured Renesas builds that wire this function in as the custom RNG - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_util.c:191-208` (bad line `:198`), enabled in `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/common/user_settings.h:117` and `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RZN2L/common/user_settings.h:119` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the function declares `uint32_t fspbuf[RANDGEN_WORDS];` but computes `word32 len = sizeof(buffer);`; repo search in this file finds no `buffer` definition, only that single use. The shipped Renesas IDE settings map `CUSTOM_RAND_GENERATE_BLOCK` to `wc_fspsm_GenerateRandBlock`, so this typo sits on an advertised build path. - impact: affected Renesas FSPSM configurations fail to build or require local patching before the custom hardware RNG path can be used, creating avoidable release friction and config drift. - recommendation: change the size calculation to `sizeof(fspbuf)` and add a compile/build smoke test for the Renesas FSPSM user-settings targets that enable `CUSTOM_RAND_GENERATE_BLOCK`. - reference: `grugbrain: boring code`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no stronger chunk-specific issue was promoted from the remaining Renesas TLS 1.3/AES utility code beyond the fixed-offset signature parsing, lock-leak deadlock, and FSPSM RNG build break above - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_util.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_util.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_aes.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the rest of this pass was dominated by backend glue, per-device key import, and hardware API shims. Spot checks found additional rough edges (for example ignored `outSz` in internal TLS13 record building and some unlock-on-failed-lock patterns), but this chunk did not establish a clearer exploit or production-impact path stronger than the three concrete issues above.

### Medium - Renesas crypto-callback device registry has an off-by-one table bound, so the 6th callback context writes past `gCbCtx[]` and cleanup can read/free past the array - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_common.c:74-75,396-397,456,478,495,498`; reachable via exported setup helpers `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_util.c:1261-1296` and `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_util.c:2244-2285` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: Renesas FSPSM/TSIP build with `WOLF_CRYPTO_CB`; application registers more than five callback contexts/devices (for example per SSL object/connection via `wc_fspsm_set_callback_ctx()` / `tsip_set_callback_ctx()` or direct `wc_CryptoCb_CryptInitRenesasCmn()`). - evidence: `MAX_FSPSM_CBINDEX` is `5`, but `gCbCtx` is declared as `gCbCtx[MAX_FSPSM_CBINDEX]` (5 slots, valid indices `0..4`). `Renesas_cmn_GetCbCtxBydevId()` and cleanup both accept `devId == INITIAL_DEVID + MAX_FSPSM_CBINDEX`, and init stores `gCbCtx[cbInfo->devId - INITIAL_DEVID] = cbInfo` after monotonically assigning `cbInfo->devId = gdevId++` with no full-table check. The sixth successful registration therefore uses index `5`, one past the array; cleanup accepts the same out-of-range index and dereferences it. - impact: out-of-bounds global-pointer write/read in the crypto callback registry can corrupt adjacent state or crash the process; if the application binds callback-context setup to connection lifecycle, an attacker can turn repeated connection attempts into process memory corruption/DoS. - recommendation: fail closed once the table is full, fix the bounds to match the actual slot count (or size the array to `MAX_FSPSM_CBINDEX + 1`), clear slots on cleanup, and avoid a global fixed-size registry for per-session contexts.

### Medium - Renesas SCE/RSIP SHA wrapper defeats streaming by buffering the full message in heap memory and replaying it at finalization - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_sha.c:298-336,419-438,492-548` - category: performance - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - status: candidate - severity: medium - evidence: `FSPSM_HashUpdate()` grows `hash->msg` to `hash->used + sz` on every update (`XMALLOC`/`XREALLOC`, with an explicit alloc-copy-free path on FreeRTOS) and appends each chunk into that buffer instead of streaming into the hardware state. `FSPSM_HashFinal()` / `FSPSM_HashGet()` then initialize a fresh hardware handle and replay the entire buffered message with one `Update(..., hash->msg, hash->used)` call before `Final()`, then free/reset the buffer. - impact: large or fragmented hash workloads lose O(1) streaming behavior, require the entire message to be resident in RAM, and incur allocator churn / repeated copying; under load this can produce avoidable latency spikes, heap pressure, and OOMs on memory-constrained Renesas targets. - recommendation: use incremental hardware updates for the supported SCE/RSIP paths instead of whole-message replay, or keep only the minimal tail/state needed for finalization and cloning rather than buffering every byte.

### Reviewed - no stronger chunk-local issue promoted from `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_aes.c` beyond the callback-registry memory corruption risk and the SHA buffering/performance cliff above - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_aes.c` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass found expensive full-buffer scratch allocation/copy patterns in the AES-GCM path, but not a clearer trust-boundary break or production failure mode from this file alone that outranked the concrete registry OOB and SHA replay issues for the current chunk.

### Medium - CAAM CryptoCb CBC/ECB helpers silently truncate non-block-multiple input instead of returning `BAD_LENGTH_E` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_aes.c:259-305,389-435`; dispatch `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_init.c:308-316,333-341`; public contract/tests `tests/api/test_aes.c:683-694`; generic AES error paths `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:5482-5488,14038-14050` - category: security/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application routes AES CBC/ECB through the CAAM CryptoCb/device path and passes a length not divisible by `WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE`. - evidence: `wc_CAAM_AesCbcCtrCommon()` and `wc_CAAM_AesEcbCommon()` do `blocks = sz / WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE` and submit only `blocks * WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE`, with no `sz % block` check; `sz < block` returns success without processing anything. `wolfcaam_init.c` dispatches CAAM cipher callbacks straight to these helpers. By contrast the repo’s CBC tests expect `BAD_LENGTH_E` on odd lengths, and the generic AES implementations have explicit `BAD_LENGTH_E` returns for CBC/ECB. - impact: malformed lengths are reported as success while trailing bytes are dropped or the whole call becomes a no-op, causing silent data corruption/integrity failures and behavior drift from the documented API contract. - recommendation: fail closed with `BAD_LENGTH_E` whenever `sz % WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE != 0` in CAAM CBC/ECB paths so CAAM matches the software implementation and tests. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - SECO CAAM `WOLFSSL_HASH_KEEP` turns incremental hash/CMAC into full-message heap buffering, enabling memory-DoS on large streamed inputs - location: feature gate `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/settings.h:2711-2714`; hash path `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_hash.c:222-243,262-281,300-319,339-358,378-397`; CMAC path `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_cmac.c:86-92,148-153`; cached-message fields `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sha.h:183-185`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sha256.h:213-215`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sha512.h:184-186`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/cmac.h:74,156` - category: performance/security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_SECO_CAAM` build and an application hashes or CMACs attacker-sized streams incrementally. - evidence: `WOLFSSL_SECO_CAAM` auto-defines `WOLFSSL_HASH_KEEP`; under that flag the CAAM update paths call `wc_Sha*_Grow(..., in, inSz, ...)` / `wc_CMAC_Grow(cmac, in, inSz)` instead of processing blocks, caching data in `msg/used/len` until final. Finalization then passes the entire accumulated buffer to hardware in one shot (`_ShaFinal(..., sha->msg, sha->used, ...)` or the CAAM CMAC final path over `cmac->msg`). - impact: APIs that look streaming become O(input size) in heap instead of O(1), so large uploads/files can exhaust memory or create severe allocator churn on embedded CAAM targets. - recommendation: enforce a bounded cached length and fail or fall back to software/true streaming once exceeded; document the non-streaming behavior explicitly for SECO CAAM builds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - non-`WOLFSSL_HASH_KEEP` CAAM hash backend appears broken at compile time: `_ShaUpdate()` dereferences undeclared `sha` instead of its `ctx`/`buffer` parameters - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_hash.c:69-166`; build surface `wolfcrypt/src/include.am:91,228`; feature gate `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/settings.h:2755-2756` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `_ShaUpdate()` is declared as `static int _ShaUpdate(byte* buffer, word32* buffLen, const byte* ctx, ...)`, but its body submits `sha->ctx` and `sha->buffer` (`wolfcaam_hash.c:95,104,130`) even though there is no local `sha` variable or macro alias in this file. This bug is in the exact `!WOLFSSL_HASH_KEEP` path used by ordinary `WOLFSSL_CAAM_HASH` builds rather than the SECO cache-all path. - impact: non-SECO CAAM hash support is likely unbuildable or badly bit-rotted, making the advertised hardware-hash backend operationally unusable and hard to review. - recommendation: replace the stray `sha->...` references with the passed `ctx`/`buffer` arguments (or pass a typed hash object instead), then add a non-`WOLFSSL_HASH_KEEP` CAAM hash build to CI. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Reviewed - no stronger chunk-specific issue promoted from `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_x25519.c`, `wolfcaam_rsa.c`, `wolfcaam_hmac.c`, `caam_error.c`, or `README.md` beyond the CAAM AES/hash/CMAC issues above - location: listed files plus adjacent callback glue `wolfcrypt/src/port/caam/wolfcaam_init.c:127-153,173-233` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: in this chunk the X25519/RSA/HMAC files are mostly thin devcrypto-or-unavailable shims with limited local logic, `caam_error.c` is error-string/logging glue, and `README.md` is a placeholder doc stub; this pass did not establish a stronger exploit path or production incident mode there than the concrete CAAM AES/hash/CMAC issues above.

### Medium - TSIP SHA `GetHash`/`Final` fail open on hardware errors and can strand the global TSIP lock, so callers can see success with stale digest bytes and later TSIP ops can deadlock - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_sha.c:364-409,411-451`; lock helper `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_util.c:2656-2683`; transcript callers `src/internal.c:10611`, `src/tls13.c:431,1385,8487` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: TSIP SHA init/update/final or mutex acquisition fails because of hardware fault, lock-state corruption, or injected fault; affected builds route SHA/SHA-256 through the TSIP backend. - evidence: `TSIPHashFinal()` and `TSIPHashGet()` call `tsip_hw_lock()` without checking its return. If `Init()` fails, or `Update()` returns non-success, both functions fall through to `tsip_hw_unlock(); return 0` / reset-success without propagating the error. If `Final()` fails or `sz != outSz`, both functions `return ret;` before `tsip_hw_unlock()`. - impact: digest consumers can proceed on uninitialized/stale output as though hashing succeeded, and one failing finalization can leave the shared TSIP engine lock held, hanging later TSIP-backed TLS/crypto work. - recommendation: check `tsip_hw_lock()` result, propagate `Init`/`Update`/`Final` failures, and use a single cleanup path that always unlocks and resets/frees state before return. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - TSIP TLS 1.3 transcript capture hard-caps peer handshakes at 8 KiB / 10 messages, so a server can remotely abort TSIP-backed client handshakes with a large but valid certificate flight - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_sha.c:145-210`; limits in `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/Renesas/renesas-tsip-crypt.h:126-133`; entry from handshake hashing `src/internal.c:10590` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_RENESAS_TSIP_TLS` TLS 1.3 client build; peer controls handshake message sizes/count (for example certificate chain length). - evidence: `HashRaw()` forwards handshake bytes to `tsip_StoreMessage()`, which buffers raw messages into fixed `MsgBag` storage of `MSGBAG_SIZE (1024 * 8)` and `MAX_MSGBAG_MESSAGES 10`. When `bag->msgIdx + 1 > MAX_MSGBAG_MESSAGES` or `bag->buffIdx + sz > MSGBAG_SIZE`, it returns `MEMORY_E` instead of falling back. - impact: a server that presents a larger chain or handshake flight than the tiny fixed bag allows can reliably force handshake failure against TSIP-enabled clients, creating a straightforward remote availability hit. - recommendation: stop buffering the whole transcript into a fixed bag; stream into a transcript hash (hardware or software), or fail over cleanly to software hashing when the TSIP transcript cache limit is exceeded. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V4 API and Web Service`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wc_tsip_MakeRsaKey()` always returns success after the TSIP keygen block, so lock/keygen failures can silently leave callers using stale or nonexistent RSA key slots - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_rsa.c:55-196`; caller `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_common.c:193` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: TSIP crypt-only RSA key generation path is enabled and the TSIP lock or `R_TSIP_GenerateRsa{1024,2048}RandomKeyIndex()` fails. - evidence: after `ret = tsip_hw_lock()` and the hardware keygen call, `wc_tsip_MakeRsaKey()` exits the guarded block and unconditionally `return 0;`. The function only updates `info->rsa*keyIdx` / key-set flags inside `if (ret == TSIP_SUCCESS)`, so failures leave prior pointers/flags intact while the callback still reports success. - impact: applications can request a fresh TSIP RSA key and silently keep using an older key or null/stale slots, causing wrong-key reuse, provisioning drift, or later crypt failures that are detached from the actual keygen fault. - recommendation: return the real lock/hardware error, and clear key pointers/key-set flags on failure so callers cannot mistake stale state for a newly generated key. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RX64 `wc_Sha*GetHash()` rejects the empty-hash state even though the same backend’s `Final()` special-cases empty input and wolfSSL transcript code calls `GetHash()` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_rx64_hw_sha.c:100-108,247-295,307-324`; callers `src/internal.c:10611`, `src/tls13.c:431,1385,8487` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_RENESAS_RX64_HASH` build reaches `wc_ShaGetHash`/`wc_Sha256GetHash` on a fresh or otherwise empty hash context. - evidence: `RX64_HashGet()` always calls `RX64_ShaCalc(hash->msg, hash->len, ...)`; `RX64_ShaCalc()` returns `BAD_FUNC_ARG` when `data == NULL || len == 0`. In contrast, `RX64_HashFinal()` explicitly returns the default SHA-1/SHA-224/SHA-256 digest bytes for empty input because wolfSSL expects empty-hash success for TLS flows. - impact: valid callers that snapshot an empty transcript/hash state fail only on the RX64 hardware backend, creating backend-specific TLS/handshake breakage and hard-to-triage interoperability failures. - recommendation: mirror `RX64_HashFinal()`’s empty-input handling in `RX64_HashGet()` so `GetHash()` and `Final()` honor the same API contract. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - TSIP and RX64 SHA ports turn streaming hash APIs into full-message buffering with exact-size realloc on every update, causing quadratic copy cost and avoidable RAM spikes on fragmented inputs - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_sha.c:318-360`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_rx64_hw_sha.c:208-239` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: both `TSIPHashUpdate()` and `RX64_HashUpdate()` grow `hash->msg` to `hash->used + sz` exactly on each call (`XMALLOC`/`XREALLOC`, or allocate-copy-free on FreeRTOS), copy the old buffer, append the new bytes, and defer all actual hashing until `*HashFinal()` / `*HashGet()`. - impact: workloads that feed many small chunks pay O(total_bytes^2) memcpy/realloc overhead and must retain the whole message in RAM, which is a material throughput and memory cliff on embedded targets and defeats the normal streaming-hash contract. - recommendation: use software incremental hashing when the hardware cannot snapshot state, or at minimum switch to geometric growth / bounded scratch buffers and document that this backend is not suitable for large streaming inputs. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_rsa.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/README.md`, and `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_rx64_hw_util.c` did not surface a stronger independent issue in this pass beyond the TSIP/RX64 findings above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this segment’s strongest repo-specific issues were the TSIP hash error-handling/keygen failures, the fixed-size TSIP TLS transcript bag, the RX64 empty-`GetHash()` contract break, and the buffering/realloc performance cliff. The remaining chunk content was mostly vendor-adapter glue, board bring-up notes, and minor build/API rough edges without a clearer exploit path or material production incident mode from this pass alone.

### High - memcached CI bind-mounts the runner’s entire `/` into a container and then `chroot`s into it, so compromised test code escapes the container boundary and gains host-filesystem write access - location: `.github/workflows/memcached.yml:103-114`, `.github/workflows/memcached.sh:13-14` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: workflow runs external memcached source plus a mutable wolfSSL patch; any malicious `make test_tls` step or test helper executes inside a container started with `-v /:/host` and then `chroot /host`. - evidence: the workflow does `docker run -v /:/host ... alpine:latest /memcached.sh`; `memcached.sh` immediately runs `chroot $HOST_ROOT make -C $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/memcached -j$(nproc) ... test_tls`. That collapses the container isolation the step appears to rely on and exposes the host runner filesystem read-write to the build/test process. - impact: a compromised upstream tarball/patch/test can tamper with the checked-out workspace, build artifacts, caches, and other runner-local state instead of being confined to the container, yielding CI runner compromise and false-green results. - recommendation: remove the `/` bind mount and `chroot`; run the tests directly on the runner or mount only the minimal workspace path needed into the container, ideally read-only except for an explicit scratch dir. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### High - socat interop CI downloads source over plain HTTP and then executes its build system, enabling network-path or mirror compromise to become CI code execution - location: `.github/workflows/socat.yml:68-80` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can tamper with traffic to `www.dest-unreach.org` or the origin serves a modified tarball. - evidence: the job fetches `http://www.dest-unreach.org/socat/download/socat-${{ matrix.socat_version }}.tar.gz`, extracts it, then runs `autoreconf`, `./configure`, `make`, and the upstream test suite on the downloaded tree. There is no TLS, checksum, or signature verification before executing archive-controlled scripts. - impact: a MITM or compromised mirror can run arbitrary code on every PR/push build for this workflow, poison results, and tamper with artifacts or the workspace. - recommendation: switch to HTTPS or a signed/tagged source with pinned SHA256, and fail closed if the digest/signature does not match before extraction. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - most interop workflows trust unpinned `wolfssl/osp` default-branch content for patch application, so one auxiliary-repo commit can arbitrarily alter this repo’s CI behavior - location: representative `.github/workflows/python.yml:117-133`, `.github/workflows/grpc.yml:81-99`, `.github/workflows/openssh.yml:84-100`, `.github/workflows/memcached.yml:61-85`, `.github/workflows/bind.yml:70-88`, plus similar `cyrus-sasl.yml`, `hostap-vm.yml`, `jwt-cpp.yml`, `krb5.yml`, `libspdm.yml`, `libvncserver.yml`, `mosquitto.yml`, `msmtp.yml`, `ntp.yml`, `openldap.yml`, `pam-ipmi.yml`, `rng-tools.yml`, `socat.yml`, `softhsm.yml`, `sssd.yml` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: compromise, force-push, or unintended change on the `wolfssl/osp` default branch. - evidence: these jobs use `actions/checkout@v4` with `repository: wolfssl/osp` but no pinned `ref`, then immediately `patch -p1 < $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/osp/...` (or equivalent) against the third-party tree. Omitting `ref` means the workflow follows mutable default-branch HEAD on every run. - impact: a single OSP branch change can inject arbitrary code into builds/tests across most compatibility jobs, or silently change patch content so regressions are hidden or fabricated without any change in this repo. - recommendation: pin each OSP checkout to an immutable commit SHA/tag and update it through reviewed PRs; for critical jobs, vendor the exact patch files into this repo to keep review local. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Arduino CI executes unpinned remote shell from mutable `master` branches, creating a second cross-repo code-execution path outside this repo’s review history - location: `.github/workflows/arduino.yml:146-169,338-371,398,427` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: compromise or force-push of `arduino/arduino-cli` `master` or `wolfssl-examples` `master`, or an accidental upstream breaking change. - evidence: the workflow installs Arduino CLI with `curl ... https://raw.githubusercontent.com/arduino/arduino-cli/master/install.sh | sh -x`; later it fetches `board_list.txt` and `compile-all-examples.sh` from `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/$REPO_OWNER/wolfssl-examples/master/...` and then `bash ./compile-all-examples.sh`. None of these downloads are pinned to an immutable commit or hash. - impact: upstream/default-branch changes can execute arbitrary shell in wolfSSL CI and make Arduino gating non-reproducible; a bad change in another repo can silently alter or sabotage this repo’s test results. - recommendation: replace raw `master` downloads with `actions/checkout` or release assets pinned to a reviewed commit/tag, and verify hashes before executing any fetched script. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: small sharp tools`

### Reviewed - no stronger chunk-local issue promoted from the remaining workflow files beyond the CI trust-boundary problems above - location: `.github/workflows/hostap-vm.yml`, `openssl-ech.yml`, `renode-stm32h753.yml`, `nginx.yml`, `python.yml`, `mono.yml`, `zephyr.yml`, `os-check.yml`, `rng-tools.yml`, `grpc.yml`, `async-examples.yml`, `mosquitto.yml`, `msmtp.yml`, `cyrus-sasl.yml`, `tls-anvil.yml`, `jwt-cpp.yml`, `sssd.yml`, `openssh.yml`, `krb5.yml`, `openldap.yml`, `xcode.yml`, `haproxy.yml`, `watcomc.yml`, `bind.yml`, `libspdm.yml`, `libssh2.yml`, `softhsm.yml`, `openvpn.yml`, `libvncserver.yml`, `ntp.yml`, `pam-ipmi.yml`, `nss.yml`, `mbedtls.yml` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass was dominated by third-party CI orchestration. Spot checks found other mutable-branch and cache-freshness rough edges, but none exposed a clearer exploit path or operational impact in this chunk than the full-host memcached mount, plain-HTTP socat download, broad unpinned OSP patch trust, and Arduino raw-script execution paths above.

### Medium - OpenSSL-compat `SSL_CTX_set_current_time_cb()` silently discards the caller’s time source, so cert validity checks still run against the default clock - location: `wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h:1309`; related declaration only `wolfssl/ssl.h:3401` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application builds through the OpenSSL compatibility layer and relies on `SSL_CTX_set_current_time_cb()` to supply a trusted time source (common on embedded/skewable clocks). An attacker can skew the local clock or the device lacks a reliable RTC. - evidence: the compat macro is `#define SSL_CTX_set_current_time_cb(ssl, cb) ({ (void)(ssl); (void)(cb); })`, which compiles away the callback with no error. Repo-wide search found only that macro plus the raw declaration of `wolfSSL_CTX_set_current_time_cb`; no implementation was found in `src/`. - impact: callers can believe they hardened certificate date validation with an external time source while wolfSSL still uses the default clock, leading to unexpected acceptance of expired/not-yet-valid certificates or avoidable outages on bad clocks. - recommendation: implement and wire the callback through the compat layer, or remove the macro and fail compilation/runtime loudly so callers do not get a silent no-op. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - OpenSSL compatibility headers assign the same global NID value `418` to both `prime239v3` and `aes_128_ecb`, creating cross-API algorithm confusion - location: `wolfssl/openssl/ec.h:53`, `wolfssl/openssl/evp.h:302`; supporting tables `src/ssl.c:13115-13116`, `wolfcrypt/src/evp.c:5327,5591`; test expectation `tests/api/test_evp_cipher.c:307` - category: security/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: OpenSSL-compat code treats NIDs as globally unique across object/cipher/curve APIs, or attacker-controlled ASN.1/object names are fed into generic NID-based policy/dispatch code. - evidence: `WC_NID_X9_62_prime239v3 = 418` in `ec.h` and `WC_NID_aes_128_ecb 418` in `evp.h`. The object table in `src/ssl.c` maps `prime239v3` to that NID, while the EVP cipher table maps the same NID to AES-128-ECB and `EVP_get_cipherbynid(418)` is tested as AES-128-ECB. - impact: generic NID lookups can misclassify an ECC curve as a cipher or vice versa, breaking allowlists/denylists, object-name translation, and any dispatch code that assumes one NID == one algorithm. That is especially risky in compatibility consumers that pass parsed OIDs or configured names through `OBJ_*` and `EVP_*_bynid` APIs. - recommendation: restore unique OpenSSL-compatible NID assignments across all compat headers and add regression tests that `OBJ_*`, `EC_*`, and `EVP_*_bynid` round-trip without cross-type collisions. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Medium - `X509_STORE_lock()` / `X509_STORE_unlock()` are advertised but are hardcoded no-ops, so OpenSSL-style trust-store synchronization does not exist in multithreaded compat builds - location: `wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h:748-749`; mutable store state `wolfssl/ssl.h:830`; mutation paths `src/x509_str.c:1248-1295,1342-1396,1564-1607,2019-2053`, `src/crl.c:1500-1561` - category: security/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: multithreaded application shares one `X509_STORE` across handshake threads and reload/update threads and relies on the OpenSSL lock API for serialization. - evidence: the compat layer defines `#define X509_STORE_lock(x) 1` and `#define X509_STORE_unlock(x) 1`; repo-wide search found no real implementation. Meanwhile the store object carries mutable lists/refcounted state (`certs`, `trusted`, `objs`, `numAdded`, `param`, `ref`) and add/update helpers mutate that state. - impact: callers can believe they serialized trust-store mutation while reads/writes still race, causing inconsistent validation state during live cert/CRL reloads and potentially crashy behavior under concurrent store updates. - recommendation: provide a real store mutex behind the compat API or remove these macros and document shared-store mutation as unsupported; also guard store mutation/read paths internally if concurrent access is allowed. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - other sharp compat macros in this chunk (for example `X509_check_purpose(x,id,ca) 0` at `wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h:889` and `SSL_CTX_add_server_custom_ext(...) 0` at `wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h:1635`) look like fail-closed feature gaps rather than a stronger new exploit than the three issues above in this pass - location: `wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h`, `wolfssl/openssl/evp.h`, `wolfssl/openssl/ec.h` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: they are concrete compatibility mismatches, but this pass did not establish a clearer privilege-affecting or data-exposing path stronger than the silent time-callback drop, the NID collision, and the fake X509 store locking.

### Medium - mbedtls/nss interop workflows execute mutable cross-run caches as trusted third-party binaries, so a poisoned or stale cache can bypass checkout/build entirely - location: `.github/workflows/mbedtls.yml:27-43,69-82`, `.github/workflows/mbedtls.sh:7-64`, `.github/workflows/nss.yml:29-54,72-84`, `.github/workflows/nss.sh:7-26` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can populate the repo Actions cache once for key `mbedtls-v3.6.2` or `nss-NSS_3_107_RTM` (for example via a compromised earlier workflow run or other cache-poisoning in repo scope). - evidence: both workflows restore `actions/cache@v4` entries keyed only by upstream version (`path: mbedtls` / `path: nss`), skip checkout and build on `cache-hit`, then later jobs restore the same cache and directly execute cached binaries (`./mbedtls/build/programs/ssl/ssl_server2`, `ssl_client2`, `./dist/Debug/bin/certutil`, `tstclnt`). - impact: trusted CI can run attacker-modified or stale external binaries and report false interop success/failure, undermining release/test integrity. - recommendation: do not execute dependency trees from mutable cross-run caches; pass immutable artifacts from the current build job, or at minimum key caches on strong content/workflow hashes and rebuild when restore provenance does not match. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `coverity-scan-fixes.yml` advertises `workflow_dispatch` but never configures the tree on manual runs, so the Coverity step goes straight to `make` on an unconfigured checkout - location: `.github/workflows/coverity-scan-fixes.yml:4,19-32,47-53` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the workflow has `workflow_dispatch`, but every `./autogen.sh` / `./configure ...` step is guarded by a specific `if: github.event.schedule == ...`; on manual dispatch none of those conditions are true, yet `vapier/coverity-scan-action@v1` still runs `command: "make"`. - impact: manual Coverity triage/reproduction is likely to fail immediately or analyze the wrong tree state, slowing down scanner investigation and remediation. - recommendation: add an unconditional default configure path for `workflow_dispatch` (or make the mode an explicit input/matrix) before invoking the Coverity action. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Reviewed - no stronger chunk-local issue promoted from the remaining workflow matrix/build files in this pass beyond the interop-cache integrity problem and the broken Coverity manual-dispatch path above - location: `.github/workflows/ipmitool.yml`, `net-snmp.yml`, `stunnel.yml`, `curl.yml`, `docker-OpenWrt.yml`, `multi-arch.yml`, `ada.yml`, `multi-compiler.yml`, `symbol-prefixes.yml`, `wolfsm.yml`, `threadx.yml`, `linuxkm.yml`, `docker-Espressif.yml`, `intelasm-c-fallback.yml`, `win-csharp-test.yml`, `pq-all.yml`, `disable-pk-algs.yml`, `trackmemory.yml`, `psk.yml`, `opensslcoexist.yml`, `no-tls.yml`, `packaging.yml`, `fil-c.yml`, `no-malloc.yml`, `wolfCrypt-Wconversion.yml`, `async.yml`, `msys2.yml`, `gencertbuf.yml`, `rust-wrapper.yml`, `cmake-autoconf.yml`, `smallStackSize.yml`, `ocsp.yml`, `codespell.yml`, `macos-apple-native-cert-validation.yml`, `memcached.sh` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass was dominated by CI/build orchestration. Spot checks found some brittle or high-cost patterns (for example host-root bind mounts in the memcached test container and static tarball caching in ThreadX), but this chunk did not establish a clearer exploit path or production-impact failure mode there than the two findings appended above.

### Medium - OpenSSL compatibility makes `X509_V_FLAG_X509_STRICT` a silent no-op, so ported callers can believe strict certificate validation is enabled when wolfSSL never records or enforces it - location: `wolfssl/openssl/x509.h:111-112`; flag API `wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h:801`, `src/x509.c:10312-10318`; absence of implementation confirmed by repo search for `X509_STRICT` in `src/` returning only unrelated cipher/signature strictness code - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application built against the OpenSSL compatibility layer calls `X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set_flags(..., X509_V_FLAG_X509_STRICT)` expecting OpenSSL-style strict chain validation. - evidence: the compat header defines `X509_V_FLAG_X509_STRICT` as literal `0` (same for `X509_V_FLAG_ALLOW_PROXY_CERTS`); `X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set_flags` only ORs the caller-supplied bits into `param->flags`; with a zero-valued flag there is no state change, and this repo has no X.509 strict-mode consumer to enforce stricter validation. - impact: hardening done in OpenSSL ports can silently disappear, letting certificates that the application intended to reject under strict mode pass wolfSSL's default verification path instead. - recommendation: do not advertise `X509_V_FLAG_X509_STRICT` as supported unless it is actually enforced; either assign a real flag and implement the checks, or reject unsupported verify flags loudly so callers do not get false assurance. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `RSA_meth_set*` / `RSA_set_method` compatibility accepts custom RSA methods but cannot store or dispatch any callbacks, creating a silent HSM/policy bypass for OpenSSL-ported code - location: method shape `wolfssl/openssl/rsa.h:82-87`; collapsing setters `wolfssl/openssl/rsa.h:251-257`; implementation use-sites `src/pk_rsa.c:77-86,119-121,1901-1903,1917`; compatibility test expectations `tests/api/test_ossl_rsa.c:646-652` - category: security/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application relies on OpenSSL `RSA_METHOD` callbacks to route private-key operations through an HSM/engine or custom authorization/audit hooks. - evidence: `WOLFSSL_RSA_METHOD` contains only `flags`, `name`, and `dynamic`; all `RSA_meth_set_pub_enc/pub_dec/priv_enc/priv_dec/init/finish/set0_app_data` macros alias to the same `wolfSSL_RSA_meth_set`; repo search in `src/pk_rsa.c` shows method state is only initialized/freed for `flags`/`name`, and `wolfSSL_RSA_set_method()` just stores the pointer and copies `meth->flags` into `rsa->flags`. No repo call-site dispatches RSA operations through method callbacks because there are no callback fields to call. - impact: code ported from OpenSSL can receive success from method-registration APIs yet still run RSA operations through wolfSSL's default path, breaking assumptions that keys never leave hardware or that custom policy hooks guard signing/decryption. - recommendation: either implement real callback-bearing `RSA_METHOD` support and dispatch through it, or make `RSA_meth_set*` / `RSA_set_method` fail clearly for non-default methods so HSM/offload users do not get a false-success port. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Reviewed - `wolfssl/openssl/x509v3.h`, `bio.h`, `crypto.h`, `dsa.h`, and `dh.h` in this chunk are mostly OpenSSL-compat declaration/macro surfaces; adjacent implementation spot checks did not promote a stronger new issue beyond the silent X.509 verify-flag no-op and RSA_METHOD callback no-op above - location: listed headers plus adjacent `src/x509.c`, `src/bio.c`, `src/pk.c`, `src/pk_rsa.c`, and `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: checks covered X509v3 config helpers/stubs, BIO pair/buffer helpers, DH/DSA getters/setters, and crypto lock shims. The strongest repo-specific risks from this segment were compatibility APIs that report success while dropping security-relevant behavior; the remaining macros in this pass were mostly alias scaffolding or expected stubs without a clearer exploit or production-impact path from this chunk alone.

### Medium - OpenSSL-compat `DES_set_key()` silently skips parity/weak-key checks unless a wolfSSL-only compile flag is set, and the advertised `DES_check_key` knob is a no-op macro - location: `wolfssl/openssl/des.h:61-65,102-114`, implementation `src/ssl_crypto.c:2479-2484,2520-2526` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses the OpenSSL DES compatibility API and relies on `DES_set_key()` / `DES_check_key` to reject weak or bad-parity DES keys; default build does not define `WOLFSSL_CHECK_DESKEY`. - evidence: `wolfSSL_DES_set_key_checked()` performs parity and weak-key rejection (`src/ssl_crypto.c:2479-2484`), but `wolfSSL_DES_set_key()` only calls that path under `#ifdef WOLFSSL_CHECK_DESKEY` and otherwise falls through to `wolfSSL_DES_set_key_unchecked()` (`:2523-2526`). The compatibility header exposes `#define DES_check_key(x) /* Define WOLFSSL_CHECK_DESKEY to check key */` instead of a working runtime control (`wolfssl/openssl/des.h:102`). - impact: OpenSSL-targeted code can silently accept weak or malformed DES/3DES keys on wolfSSL builds that appear compatible at compile time, weakening legacy encryption or key-wrap paths and making migrations fail open instead of rejecting unsafe keys. - recommendation: make `DES_set_key()` default to the checked behavior expected by OpenSSL callers, or return a hard failure when weak-key checking is unavailable; do not expose `DES_check_key` as a no-op compatibility macro. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - OpenSSL FIPS DRBG entropy/nonce callback API is accepted but never used, so callers cannot actually control the DRBG seed source they think they configured - location: API contract `wolfssl/openssl/fips_rand.h:34-49,68-80`, implementation `src/ssl.c:20149-20160,20129-20131,20179,20196` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application relies on `FIPS_drbg_set_callbacks()` / `FIPS_drbg_*` compatibility APIs to supply approved entropy/nonce callbacks or deterministic test vectors. - evidence: `wolfSSL_FIPS_drbg_set_callbacks()` only stores the callback pointers into `ctx` (`src/ssl.c:20156-20160`). Repo-wide search for `entropy_get`, `entropy_clean`, `none_get`, and `nonce_clean` finds no call sites beyond that setter. Actual instantiate/reseed/generate paths call `wc_InitRngNonce()` / `wc_InitRng()`, `wc_RNG_DRBG_Reseed()`, and `wc_RNG_GenerateBlock()` directly (`src/ssl.c:20129-20131,20179,20196`). - impact: applications can believe they forced entropy through a specific FIPS boundary, HSM, or deterministic test hook while wolfSSL still seeds and generates from its own default RNG path. That creates silent policy drift and can invalidate FIPS/test assumptions or deployment controls around entropy provenance. - recommendation: either wire the configured callbacks into instantiate/reseed/generate, or fail `FIPS_drbg_set_callbacks()` / related operations as unsupported instead of accepting configuration that is ignored. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - SHA3 OpenSSL wrapper over-allocates its opaque context by a factor of pointer size, inflating every SHA3-bearing EVP digest context by ~8x on 64-bit builds - location: `wolfssl/openssl/sha3.h:39-48`; cast-based use `src/ssl_crypto.c:805,822,841,864,881,900,923,940,959,982,999,1018`; embedding sites `wolfssl/openssl/evp.h:189-222,239` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: when `WOLFSSL_SHA3` is enabled, `WOLFSSL_SHA3_CTX` is declared as `ALIGN16 void* holder[sizeof(wc_Sha3)]` (`wolfssl/openssl/sha3.h:46`) instead of dividing by `sizeof(void*)` like the non-SHA3 fallback does (`:48`). The implementation then casts the wrapper directly to `wc_Sha3*` (`src/ssl_crypto.c:805,864,923,982` etc.), so only the first `sizeof(wc_Sha3)` bytes are used and the remainder is dead padding. `wolfssl/openssl/evp.h` embeds these SHA3 contexts in the digest union (`:189-222`) carried by `struct WOLFSSL_EVP_MD_CTX` (`:239`). - impact: on 64-bit targets a ~424-byte `wc_Sha3` turns into a ~3392-byte wrapper, bloating each SHA3-capable EVP digest context by roughly 3 KB. High-concurrency or stack-heavy code using OpenSSL-style EVP contexts pays avoidable memory-cache and stack-footprint costs for every instance. - recommendation: size the holder in bytes, not pointers (for example `byte holder[sizeof(wc_Sha3)]` with `ALIGN16`, or divide by `sizeof(void*)` with rounding as done elsewhere), and add a static assert on `sizeof(WOLFSSL_SHA3_CTX) >= sizeof(wc_Sha3)` instead of relying on manual formulas. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - OpenSSL-style config/module initialization is mapped to success/no-op shims, so applications can believe security-relevant config modules were loaded when wolfSSL ignored them - location: compatibility macros `wolfssl/openssl/conf.h:104-112`, `wolfssl/openssl/objects.h:72`; implementations `src/conf.c:413-422`, `src/ssl.c:14626-14637` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `CONF_modules_load` is aliased to `wolfSSL_CONF_modules_load`, whose implementation logs `"wolfSSL_CONF_modules_load doesn't load anything new."` and returns `WOLFSSL_SUCCESS` (`src/conf.c:418-422`). `OPENSSL_load_builtin_modules()` is hard-mapped to `WC_DO_NOTHING` (`wolfssl/openssl/objects.h:72`). The nearby object-registration path is also unsupported (`wolfSSL_OBJ_create` is a stub at `src/ssl.c:14632-14637`). - impact: OpenSSL-compat consumers can start successfully under wolfSSL while assuming `openssl.cnf` modules, built-in module init, or related object/config side effects were applied.
