That silent config drift is operationally dangerous for deployments that gate security posture on startup config rather than hard-coded settings. - recommendation: fail closed for unsupported module/object initialization instead of returning success or a macro no-op; document the unsupported config surface explicitly so migrations cannot assume security config was honored. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - this header-only compatibility chunk did not surface a stronger concrete issue in `wolfssl/openssl/aes.h`, `pkcs7.h`, `ocsp.h`, `obj_mac.h`, `md5.h`, `hmac.h`, `ecdsa.h`, `err.h`, `opensslv.h`, or `compat_types.h` beyond the no-op compatibility shims, DES check bypass, ignored DRBG callbacks, and SHA3 context bloat above - location: listed headers plus adjacent implementations in `src/ssl_crypto.c`, `src/ssl_p7p12.c`, `src/ocsp.c`, `src/conf.c`, `src/ssl.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks confirmed the more concrete risks in this chunk were semantic mismatches in the compatibility layer itself; the remaining items here were mostly typedef/macro exposure and feature gating without a clearer exploit path or production-impacting bug from this segment alone.

### Medium - OpenSSL-compat `X509_STORE_CTX` policy setters silently drop most per-verification certificate policy - location: `wolfssl/openssl/x509_vfy.h:35-44`, `wolfssl/openssl/ssl.h:729-736,889`, `src/x509_str.c:839-858`, `src/x509.c:10351` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses wolfSSL's OpenSSL compatibility X.509 verification APIs and relies on per-context `X509_STORE_CTX_set_purpose()` / `X509_STORE_CTX_set_flags()` rather than store-global configuration, especially in OpenSSL-ported code that does not expect these setters to be stubs. - evidence: `x509_vfy.h` exports both setters; `wolfSSL_X509_STORE_CTX_set_purpose()` immediately `(void)purpose; WOLFSSL_STUB(...); return 0;`; `wolfSSL_X509_STORE_CTX_set_flags()` only honors `WOLFSSL_PARTIAL_CHAIN` (`ctx->flags |= WOLFSSL_PARTIAL_CHAIN`) even though compat flags for `X509_V_FLAG_CRL_CHECK`, `X509_V_FLAG_CRL_CHECK_ALL`, and `X509_V_FLAG_NO_CHECK_TIME` are defined in `ssl.h`; `ssl.h` also maps `X509_check_purpose(x, id, ca)` to constant `0`; `src/x509.c:10351` notes `WOLFSSL_X509_VERIFY_PARAM` does not record purpose, trust, depth, or auth level. - impact: OpenSSL-ported callers can believe they tightened cert-validation policy on a specific verification while wolfSSL silently ignores most of it, leading to missing per-request revocation/purpose enforcement or hard-to-audit accept/reject drift from OpenSSL semantics. - recommendation: either implement purpose/flag propagation and enforcement in `wolfSSL_X509_verify_cert()` / verify params, or fail loudly for unsupported policy setters instead of exporting partial no-op compatibility APIs; add regressions for ctx-level CRL and purpose handling. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - remaining files in this OpenSSL-compat header chunk did not yield a stronger concrete issue than the X509 policy-no-op above - location: `wolfssl/openssl/{cmac.h,txt_db.h,tls1.h,stack.h,lhash.h,md4.h,buffer.h,pkcs12.h,rc4.h,ripemd.h,ed25519.h,ed448.h,modes.h,ecdh.h,kdf.h,ec25519.h,ec448.h,safestack.h,rand.h,srp.h,asn1t.h,ossl_typ.h,camellia.h,cms.h,engine.h,opensslconf.h,include.am,ssl23.h,ui.h}` with adjacent implementations in `src/{ssl_crypto.c,conf.c,ssl.c,pk.c,pk_ec.c,ssl_sk.c}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: most files here are thin declarations, constant aliases, or empty compatibility stubs. Adjacent spot checks showed bounded TXT_DB field counts (`src/conf.c:55`, `wolfssl/openssl/txt_db.h:27,35`), CTS helpers rejecting invalid/null inputs before block processing (`src/ssl_crypto.c:3358-3359,3413-3414`), and the ECX/CMAC wrappers doing basic length/null checks rather than exposing a stronger new trust-boundary break from this chunk alone.

### Medium - `NO_WOLFSSL_BN_CTX` leaves a fake non-NULL `BN_CTX` handle and dangling helper macros in the OpenSSL compatibility layer - location: `wolfssl/openssl/bn.h:218-224,299-300`, implementation guard `src/ssl_bn.c:2398-2509`, adjacent BN ops `src/ssl_bn.c:1916,1954,2313` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: when `NO_WOLFSSL_BN_CTX` is set, the header rewrites `BN_CTX_new()` to `((BN_CTX*)-1)` and `BN_CTX_free(x)` to a no-op, but still maps `BN_CTX_get` / `BN_CTX_start` to wolfSSL functions unconditionally. Those helpers are only compiled under `#ifndef NO_WOLFSSL_BN_CTX` in `src/ssl_bn.c`. Many BN operations also ignore the passed context entirely (`(void)ctx`), so the invalid sentinel can survive NULL checks and only fail later when real `BN_CTX` semantics are used. - impact: OpenSSL-ported code built with `NO_WOLFSSL_BN_CTX` can mis-detect allocation success, then hit unresolved-symbol/build failures or invalid-handle behavior non-locally when it later uses `BN_CTX_get/start`, making this config a compatibility trap that is hard to reason about. - recommendation: if `BN_CTX` support is disabled, hide the whole `BN_CTX` surface consistently; at minimum make `BN_CTX_new()` return `NULL` and gate `BN_CTX_get/start` declarations/macros on the same feature flag. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Low - OpenSSL BN Montgomery/flag APIs are mostly decorative and silently discard caller optimization hints - location: `wolfssl/openssl/bn.h:196-202,254-255,304`, implementation `src/ssl_bn.c:1910-1927,2515-2520`, math backend `wolfcrypt/src/integer.c:963,3749` - category: performance/complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `BN_mod_exp_mont(a,b,c,d,e,f)` explicitly discards `f` and calls plain `wolfSSL_BN_mod_exp`, `BN_set_flags(x1, x2)` is hard-coded to `WC_DO_NOTHING`, and `wolfSSL_BN_mod_exp()` itself ignores `ctx` then goes straight to `mp_exptmod`. `wolfSSL_BN_MONT_CTX_new()` returns `(WOLFSSL_BN_MONT_CTX*)&mont`, while repo search found no concrete `struct WOLFSSL_BN_MONT_CTX` definition outside the forward declaration in the header. The backend `mp_exptmod()` just dispatches to `s_mp_exptmod(..., 1)`, so the advertised Montgomery handle never influences the call path. - impact: OpenSSL-compat callers can carry around `BN_MONT_CTX` objects and set BN flags believing they selected a precomputed or specialized exponentiation path, but every call still uses the same generic mod-exp path, creating avoidable big-int throughput loss and an audit-hostile API that preserves names while dropping semantics. - recommendation: either implement real `BN_MONT_CTX` / flag-backed behavior or remove these compatibility shims so unsupported semantics fail clearly instead of silently degrading. - reference: `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Reviewed - no stronger chunk-specific issue promoted from `wolfssl/openssl/pem.h`, `wolfssl/openssl/sha.h`, or `wolfssl/openssl/asn1.h` beyond the BN compatibility traps above - location: `wolfssl/openssl/pem.h`, `wolfssl/openssl/sha.h`, `wolfssl/openssl/asn1.h` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: adjacent implementation checks reduced the headline risk here. The SHA wrappers assert storage size against real wolfCrypt structs at init (`src/ssl_crypto.c:208,357,416,505,564`), ASN.1 compatibility macros are at least exercised in `tests/api/test_ossl_asn1.c:2357-2423`, and the PEM header’s sharpest edge in this chunk is a declared stub (`wolfssl/openssl/pem.h:283` hard-wires `PEM_write_bio_ECPKParameters(out, x)` to `0`) rather than a clearer trust-boundary break from this header-only segment alone.

### Medium - safe Rust AES wrappers accept arbitrary output element types and then write raw ciphertext/plaintext bytes into them, enabling UB in caller code - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/aes.rs:165-177,342-364,541-553,958-977,1326-1348,1557-1570,1804-1816,2015-2028,2404-2519` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: change all binary buffer parameters to `&[u8]` / `&mut [u8]` (or `&mut [MaybeUninit<u8>]` for write-only outputs) and drop the unconstrained `I/O/A/N` generics so safe Rust cannot smuggle `bool`, `char`, enums, `NonZero*`, etc. across the FFI boundary. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: any Rust consumer uses the safe API with an output slice whose element type cannot hold arbitrary byte patterns. The current public signatures allow this. - evidence: representative methods are generic over output types (`pub fn encrypt<I,O>`, `pub fn decrypt<I,O,N,A>`, etc.), compute byte lengths with `size_of_val(...) as u32`, and pass `dout.as_mut_ptr() as *mut u8` / `auth_tag.as_mut_ptr() as *mut u8` into wolfCrypt with no type bound restricting the destination to bytes. - impact: a supposedly safe Rust API can create invalid Rust values and undefined behavior when FFI writes arbitrary crypto output into non-byte typed slices, undermining the wrapper’s memory-safety boundary.

### Medium - `XTSStream::new()` fabricates initialized FFI state with `assume_init()` on uninitialized memory, so constructing the safe wrapper itself is UB - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/aes.rs:2285-2314`; backing C type `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:474-476`; later initialization writes `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:15535-15536,16015-16016` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: store the stream state as `MaybeUninit<sys::XtsAesStreamData>` until `wc_AesXtsEncryptInit()` / `wc_AesXtsDecryptInit()` populate it, or zero-initialize it explicitly instead of calling `assume_init()` on uninitialized memory. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: `aes_xts_stream` build; Rust caller only needs to call `XTSStream::new()` / `new_ex()`. - evidence: `new_ex()` does `let ws_xtsaesstreamdata: MaybeUninit<sys::XtsAesStreamData> = MaybeUninit::uninit();` followed by `let ws_xtsaesstreamdata = unsafe { ws_xtsaesstreamdata.assume_init() };`. The underlying FFI struct is just `tweak_block` plus `bytes_crypted_with_this_tweak`, and the C init functions later overwrite those fields, so the wrapper had no reason to materialize an initialized Rust value before FFI setup. - impact: safe construction of the wrapper invokes undefined behavior at the Rust/FFI boundary, creating miscompilation and memory-safety risk before any encryption call is made.

### Medium - Rust AES and SHA wrappers silently truncate 64-bit slice lengths to 32-bit wolfCrypt arguments, so buffers above 4 GiB are only partially processed while APIs still report success - location: representative AES casts `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/aes.rs:167-169,344-352,961-969,1329-1337,1560-1563,2406-2443`; representative SHA/SHAKE casts `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/sha.rs:173,376,579,782,985,1188,1391,1594,1797,2004,2035,2097,2274,2305,2367` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: reject any buffer length above `u32::MAX` or internally chunk large inputs across repeated wolfCrypt update calls instead of `as u32` truncation. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: 64-bit Rust caller passes a slice larger than `u32::MAX` bytes (for example a large mapped file or huge in-memory buffer). - evidence: the wrapper repeatedly does `size_of_val(...) as u32` / `len() as u32` before calling `wc_Aes*`, `wc_Sha*`, and `wc_Shake*` APIs, with no bounds check on the original `usize`. If the Rust slice exceeds 4 GiB, the length wraps modulo 2^32 but the method still returns `Ok(())` when the truncated native call succeeds. - impact: two large inputs that differ only after the first wrapped 32-bit length can hash/encrypt differently at the application layer but identically through the wrapper’s actual processed prefix, causing silent integrity/correctness failures on large data.

### Reviewed - no stronger chunk-local issue was promoted from the remaining SHA wrapper boilerplate beyond the 32-bit length truncation above - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/sha.rs` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the rest of `sha.rs` is largely repetitive thin FFI forwarding over byte slices with explicit digest-size checks and matching `Drop` frees; this pass did not find a clearer exploit path in those sections than the unchecked `usize` to `u32` truncation already noted.

### Medium - Rust `RSA::set_rng()` / `Curve25519Key::set_rng()` persist a borrowed `RNG` pointer in C state without any lifetime pinning, so safe Rust can trigger use-after-free during later blinded private operations - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/rsa.rs:1115-1116,1154-1159`, `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.rs:459-473`, `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/random.rs:57-58,383-392`; C storage/sinks `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c:3839,3909,4029,4284,5271-5276`, `wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.c:749,809,1186-1190` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Rust wrapper consumer calls `set_rng()`, then drops or moves that `RNG` before a later RSA private decrypt/verify/PSS op with `WC_RSA_BLINDING` or Curve25519 blinded op with `WOLFSSL_CURVE25519_BLINDING`; no `unsafe` Rust is required. - evidence: the safe wrappers forward `&mut rng.wc_rng` into `wc_RsaSetRNG()` / `wc_curve25519_set_rng()`, and the C side stores it verbatim as `key->rng = rng;`. `RNG` is a plain movable Rust struct containing `wc_rng: sys::WC_RNG` and its `Drop` frees that state with `wc_FreeRng()`. The key types do not retain any lifetime marker or pinned reference tying their validity to the borrowed `RNG`, while later C private-op paths read `key->rng`. - impact: safe Rust code can leave wolfCrypt holding a dangling RNG pointer, causing crashes/memory corruption or broken blinding during later signature/decrypt/shared-secret operations. - recommendation: encode the borrow in the Rust type system (for example a lifetime-carrying/pinned RNG reference on key types) or pass RNG per operation instead of persisting a raw pointer; if that cannot be expressed safely, make `set_rng()` unsafe and clear stored RNG pointers on teardown. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Rust Ed448 wrapper silently truncates context lengths to 8 bits, so contexts of 256+ bytes sign/verify under the wrong domain separator instead of failing - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/ed448.rs:678,740,793,846,900,965,1020,1075,1129`; C API contract `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ed448.h:117,121,125,129,135,139,150,154,158` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application passes attacker-influenced or configuration-derived Ed448 context data longer than 255 bytes through the Rust wrapper. - evidence: all Ed448 sign/verify/init methods do `context_size = context.len() as u8;` before calling C; the underlying wolfCrypt API takes `byte contextLen`. That means a 256-byte context becomes `0`, 257 becomes `1`, etc., and the wrapper never rejects oversize input. - impact: callers can produce or accept signatures under a different context than intended, collapsing domain separation between distinct long contexts with the same low byte and causing authentication/interoperability failures when peers enforce the real 255-byte Ed448 context limit. - recommendation: reject `context.len() > u8::MAX` before the FFI call and document/test the 255-byte maximum explicitly. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no stronger chunk-local issue was promoted from the remaining Rust Ed448/RSA/Curve25519 wrapper methods beyond the persisted-RNG lifetime unsoundness and Ed448 context truncation above - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/ed448.rs`, `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/rsa.rs`, `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.rs` - category: security review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the rest of this chunk is mostly thin FFI forwarding into wolfCrypt C APIs already reviewed elsewhere; spot checks did not establish a clearer exploit path or production-failure mode stronger than the two wrapper-specific issues above.

### High - QAT sync AES-GCM copies caller-sized IVs into a fixed 16-byte heap buffer, enabling heap overflow through the public GCM API - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist_sync.c:973-974,1050,1156-1179,1264-1288`; caller validation/dispatch `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:9968,9986,10701,10712` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_INTEL_QA_SYNC`/`WOLF_CRYPTO_CB` build with AES-GCM routed to the Intel QAT sync backend; caller can supply `ivSz > 16` (the public AES-GCM API only rejects `ivSz == 0`). - evidence: `wc_AesGcmEncrypt()` / `wc_AesGcmDecrypt()` pass caller-controlled `iv`/`ivSz` into `wc_CryptoCb_AesGcm*`; `IntelQaSymSync_CryptoDevCb()` forwards `info->cipher.aesgcm_* .ivSz` to `IntelQaSymAesGcm*`; `IntelQaSymCipher()` allocates `ivBuf = XMALLOC(WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE, ...)` and immediately does `XMEMCPY(ivBuf, iv, ivSz)` before setting `opData->ivLenInBytes = ivSz`. - impact: a single oversized nonce length can smash the heap in the caller process, causing crash and potentially code execution; this is locally reachable from the documented GCM API and remotely reachable where nonce length is attacker-influenced. - recommendation: allocate IV storage from `ivSz` (or reject unsupported lengths before copy) and enforce a clear backend-specific IV-length contract before entering the QAT path. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-1.x`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`

### High - QAT sync symmetric ops ignore `cpaCySymPerformOp()` status and return an uninitialized `ret`, so QAT backpressure/failures can hand back plaintext-or-stale buffers with undefined success reporting - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist_sync.c:921,1041,1084-1113`; contrast status handling in `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist.c:2457,2463` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Intel QAT sync backend is enabled and `cpaCySymPerformOp()` returns `CPA_STATUS_RETRY`/failure (for example under device backpressure or a rejected request). - evidence: `IntelQaSymCipher()` declares `int ret;` without initialization, opens the session with `IntelQaSymOpen(dev, &setup, NULL)`, calls `status = cpaCySymPerformOp(...)`, never feeds `status` through `IntelQaHandleCpaStatus()` and never sets `ret = 0` on success, then unconditionally `XMEMCPY(out, dataBuf, inOutSz)` and `return ret;`. `dataBuf` started as a copy of caller input, so failed encrypt/decrypt requests copy the original input back out; encrypt also copies `authTagBuf` back to the caller even though the QAT op may not have produced a tag. The async backend loops on `IntelQaHandleCpaStatus()` for the same operation class instead of ignoring status. - impact: under load or transient QAT failures, callers can receive bogus crypto output (plaintext echoed as ciphertext, ciphertext echoed as “plaintext”, or stale/uninitialized tags) with undefined success/error signaling, breaking confidentiality/integrity and creating hard-to-reproduce production faults. - recommendation: initialize `ret = 0`, handle `CPA_STATUS_RETRY`/errors exactly as the async backend does before copying any output, and fail closed without exposing `dataBuf`/`authTagBuf` on non-success status. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-11.x`, `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`

### Medium - QAT sync AES/DES3 path dereferences failed heap allocations before NULL checks, turning oversized or low-memory requests into process crashes instead of clean errors - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist_sync.c:970-979,991-995` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Intel QAT sync backend is enabled and allocation pressure makes one of the request buffers fail; attacker can often amplify this by supplying large `inOutSz`/AAD sizes through the symmetric APIs. - evidence: `IntelQaSymCipher()` does `dataBuf = XMALLOC(dataLen, ...)` then immediately `XMEMCPY(dataBuf, in, inOutSz)`, `ivBuf = XMALLOC(...)` then `XMEMCPY(ivBuf, iv, ivSz)`, and for AAD does `authInBuf = XMALLOC(...)` then `XMEMCPY(authInBuf, authIn, authInSz)` before the later NULL checks (`if (ivBuf == NULL || metaBuf == NULL || dataBuf == NULL || authTagBuf == NULL)` / `if (authInBuf == NULL)`). - impact: instead of returning `MEMORY_E`, memory pressure can crash the process in the QAT backend, giving an easy request-driven DoS on services that expose these cipher operations. - recommendation: check every allocation result before first use, and keep the error path fail-closed with no dereference/copy until all required buffers are proven non-NULL. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 v5.0.0-15.x`

### Medium - Rust ECC import helpers leak initialized native `ecc_key` state on error, so repeated malformed key input can grow the process heap until DoS - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/ecc.rs:621-640,684-703,753-776,827-850,886-910,946-969,1014-1036`; native init allocates big-int state in `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:6200-6244`; failing decoders/imports reached via `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:37005,37287` and `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:11147,11348,11781,11789,11807` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Rust application feeds attacker-controlled or retryable invalid ECC keys into `ECC::import_der`, `import_public_der`, `import_private_key[_ex]`, `import_raw[_ex]`, or `import_unsigned`. - evidence: these wrappers call `wc_ecc_init_ex(...)`, `assume_init()` into a raw `sys::ecc_key`, then return `Err(rc)` on downstream decode/import failure without `wc_ecc_free(&mut wc_ecc_key)`. Unlike `import_x963[_ex]`, they never wrap the initialized key in a Rust type before the failing call. `wc_ecc_init_ex` sets up `key->pubkey.{x,y,z}` and `mp_init_multi(...)`, so there is native state to free. - impact: each malformed import attempt can strand native ECC allocations; a service that parses untrusted keys or cert material through this wrapper can be driven into heap growth and eventual failure. - recommendation: after successful `wc_ecc_init_ex`, either construct an RAII wrapper immediately so `Drop` runs on `Err`, or add explicit `wc_ecc_free` on every post-init error path. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Rust ECC raw-key helpers pass arbitrary byte slices to `const char*` hex-string APIs without enforcing NUL termination, enabling native out-of-bounds reads from safe Rust - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/ecc.rs:886-906,946-965`; C string APIs `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ecc.h:884,887`; native consumers `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:11531,11548,11665,11695,11820` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Rust caller supplies ordinary or attacker-controlled `&[u8]` to `ECC::import_raw()` / `import_raw_ex()` without a trailing `\0` byte. - evidence: the wrapper just casts `qx/qy/d/curve_name` with `as *const c_char` and calls `wc_ecc_import_raw[_ex]`. The native code treats those as C strings: `mp_read_radix(key->pubkey.x, qx, MP_RADIX_HEX)`, `mp_read_radix(key->pubkey.y, qy, ...)`, `mp_read_radix(key->k, d, ...)`, and `XSTRLEN(curveName)` for curve matching. - impact: the C side can read past the Rust slice until an incidental NUL byte, causing crashes or adjacent-memory disclosure in processes that import untrusted hex key material through the supposedly safe wrapper. - recommendation: change these APIs to accept `&CStr`/`CString` (or validate exactly one trailing NUL and reject interior NULs) before crossing FFI. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Rust `ECC::set_rng()` stores a borrowed `RNG` pointer inside C key state without any lifetime tie, so later blinded ECC operations can dereference freed memory from safe Rust - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/ecc.rs:1768-1770`; `RNG` free in `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/random.rs:383-392`; C storage/sink `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:13768-13777,4917-4926` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller invokes `ECC::set_rng(&mut rng)`, then drops or moves that `RNG` before a code path that uses `key->rng` (for example timing-resistant/blinded ECC operations such as ECDH). - evidence: the safe wrapper forwards `&mut rng.wc_rng` to `wc_ecc_set_rng`; the C API persists it with `key->rng = rng;`. The Rust `RNG` owns that state and calls `wc_FreeRng` in `Drop`, but the `ECC` wrapper keeps no lifetime marker or pinning. Later ECC code reads `private_key->rng` directly. - impact: a safe-Rust caller can create a dangling native pointer and trigger use-after-free, with crash or memory-corruption potential in later ECC operations. - recommendation: make `set_rng` unsafe or model the borrow in the type system (`PhantomData<&'a mut RNG>` / pinned handle) so an `ECC` key cannot outlive the RNG pointer it stores. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Rust Ed25519 wrapper silently truncates context lengths to 8 bits in sign/verify APIs, so 256+ byte contexts use the wrong domain separator instead of failing - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.rs:719,780,833,886,979,1043,1098,1153,1206`; C API contract `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ed25519.h:131-164`; native signatures `wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.c:364,564,583,847,877,960,979` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application passes attacker-influenced or configuration-derived Ed25519 contexts longer than 255 bytes into `sign_msg_ctx`, `sign_hash_ph`, `sign_msg_ph`, `sign_msg_ex`, `verify_msg_ctx`, `verify_hash_ph`, `verify_msg_ph`, `verify_msg_ex`, or `verify_msg_init`. - evidence: each wrapper does `let context_size = context.len() as u8` with no bounds check, while the underlying C APIs all take `byte contextLen`. In contrast the Dilithium wrapper in this repo explicitly rejects `ctx.len() > 255` before FFI. - impact: a context like 300 bytes is silently treated as its first 44 bytes, breaking caller-expected domain separation and allowing signatures to be produced/accepted under an unintended truncated context namespace. - recommendation: mirror the Dilithium wrapper and return `BUFFER_E` when `context.len() > 255` before casting to `u8`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no stronger new wrapper-local issue was promoted from `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.rs` beyond the existing `WOLFSSL_DILITHIUM_ASSIGN_KEY` ownership-toggle finding already in the inbox - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/dilithium.rs` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass found the Dilithium wrapper already bounds-checks `ctx.len() > 255` before FFI and constructs RAII wrappers early enough that generation/setup failures unwind through `Drop`; no clearer chunk-local exploit path outranked the previously noted aliasing import hazard.

### High - async Intel QAT AES-GCM decrypt ignores the hardware authentication result and can accept forged ciphertext - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist.c:83,2208-2273,2526-2537`; public entry `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:10734`; contrast `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist_sync.c:1084-1092` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_INTEL_QA` build using the async AES/QAT path for GCM decrypt (`QAT_CIPHER_ASYNC=1`). - evidence: `wc_AesGcmDecrypt()` dispatches to `IntelQaSymAesGcmDecrypt()`, which routes decrypt through `IntelQaSymCipher(..., CPA_CY_SYM_HASH_AES_GCM, ...)`. The async callback signature includes `CpaBoolean verifyResult`, but `IntelQaSymCipherCallback()` immediately `(void)verifyResult` and returns success on any `CPA_STATUS_SUCCESS`, copying plaintext out before setting `ret = 0`. The synchronous QAT path in `quickassist_sync.c` explicitly checks `if (verifyResult == CPA_FALSE)` and fails, showing the async path dropped the auth decision. - impact: tampered AES-GCM records can be reported as successful decrypts and handed to callers, breaking AEAD integrity for any TLS/HPKE/application traffic using this backend. - recommendation: fail closed on `verifyResult == CPA_FALSE` (for example `AES_GCM_AUTH_E`), do not copy plaintext/tag on auth failure, and add an async-QAT bad-tag regression. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Intel QAT partial HMAC/hash buffering can write past a fixed 2-entry cache - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/intel/quickassist.h:51,303-305`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist.c:2954-3179`, HMAC entrypoints `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:907,1036` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `QAT_HASH_ENABLE_PARTIAL` build on hardware advertising partial-hash support; caller uses incremental HMAC/auth-hash updates with fragmented input sizes. - evidence: the partial-hash state has `buffers[MAX_QAT_HASH_BUFFERS]` / `buffersSz[...]` with `MAX_QAT_HASH_BUFFERS` hard-coded to `2`, but `IntelQaSymHashPartial()` appends with `buffers[*bufferCount] = ...; (*bufferCount)++;` at multiple sites (`3031-3052`, `3082-3105`, `3130-3132`) with no bounds check. Auth mode intentionally leaves one buffer cached (`hashMode == CPA_CY_SYM_HASH_MODE_AUTH && *bufferCount <= 1` at `3118`), so a sequence like a 1.5-block HMAC update followed by another block can accumulate prior cached data, a flushed tmp block, and another aligned chunk, hitting index `2` out of bounds. - impact: request-shaped HMAC/hash updates can corrupt adjacent memory or crash the process in the QAT offload path. - recommendation: bound-check every append, flush/submit before a third buffer, or replace the fixed 2-slot cache with dynamically sized buffering; add a regression around fragmented HMAC updates across block boundaries. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Intel QAT DRBG chunking uses the previous chunk length instead of the current offset, causing out-of-bounds writes on large `wc_RNG_GenerateBlock()` requests - location: `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:1381`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist.c:4552-4568,4648-4686` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `QAT_ENABLE_RNG` build; caller requests more than ~128 KiB from `wc_RNG_GenerateBlock()` / `IntelQaDrbg()` in one call. - evidence: `wc_RNG_GenerateBlock()` dispatches to `IntelQaDrbg(&rng->asyncDev, output, sz)`. The QAT chunk loop is `while (ret == 0 && idx < rngSz)`, but computes `gen = rngSz - gen` instead of `rngSz - idx`, then sets `dev->qat.out = &rngBuf[idx]`; `IntelQaDrbgCallback()` copies `pOut->dataLenInBytes` bytes into that pointer. After the first `0xFFFF` chunk, requests larger than `131070` bytes keep reusing `65535`-byte chunks and the final iteration overruns the caller buffer. - impact: large RNG fills can corrupt process memory or crash the application instead of just returning random bytes. - recommendation: compute remaining bytes from `idx`, cap the final chunk correctly, and add regressions for sizes around `131070`, `131071`, and `200000`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - Intel QAT thread-affinity mapping binds to the instance index instead of the CPU bit reported by the device - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist.c:426-432,532`, `wolfcrypt/src/async.c:1034-1042` - category: performance - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the code walks each instance's `coreAffinity` bitmask with loop variable `j`, but stores `coreAffinity = i` and later `g_cyInstMap[i] = coreAffinity`; `IntelQaInit()` then calls `wc_AsyncThreadBind(thread, g_cyInstMap[devId])`, and `wc_AsyncThreadBind()` feeds that value directly to `CPU_SET(logicalCore, ...)`. The selected logical core is therefore the QAT instance number, not the advertised affinity bit. - impact: accelerator threads can be pinned to the wrong CPUs, defeating intended locality/NUMA placement and reducing throughput on multi-instance systems. - recommendation: store the discovered CPU bit (`j`) or an explicit affinity policy rather than the instance index, and add a sanity check that the chosen core is actually present in the instance affinity mask. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Medium - `USE_QAE_THREAD_LS` makes QAT NUMA frees thread-affine, so polling-thread cleanup can leak pinned mappings until the accelerator starves - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist_mem.c:210-219,428-429,574-587,1023-1068`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist.c:155,178-180,795`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist_sync.c:294,314-316,677`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/README.md:87` - category: security/performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_INTEL_QA` build with `USE_QAE_THREAD_LS` and async polling enabled; frees happen from a different thread than the allocator (for example the QAT polling thread). - evidence: the NUMA allocation registries (`g_pUserMemList`, `g_avail_size`, `g_userMemListCount`) are declared `QAE_THREAD_LS`, and allocations stamp `header->threadId = pthread_self()`. `qaeMemFreeNUMA()` does not consult that `threadId`; it resolves the pointer only through `userMemLookupByVirtAddr()` against the current thread’s TLS list, and on miss prints `userMemLookupByVirtAddr failed` then returns without `munmap`/`DEV_MEM_IOC_MEMFREE`. The async backend explicitly creates a polling thread (`IntelQaPollingThread` / `pthread_create`) and `IntelQaPoll()` invokes `freeFunc(dev)` there. The README recommends `USE_QAE_THREAD_LS` as a performance option. - impact: valid async traffic can leak each NUMA/QAE allocation when completion cleanup runs on the polling thread, eventually exhausting pinned QAT memory and turning crypto work into a request-driven DoS. - recommendation: keep the QAE allocation registry process-global (or key it by pointer, not thread), and make cross-thread free a supported path instead of a leak; add a regression that allocates on one thread and frees on the polling/completion thread. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `USE_QAE_STATIC_MEM` turns large-buffer misses into pinned-memory leaks: the reuse cache truncates sizes to 16 bits, and once the 16k slot table fills `qaeMemAllocNUMA()` returns after `mmap`/ioctl without cleanup - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/quickassist_mem.c:115-123,211-215,741-748,772-785,946,1005,1011-1016,1078-1083`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/README.md:86` - category: performance/security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_INTEL_QA` build with `USE_QAE_STATIC_MEM`; workloads request QAT NUMA buffers above 64 KiB or otherwise force fresh page allocations until `QAE_USER_MEM_MAX_COUNT` is reached. - evidence: the real per-page `available_size` field is 32-bit, but the static reuse cache stores it in `uint16_t g_avail_size[]` and `userMemLookupBySize()` compares that truncated cache against 32-bit `size`, so pages with more than 65535 bytes free can be treated as too small. On a miss, `qaeMemAllocNUMA()` successfully issues `DEV_MEM_IOC_MEMALLOC`, `mmap`s the page, and only then calls `userMemListAdd()`. If the table is full (`g_userMemListCount >= QAE_USER_MEM_MAX_COUNT`), it prints `Error on mem list add` and returns `NULL` without `munmap()` or `DEV_MEM_IOC_MEMFREE`. - impact: large requests bypass reuse, accelerate slot exhaustion, and after the static table fills each additional miss strands another mapped/pinned QAT allocation, causing unbounded memory growth and eventual crypto allocation failures under load. - recommendation: store cached availability in a 32-bit type, recycle or bound the static table sanely, and unwind with `munmap` + `DEV_MEM_IOC_MEMFREE` on any post-allocation bookkeeping failure. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/README.md` mostly documents known QAT tuning knobs; this pass did not promote a stronger standalone issue from the remaining guidance beyond the two concrete `USE_QAE_*` allocator failure modes above - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/intel/README.md` - category: security/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the other notable README sharp edge is the suggestion to build with `--disable-param-check` for some hardware-hash paths, but in this chunk that remained documentation of an existing backend limitation rather than a clearer repo-local exploit path than the allocator leaks above.

### Medium - Espressif RSA accelerator wrappers fail open on hardware errors: timeout/fault status is dropped or overwritten, so callers can get `MP_OKAY` with stale or uninitialized math results - location: classic-ESP32 ignored waits `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_mp.c:2181,2200,2848`; common error clobber on unlock `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_mp.c:1861,2208,2300,2398,2498,2855,2948,3046,3137`; entry from big-int backend `wolfcrypt/src/tfm.c:312,2881,2974,3077,4437` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: ESP32 hardware math enabled; RSA/ECC/DH math reaches `esp_mp_mul`, `esp_mp_mulmod`, or `esp_mp_exptmod`; accelerator timeout/fault occurs (silicon glitch, contention, fault injection, or wedged peripheral). - evidence: `wait_until_done()` returns `WC_HW_E` on timeout, but the classic ESP32 `mulmod`/`exptmod` paths call it without storing the return (`esp32_mp.c:2181,2200,2848`) and continue reading MMIO state. In other SoC branches the timeout is assigned to `ret`, but later cleanup does `ret = esp_mp_hw_unlock();`, which overwrites the earlier failure with unlock success before post-processing `tmpZ`/`Z`. - impact: hardware math failures become silent success, so private/public key operations can continue on stale register contents or uninitialized temporaries instead of failing closed, producing corrupt signatures/plaintexts/shared secrets and hard-to-triage TLS/crypto failures under fault conditions. - recommendation: preserve the first hardware/process error through cleanup (`unlock_ret` separate from `ret`), check every `wait_until_done()` result before reading result registers, and zero/poison result temporaries on failure so callers cannot consume stale state. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `wc_esp_wait_until_idle()` either spins forever or logs-and-continues on timeout, so a wedged SHA peripheral can deadlock callers or be used while still busy - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_sha.c:856-883`; callers `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_sha.c:1884,2082,2109,2117,2126,2171` - category: security/performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: ESP32 SHA hardware backend enabled; peripheral stays busy because of lock misuse, hardware fault, or prior incomplete operation. - evidence: on RISC-V targets `wc_esp_wait_until_idle()` decrements `loop_ct` from `WC_ESP_MAX_IDLE_WAIT` but, when it expires, only logs `"Too long to exit wc_esp_wait_until_idle"` and still returns success. On ESP32/S2/S3 the busy loops (`while (REG_READ(SHA_BUSY_REG))`, `while ((DPORT_REG_READ(SHA_1_BUSY_REG) != 0) || ...)`) have no timeout at all. `wc_esp_process_block()` and `wc_esp_digest_state()` treat the return as success and continue accessing SHA registers. - impact: one stuck SHA engine can hang hash/TLS threads indefinitely on Xtensa parts, while RISC-V parts can read/write the engine despite a timeout, risking stale digest state and handshake/data-integrity failures instead of a clean fallback. - recommendation: make idle wait bounded on every target and return an error on expiry; have callers abort/unroll and fall back to software hashing instead of proceeding against busy hardware. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - ESP32-C3 RSA unlock appears to write the wrong MMIO addresses/masks, so every hardware-RSA unlock can miss power-down and touch unrelated registers - location: lock path `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_mp.c:460-463`; mismatched unlock path `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_mp.c:566-574` - category: complexity/security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `CONFIG_IDF_TARGET_ESP32C3` build uses the RSA hardware math backend. - evidence: lock enables the accelerator with `DPORT_REG_SET_BIT((volatile void *)(SYSTEM_PERIP_CLK_EN1_REG), SYSTEM_CRYPTO_RSA_CLK_EN)` and `DPORT_REG_CLR_BIT((volatile void *)(SYSTEM_RSA_PD_CTRL_REG), SYSTEM_RSA_MEM_PD)`. The C3 unlock path does not mirror that: it calls `DPORT_REG_CLR_BIT((volatile void *)(DR_REG_RSA_BASE + SYSTEM_CRYPTO_RSA_CLK_EN), SYSTEM_PERIP_CLK_EN1_REG)` and `DPORT_REG_SET_BIT((volatile void *)(DR_REG_RSA_BASE + SYSTEM_RSA_MEM_PD), SYSTEM_RSA_PD_CTRL_REG)`, swapping register-vs-mask roles and adding a `DR_REG_RSA_BASE` offset not used in the enable path. - impact: the accelerator may remain clocked/powered after use or the driver may scribble unrelated MMIO locations on every unlock, causing intermittent crypto hangs/state corruption that are non-local and hard to review. - recommendation: make the C3 unlock sequence the exact inverse of the lock sequence, using the same register addresses and bit masks in the correct argument order; add a hardware smoke test that lock/unlock toggles the intended registers only. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Espressif `set_time_wait_for_ntp()` fails open on legacy SNTP builds and returns success without any verified sync, so callers can proceed with TLS on stale build-time clocks - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_sdk_time_lib.c:338-438,442-480`; affected callers `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_client/main/main.c:203,269`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_server/main/main.c:201,267` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `USE_WOLFSSL_ESP_SDK_TIME` build on the `<esp_sntp.h>` path where `HAS_ESP_NETIF_SNTP` is not defined; application treats `set_time_wait_for_ntp()` as the readiness gate before certificate validation or other time-sensitive network work. - evidence: `set_time()` first seeds the system clock from `LIBWOLFSSL_VERSION_GIT_HASH_DATE` or `set_fixed_default_time()` and starts SNTP setup. But `set_time_wait_for_ntp()` starts with `int ret = 0;`; when `HAS_ESP_NETIF_SNTP` is absent it only logs `"HAS_ESP_NETIF_SNTP not defined"`, then falls through to `if (ret == ESP_OK)` and `return ret;`. - impact: devices can believe NTP sync completed while still using stale git/build fallback time, leading to incorrect X.509 not-before/not-after decisions and intermittent TLS acceptance/rejection drift that is detached from the real clock-sync failure. - recommendation: initialize `ret` to failure, implement a real legacy-SNTP wait/status path, and fail closed until time is actually synchronized or explicitly marked approximate. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `set_time_from_string()` passes a partially uninitialized `struct tm` to `mktime()`, so stack garbage can skew the system clock used for certificate checks - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_sdk_time_lib.c:273-323` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: any `USE_WOLFSSL_ESP_SDK_TIME` build that calls `set_time_from_string()` directly or via `set_time()` when `LIBWOLFSSL_VERSION_GIT_HASH_DATE` is compiled in. - evidence: the function declares `struct tm this_timeinfo;` without initialization, assigns only `tm_mon` (if a month-string match is found), `tm_mday`, `tm_hour`, `tm_min`, `tm_sec`, and `tm_year`, then immediately calls `mktime(&this_timeinfo)`. `tm_isdst` and the remaining fields are left undefined, and an unrecognized month leaves `tm_mon` undefined too. - impact: boot-time clock seeding can vary with stack contents or malformed input, producing hour/month skew and inconsistent cert-validity behavior near issuance/expiry boundaries. - recommendation: zero-initialize `struct tm`, set `tm_isdst = -1`, reject unknown month strings, and fail rather than calling `mktime()` on partially initialized state. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Espressif SNTP setup loops over `CONFIG_LWIP_SNTP_MAX_SERVERS` but the built-in server table only has 3 entries, causing out-of-bounds pointer reads on larger sdkconfig values - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_sdk_time_lib.c:105-119,158-160,393-406` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `NTP_SERVER_LIST` hardcodes 3 hosts and `char* ntpServerList[NTP_SERVER_COUNT] = NTP_SERVER_LIST;`, but `set_time()` iterates `for (i = 0; i < CONFIG_LWIP_SNTP_MAX_SERVERS; i++)` and reads `ntpServerList[i]` before `strncmp()` / `sntp_setservername()`. The code warns only when `NTP_SERVER_COUNT > CONFIG_LWIP_SNTP_MAX_SERVERS`, not when the config exceeds the array length. - impact: builds with `CONFIG_LWIP_SNTP_MAX_SERVERS > 3` can dereference garbage pointers during boot-time time setup, causing crashes or bogus NTP host configuration. - recommendation: bound the loop with `min(NTP_SERVER_COUNT, CONFIG_LWIP_SNTP_MAX_SERVERS)` or size/fill the table to the config limit. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_util.c` did not surface a stronger default-on exploit path in this pass beyond debug/diagnostic logging and helper glue; the concrete chunk-specific issues were in `esp_sdk_time_lib.c` time seeding/sync behavior - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_util.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this file is dominated by mutex wrappers, watchdog toggles, macro/version reporting, and debug helpers (`esp_show_mp`, `hexToBinary`, `show_binary`). The sharper edges here were mostly opt-in debug disclosures (`SHOW_SSID_AND_PASSWORD`, git/path/CMake output logging) rather than a clearer always-on exploit or production-failure path stronger than the time-helper bugs above.

### Medium - Rust ChaCha20-Poly1305 one-shot wrappers omit the required input/output length equality check, so safe callers can drive C out-of-bounds writes - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.rs:65-89,107-131,203-212`; C API contract `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/chacha20_poly1305.h:90-105` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: safe Rust caller passes a `ciphertext` buffer shorter than `plaintext` to `encrypt()` or a `plaintext` buffer shorter than `ciphertext` to `decrypt()`. - evidence: the one-shot wrappers validate only key/IV/tag sizes, then pass raw output pointers to `wc_ChaCha20Poly1305_Encrypt(... inPlaintextLen, byte* outCiphertext, ...)` / `wc_ChaCha20Poly1305_Decrypt(... inCiphertextLen, ... byte* outPlaintext)`. Those C APIs take no output-length argument, so they write exactly the input length. In the same module, the streaming path’s `update_data()` explicitly enforces `din.len() == dout.len()`, showing the missing invariant locally. - impact: pure safe Rust can trigger out-of-bounds writes and memory corruption/UB inside the FFI call instead of getting a `BUFFER_E`. - recommendation: reject length mismatches before the FFI call (as `update_data()` already does), or allocate/return correctly-sized output buffers instead of accepting arbitrary mutable slices. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - safe Rust RNG wrappers accept arbitrary element types and reinterpret them as raw byte buffers, making `generate_block()` / `new_with_nonce()` unsound - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/random.rs:129-170,339-344`; C API contract `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/random.h:365-368` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: safe Rust caller passes non-byte types such as `bool`, `char`, enums, or padded structs into `RNG::generate_block<T>()` or `RNG::new_with_nonce[_ex]<T>()`. - evidence: both APIs are generic over `T`, cast the slice pointer to `*mut u8`, compute `size_of_val(...) as u32`, and call byte-oriented C functions expecting `byte* nonce` / `byte* output`. `generate_block()` therefore writes arbitrary bytes into arbitrary Rust values, and `new_with_nonce_ex()` reads raw bytes from arbitrary `T` values/padding even though the nonce is logically input-only. - impact: safe code can create invalid Rust values or expose uninitialized padding, leading to UB, memory corruption, or accidental data disclosure across the FFI boundary. - recommendation: restrict nonce/input buffers to `&[u8]` and RNG output buffers to `&mut [u8]` (or `&mut [MaybeUninit<u8>]` for explicit uninitialized output handling); do not expose arbitrary `T` here. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - this Rust crypto test chunk mostly strengthens already-filed wrapper unsoundness rather than adding a stronger new repo issue - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/tests/{test_dh.rs,test_blake2.rs,test_rsa.rs,test_curve25519.rs,test_random.rs,test_hkdf.rs,test_cmac.rs,test_hmac.rs,test_prf.rs,test_wolfcrypt.rs}` with adjacent APIs in `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/{random.rs,rsa.rs,curve25519.rs,dh.rs,blake2.rs,cmac.rs,hmac.rs,hkdf.rs,prf.rs,lib.rs}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `test_random.rs:test_rng_generate_block_u32` explicitly treats `RNG::generate_block(&mut [u32])` as supported safe usage, directly reinforcing the existing finding that the public RNG wrapper writes raw bytes into arbitrary Rust element types. `test_curve25519.rs:test_shared_secret[_ex]` and `test_rsa.rs:test_rsa_encrypt_decrypt` / `test_rsa_pss` / `test_rsa_ssl` also exercise the already-filed `set_rng()` lifetime hazard by making persisted RNG pointers part of normal safe API use. The remaining tests are mostly known-answer / round-trip coverage and did not expose a stronger new trust-boundary break than those existing wrapper findings.

### Medium - Rust SRTP/SRTCP KDF wrappers accept arbitrary-length index slices even though the C backend reads fixed 6-byte SRTP / 4-byte SRTCP indices, so safe Rust callers can trigger out-of-bounds reads and wrong key derivation - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/kdf.rs:585-596,635-643,685-696,735-743`; sink `wolfcrypt/src/kdf.c:889-920,1036-1052,1096-1099,1221-1226,1294-1299`; constants `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/kdf.h:140,142,146-147` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: safe Rust code passes a truncated `idx: &[u8]` into `srtp_kdf*()` or `srtcp_kdf*()` (for example from parsed RTP/SRTCP packet state or caller-derived counter bytes). - evidence: the wrapper signatures take unconstrained `idx: &[u8]` and forward only `idx.as_ptr()` to `wc_SRTP_KDF[_label]` / `wc_SRTCP_KDF[_label]` with no length check. The backend then hardcodes `WC_SRTP_INDEX_LEN` (6) for SRTP and `WC_SRTCP_32BIT_IDX -> WC_SRTCP_INDEX_LEN` (4) for `wc_SRTCP_KDF`, and `wc_srtp_kdf_first_block()` directly reads `idx[i]` / `idx[0]`. - impact: a short Rust slice lets C read past the slice boundary into adjacent process memory, which is UB in a supposedly safe wrapper and can yield crashes or silently wrong SRTP/SRTCP traffic keys. - recommendation: make the safe API encode the invariant with fixed-size arrays (`[u8; 6]` for SRTP, `[u8; 4]` plus an explicit 48-bit SRTCP variant), or reject short slices before the FFI call. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - aside from the SRTP/SRTCP index-length FFI hazard above, this DH/LMS/TLS13-KDF wrapper chunk was mostly thin RAII and size-check forwarding over existing C APIs, and this pass did not promote a stronger repo-specific exploit or production-impact issue from `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/dh.rs`, `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/lms.rs`, or the remaining `kdf.rs` helpers - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks covered DH named-group comparison and key-check forwarding, LMS key-ID/export/sign/verify wrappers, and the HKDF/PBKDF/SSH KDF wrappers. Most paths either already validate output sizes locally or inherit C-side contracts without adding a clearer new break beyond the SRTP/SRTCP short-slice FFI issue.

### High - safe Rust one-shot `ChaCha20Poly1305::{encrypt,decrypt}` never checks output-buffer length before passing raw pointers into C APIs that write `input.len()` bytes - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.rs:65-81,107-123`, contrast only streaming length check at `:203-210`; native API contract `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/chacha20_poly1305.h:94-95,103-105` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: any Rust caller can pass a shorter `ciphertext`/`plaintext` output slice than the input slice to the safe one-shot API; no `unsafe` is required. - evidence: the wrapper forwards `ciphertext.len() as u32` / `plaintext.len() as u32` into `sys::wc_ChaCha20Poly1305_Decrypt(...)` / `...Encrypt(...)` while handing only `plaintext.as_mut_ptr()` / `ciphertext.as_mut_ptr()` to C. Repo search found no one-shot equality check; the only `din.len() != dout.len()` guard in this file is for streaming `update_data()` at `src/chacha20_poly1305.rs:204`. The underlying C prototypes take an input length and an output pointer, but no output-buffer length (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/chacha20_poly1305.h:94-95,103-105`). - impact: a supposedly safe Rust call can drive out-of-bounds writes and memory corruption in the caller process. - recommendation: reject mismatched input/output lengths in the one-shot wrappers before FFI, or redesign the API to allocate/return exact-length outputs rather than accepting arbitrary `&mut [u8]`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - safe Rust `ECC::rs_hex_to_sig()` accepts arbitrary byte slices but casts them to `const char*` hex strings, enabling native out-of-bounds reads unless the slices are NUL-terminated - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/ecc.rs:1218-1223`; C API `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ecc.h:876`; native consumers `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:11366,11398-11400`; test evidence `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/tests/test_ecc.rs:107-119` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Rust caller passes ordinary or attacker-controlled `&[u8]` hex data to `ECC::rs_hex_to_sig()` without a trailing `\0`. - evidence: the wrapper signature is safe (`pub fn rs_hex_to_sig(r: &[u8], s: &[u8], ...)`) but immediately does `let r_ptr = r.as_ptr() as *const c_char; let s_ptr = s.as_ptr() as *const c_char; sys::wc_ecc_rs_to_sig(r_ptr, s_ptr, ...)`. The C side expects C strings and feeds them to `mp_read_radix(...)`. The test helper has to append `'\0'` manually before calling this API, which confirms the hidden contract. - impact: the C parser can read past the Rust slice until an incidental NUL byte, causing crashes or adjacent-memory disclosure from safe Rust. - recommendation: take `&CStr`/`CString` (or parse hex into bytes in Rust and call the raw-binary API) instead of accepting unconstrained byte slices as C strings. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/tests/test_aes.rs`, `test_lms.rs`, and `test_dilithium.rs` in this chunk mostly exercised already-reported wrapper issues or correct bounds checks rather than surfacing a stronger new bug - location: listed tests with adjacent wrapper code in `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/{aes.rs,lms.rs,dilithium.rs}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `test_aes.rs` corroborates the previously noted `XTSStream::new()` UB path and generic AES output-type hazards; `test_dilithium.rs` exercises the wrapper’s explicit `ctx.len() > 255` / seed-size checks rather than showing a clearer flaw; `test_lms.rs` uses `unsafe set_context()` with boxed callback state as documented and did not expose a stronger safe-API lifetime break from this chunk alone.

### Reviewed - this Rust test chunk mainly exercises already-filed wrapper edge cases and did not surface a stronger new repo-specific issue than the existing Ed25519/Ed448 context truncation, SRTP/SRTCP index-length hazard, HKDF empty-IKM bug, and SHA length-truncation findings - location: `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/tests/{test_ed448.rs,test_mlkem.rs,test_kdf.rs,test_ed25519.rs,test_sha.rs}` with adjacent APIs in `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/src/{ed448.rs,ed25519.rs,kdf.rs,mlkem.rs,sha.rs}` - category: security review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `test_ed25519.rs` and `test_ed448.rs` only use 3-byte contexts and exact-sized signatures/keys, while the wrapper-side bug is the already-filed unchecked `context.len() as u8` in `src/ed25519.rs:719,780,833,886,979,1043,1098,1153,1206` and `src/ed448.rs:678,740,793,846,900,965,1020,1075,1129`. `test_kdf.rs` uses valid 6-byte/4-byte SRTP/SRTCP indices and short HKDF inputs; the sharper adjacent issues remain the previously logged SRTP/SRTCP fixed-index FFI hazard in `src/kdf.rs:585-596,635-643,685-696,735-743` and the already-filed native empty-IKM HKDF extract bug exercised by `tls13_hkdf_extract(..., None, None, ...)` via `src/kdf.rs:297-359` / `wolfcrypt/src/kdf.c:355-424`. `test_mlkem.rs` round-trips exact-size buffers through `MlKem::{encode,decode}_{public,private}_key` and `encapsulate/decapsulate`; adjacent wrapper checks already enforce exact ct/ss sizes at `src/mlkem.rs:475-480,543-548,608`, and this pass did not establish a stronger exploit path from these tests alone. `test_sha.rs` is fixed-size KAT coverage and did not add a clearer issue beyond the already-filed `usize`→`u32` truncation pattern in `src/sha.rs`.

### Medium - Espressif AES wrappers mishandle mutex failures, so timed-out callers can disable or drive the shared AES peripheral without owning the lock - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_aes.c:63-121,135-142,435-493,512-552`; lock semantics `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_util.c:125-172` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: multi-tasked `WOLFSSL_ESPIDF` build using the ESP32 AES hardware path; a second task only needs `esp_CryptHwMutexLock(..., 5000)` to time out while another task is using AES. - evidence: `esp_aes_hw_InUse()` returns an error on lock timeout, but `wc_esp32AesDecrypt()` ignores that return (`esp_aes_hw_InUse();` at `:478`) and still calls `esp_aes_hw_Set_KeyMode()` / `esp_aes_bk()`. `wc_esp32AesEncrypt()` and `wc_esp32AesCbcEncrypt()` do store the lock result but unconditionally call `esp_aes_hw_Leave()` on exit (`:458`, `:552`). `esp_aes_hw_Leave()` always `periph_module_disable(PERIPH_AES_MODULE);` then `esp_CryptHwMutexUnLock(&aes_mutex);`, and the underlying unlock path calls `xSemaphoreGive(*mutex)` (`esp32_util.c:171`). - impact: a timed-out caller can disable the AES peripheral out from under the real owner or program/use the hardware without the mutex, causing corrupted ciphertext/plaintext, spurious TLS/application crypto failures, and potential RTOS assert/crash under contention. - recommendation: track whether the lock was actually acquired and only call `esp_aes_hw_Leave()` when it was; in `wc_esp32AesDecrypt()` propagate the `esp_aes_hw_InUse()` result and return before touching registers on failure. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Espressif WiFi helper logs the configured PSK directly on connection failures, exposing credentials to serial/syslog/cloud log sinks - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_sdk_wifi_lib.c:224,238,432-438` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: builds that use `esp_sdk_wifi_init_sta()` / `wc_wifi_init_sta()` with `EXAMPLE_ESP_WIFI_PASS` configured and have readable device logs; no code execution is needed, only a failed join or `SHOW_SSID_AND_PASSWORD`. - evidence: failure paths call `ESP_LOGI(TAG, "Failed to connect to SSID:%s, password:%s", EXAMPLE_ESP_WIFI_SSID, EXAMPLE_ESP_WIFI_PASS);` at `:224`, `:238`, and again under `SHOW_SSID_AND_PASSWORD` at `:437`; the same debug block logs the password on success at `:432-434`. - impact: WiFi credentials cross the trust boundary from configuration into logs, so anyone with UART access, collected diagnostics, or centralized log access can recover the AP password. - recommendation: never log the raw password; remove it from all log lines or replace it with a constant redaction/token-length indicator even in debug builds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V16 Security Logging and Error Handling`

### Medium - ESP8266 IDF5 WiFi connect helper registers an IP callback that can never set the connected bit, then waits forever on `portMAX_DELAY` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_sdk_wifi_lib.c:135-156,176-179,210-214` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: in the `CONFIG_IDF_TARGET_ESP8266` handler, all logic is nested under `if (event_base == WIFI_EVENT)`, but the success branch is `else if(event_id == IP_EVENT_STA_GOT_IP)` and sets `WIFI_CONNECTED_BIT`. The init path still registers that same handler for `IP_EVENT_STA_GOT_IP` (`:178-179`) and then blocks in `xEventGroupWaitBits(..., portMAX_DELAY)` (`:210-214`). - impact: on a successful connect the IP event callback is a no-op because `event_base` is `IP_EVENT`, so `WIFI_CONNECTED_BIT` is never set and `esp_sdk_wifi_init_sta()` can hang indefinitely instead of returning success. - recommendation: handle `IP_EVENT` at the top level (`event_base == IP_EVENT && event_id == IP_EVENT_STA_GOT_IP`) and add a finite wait/cleanup path so a broken event flow cannot deadlock initialization forever. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_sdk_mem_lib.c` and `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/README.md` did not surface a stronger independent issue in this pass beyond the AES lock-lifecycle bugs and WiFi helper credential/event-flow problems above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `esp_sdk_mem_lib.c` is mostly diagnostic memory-map logging and allocator glue; this pass did not establish a clearer exploit path from it than the chunk’s concrete AES and WiFi issues. `README.md` was build/usage documentation only.

### Medium - `--rpk` in the sample client disables all peer authentication instead of configuring trusted raw-public-key verification - location: `examples/client/client.c:3437-3447,3702-3703,4204` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: operator copies the example client or runs it with `--rpk` expecting authenticated TLS raw public keys. Active network attacker can present any raw public key (or any cert if RPK negotiation falls back) because verification is turned off. - evidence: the `useRPK` branch only sets client/server cert types to `WOLFSSL_CERT_TYPE_RPK` and then forces `doPeerCheck = 0`; later `doPeerCheck == 0` maps to `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE, NULL)`, and hostname checks are skipped because `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` is gated on `doPeerCheck`. No trust store or pinned peer key is configured in the RPK path. - impact: the example's advertised RPK mode is effectively unauthenticated TLS, enabling trivial MITM when reused as integration code. - recommendation: keep verification enabled in RPK mode and require an explicit trusted peer raw public key / trust store setup; if authenticated RPK is not implemented here, fail `--rpk` instead of silently dropping to `VERIFY_NONE`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - DTLS-SRTP test mode prints exported SRTP keying material directly to stdout - location: `examples/client/client.c:1987-2008,4583-4594` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: example client built with `WOLFSSL_SRTP` and run with `--srtp`; stdout/stderr is captured by logs, CI, terminal history, or a less-privileged observer. - evidence: `client_srtp_test()` calls `wolfSSL_export_dtls_srtp_keying_material()` and then unconditionally prints every byte with `printf("DTLS SRTP: Exported key material: "); ... printf("%02X", *p);`. The function is invoked on live sessions whenever `dtlsSrtpProfiles != NULL`. - impact: session keying material is exposed outside the TLS boundary, allowing anyone with log access to derive SRTP traffic keys and decrypt or forge that session's media. - recommendation: never print exported keying material in normal examples; restrict this to an explicit test-only debug path redacted by default, or compare secrets in-memory without logging them. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V16 Security Logging and Error Handling`

### Medium - non-blocking post-handshake I/O busy-spins for up to 10 seconds because the helper loops retry `wolfSSL_read`/`write` without any readiness wait - location: `examples/client/client.c:1039-1061,1074-1119,1140-1164,4213,4604` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `-N` sets the socket non-blocking (`tcp_set_nonblocking(&sockfd)`), then normal data exchange still goes through `ClientWriteRead()`. `ClientWrite()` loops on `WOLFSSL_ERROR_WANT_WRITE/READ` (and async pending) with no `tcp_select`, sleep, or backoff. `ClientRead()` likewise loops on `WANT_READ/WANT_WRITE`, only checking elapsed wall time until `MAX_NON_BLOCK_SEC`. Only the DTLS branch in `ClientWriteRead()` performs `tcp_select()`. - impact: a peer that stops reading or delays app data can pin a core in a tight retry loop for up to 10 seconds per operation, making the example's non-blocking pattern expensive to reuse and easy to turn into request-driven CPU burn. - recommendation: mirror `NonBlockingSSL_Connect()` and wait on socket readiness before retrying app-data reads/writes; keep one non-blocking helper with explicit select/poll semantics instead of separate busy-loop wrappers. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - the remaining chunk-local project files are mostly packaging/build scaffolding; no stronger repo-specific issue was promoted from `examples/client/client.h`, `client.vcxproj`, `client.vcproj`, `client.sln`, or `include.am` beyond the example-client authentication/logging/spin issues above - location: `examples/client/client.h`, `examples/client/client.vcxproj`, `examples/client/client.vcproj`, `examples/client/client.sln`, `examples/client/include.am` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found legacy Visual Studio metadata and routine example build wiring, but this pass did not establish a clearer exploit path or material operational failure mode there than the concrete `client.c` findings appended above.

### High - SE050 AES offload self-deadlocks on every key setup by re-locking the global hardware mutex inside `se050_aes_set_key()` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/nxp/se050_port.c:386-396,129-154`; supporting mutex wrapper `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:1777-1808` and common non-recursive init `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:2093,2119` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_SE050_CRYPT && !NO_AES` build using the SE050 AES path; remotely reachable anywhere attacker input causes AES key setup (for example normal TLS/session encryption setup). - evidence: `se050_aes_set_key()` acquires `wolfSSL_CryptHwMutexLock()` and then immediately calls `wc_se050_erase_object(aes->keyId)`; `wc_se050_erase_object()` also calls `wolfSSL_CryptHwMutexLock()`. The global lock is `wcCryptHwMutex`, and common builds initialize ordinary mutexes with `pthread_mutex_init(m, NULL)` then lock with `pthread_mutex_lock(m)`, so the second lock attempt deadlocks the same thread. - impact: the first SE050 AES key setup can hang the calling thread/process indefinitely, giving a trivial availability break for any service that enables SE050 AES offload. - recommendation: never call `wc_se050_erase_object()` while already holding the hardware mutex; split out an internal unlocked erase helper or move erase before the outer lock and audit all nested `wolfSSL_CryptHwMutexLock()` call chains. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### High - multiple SE050 APIs return while still holding the global crypto-hardware mutex, so one error or documented size-query call can permanently wedge all later hardware crypto in-process - location: representative `wolfcrypt/src/port/nxp/se050_port.c:571-577` (`wc_se050_insert_binary_object` bad key ID), `625-645` (`wc_se050_get_binary_object` `out == NULL` length-only path and too-small buffer path), `1173-1180` (`se050_rsa_sign` unsupported pad/hash), `1328-1335`, `1516-1523`, `1674-1681`, `1873-1879` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: SE050 build; any caller hits these validation/error branches. The `wc_se050_get_binary_object(..., NULL, &outSz)` path is explicitly documented in the function comment as a supported length query. - evidence: each function acquires `wolfSSL_CryptHwMutexLock()` and then has direct `return BAD_FUNC_ARG`, `return BAD_LENGTH_E`, or `return WC_NO_ERR_TRACE(LENGTH_ONLY_E)` branches before `wolfSSL_CryptHwMutexUnLock()`. Because the lock is process-global (`wcCryptHwMutex`), the leaked lock blocks every later SE050/DCP/LTC user sharing that mutex. - impact: a single malformed request or legitimate length-only query can deadlock subsequent hardware-backed crypto operations until process restart. - recommendation: route all exits through one cleanup label that always unlocks; add regression tests for every early-return branch after lock acquisition, especially the documented `LENGTH_ONLY_E` path. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Medium - i.MX RT DCP `wc_Sha256GetHash()` / `wc_ShaGetHash()` report success even when the hardware finish step fails, returning zeroed or invalid digests to callers - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/nxp/dcp_port.c:353-369,465-481` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_IMXRT_DCP` build and `DCP_HASH_Finish()` fails or returns the wrong digest length (hardware fault, driver error, bad state). - evidence: both functions zero the output buffer, call `DCP_HASH_Finish()`, set `ret = WC_HW_E` on failure/mismatched `outlen`, then unconditionally `return 0;` instead of returning `ret`. - impact: callers cannot detect hash-generation failure and may accept all-zero or otherwise invalid digests as if hashing succeeded, undermining integrity checks built on these snapshot APIs. - recommendation: return `ret`, not constant success; add tests that inject failing `DCP_HASH_Finish()` / wrong-length responses and assert non-zero error returns with no digest use. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - ordinary SE050 sign/verify/decrypt/shared-secret calls silently persist caller-supplied software keys as long-lived SE050 objects, changing key lifecycle and retention without explicit opt-in - location: entry points `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c:3393,3549`, `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:6493,9327`, `wolfcrypt/src/ed25519.c:371`; persistent import sinks `wolfcrypt/src/port/nxp/se050_port.c:1217-1252,1382-1438,1563-1595,1728-1760,2054-2114,2226-2285,2623-2681,2858-2895`; related docs only discuss explicit insertion helpers and non-auto-delete of generated keys at `wolfcrypt/src/port/nxp/README_SE050.md:394` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses SE050-backed RSA/ECC/Ed25519 ops with an in-memory key whose `keyIdSet == 0`. - evidence: these paths export the caller key to DER, allocate an SE050 object with `kKeyObject_Mode_Persistent`, store the key, then set `key->keyId` / `key->keyIdSet = 1` on success; there is no success-path erase. This happens inside normal crypto operations, not just the explicit `wc_se050_*_insert_*` helpers. - impact: private or public keys that callers expected to remain transient in process memory become persistent SE050 objects reusable by later code with SE050 access; this also accumulates opaque objects and can exhaust limited secure-element storage. - recommendation: make persistent import explicit-only via the dedicated insert APIs; for ad-hoc acceleration use transient objects or auto-erase imported keys after the operation unless the caller explicitly opts into retention. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - STM32 sample config silently compiles out X.509 time validation on boards without RTC support, so TLS can accept expired/not-yet-valid certificates - location: `examples/configs/user_settings_stm32.h:38-41,800-801`, usage docs `examples/configs/README.md:34-36`, validation gates `src/x509.c:7129,9883`, `src/internal.c:10272`, `src/dtls13.c:1574` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator follows the documented `copy ... rename to user_settings.h` workflow and uses the STM32 sample on a target without `HAL_RTC_MODULE_ENABLED` (common on smaller boards) while doing TLS peer authentication. - evidence: the sample enables TLS 1.2/1.3 by default, then unconditionally `#define NO_ASN_TIME` when the RTC HAL module is absent; core cert and handshake date checks are compiled only under `#ifndef NO_ASN_TIME`. - impact: remote peers can present expired or not-yet-valid certificates and still pass chain validation, defeating a basic PKI freshness control on copied example builds. - recommendation: do not auto-enable `NO_ASN_TIME` in a TLS-capable sample; require a user-supplied time source / explicit insecure override, or fail closed with a loud build/runtime warning until time is available. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - ESP-IDF/PlatformIO example configs force `SINGLE_THREADED` even though the shipped ESP-IDF examples run wolfSSL in FreeRTOS tasks, disabling internal mutexes in a common multi-task deployment model - location: `examples/configs/user_settings_espressif.h:365`, `examples/configs/user_settings_platformio.h:80`, `examples/configs/user_settings_arduino.h:245,254`; RTOS task use `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_client/main/client-tls.c:618`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_server/main/server-tls.c:453`; mutexs compiled out under `src/internal.c:7657-7732,39994-40537`, `src/ssl.c:738-900,2442-2446` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: user copies one of these sample headers into a FreeRTOS/ESP-IDF/Arduino app and calls wolfSSL from more than one task, or shares contexts/caches/keys across tasks. - evidence: the configs hard-define `SINGLE_THREADED`; the example apps themselves create TLS tasks with `xTaskCreate(...)`; core library mutex protection for heap state, key contexts, and dup-write state is guarded by `#ifndef SINGLE_THREADED`. - impact: a sample meant for RTOS targets silently removes concurrency protection, creating race-prone builds that can manifest as memory corruption, handshake instability, or nondeterministic failures once real applications use multiple tasks. - recommendation: stop enabling `SINGLE_THREADED` in RTOS-oriented default samples; keep it only for explicitly single-task benchmarks/tests and add a compile-time warning when it is combined with ESP-IDF/FreeRTOS builds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - shipped PlatformIO sample header is syntactically broken by an unterminated/misaligned comment block, so the documented copy-to-`user_settings.h` workflow fails before build - location: `examples/configs/user_settings_platformio.h:23-35`, shipped via `examples/configs/include.am:17`, usage docs `examples/configs/README.md:34-36` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the file opens `/* This is a sample PlatformIO user_settings.h for wolfSSL` at line 23, opens another `/*` on line 24, closes only once on line 26, then leaves stray `*` lines plus an orphan `*/` after `#include "sdkconfig.h"`; this is not valid preprocessor input. - impact: PlatformIO users following the published example path get a broken config file instead of a working starting point, creating avoidable integration failures and making later config drift harder to reason about. - recommendation: fix the comment delimiters, remove the stray text after `#include`, and add a CI smoke test that preprocesses/compiles every distributed `examples/configs/user_settings_*.h` as a standalone `user_settings.h`. - reference: `grugbrain: boring code`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`
