### Reviewed - remaining config examples in this chunk were mostly feature bundles or known-unsafe test/example knobs, and this pass did not promote a stronger new issue beyond the STM32 time-check disable, RTOS `SINGLE_THREADED` trap, and broken PlatformIO header - location: `examples/configs/{user_settings_all.h,user_settings_openssl_compat.h,user_settings_wolftpm.h,user_settings_wolfssh.h,user_settings_rsa_only.h,user_settings_wolfboot_keytools.h,user_settings_fipsv5.h,user_settings_pq.h,user_settings_baremetal.h,user_settings_pkcs7.h,user_settings_ca.h,user_settings_dtls13.h,user_settings_tls13.h,user_settings_tls12.h,user_settings_fipsv2.h,user_settings_eccnonblock.h,user_settings_min_ecc.h,user_settings_curve25519nonblock.h,user_settings_EBSnet.h}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: most of these files are example feature selectors with expected tradeoffs; sharper edges like `HAVE_NULL_CIPHER` in `user_settings_all.h` or `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST` in parts of the STM32 family map to issues already tracked elsewhere rather than a stronger new chunk-local finding.

### Medium - C# `EciesSetInfo()` frees the KDF-info buffer immediately after a native setter that only stores a borrowed pointer, so later ECIES encrypt/decrypt uses dangling memory - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/wolfCrypt.cs:1312-1334`; native store/sink `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:13874-13880,14313-14329,14718-14734`; context free `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:14088-14093` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: managed caller uses `EciesSetInfo(ctx, info)` and then later calls `EciesEncrypt()`/`EciesDecrypt()` on that context; no unsafe code is required. - evidence: the wrapper allocates `infoPtr`, copies the managed `byte[]`, calls `wc_ecc_ctx_set_info(ctx, infoPtr, info.Length)`, then unconditionally `Marshal.FreeHGlobal(infoPtr)` in `finally`. The native setter only assigns `ctx->kdfInfo = info; ctx->kdfInfoSz = (word32)sz;` and later passes that pointer into `wc_HKDF` / `wc_X963_KDF`; `wc_ecc_ctx_free()` frees only `ctx`, not a copied `kdfInfo` buffer. - impact: safe C# code creates a use-after-free read in native KDF processing, causing crashes or deriving ECIES keys from stale/reallocated heap bytes instead of the caller-provided shared-info. - recommendation: make the native setter copy `info` into ctx-owned storage, or keep the managed buffer pinned/alive for the entire `ecEncCtx` lifetime and release it only when the context is destroyed. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### High - `Ed25519InitKey()` allocates only 64 bytes for a native `ed25519_key` and then calls `wc_ed25519_init()` on it - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/wolfCrypt.cs:2168-2182`, native layout `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ed25519.h:84-98` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: any C# consumer calls `Ed25519InitKey(out key)`; no malformed input or unsafe code is required. - evidence: the wrapper does `key = Marshal.AllocHGlobal(ED25519_SIG_SIZE)` where `ED25519_SIG_SIZE = 64`, then `wc_ed25519_init(key)`. The native struct already contains `p[32]` and `k[64]` before flags/metadata (`ed25519.h:85-86,97-98`), so the buffer is undersized before init starts. - impact: safe C# API use can trigger native heap corruption/crash during Ed25519 key initialization. - recommendation: stop hand-allocating raw key memory; use `wc_ed25519_new()`/`wc_ed25519_delete()` or a correct managed interop struct size. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### High - raw Curve25519 import wrappers pass the caller's key bytes as both input and `curve25519_key*`, so native import writes into an undersized/non-initialized object - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/wolfCrypt.cs:2606-2647`; API contract `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/curve25519.h:236,257`; native key layout `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/curve25519.h:118,124-125,136,148-149` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: any C# consumer calls `Curve25519ImportPrivateKey()` or `Curve25519ImportPublicKey()`; normal 32-byte X25519 keys are enough. - evidence: both wrappers `AllocHGlobal(<key>.Length)` and then call `wc_curve25519_import_private(key, privateKey.Length, key)` / `wc_curve25519_import_public(key, publicKey.Length, key)`. But the native API expects raw bytes plus a separate `curve25519_key*`; that struct includes an `ECPoint p`, `byte k[32]`, `WC_RNG*`, and flags, which is far larger than the input buffer. - impact: safe C# callers can trigger native memory corruption and crashes when importing raw X25519 keys. - recommendation: allocate/init a real `curve25519_key` with `wc_curve25519_new()`/`wc_curve25519_init()`, pass the managed byte buffer only as the input parameter, and free via the matching native destructor. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### High - `EciesGetOwnSalt()` frees a borrowed pointer returned by wolfCrypt, causing invalid free / heap corruption in the C# wrapper - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/wolfCrypt.cs:1160-1194`; native getter `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:13843,13851,13861` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: any C# consumer calls `EciesGetOwnSalt()` on a valid ECIES context. - evidence: the wrapper copies from `saltPtr = wc_ecc_ctx_get_own_salt(ctx)` and then unconditionally `Marshal.FreeHGlobal(saltPtr)` in `finally`. The native getter returns `ctx->clientSalt` or `ctx->serverSalt`, i.e. internal context-owned storage, not a caller-owned allocation. - impact: a safe wrapper call can free an interior/native-owned pointer, corrupting the process heap or crashing later ECIES operations. - recommendation: treat the return as borrowed memory: copy the 16 bytes out and never free the returned pointer. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - 5-argument `AesGcmEncrypt()` recurses into itself instead of the real implementation, so the no-AAD path dies with `StackOverflowException` - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/wolfCrypt.cs:2828-2888` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the overload `AesGcmEncrypt(IntPtr aes, byte[] iv, byte[] plaintext, byte[] ciphertext, byte[] authTag)` returns `AesGcmEncrypt(aes, iv, plaintext, ciphertext, null);`, which matches its own 5-parameter signature, not the 6-parameter method that actually calls `wc_AesGcmEncrypt` at `:2856`. - impact: any wrapper consumer encrypting AES-GCM without AAD hits an uncatchable stack overflow and process termination instead of encryption. - recommendation: forward to the 6-parameter overload with `authTag` preserved and `addAuth = null`, and add a regression for the no-AAD overload. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - TLS socket send callback ignores short writes and always tells wolfSSL the full record was sent - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/wolfSSL.cs:838-869` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `wolfSSLCbIOSend()` only checks whether `con.Send(...) == 0`, but otherwise unconditionally `return sz;`; it never returns the actual byte count from `Socket.Send`. - impact: under TCP backpressure or nonblocking/timeout behavior, wolfSSL can advance as if a full TLS record was transmitted when only a prefix was written, leading to handshake failures, record corruption, and hard-to-reproduce connection resets. - recommendation: return the real send count, or loop until all bytes are written / return the proper WANT_WRITE error. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - no stronger standalone issue was promoted from `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/X509.cs` or `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp.csproj` beyond the wrapper ownership and API-footgun bugs above - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/X509.cs`, `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp.csproj` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass did not find a clearer trust-boundary break in `X509.cs` than the C# wrapper memory/ownership bugs already noted, and the project file mostly contains build settings plus pre-build copying of example cert files.

### Medium - certificate-generation APIs default RSA signatures to SHA-1 in common builds, so callers who rely on `wc_InitCert()` defaults can mint weak or rejected certificates/CSRs - location: `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:29163-29165,35107-35116`; misleading docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/asn_public.h:5,533-540` / public header `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/asn_public.h:525-536` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses `wc_InitCert()` / `wc_InitCert_ex()` and then signs with `wc_MakeSelfCert()` or `wc_SignCert()` without explicitly overriding `cert->sigType`; build has SHA-1 enabled (`!NO_SHA`). - evidence: `wc_InitCert_ex()` sets `cert->sigType = CTC_SHAwRSA;` with only a fallback to `CTC_SHA256wRSA` when SHA-1 is compiled out, and `wc_MakeSelfCert()` immediately signs with `wc_SignCert(cert->bodySz, cert->sigType, ...)`. The docs still describe the default as `SHA_WITH_RSA`, reinforcing reliance on the insecure default. - impact: newly generated certificates or requests can be SHA-1 signed by default, weakening collision resistance and causing modern PKI/tooling to reject the output or silently downgrade private PKI security. - recommendation: switch the default RSA cert signature to SHA-256 or stronger in `wc_InitCert_ex()`, require explicit opt-in for SHA-1, and update the docs/examples to stop advertising the legacy default. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `wc_SetCustomExtension()` stores borrowed OID/value pointers in `Cert` without copying, so later certificate generation can dereference freed or mutated caller memory - location: public API docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/asn_public.h:3790-3837`; stored pointer fields `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/asn_public.h:349-353,504,636`; sink `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:31867-31885,35604-35634` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application passes stack, temporary, or mutable buffers to `wc_SetCustomExtension()` and generates/signs the certificate after those buffers are freed, reused, or modified. - evidence: `wc_SetCustomExtension()` does `ext->oid = (char*)oid; ext->val = (byte*)der; ext->valSz = (int)derSz; cert->customCertExtCount++;` into the `Cert.customCertExt[NUM_CUSTOM_EXT]` array; later cert encoding iterates `customCertExt[i]` and feeds the saved pointers to OID encoding and `SetASN_Buffer(...)`. The doc comment explicitly warns the function `does NOT copy` and the pointed-to data must not change until generation completes. - impact: a normal multi-step cert-building flow can turn a stale pointer or post-call mutation into crashes, out-of-bounds reads, or issuance of certificates with attacker-corrupted extension contents. - recommendation: deep-copy OID/value data into `Cert` state (or add an owned API that does), keep stored pointers `const`, and leave the zero-copy variant explicit/unsafe if it must remain for compatibility. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - this `doc/dox_comments/header_files/asn_public.h` chunk mostly mirrored public ASN/cert APIs; the strongest chunk-local issues promoted were the default SHA-1 cert-signing behavior and the zero-copy custom-extension lifetime hazard above - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files/asn_public.h` with implementation in `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c` / `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/asn_public.h` - category: security review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining content was predominantly API documentation/examples for existing encode/decode helpers; spot checks did not show a stronger new exploit path than the two persisted certificate-generation issues above.

### High - MAXQ10XX secure-element key import falls back to a repo-shipped test private key when `MAXQ10XX_PRODUCTION_KEY` is omitted - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/maxim/maxq10xx.c:135-150,313-320,631-656,783-811,896-924` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: MAXQ1065/MAXQ108X build ships without `MAXQ10XX_PRODUCTION_KEY`; the device trusts the matching import public key object `PUBKEY_IMPORT_OBJID` (`0x1000`) for `MXQ_ImportKey`. - evidence: absent the build macro, the port compiles a static `KeyPairImport[]` under the comment `/* TEST KEY. This must be changed for production environments!! */`; `LoadDefaultImportKey()` copies that key, and every AES/ECC provisioning path signs `key_buff` with it before calling `MXQ_ImportKey(..., PUBKEY_IMPORT_OBJID, ...)`. - impact: anyone who can recover the shipped firmware/source gets the import private key used to authorize persistent MAXQ key import, allowing forged AES/ECC key blobs to be accepted by affected devices and defeating the secure-element import root. - recommendation: remove the built-in test private key from production code, fail build/runtime when no production import key is provisioned, and require a device- or product-unique import root provisioning step. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - MAXQ108X TLS 1.3 hardware callbacks keep session secrets and key selection in file-global state, so overlapping connections can clobber each other's active hardware objects - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/maxim/maxq10xx.c:106-131,167-181,2577-2671,3093-3675,3753-3818,3822-3870` - category: security/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_MAXQ108X` TLS 1.3 build handles more than one connection/context over time or concurrently. - evidence: TLS 1.3 object IDs, handshake/application key IDs, PSK/handshake mode flags, HMAC state, and `tls13active` are all `static` globals (`tls13_*`, `local_is_psk`, `is_hs_key`, `mac_key_obj_id`, `mac_comp_active`); `maxq10xx_perform_tls13_record_processing()` and HMAC/HKDF callbacks select keys directly from those globals; `maxq10xx_SetupPkCallbacks()` also gates setup behind the global `init_pk_callbacks`. - impact: one handshake can free/reassign temp key IDs or flip key-selection state used by another connection, causing wrong-key record protection/MACs, handshake failures, and hard-to-debug cross-session corruption on threaded or multi-context deployments. - recommendation: move TLS 1.3 state into per-`WOLFSSL`/per-callback context, remove the process-global one-time init gate, and reset/free per-session object IDs at handshake teardown. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### High - MAX3266X MAA zero-pad helper dereferences `exp` when it is NULL and compares the wrong memory for zero checks, making hardware `mp_*mod` paths crashy/unreliable - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/maxim/max3266x.c:1276-1358,1464-1495,1544-1646`; macro mapping `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/wolfmath.h:162-167` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `MAX3266X_MATH` build reaches hardware modular arithmetic (for example RSA/ECC operations using `mp_mod`, `mp_addmod`, `mp_submod`, `mp_mulmod`, `mp_sqrmod`, or `mp_exptmod`). - evidence: `wc_MXC_MAA_zeroPad()` is called with `exp == NULL` from non-EXP wrappers such as `wc_MXC_MAA_mulmod`, `wc_MXC_MAA_sqrmod`, `wc_MXC_MAA_addmod`, and `wc_MXC_MAA_submod`, but the shared `SQ/MUL/SQMUL/ADD/SUB` case still executes `XMEMCMP(zero_tmp, mod, (exp->used*sizeof(mp_digit)))`. The EXP case also checks `XMEMCMP(zero_tmp, exp, ...)` / `XMEMCMP(zero_tmp, mod, ...)` against the `mp_int` structs instead of `exp->dp` / `mod->dp`. `wolfmath.h` maps the generic `mp_*mod` APIs onto these hardware wrappers. - impact: request-driven cryptographic operations on MAX3266X can crash the process on non-exponentiation math and can mis-handle zero-operand validation on exponentiation/mod paths, breaking RSA/ECC correctness and availability. - recommendation: use `exp->dp` / `mod->dp` with the correct operand lengths, never touch `exp` outside the EXP path, and add regressions for every `mp_*mod` wrapper. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - MAX3266X SHA hardware path buffers the entire message in heap before hashing, turning streaming hashes into O(n) memory and copy overhead - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/maxim/max3266x.c:139-158,723-834,863-1178`; docs `wolfcrypt/src/port/maxim/README.md:87-89` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: update paths call `_wc_Hash_Grow()` to append each chunk into `sha->{msg,used,len}`; Final calls `wc_MXC_TPU_SHA_GetHash(*msg, *used, ...)` once over the accumulated buffer and only then frees it. The README explicitly states the SDK currently expects the whole message buffer. - impact: large or concurrent hashes incur full-message heap copies, realloc churn, and copy-on-copy behavior in `wc_MXC_TPU_SHA_Copy()`, which can wipe out any TPU speedup and trigger memory pressure on constrained MAX3266x systems. - recommendation: restrict the hardware path to bounded one-shot inputs, or implement chunked/streaming hashing and fall back to software once buffering would exceed a safe threshold. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - AES AEAD decrypt examples contradict their own failure contract and omit the required output zeroization, encouraging reuse of unauthenticated plaintext - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files/aes.h:420-455,1141-1176,1257-1306,1496,1561,1687` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: downstream developers copy these documented decrypt flows into applications; attacker can submit forged GCM/SIV/EAX ciphertexts or tags. - evidence: the docs explicitly warn that on decrypt failure `the output data is undefined` and callers must `unconditionally zeroize` it (`aes.h:420,1141,1257`), but the adjacent examples either ignore the return entirely (`wc_AesGcmDecrypt(...);` at `:455`) or branch on failure without clearing `out` (`wc_AesSivDecrypt(...)` at `:1173`; `wc_AesEaxDecryptFinal(...)` examples at `:1496,:1561,:1687`). For EAX the real one-shot API is `wc_AesEaxDecryptAuth` (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:845`), and its implementation decrypts before the auth verdict via `wc_AesEaxDecryptUpdate` then `wc_AesEaxDecryptFinal` (`wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:16703,16707`). - impact: copied integrations can retain, log, or act on attacker-controlled plaintext remnants after tag failure instead of scrubbing them, turning bad-tag traffic into application-level data confusion/leakage. - recommendation: fix every AEAD decrypt example to check the return and zeroize the plaintext buffer on any nonzero result; align the one-shot EAX example with `wc_AesEaxDecryptAuth`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - AES doxygen header drifted from the real API, with nonexistent EAX symbols, duplicate CTS declarations, and error-handling examples that discard real return codes - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files/aes.h:23,315,350,1230,1293,1770,1805` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: docs call `wc_AesEaxEncrypt` / `wc_AesEaxDecrypt` (`:1230,:1293`), but the public header exports `wc_AesEaxEncryptAuth` / `wc_AesEaxDecryptAuth` (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:837,845`); `wc_AesCtsEncrypt` is declared twice in the docs header (`:1770,:1805`) while the real header has one declaration (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:885`); multiple examples use `if (ret = wc_AesInit(...) != 0)` (`:23,:315,:350`), so `ret` becomes a boolean instead of the actual wolfCrypt error code. - impact: copy-paste consumers hit compile failures or lose the underlying error code, and the generated AES docs become audit-hostile because they no longer reliably describe the shipped API. - recommendation: derive these docs from the real public declarations/examples or add CI that diff-checks `doc/dox_comments/header_files/aes.h` against `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h` and compile-tests documented snippets. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - DTLS multicast example mode uses a null cipher plus hard-coded shared secrets, so any party that can reach the multicast group can decrypt and forge traffic - location: `examples/server/server.c:2317-2318,3221-3223,3310-3322` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: remove this runtime mode from the generic example path, or gate it behind an explicit test-only compile flag and require externally supplied per-deployment secrets/nonces instead of fixed constants and `WDM-NULL-SHA256`. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: operator enables multicast mode with `-3`; attacker can join or observe that multicast segment. - evidence: the `-3` option sets `doMcast`/`mcastID`; the server then forces cipher list `"WDM-NULL-SHA256"` and seeds `wolfSSL_set_secret()` with fixed buffers `pms=0x23...`, `cr=0xA5...`, `sr=0x5A...` under the comment `/* DTLS multicast secret for testing only */`. - impact: there is no per-session secret here; anyone with the code or one packet capture can derive the same traffic keys and inject or read multicast traffic.

### Medium - SRTP test mode prints exported DTLS-SRTP keying material in cleartext, leaking live media keys to logs/terminals - location: `examples/server/server.c:1520-1554,3786-3796` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V16 Security Logging and Error Handling` - recommendation: never print SRTP exporter output outside dedicated test binaries; redact it or guard the dump behind a compile-time debug macro that is off by default. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: server is run with `--srtp` and stdout/stderr or captured logs are accessible to another user/process/log pipeline. - evidence: `server_srtp_test()` calls `wolfSSL_export_dtls_srtp_keying_material()` and immediately hex-dumps the entire secret with `printf("DTLS SRTP: Exported key material: "); ... printf("%02X", *p);`; the function is invoked after handshake when `dtlsSrtpProfiles != NULL`. - impact: anyone who can read the logs can decrypt or forge the corresponding SRTP/SRTCP traffic.

### Low - Win32 Visual Studio example project explicitly disables `/SAFESEH`, weakening exploit mitigation for x86 Windows builds of the server example - location: `examples/server/server.vcxproj:245,266,360` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: enable safe exception handlers for supported x86 toolchains, or document and isolate the exception if a specific object file truly prevents `/SAFESEH`. - status: candidate - severity: low - exploitability/preconditions: the example is built for 32-bit Windows with this project file and some memory-corruption bug is reachable. - evidence: the project sets `<ImageHasSafeExceptionHandlers>false</ImageHasSafeExceptionHandlers>` in Win32 configurations instead of keeping the linker mitigation enabled. - impact: x86 exception-handler hijacking remains available, making memory-corruption bugs in the example or linked code easier to turn into code execution.

### Reviewed - `examples/server/server.h`, `examples/server/include.am`, `examples/server/server.vcproj`, and `examples/server/server.sln` did not expose a stronger chunk-local issue than the multicast fixed-secret mode, SRTP key dump, and x86 `/SAFESEH` hardening gap above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass mainly found build/distribution scaffolding and declarations; the concrete repo-specific problems in this chunk were concentrated in `server.c` runtime test modes and the newer Win32 project hardening settings.

### High - safe Ada wrappers pass caller-controlled lengths into raw byte-pointer wolfCrypt APIs without checking Ada array bounds, so safe callers can trigger native out-of-bounds reads/writes - location: `wrapper/Ada/wolfssl.ads:546-552,574-592,707-730`, `wrapper/Ada/wolfssl.adb:969-1005,1089-1137,1443-1529`; sinks `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/pwdbased.h:56`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:536-543`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/rsa.h:390-397` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: any Ada caller passes `Key_Length`/`Length`/`Size` larger than the actual `Byte_Array` objects to `PBKDF2`, `AES_Set_Key`, `AES_Set_Cbc_Encrypt`, `AES_Set_Cbc_Decrypt`, or the RSA key-decode helpers; no `Unchecked_Conversion` or other unsafe escape hatch is needed. - evidence: the public Ada APIs keep explicit size parameters and only require `Is_Valid(...)` on the crypto object. The implementations forward `int (Key_Length/Length/Size)` directly into `wc_PBKDF2`, `wc_AesSetKey`, `wc_AesCbcEncrypt/Decrypt`, `wc_RsaPublicKeyDecode`, and `wc_RsaPrivateKeyDecode` while passing Ada arrays as the backing buffers. The native APIs only see raw pointers plus the supplied lengths, so they cannot enforce the original Ada bounds. - impact: safe Ada can drive native out-of-bounds writes (`PBKDF2`, `AES_Set_Cbc_*`) or reads (`AES_Set_Key`, `Rsa_*_Key_Decode`), causing memory corruption, crashes, or parsing of adjacent memory. - recommendation: remove redundant caller-supplied length arguments and derive them from `'Length`; otherwise reject any `Size > Input'Length`, `Size > Output'Length`, `Length > Key'Length`, `Key_Length > Output'Length`, and enforce exact IV/key-format invariants before calling C. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### High - Ada `Rsa_Set_RNG` stores a borrowed `WC_RNG*` inside `RsaKey`, but `Free_RNG` can free it first, leaving later RSA operations with a dangling pointer - location: `wrapper/Ada/wolfssl.adb:847-858,1013-1026,1146-1234`, `wrapper/Ada/ada_binding.c:185-203`; sink `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c:3801,3937,5271-5276` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: safe Ada code calls `Rsa_Set_RNG(RSA, RNG)`, later `Free_RNG(RNG)`, then uses the RSA key for an operation that consumes `key->rng` such as `Rsa_SSL_Sign` or `RSA_Public_Encrypt`. - evidence: `ada_binding.c` forwards `ada_RsaSetRNG` to `wc_RsaSetRNG`, and `wc_RsaSetRNG` stores `key->rng = rng` in `rsa.c:5271-5276`. `Free_RNG` calls `wc_rng_free` and only nulls the Ada handle, not the pointer cached inside the RSA key. Later RSA paths load `rng = key->rng` from the key (`rsa.c:3801`, `:3937`). - impact: the safe Ada binding permits use-after-free in later RSA operations, leading to crashes or reuse of stale heap contents during cryptographic operations. - recommendation: make RNG attachment own or refcount the RNG for at least the RSA key lifetime, provide a detach/clear step before `Free_RNG`, or redesign the API so RNG borrowing cannot outlive the call that uses it. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - doc HTML theming/menu files and the remaining Ada build/runtime scaffolding in this chunk did not surface a stronger standalone issue than the Ada FFI length and RNG-lifetime hazards above - location: `doc/formats/html/html_changes/{customdoxygen.css,doxygen.css,menu.js,menudata.js,tabs.css}`, `wrapper/Ada/{wolfssl-full_runtime.adb,wolfssl-full_runtime.ads,README.md,alire.toml,include.am,wolfssl.gpr,default.gpr,restricted.adc,.gitignore,user_settings.h}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the HTML changes are mostly static Doxygen styling/menu data. The DTLS peer helper copies the caller socket address into wolfSSL-owned storage through `wolfSSL_dtls_set_peer`/`SockAddrSet` (`src/ssl.c:1252-1298`), so this pass did not confirm a stack-pointer lifetime bug there. The remaining files are packaging/build metadata or feature bundles and did not expose a clearer trust-boundary break in this chunk.

### Medium - legacy `wc_DsaSign()` / `wc_DsaVerify()` silently hardcode a 20-byte digest, so callers using SHA-256+ DSA only sign/verify the first 160 bits - location: public API docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/dsa.h` (`wc_DsaSign`, `wc_DsaVerify`); implementation `wolfcrypt/src/dsa.c:662,1003` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses the older non-`_ex` DSA APIs with any digest longer than SHA-1, especially 2048/3072-bit DSA where a 256-bit hash is expected. - evidence: `wc_DsaSign()` is just `return wc_DsaSign_ex(digest, WC_SHA_DIGEST_SIZE, out, key, rng);` and `wc_DsaVerify()` is just `return wc_DsaVerify_ex(digest, WC_SHA_DIGEST_SIZE, sig, key, answer);`, while the docs describe a generic `digest` pointer without warning that only 20 bytes are consumed. - impact: callers can think they are authenticating a SHA-256 digest when the implementation only covers the first 20 bytes, silently downgrading signature strength and causing protocol/interoperability surprises that are hard to spot in review. - recommendation: either deprecate these APIs as SHA-1-only with loud docs/runtime guards, or make them reject non-SHA-1 use and steer callers to `wc_DsaSign_ex()` / `wc_DsaVerify_ex()` with explicit digest length. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - DSA PEM private-key export still defaults to MD5 + PBKDF1 with 1 iteration, making password-protected private keys cheap to crack offline - location: DSA PEM export path `src/pk.c:2115-2116`; KDF/encryption sink `wolfcrypt/src/wc_encrypt.c:261-276`; related public docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/wc_encrypt.h`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files/pwdbased.h` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application exports an encrypted DSA private key through the legacy PEM-writing path (for example `wolfSSL_PEM_write_mem_DSAPrivateKey`) and an attacker obtains the PEM file. - evidence: `wolfSSL_PEM_write_mem_DSAPrivateKey()` passes `WC_MD5` into `EncryptDerKey(...)` at `src/pk.c:2115-2116`; the legacy PKCS#5 branch in `wc_BufferKeyEncrypt()` derives the key with `wc_PBKDF1(key, password, passwordSz, info->iv, PKCS5_SALT_SZ, 1, (int)info->keySz, hashType)` before CBC encryption. - impact: encrypted exported keys rely on an obsolete single-iteration MD5-based KDF, so password guessing is far faster than users expect from a modern “encrypted private key” export. - recommendation: stop defaulting PEM private-key encryption to PKCS#5/PBKDF1+MD5; prefer PKCS#8 with PBKDF2/scrypt and a modern digest, and require explicit opt-in for legacy OpenSSL-compatible PEM encryption. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_SetGlobalHeapHint()` creates a process-global dangling heap pointer if the backing static heap is later unloaded, and the fallback allocator paths keep using it - location: API docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/memory.h`; setter/getter `wolfcrypt/src/memory.c:972-989`; fallback use in `XMALLOC/XFREE/XREALLOC` paths `:1011-1050,:1200-1233,:1347-1363`; unload path `:795-800` - category: security/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application sets a global heap hint for NULL-heap operations, then calls `wc_UnloadStaticMemory()` or rotates/reuses that heap while wolfSSL/wolfCrypt code still allocates with `heap == NULL`. - evidence: `globalHeapHint` is a static process-global pointer set by `wolfSSL_SetGlobalHeapHint()` and read by allocator fallbacks whenever `heap == NULL`; `wc_UnloadStaticMemory()` frees the heap mutex but does not clear or invalidate `globalHeapHint`. - impact: later allocations/frees can run against stale heap metadata or a destroyed mutex, causing crashes, allocator corruption, or hard-to-reason memory failures far from the original unload site. - recommendation: clear `globalHeapHint` during unload (or refuse unload while it is active), make the global hint lifecycle explicit and synchronized, and avoid mutable process-global allocator state for normal operation. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wolfSSL_SetAllocators()` mutates global allocator callbacks with no lifecycle or concurrency guard, so changing allocators after startup can free objects with a different heap than they were allocated from - location: API docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/memory.h`; global callback storage/update `wolfcrypt/src/memory.c:108-118`; callback use in alloc/free/realloc paths `:348-352,:397-444,:498-502,:1556-1649` - category: security/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application or plugin code calls `wolfSSL_SetAllocators()` after wolfSSL objects already exist, or races allocator replacement against active allocations/frees. - evidence: the library stores `malloc_function`, `free_function`, and `realloc_function` as writable static globals and rewrites them immediately in `wolfSSL_SetAllocators()`; later allocation and free paths dereference whatever callbacks are current at the time of each call. - impact: memory allocated under allocator A can later be reallocated/freed through allocator B, producing heap corruption, leaks, or crashes that are extremely non-local and difficult to debug. - recommendation: make allocator registration one-time pre-init only, or guard it with process-wide init state/locking and reject changes after the first wolfSSL allocation. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `doc/dox_comments/header_files/{random.h,cmac.h,blake2.h,sha256.h}` mostly mirrored existing public APIs in this pass; the chunk-specific concrete issues promoted were the DSA digest-length/API footgun, legacy PEM key-encryption defaults, and global memory-policy state hazards above - location: listed header-doc files with adjacent implementations in `wolfcrypt/src/{random.c,cmac.c,blake2b.c,blake2s.c,sha256.c,memory.c}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found mostly documentation/examples and thin wrappers over existing code paths; no stronger default-on exploit path surfaced here beyond the concrete DSA/PEM/memory-state issues appended above.

### Medium - `wolfSSL_iotsafe_on_ex()` copies caller-sized slot IDs into fixed-size `IOTSAFE` fields, so mismatched `id_size` corrupts stack memory - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/iotsafe/iotsafe.c:1571-1582`, field sizes in `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/iotsafe/iotsafe.h:79-97`, docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/iotsafe.h:30-31,117-118` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application calls the public IoT-Safe API with `id_size` larger than the build's compiled `IOTSAFE_ID_SIZE` width; the docs encourage this by advertising `wolfSSL_iotsafe_on_ex()` for IDs of "two or more bytes" while the struct only supports 1, 2, or 4 byte fields. - evidence: `wolfSSL_iotsafe_on_ex()` allocates `IOTSAFE iotsafe;` on the stack and does four `XMEMCPY(..., id_size)` writes into `privkey_id`, `ecdh_keypair_slot`, `peer_pubkey_slot`, and `peer_cert_slot` with no bounds check before `wolfSSL_set_iotsafe_ctx(ssl, &iotsafe)`. The backing fields are compile-time-sized scalars (`byte`, `word16`, or `word32`). - impact: oversized `id_size` values cause out-of-bounds stack writes during TLS session setup, leading to crashes or adjacent-state corruption. - recommendation: reject any `id_size` that does not exactly match `sizeof(iotsafe.privkey_id)` (or redesign `IOTSAFE` to store variable-length IDs safely), and fix the docs to stop implying arbitrary-length support. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - IoT-Safe CSIM transport is process-global and unsynchronized, so concurrent sessions can overwrite each other's APDU command/response state - location: docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/iotsafe.h:125-150`; globals and setters `wolfcrypt/src/port/iotsafe/iotsafe.c:57-58,76-77,80-94,1530-1537` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: multithreaded or multi-context application uses more than one IoT-Safe session/context at a time, or rebinds callbacks while another session is active. - evidence: the port keeps `csim_read_cb`, `csim_write_cb`, `csim_read_buf`, and `csim_cmd` as file-global statics; `wolfIoTSafe_SetCSIM_{read,write}_cb()` simply swaps those globals for all contexts; repo search found no locking in `wolfcrypt/src/port/iotsafe/iotsafe.c`. The docs explicitly say the callbacks are global and change for all contexts. - impact: concurrent IoT-Safe operations can interleave AT+CSIM requests and replies, causing wrong-slot operations, handshake failures, or cross-session data mix through the shared modem path. - recommendation: move callbacks and command/response buffers into per-context state and serialize modem access with a mutex; if only one shared modem is supported, enforce single-owner access explicitly instead of hidden globals. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wc_DeCompressDynamic()` public docs describe `max` as an absolute byte cap, but the implementation treats it as a decompression multiplier - location: docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/compress.h:170-183,198`; implementation `wolfcrypt/src/compress.c:193-203,218,226,269,275` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application follows the public example and passes an intended byte cap such as `1024*1024` while decompressing attacker-controlled input. - evidence: the docs say `\param max maximum size to allocate for output buffer` and show `int max = 1024 * 1024; // 1MB max`, but `compress.c` documents `maxSz` as a "max decompression multiplier", seeds `tmpSz = inSz * i`, and grows by `newSz = tmpSz + inSz` until `i >= maxSz`. - impact: callers can accidentally permit output growth up to roughly `input_size * max`, turning a supposed 1 MB cap into gigabyte-scale allocation/OOM exposure. - recommendation: either enforce `max` as an absolute byte ceiling in code, or rename the parameter/docs to `maxMultiplier` and add a separate byte-capped API. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - the remaining files in this doc-header chunk mostly mirrored existing optional crypto APIs and examples; this pass did not promote a stronger concrete issue from `doc/dox_comments/header_files/{des3.h,ascon.h,types.h,hash.h,srp.h,kdf.h,asn.h,cryptocb.h,pkcs11.h,coding.h,sha.h,camellia.h,poly1305.h,psa.h,siphash.h,chacha.h,md5.h,arc4.h,md2.h,pem.h,ripemd.h,md4.h,logging.h,ocsp.h,error-crypt.h,bn.h,tfm.h,doxygen_groups.h,doxygen_pages.h}` beyond the IoT-Safe state/size bugs and decompression contract issue above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks were primarily API documentation for existing public/optional algorithms and helper surfaces; this pass avoided filing generic "legacy algorithm exists" notes without a stronger default-on exploit path or adjacent-code failure mode.

### Medium - RX72N demo client feeds server-controlled plaintext into `printf(buf)`, creating a format-string sink after `wolfSSL_read()` - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/wolfssl_demo.c:92-107,483-490` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: keep `msg()` as a sink for already-formatted text only and print with `printf("%s", buf)`/`fputs(buf, stdout)` instead of `printf(buf)`; do not let peer/log data become the format string. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: attacker controls the connected TLS peer (or any reused deployment's application-level server response) and can send `%` sequences. - evidence: `wolfSSL_read(ssl, rcvBuff, ...)` stores peer bytes, `rcvBuff[ret] = '\0'`, then `msg(..., "Received: %s\n\n", rcvBuff)` copies that data into `buf` with `vsnprintf`, and `msg()` finally calls `printf(buf);`. - impact: peer data containing format tokens can disclose stack contents or crash the client; `%n` may also become a write primitive on libc builds that support it.

### Medium - shipped RX72N TSIP demo embeds a reusable provisioning blob and wrapped client private keys, so products that reuse `key_data.c` share the same hardware-backed client identity - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/key_data.c:30-177`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/wolfssl_demo.c:386-425,607-610`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/key_data.h:38-57` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: do not ship real `g_key_block_data` values in the repo or example firmware; require per-device manufacturing/provisioning and make demo/test blobs fail the build outside explicit test configurations. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: integrator deploys the sample `key_data.c` unchanged on TSIP-enabled boards. - evidence: `g_key_block_data` contains concrete `encrypted_provisioning_key`, `encrypted_user_rsa2048_private_key`, and `encrypted_user_ecc256_private_key` arrays; the client demo feeds them directly into `wc_tsip_inform_user_keys_ex()` and `tsip_use_PrivateKey_buffer_TLS()` for live TLS client authentication. - impact: deployments lose device uniqueness; anyone with the shipped firmware/source and another compatible board can clone the same client-auth material and impersonate a deployed device/backend identity.

### Medium - RX72N demo hardwires TLS validation to a fake Sep-2024 clock that advances per call, so certificate freshness checks run against compile-time constants instead of real time - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/user_settings.h:185-189`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/wolfssl_demo.c:70-76,130-135`; validation uses `XTIME(0)` in `src/internal.c:10296-10300` and `src/x509.c:7129-7191` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration` - recommendation: confine the override to benchmark-only builds, or require RTC/NTP-backed time before enabling TLS verification; fail closed rather than shipping a stub `time()`. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: build uses this demo `user_settings.h` for TLS without replacing the clock source. - evidence: `user_settings.h` globally sets `TIME_OVERRIDES`, `XTIME time`, and `XGMTIME(c,t) gmtime(c)`; `wolfssl_demo.c` implements `time()` as `((YEAR-1970)*365+30*MON)*24*60*60 + tmTick++` with `YEAR 2024` and `MON 9`, i.e. not wall-clock time. - impact: expired certificates that were still valid around Sep 2024 can be accepted long after expiry, while legitimately future-dated certs can fail unexpectedly; validation also varies with call count rather than elapsed time.

### Reviewed - `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/wolfssl_tsip_unit_test.c`, `wolfssl_demo.h`, and `key_data.h` mostly contained TSIP KAT/task wiring and declarations; this pass did not promote a stronger independent issue from them beyond the demo client's format-string sink, shipped TSIP key blobs, and fake clock - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found test-vector code, task orchestration, and declarations, but no clearer exploit path than the concrete issues appended above.

### Medium - public HTTP request builder truncates `Content-Length` to 16 bits, so bodies above 65535 bytes are advertised with the wrong length - location: `src/wolfio.c:2025-2100`, declaration `wolfssl/wolfio.h:748-751`, documented API `doc/dox_comments/header_files/wolfio.h` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application uses public `wolfIO_HttpBuildRequest()` / `_ex()` with `reqSz > 65535` and then sends the real body on a reused HTTP connection or through an intermediary that trusts `Content-Length`. - evidence: `wolfIO_HttpBuildRequest_ex()` allocates only `char reqSzStr[6]` and formats the body length with `reqSzStrLen = wolfIO_Word16ToString(reqSzStr, (word16)reqSz);`, then emits that truncated value in the `Content-Length:` header via `XSTRNCPY((char*)buf, reqSzStr, ...)`. The public API takes `int reqSz`, so larger caller-supplied lengths silently wrap modulo 65536 in the header instead of failing. - impact: recipients can read only the truncated prefix and treat the remaining bytes as the next request/data, causing request desynchronization, body truncation, or proxy/request-smuggling style failures in callers that reuse the socket. - recommendation: format `reqSz` with a full-width integer type (`int`/`size_t`) and a larger scratch buffer, or reject values above 65535 until the API can represent them correctly; add a regression for `reqSz` around `65535/65536`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V4 API and Web Service`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - `doc/dox_comments/header_files/rsa.h` did not surface a stronger repo-local security/performance issue in this pass beyond minor documentation/name mismatches already handled by the real header/implementation - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files/rsa.h` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: adjacent code checks for the documented PSS verify helpers are implemented in `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c`, and this doc chunk mostly contains comment drift/typos (for example `digentLen`) rather than a clearer exploit path than the code-backed issues already in the inbox.

### Reviewed - `doc/dox_comments/header_files/wolfio.h` mainly mirrors public socket/HTTP helper declarations; this pass promoted the concrete `Content-Length` truncation bug above and did not add a stronger second issue from the remaining callback documentation alone - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files/wolfio.h` with adjacent `src/wolfio.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: other notable wolfio sharp edges in adjacent code (OCSP/CRL SSRF, header injection, unwired cookie callback) are already present in the inbox, so this chunk only added the new large-body `Content-Length` truncation finding.

### Medium - generic `wc_SignatureGenerate*()` helpers always verify the freshly generated signature, so the default sign API path does two asymmetric operations per request - location: `wolfcrypt/src/signature.c:352-355,451-453,463-466`; public API/docs `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/signature.h:60-76`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files/signature.h:142-145,244-247,295-347` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `wc_SignatureGenerateHash()` is just `return wc_SignatureGenerateHash_ex(..., rng, 1);` and `wc_SignatureGenerate()` is just `return wc_SignatureGenerate_ex(..., rng, 1);`. In the shared `_ex` path, a successful sign immediately does `if (ret == 0 && verify) ret = wc_SignatureVerifyHash(...)`. The public non-`_ex` APIs therefore force a full verify on every successful sign, while the docs present verification as an optional `_ex` feature. - impact: any hot path using the generic signature helpers (certificate minting, token signing, firmware signing, test harnesses) pays roughly 2x asymmetric CPU/latency by default, cutting throughput and making the convenience API unexpectedly expensive unless callers discover and switch to the `_ex(..., verify=0)` variants. - recommendation: make `wc_SignatureGenerate()` / `wc_SignatureGenerateHash()` sign-only by default and reserve post-sign verification for the explicit `_ex(..., verify)` APIs or a separate debug/fault-detection knob. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - this docs-heavy chunk did not surface a stronger new security bug in the native `ed448`, `curve448`, `sakke`, `chacha20_poly1305`, `sha3`, or EVP compatibility code than issues already in the inbox; the main new material issue here was the default double-work in generic signature generation - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files/{sakke.h,ed448.h,chacha20_poly1305.h,sha3.h,curve448.h,signature.h,evp.h}` with spot-checks in `wolfcrypt/src/{sakke.c,ed448.c,curve448.c,chacha20_poly1305.c,sha3.c,signature.c,evp.c}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks confirmed existing tracked edges rather than a stronger new exploit: raw Curve448 peer-key validation remains separately tracked; `wc_ChaCha20Poly1305_Decrypt()` already zeroizes plaintext on auth failure (`wolfcrypt/src/chacha20_poly1305.c:115-124,141`); Ed448 public-key import does perform the documented `trusted` check against an existing private key (`wolfcrypt/src/ed448.c:1010-1068,1139-1168`); and the SHAKE copy prototype mismatch in headers is currently a type-alias/documentation hazard (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sha3.h:181,223,235`; impl `wolfcrypt/src/sha3.c:1902,2142`) rather than a clearer standalone exploit path.

### Reviewed - Japanese AES/ASN header docs mirror already-tracked security/complexity hazards from the English docs; this pass did not promote a stronger new chunk-local issue - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/aes.h`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/asn_public.h` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the localized docs repeat the same already-filed problems: AEAD decrypt examples warn that plaintext is undefined and must be zeroized on failure but still show `wc_AesGcmDecrypt(...)`, `wc_AesSivDecrypt(...)`, and `wc_AesEaxDecryptFinal(...)` flows without any buffer wipe (`aes.h:332-364,998-1024,1097-1126,1320,1383,1500`); they still document nonexistent one-shot EAX symbols `wc_AesEaxEncrypt`/`wc_AesEaxDecrypt` while the real API exports `wc_AesEaxEncryptAuth`/`wc_AesEaxDecryptAuth` (`aes.h:1074-1139`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:837-845`), and they duplicate `wc_AesCtsEncrypt` declarations (`aes.h:1577,1609`, real header `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:885`). The ASN docs likewise still advertise the default cert sig as `SHA_WITH_RSA` (`asn_public.h:5`) while `wc_InitCert_ex()` sets `cert->sigType = CTC_SHAwRSA` and `wc_MakeSelfCert()` signs with that default (`wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:29150-29163,35107-35116`), and they explicitly say `wc_SetCustomExtension()` does not copy the OID/value while the implementation stores borrowed pointers (`asn_public.h:1880-1919`; `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:35604-35634`).

### Low - HKDF API docs falsely claim `type == 0` defaults to MD5, but the implementation rejects it with `BAD_FUNC_ARG` - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files/hmac.h:130,184,236,292,346`; implementation `wolfcrypt/src/hmac.c:68-80,1553-1562,1626,1730-1739` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the docs for `wc_HKDF`, `wc_HKDF_Extract[_ex]`, and `wc_HKDF_Expand[_ex]` repeatedly say the hash type "defaults to MD5 if 0 or NULL is given". The implementation routes these APIs through `wc_HmacSizeByType(type)`, and `wc_HmacSizeByType()` returns `BAD_FUNC_ARG` for any type outside the explicit hash enum list; there is no `type == 0` fallback path in the HKDF code. - impact: integrators following the published API contract can ship code paths that unexpectedly fail key derivation at runtime, or mask the failure with ad-hoc fallback/error suppression around security-critical KDF setup. - recommendation: remove the MD5-default claim from the docs and explicitly document that `type` must be a valid hash enum; if compatibility fallback is ever desired, implement it explicitly rather than implying hidden MD5 behavior. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - this doc-header chunk mostly surfaced documentation around already-filed code issues rather than a stronger new exploit path - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files/{dh.h,ed25519.h,curve25519.h,quic.h,eccsi.h,sha512.h,wc_port.h}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: adjacent code checks showed the sharper repo-local issues for these APIs are already tracked elsewhere in the inbox, including X25519 peer-key validation / all-zero shared-secret handling in `wolfcrypt/src/curve25519.c`, borrowed RNG-pointer storage via `wc_curve25519_set_rng()`, and the `wolfCrypt_Init()` init-refcount wedge in `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c`. This pass did not promote a stronger new issue from the remaining header comments beyond the HKDF contract mismatch above.

### Low - Japanese public crypto docs drift from the shipped APIs with compile-breaking examples and nonexistent symbols across SHA3, Curve25519/448, and PKCS7 - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/sha3.h:898,966,1213`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/curve25519.h:494`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/curve448.h:492`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/pkcs7.h:73` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the docs example calls `wc_Shake128_Final(&shake, hash)` even though the public header requires `wc_Shake128_Final(wc_Shake* shake, byte* hash, word32 hashLen)` (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sha3.h:217`); the docs advertise `wc_curve25519_check_public_ex(...)` / `wc_curve448_check_public_ex(...)`, but repo search only finds the real exported APIs `wc_curve25519_check_public(...)` (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/curve25519.h:263`) and `wc_curve448_check_public(...)` (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/curve448.h:124`); the PKCS7 free example uses `wc_PKCS7_Free(pkcs7)` while the real signature is `wc_PKCS7_Free(wc_PKCS7* pkcs7)` (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/pkcs7.h:409`). - impact: Japanese-speaking integrators copying the published API docs hit immediate build failures or chase wrong symbols on core crypto flows, and the duplicated doc set becomes hard to trust during review/support. - recommendation: generate the localized headers from canonical declarations/examples or add CI that compile-checks documented snippets and diffs doc prototypes/examples against `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/*.h`. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - this doc-only chunk did not surface a stronger new native security issue beyond already-tracked Curve25519/Curve448 peer-key validation findings and prior PKCS7 decoder issues; the main chunk-local problem was localized doc/API drift - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/{sha3.h,curve25519.h,curve448.h,pkcs7.h}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks against `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/{sha3.h,curve25519.h,curve448.h,pkcs7.h}` and `wolfcrypt/src/{sha3.c,pkcs7.c}` found the strongest new evidence in mismatched docs/examples, while the more substantive code issues in these areas are already represented elsewhere in the inbox.

### Medium - public ECIES `wc_ecc_ctx_set_info()` stores a borrowed KDF-info pointer without copying it, and the public docs do not warn about the lifetime requirement, so later encrypt/decrypt can read freed or mutated caller memory - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/ecc.h:1539-1558`, implementation `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:13874-13880,14313-14329,14718-14734` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application passes stack, temporary, or mutable `info` to `wc_ecc_ctx_set_info()` and later calls `wc_ecc_encrypt()` / `wc_ecc_decrypt()` after that buffer is freed, reused, or modified. - evidence: the setter only assigns `ctx->kdfInfo = info; ctx->kdfInfoSz = (word32)sz;`; later ECIES encrypt/decrypt feed `ctx->kdfInfo` and `ctx->kdfInfoSz` into `wc_HKDF(...)` / `wc_X963_KDF(...)`. The Japanese public docs describe setting context info but do not say the buffer is borrowed or must outlive the context. - impact: use-after-free reads, crashes, or key derivation from attacker-mutated bytes, breaking ECIES confidentiality/integrity assumptions in callers that expect setter semantics to copy inputs. - recommendation: copy `info` into ctx-owned storage and free it on reset/free, or make the borrowed-pointer contract explicit and add a safe owned variant. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - public `wc_RsaSetRNG()` persists a borrowed `WC_RNG*` inside `RsaKey`, but the API docs omit the lifetime/ownership contract and later RSA paths dereference that saved pointer - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/rsa.h:75-95`, implementation `wolfcrypt/src/rsa.c:5271-5276,725,3801-4006` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application calls `wc_RsaSetRNG(&key, &rng)`, then frees/reinitializes/reuses `rng` before a blinded/private RSA operation or keygen that consumes `key->rng`. - evidence: `wc_RsaSetRNG()` stores `key->rng = rng;`; later RSA code repeatedly loads `rng = key->rng`. The docs only say the RNG is associated with the key and provide no detach API or warning that the RNG object must outlive all later key use. - impact: ordinary C API misuse turns into a dangling-pointer read during private-key operations, causing crashes or undefined blinding/keygen behavior far from the original setup call. - recommendation: prefer per-operation RNG parameters or an owned/refcounted attachment; at minimum document the borrowed lifetime explicitly and support clearing `key->rng` before RNG teardown. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/{hmac.h,quic.h}` did not yield a stronger chunk-local issue than the ECIES/RSA lifetime hazards above - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/hmac.h`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/quic.h` with spot checks in `src/quic.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: QUIC transport params are deep-copied in `src/quic.c:232-239,402`, while QUIC method tables are at least explicitly documented as borrowed (`src/quic.c:336-339,353,366,833,920`); the remaining HMAC docs in this chunk were mainly API descriptions and did not expose a stronger adjacent-code exploit path on this pass.

### Medium - ASCON AEAD decrypt examples write plaintext before tag verification and never zeroize it on `ASCON_AUTH_E`, encouraging callers to keep unauthenticated plaintext - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/ascon.h:390-393,437-440`; implementation behavior `wolfcrypt/src/ascon.c:462,477,485,492-515` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: downstream copies the documented incremental ASCON decrypt flow and processes/logs `plaintext` after a forged ciphertext or tag is supplied. - evidence: the docs show `wc_AsconAEAD128_DecryptUpdate(&a, plaintext, ciphertext, ...)` followed by `wc_AsconAEAD128_DecryptFinal(&a, tag) != 0` with only generic error handling and no buffer wipe. In code, `wc_AsconAEAD128_DecryptUpdate()` writes plaintext into the caller buffer via `xorbufout(...)` before authenticity is known, and `wc_AsconAEAD128_DecryptFinal()` only compares the tag and sets `ret = ASCON_AUTH_E;` on mismatch; it does not scrub the already-written output. - impact: applications that follow the example can retain or act on attacker-controlled plaintext remnants after authentication failure, creating data-confusion and leakage risks similar to other AEAD misuse bugs. - recommendation: document that plaintext is undefined until `DecryptFinal()` succeeds, zeroize the output buffer on any nonzero final result in every example, and consider a one-shot/helper API that scrubs output on auth failure. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - this Japanese header-doc chunk mostly mirrored public APIs or issues already filed from adjacent code; this pass did not promote a stronger new issue from `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/{dh.h,evp.h,wolfio.h,memory.h,iotsafe.h,cmac.h,hash.h,des3.h,srp.h,kdf.h,wc_encrypt.h,chacha20_poly1305.h,dsa.h,signature.h}` beyond the ASCON auth-failure example above and the already-tracked native issues in `src/wolfio.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/{memory.c,port/iotsafe/iotsafe.c,signature.c,dsa.c}` - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks here were dominated by documentation/example drift and localization of existing APIs; the concrete chunk-local risks already had stronger code-backed findings elsewhere in the inbox (global allocator state, IoT-Safe ID/global callback bugs, DSA SHA-1-only wrappers, HTTP `Content-Length` truncation, and default double-verify in generic signing helpers).

### High - STSAFE-A100/A110 integration ships fixed host MAC/cipher keys and auto-provisions them into blank devices, collapsing the secure-element host-auth boundary across every copied build - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/st/stsafe.c:82-92,557-583,625,663,881`; easy-enable docs `wolfcrypt/src/port/st/README.md:84,92-99` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_STSAFEA100` build leaves `STSAFE_HOST_KEY_MAC` / `STSAFE_HOST_KEY_CIPHER` undefined; attacker can recover the firmware/source defaults or has another device built from the same image, plus access to the STSAFE command channel/device. - evidence: the port hardcodes `g_host_mac_key` / `g_host_cipher_key` under the comment `NOTE: These are example keys - real implementations should store securely`. `stsafe_check_host_keys()` queries the host-key slot and, when absent, writes those same 32 bytes with `StSafeA_PutAttribute(... STSAFE_A_HOST_KEY_SLOT_TAG, hostKeys, sizeof(hostKeys), ...)`. Later protected operations use `STSAFE_A_HOST_C_MAC` in `StSafeA_GenerateKeyPair(...)` and `StSafeA_EstablishKey(...)`, so every build that keeps the defaults shares the same host-auth secret. - impact: the secure element no longer has a device-unique or deployment-unique trusted host. Anyone who knows the shipped defaults can impersonate the host for MAC-protected STSAFE commands, provision blank parts with the repo key, and break isolation between devices using the same image. - recommendation: remove the built-in fallback keys, fail init when production host keys are not injected, and require per-product/per-device provisioning from secure storage instead of source-level constants. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - STSAFE ECDSA verification ignores `hashSz` and always reads/verifies `key_sz` bytes, so SHA-224 ECDSA verification on P-256 overreads the digest buffer and feeds the secure element the wrong message bytes - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/st/stsafe.c:393-397,802-808,1180,1755-1756`; callback contract `doc/dox_comments/header_files/ssl.h:9459`; wolfCrypt hashlen propagation `wolfcrypt/src/cryptocb.c:724-728`; SHA-224 ECDSA support `src/ssl.c:13020` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: STSAFE PK callback or crypto-callback verification is enabled, and the verified ECDSA signature uses a digest shorter than the curve size (for example `ecdsa-with-SHA224` on P-256). Remote cert verification can hit this through a peer chain using that algorithm. - evidence: `SSL_STSAFE_VerifyPeerCertCb()` explicitly discards `hashSz` with `(void)hashSz;`. The A120 path calls `stse_ecc_verify_signature(... pHash, (uint16_t)key_sz, ...)`, and the A100 path sets `Hash->Length = key_sz; XMEMCPY(Hash->Data, pHash, key_sz);`. The crypto-callback path likewise passes `info->pk.eccverify.hash` into `stsafe_verify(...)` while `cryptocb.c` separately tracks the real `hashlen`. - impact: for P-256/SHA-224 the code reads 32 bytes from a 28-byte digest buffer, causing out-of-bounds reads and verification against unintended adjacent memory; operationally this can crash verification or produce incorrect accept/reject results for attacker-supplied certificates/signatures. - recommendation: honor `hashSz`/`hashlen`, reject unsupported digest-vs-curve combinations explicitly, and when truncation is required by ECDSA rules truncate from the provided digest length without ever copying past the caller buffer. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - `wolfcrypt/src/port/st/stm32.c`, `STM32MP13.md`, `STM32MP25.md`, and the ST port README were otherwise mostly straightforward HAL/build glue in this pass; the main promoted issues were the STSAFE default host-key trust collapse and digest-length misuse above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: STM32 hash/AES helpers were largely thin register/HAL shims; docs were mostly platform build notes and benchmark output. This pass did not promote a stronger chunk-local exploit or production failure mode beyond the two STSAFE findings above.

### Medium - Japanese Ed25519/Ed448 API docs are materially out of sync with the shipped headers, with multiple unbuildable examples and wrong contracts on core signing/key APIs - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/{ed25519.h,ed448.h}`; real APIs `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/{ed25519.h,ed448.h}`; return-code behavior `wolfcrypt/src/ed448.c:968-978,1211-1253,1306-1451` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `ed25519.h` examples use nonexistent `ed_25519`/`wc_ed25519_init_key` (`:500-501,534-535,569,607,647,832`), ship an incomplete `wc_ed25519ctx_verify_msg(..., &key, );` call (`:329-330`), and pass `&pubSz` for both private/public lengths in `wc_ed25519_export_key(&key, priv, &pubSz, pub, &pubSz)` (`:800`) although the real prototype is `(... byte* priv, word32* privSz, byte* pub, word32* pubSz)` (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ed25519.h:216-217`). `ed448.h` passes `&pubSz` to `wc_ed448_make_public` (`:25`) though the real API takes `word32 pubKeySz` by value (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ed448.h:110-111`), omits the required context arguments in `wc_ed448_sign_msg(..., &key)` (`:102`) while the real signature requires `context, contextLen` (`wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ed448.h:116-117`), uses nonexistent `ed_448`/`wc_ed448_init_key` (`:405-406,439-440,474,512,552,734`), and advertises `ECC_BAD_ARG_E`/`BAD_FUNC_ARGS` (`:611,646,683,723,756`) where the implementation returns `BAD_FUNC_ARG`. - impact: copy/paste users of the shipped Japanese crypto docs hit compile failures or code against the wrong API/error contract, making integrations and review of signature/key handling needlessly error-prone. - recommendation: generate these doxygen headers from the real public declarations, compile-test every documented snippet, and diff localized headers against `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/{ed25519,ed448}.h` in CI. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - SAKKE table-setting APIs store borrowed caller buffers inside `SakkeKey` and later dereference them, but the public API/docs expose no ownership-safe lifecycle and no RSK-table clear helper - location: public surface `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/sakke.h:203-212`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/sakke.h:116-135`; pointer stores `wolfcrypt/src/sakke.c:2368-2370,6581-6582`; later use `wolfcrypt/src/sakke.c:6651,6912-6913`; lifecycle comments `wolfcrypt/src/sakke.c:6494,6591` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application passes stack, temporary, or later-freed precomputation buffers into `wc_SetSakkePointITable()` or `wc_SetSakkeRsk()` and later calls SAKKE operations on that key. - evidence: `wc_SetSakkeRsk()` just assigns `key->rsk.table = table; key->rsk.tableLen = len; key->rsk.set = 1;`; `wc_SetSakkePointITable()` just assigns `key->i.table = table; key->i.tableLen = len;`. Later `sakke_compute_point_r()` uses `key->i.table` in `sakke_mulmod_point(...)` (`:6651`) and `wc_DeriveSakkeSSV()` uses `key->rsk.table`/`tableLen` in `sakke_pairing(...)` (`:6912-6913`). The source comments explicitly say generated point-I tables keep only a reference and `wc_ClearSakkePointITable()` must be called once the table is disposed of, but the public headers/Japanese docs are bare declarations and there is no equivalent clear/detach API for `rsk.table`. - impact: later decapsulation/point computation can read dangling or repurposed memory, causing crashes or deriving the wrong shared secret from stale attacker-influenced heap contents. - recommendation: deep-copy supplied tables into key-owned storage, or make borrowing explicit with `const` pointers plus documented lifetime rules and symmetric clear/detach helpers for both point-I and RSK tables. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/eccsi.h` was declaration-only in this chunk; spot checks in `wolfcrypt/src/eccsi.c` did not surface a stronger new issue beyond the SAKKE lifetime hazard and the Ed25519/Ed448 doc drift above - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/eccsi.h` with adjacent `wolfcrypt/src/eccsi.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: nearby ECCSI setters such as `wc_SetEccsiHash()` copy into key state (`wolfcrypt/src/eccsi.c:1697-1698`), and this pass did not confirm a comparable new borrow/lifetime bug from the declaration-only header alone.

### Medium - Ada TLS client verifies only the CA chain, not the server identity, so any cert chaining to the trusted CA can impersonate the target host - location: `wrapper/Ada/examples/src/tls_client.adb:247-249,292-343,387-396`; supporting API gap `wrapper/Ada/wolfssl.ads`, `wrapper/Ada/wolfssl.adb` (no hostname/SNI binding helper found), native API `wolfssl/ssl.h:3358,4503-4505` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: take the expected DNS name/IP as input and bind it into verification before `Connect` via `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` (and SNI for hostnames), or expose that API in the Ada binding and fail closed when no identity is provided. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can present any certificate chaining to `../../../certs/ca-cert.pem` (for example another cert from the same test CA, a misissued cert, or a MITM in deployments that reuse the sample CA). - evidence: the client accepts an arbitrary IPv4 address from `Argument (1)`, sets `Verify_Peer`, loads `CA_FILE`, and calls `WolfSSL.Connect (Ssl)` with no hostname/IP check. Repo search found the native hostname-verification API in `wolfssl/ssl.h`, but no corresponding use in the Ada example or wrapper. The bundled `certs/server-cert.pem` SANs are only `DNS:example.com` and `IP Address:127.0.0.1`, yet the client accepts any IPv4 literal. - impact: the peer is authenticated only as “signed by this CA,” not as the requested server, enabling wrong-server acceptance and CA-sibling/MITM impersonation.

### Medium - PSK mode hard-codes both the identity and shared secret on client and server, so anyone with the sample source/binary can authenticate as either side - location: `wrapper/Ada/examples/src/tls_server.adb:123-149,335-337`, `wrapper/Ada/examples/src/tls_client.adb:74-104,366-368` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: remove fixed PSKs from the generic example path, or require runtime-supplied per-deployment secrets and clearly gate the mode as test-only. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: operator runs `tls_server_main --psk` / `tls_client_main ... --psk` or copies this example into a real deployment. - evidence: both callbacks hard-code `Identity_String := "Client_identity"` and the PSK bytes `0x1a2b3c4d` (`Character'Val (26/43/60/77)`), and `--psk` wires those callbacks directly into the TLS context/session. There is no config, prompt, or secret-loading path. - impact: PSK authentication collapses to a published constant; unauthorized clients can connect to the server, and rogue servers can impersonate the expected peer to the client.

### Medium - DTLS server pre-consumes and discards the first UDP packet without saving the sender, then calls `Accept_Connection` without ever binding a DTLS peer, making the example handshake brittle or non-functional - location: `wrapper/Ada/examples/src/tls_server.adb:226,376-403`, `wrapper/Ada/examples/src/spark_sockets.adb:121-135` - category: performance - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad` - recommendation: return the peer address and first datagram from `Receive_Socket`, then call `wolfSSL_dtls_set_peer()` on the server-side `Ssl` before `Accept_Connection`, or `connect()` the UDP socket to the discovered peer instead of discarding the packet. - status: candidate - severity: medium - evidence: in DTLS mode the server does `SPARK_Sockets.Receive_Socket (Socket => L.Socket);` before creating/accepting the `Ssl`. `Receive_Socket` reads into local `Item`/`Last`/`From` and throws them away. Later the server only `Attach`es the listening UDP socket and calls `WolfSSL.Accept_Connection (Ssl)`. Repo search found `DTLS_Set_Peer` used only on the client side, not on the Ada server path. - impact: the initial ClientHello is eaten and lost, and the server never learns which peer to bind for the DTLS session, causing extra retransmits, handshake stalls, or a broken DTLS example under real network conditions.

### Medium - the Ada TLS server exposes a built-in authenticated remote shutdown command, and the bundled sample credentials make that DoS path trivially reachable by anyone reusing the example assets - location: `wrapper/Ada/examples/src/tls_server.adb:86-88,447-452`, `wrapper/Ada/examples/src/tls_client.adb:164-166,308-328`, `wrapper/Ada/examples/src/tls_client_main.adb`, `certs/client-cert.pem`, `certs/client-key.pem` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V2 Validation and Business Logic`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: remove the message-triggered shutdown path from the network protocol, or gate it behind an explicit local/admin-only control that is not reachable from normal client traffic. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: server is run with the bundled cert/PSK setup, or an integrator copies the example behavior into a service. - evidence: after `WolfSSL.Read`, the server checks `if Input.Buffer (1 .. 8) = "shutdown" then Shall_Continue := False;`. The same example tree ships the matching client certificate/key paths (`../../../certs/client-cert.pem`, `../../../certs/client-key.pem`) and the PSK mode also uses a published shared secret. - impact: any client that can authenticate with the sample credentials can terminate the server with a single application message, turning the example protocol itself into a remote kill switch.

### Reviewed - `wrapper/Ada/examples/src/{rsa_verify_main.adb,aes_verify_main.adb,sha256_main.adb,spark_terminal.adb,spark_terminal.ads,tls_server.ads,tls_client.ads,tls_server_main.adb,tls_client_main.adb}` were otherwise mostly demo entrypoints, wrappers, or static test vectors in this pass; the sharper chunk-local issues were the missing hostname binding, fixed PSK secret, DTLS packet-discard flow, and network-triggered shutdown above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the remaining code was largely thin sample wiring around previously filed Ada binding hazards, without a stronger new exploit path in this chunk beyond the concrete issues appended here.

### Medium - `scripts/openssl.test` builds `openssl s_client` with `eval`, so a quote in the checkout path reaches shell execution - location: `scripts/openssl.test:9,398-425` with cert/key/CA paths populated from `${CERT_DIR}` throughout setup (for example `:1077-1079`) - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_OPENSSL_TEST` enabled and the test is run from a repo path / `$PWD` containing shell metacharacters or a double quote. - evidence: `CERT_DIR="$PWD/$(dirname "$0")/../certs"`; `do_openssl_client()` copies cert/key/CA path strings into `openssl_cert2` / `openssl_key2` / `openssl_caCert2` and then executes `echo "Hello" | eval "$OPENSSL s_client ... \"$openssl_cert2\" ... \"$openssl_key2\" ... \"$openssl_caCert2\""` at lines `421` and `425`. A `"` in the path breaks out of the quoted argument and injects shell syntax. - impact: arbitrary command execution as the test user/CI runner when the OpenSSL interop suite runs from a maliciously named directory. - recommendation: remove `eval`; build the `s_client` argv as an array and exec it directly, or otherwise avoid re-parsing a shell command string. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - TLS 1.3 early-data checks only fail when both counters are wrong, so partial regressions can pass CI - location: `scripts/tls13.test:259-281,320-325` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: after counting `Early Data Client message` and `Early Data was`, the script only flags failure when both expected counts are wrong: `if [ $session_ticket -eq 0 -a $ed_srv_msg_cnt -ne 2 -a $ed_srv_status_cnt -ne 2 ]` and later `if [ $ed_srv_msg_cnt -ne 2 -a $ed_srv_status_cnt -ne 1 ]`. If either count regresses alone, the condition is false and the test still passes. - impact: broken TLS 1.3 early-data behavior can slip through the test suite as a false green, weakening coverage on a security-sensitive path. - recommendation: fail on either mismatch (`||` / separate assertions) and keep the session-ticket precondition as its own check. - reference: `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - `ocsp-stapling.test` is internet-dependent by default and contacts `login.live.com` unless the caller explicitly disables external tests - location: `scripts/ocsp-stapling.test:6,14-15,340-345` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the script states it `makes connection(s) to the public Internet`, only skips when `WOLFSSL_EXTERNAL_TEST == "0"`, then runs `retry_with_backoff 3 ./examples/client/client -C -h "$server" -p 443 ...` with `server=login.live.com`.
