Unset means “run”, not “skip”. - impact: default test runs depend on third-party uptime, outbound network policy, and remote certificate/service changes, causing nondeterministic failures or hangs unrelated to repo changes. - recommendation: make external-network coverage opt-in (`WOLFSSL_EXTERNAL_TEST=1`) and keep the default suite hermetic/local-only. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - OCSP stapling scripts mix proper ready-file probes with fixed `sleep 0.1/0.2`, creating race-driven flakes that whole-script retries then mask - location: `scripts/ocsp-stapling-with-wolfssl-responder.test:462-476,540,556,622,666,737,750`, `scripts/ocsp-stapling.test:379-392,458,473`, `scripts/ocsp-stapling2.test:376-415,469,574`, `scripts/ocsp-stapling_tls13multi.test:396-422,477,497,511` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: these scripts already implement `wait_for_readyFile`, but several later DTLS/revoke cases start a server and then rely on `sleep 0.1` or `sleep 0.2` before connecting instead of waiting for readiness. The same files also use `RETRIES_REMAINING` to rerun the whole script on failure. - impact: CI outcomes depend on machine speed/load rather than product behavior; slower builders can fail spuriously, while retries increase runtime and blur real startup regressions. - recommendation: replace the fixed sleeps with the existing ready-file/socket readiness checks everywhere and drop retry masking once startup synchronization is consistent. - reference: `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `scripts/pem.test`, `scripts/sniffer-testsuite.test`, and `scripts/ocsp-responder-openssl-interop.test` did not expose a stronger chunk-local issue than the `eval` sink and the non-hermetic/flaky test logic above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `ocsp-responder-openssl-interop.test` uses `mktemp` for its `/tmp` artifacts, and `sniffer-testsuite.test`'s X25519 capability probe matches the current `snifftest` help text typo (`sslSniffer/sslSnifferTest/snifftest.c:470` prints `x22519`). This pass did not confirm a clearer exploit path or production failure mode in those files.

### Medium - shipped MCAPI MPLAB project settings compile out X.509 time validation, so builds accept expired/not-yet-valid certificates - location: `mcapi/user_settings.h:261-262`; project use `mcapi/wolfssl.X/nbproject/configurations.xml:143`, `mcapi/wolfcrypt_mcapi.X/nbproject/configurations.xml:114`, `mcapi/wolfcrypt_test.X/nbproject/configurations.xml:114`; validation gates `src/x509_str.c:336,391-398,405-412`, `src/internal.c:10272,39034` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: PIC32 builds use the provided `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` projects/header and perform certificate verification without replacing the time source. - evidence: the distributed MCAPI `user_settings.h` emits `#warning Time/RTC disabled` and defines `NO_ASN_TIME`; the bundled MPLAB project configs enable `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS`; wolfSSL's cert date checks are compiled only under `#ifndef NO_ASN_TIME`, so the `wc_ValidateDate` / `XVALIDATE_DATE` paths are removed entirely in these builds. - impact: devices built from the shipped MCAPI project files can trust expired or not-yet-valid server/client certificates, weakening TLS peer authentication and enabling replay of stale credentials or acceptance of misissued future-dated certs. - recommendation: do not ship `NO_ASN_TIME` in the default MCAPI project settings; require a real RTC/NTP-backed time source for verification or fence the no-time build behind an explicit test-only macro that disables peer auth by design. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - `CRYPT_ECC_DHE_SharedSecretMake()` stores a temporary RNG inside the ECC key and frees it, leaving a dangling `key->rng` for later operations - location: `mcapi/crypto.c:703-731`; enabled by `mcapi/user_settings.h:83`; stored field `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/ecc.h:500,622`; sink/use `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:4917-4926,13768-13777` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `ECC_TIMING_RESISTANT` build (enabled in the shipped MCAPI settings) calls `CRYPT_ECC_DHE_SharedSecretMake()` more than once on the same `CRYPT_ECC_CTX`, or later reaches another wolfCrypt ECC path that consults `key->rng`. - evidence: the wrapper creates stack `WC_RNG rng`, calls `wc_ecc_set_rng((ecc_key*)priv->holder, &rng)`, performs `wc_ecc_shared_secret(...)`, then `wc_FreeRng(&rng)` without clearing the borrowed pointer from the key. The `ecc_key` struct persists `WC_RNG* rng`, and `wc_ecc_shared_secret()` uses `private_key->rng` when it is non-NULL. - impact: subsequent shared-secret or related ECC operations can dereference a freed/stale RNG pointer, causing crashes, undefined behavior, or incorrect timing-resistance behavior in repeated key-agreement flows. - recommendation: treat the temporary RNG as call-scoped only: clear the key's RNG pointer before `wc_FreeRng(&rng)` (or avoid storing it on the key at all) and add a regression that repeats `CRYPT_ECC_DHE_SharedSecretMake()` on one key. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `mcapi/mcapi_test.c`, `mcapi/crypto.h`, `mcapi/PIC32MZ-serial.h`, `mcapi/README`, and `mcapi/include.am` in this chunk were mainly sample wrapper/test scaffolding; the concrete issues promoted here were the shipped no-time TLS build setting and the ECC RNG lifetime bug above - location: listed files with implementation cross-checks in `mcapi/crypto.c`, `src/x509_str.c`, and `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining chunk-local defects were mostly test cleanup/build-footgun level (for example sample-only project/build drift) rather than a stronger trust-boundary break or material performance failure.

### Medium - `google.test` disables certificate verification against the public Internet, so a MITM/captive portal can make the external TLS checks pass even when chain or hostname validation is broken - location: `scripts/google.test:29-35`; sink/flag semantics `examples/client/client.c:1247,2433-2434` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: external test is enabled via `WOLFSSL_EXTERNAL_TEST`; attacker can intercept traffic to `www.google.com` on the test path. - evidence: the script runs `./examples/client/client -X -C -h $server -p 443 -g -d` for both TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3. The client help and option parser define `-d` as `Disable peer checks` and set `doPeerCheck = 0`. - impact: the repo's Google interoperability check can succeed against an attacker-presented certificate, masking regressions in certificate/path/hostname verification and giving false assurance from CI/test runs. - recommendation: remove `-d` from this test and verify with a pinned CA or `--sys-ca-certs` plus hostname checks as in `external.test`; if the goal is only reachability, rename it so it is not treated as a security/interoperability check. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `crl-revoked.test` recursively copies the entire checkout into a temp dir on every run just to create/remove hash symlinks under `certs/`, turning one CRL test into O(repo size) disk I/O - location: `scripts/crl-revoked.test:31-35,149-167,198-203` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the script's workaround is `RUNNING_DIR=$(mktemp -d); cp -rp . $RUNNING_DIR/.; cd $RUNNING_DIR`, but the later mutation is limited to `CERT_DIR=certs` where it creates/removes `*.0` and `*.r0` symlinks for the hash-dir CRL case. - impact: every run does a full-tree recursive copy of this large repo before starting the actual CRL check, adding avoidable CI wall time, filesystem churn, and temporary disk pressure. - recommendation: stage only the `certs/` subtree (or just the files needed for the hash-dir test) into a temp workdir, or create the hashed cert/CRL links in a dedicated temporary certificate directory instead of cloning the whole source tree. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: small sharp tools`

### Low - `benchmark.test` claims to use a unique benchmark port, but hardcodes a shared port and shared ready-file path, making concurrent runs interfere with each other - location: `scripts/benchmark.test:28-40,65-94` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the script comment says `Use unique benchmark port so it won't conflict with any other tests`, but it sets `bench_port=11113` and polls a fixed `/tmp/wolfssl_server_ready` path instead of using the per-run `-p 0` + ready-file pattern used by the other test scripts in this chunk. - impact: parallel `make check` runs, stray local services, or stale `/tmp/wolfssl_server_ready` state can cause flaky failures, cross-talk with the wrong server, or mis-measured benchmark results. - recommendation: switch the benchmark script to the same ephemeral-port ready-file mechanism already used by `resume.test`, `psk.test`, and `trusted_peer.test`, and use `mktemp` for any coordination file. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - `scripts/{trusted_peer.test,dtls.test,crl-gen-openssl.test,psk.test,rsapss.test,resume.test,pkcallbacks.test,ocsp.test,external.test,dtlscid.test,ping.test,unit.test.in}` plus the bundled `sniffer-*.sslkeylog` / `sniffer-*.out` fixtures did not surface a stronger chunk-local issue than the Google external test's disabled verification, the full-tree copy in `crl-revoked.test`, and the shared benchmark coordination state above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: most remaining scripts were test orchestration around example binaries or static keylog/output fixtures; no clearer additional exploit path or material production-impacting bug emerged from this chunk beyond the concrete issues appended here.

### Medium - `sniffer-gen.sh` guesses the server PID by subtracting 1 from a background pipeline PID, so cleanup can signal the wrong process and leave the real test server running - location: `scripts/sniffer-gen.sh:14-15,30-40` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `run_test()` backgrounds `./examples/server/server ... | prepend ... &`, stores `server_pid=$!`, then mutates it with `((server_pid--)) # Get the first PID in the pipe`; both `run_test()` and the EXIT trap later do `kill $server_pid`. Bash does not guarantee that `PID-1` is the first command in a pipeline. - impact: on a busy CI/desktop host, the script can send SIGTERM to an unrelated same-user process while the actual server keeps listening on port 11111, producing flaky captures and destructive side effects outside the test. - recommendation: stop deriving a process ID from pipeline layout; launch the server without a pipe, or use a wrapper/coproc/process substitution that returns the real server PID before logging output. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Low - `benchmark_compare.sh`'s documented non-CSV mode is effectively broken because the second parsing pass only accepts comma-containing lines - location: `scripts/benchmark_compare.sh:4-6,120-156` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the header says files without `.csv` use the standard benchmark output format, and pass 2 sets `mode` from `MiB/s` / `ops/sec`; but it then parses results only inside `if [[ $line == *","* ]]; then`, so plain benchmark output never reaches `getAlgo`, `getValue`, or `printData`. - impact: standard benchmark comparisons silently produce empty/misleading output, so performance regressions can be missed unless callers happen to use CSV input. - recommendation: only gate on commas in CSV mode, or reuse the first-pass non-CSV parsing logic for the second file as well; add a regression test for two plain-text benchmark outputs. - reference: `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - STM32L4 build helper emits validation-only `HAVE_FORCE_FIPS_FAILURE` into generated `user_settings.h`, enabling a public FIPS failure-injection API by default - location: `scripts/stm32l4-v4_0_1_build.sh:95-99`; exposed API `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/fips_test.h:106-108` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator uses `scripts/stm32l4-v4_0_1_build.sh` as-is to prepare a deployable STM32L4 build. - evidence: the script comments this block as `For operational testing use only in validation effort` but unconditionally writes `#define HAVE_FORCE_FIPS_FAILURE`; under that macro the public FIPS header exposes `wolfCrypt_SetStatus_fips(int status)` as a function `to force failure mode for operational testing`. - impact: builds produced from this helper ship with a validation-only fault-injection surface enabled, increasing the chance of accidental or reachable runtime transitions into FIPS failure mode and creating audit/certification confusion. - recommendation: leave `HAVE_FORCE_FIPS_FAILURE` disabled by default, require explicit test-only opt-in, and emit a loud warning when generating a header with the macro enabled. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - no stronger standalone issue was promoted from `scripts/asn1_oid_sum.pl`, `scripts/dertoc.pl`, `scripts/user_settings_asm.sh`, `scripts/makedistsmall.sh`, `scripts/memtest.sh`, `scripts/cleanup_testfiles.sh`, or `scripts/aria-cmake-build-test.sh` beyond the process-handling, benchmark parser, and STM32 validation-config findings above - location: listed scripts - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass found mostly build/package/test helpers and generator scripts; remaining sharp edges were lower-signal shell hygiene issues without a clearer repo-specific exploit path or material production failure mode than the findings appended above.

### Medium - default hostname verification accepts partial-wildcard SANs like `DNS:*localhost`, enabling wrong-host certificate matches unless callers add stricter policy - location: wildcard fixture `certs/test/server-goodaltwild.pem`; positive test wiring `tests/test.conf:1886-1893`; matcher `src/internal.c:13194-13245`; advertised hardening flags stubbed in `src/x509.c:15511-15516` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: client uses wolfSSL's default hostname verification and trusts a CA/private PKI that can issue partial-wildcard DNS SANs. - evidence: the shipped "good" test certificate contains `DNS:*localhost`, and `tests/test.conf` treats it as a success case with `-h localhost`; the matcher logic documents only left-most wildcard placement (`src/internal.c:13231,13245`) rather than whole-label-only semantics, while `WOLFSSL_NO_PARTIAL_WILDCARDS` / `WOLFSSL_MULTI_LABEL_WILDCARDS` log `not yet implemented` in `src/x509.c`. - impact: a peer presenting a CA-signed partial-wildcard cert can satisfy hostname checks for hosts it should not, causing wrong-server acceptance / MITM in deployments that rely on default verification behavior. - recommendation: reject partial-label wildcards by default and only allow RFC 6125-style whole-label `*.` matches; implement the advertised no-partial/multi-label flag behavior and add regression tests that `*localhost` and similar partial patterns fail by default. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - this cert-fixture chunk otherwise appears to be intentional parser/verification coverage, and spot checks found the oversized-altname / oversized-name-constraint and embedded-NUL certs are already exercised as negative cases rather than silently accepted - location: `tests/api/test_ossl_x509.c:1558-1595`, `tests/test-fails.conf:6-58,85,103-109`, fixtures under `certs/test/` - category: security review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations_ex()` is expected to fail on `cert-over-max-altnames.pem` and `cert-over-max-nc.pem` (`ExpectIntNE` at `tests/api/test_ossl_x509.c:1567-1568,1595`), and the null-byte / bad-name / bad-signature fixtures are listed in `tests/test-fails.conf` as expected failures.

### Medium - `examples/benchmark/tls_bench` client loads a CA but never enables peer or hostname verification, so a MITM/wrong server can complete the benchmark TLS session - location: `examples/benchmark/tls_bench.c:968-999,1116`, host resolution `:871-877`; default ctx behavior `src/ssl.c:452,512,558`; hostname API available at `wolfssl/ssl.h:3358` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: user runs `tls_bench` against any network-reachable host or through an on-path attacker. - evidence: the client creates a fresh `WOLFSSL_CTX`, loads a CA with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer(...)`, then calls `wolfSSL_connect(cli_ssl)` without any `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)` or `wolfSSL_check_domain_name(...)`. wolfSSL context creation defaults verification to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c`. - impact: the benchmark can silently talk to an attacker-controlled or wrong endpoint while reporting successful TLS throughput/latency, and any application data sent during the run is exposed to that peer. - recommendation: explicitly set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` and bind the expected DNS name/IP before `wolfSSL_connect()`, or mark the example as insecure/test-only and fail closed by default. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - IoT-Safe demo ships with `NO_ASN_TIME`, disabling certificate validity-period checks even though both sides set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` - location: `IDE/iotsafe/user_settings.h:113-117`; peer verification enabled in `IDE/iotsafe/memory-tls.c:230,340`; date checks compiled under `src/x509_str.c:336,432` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator reuses the provided IoT-Safe `user_settings.h` in any build that relies on X.509 verification. - evidence: the sample defines `TIME_OVERRIDES`, `WOLFSSL_USER_CURRTIME`, and `NO_ASN_TIME`; the demo still calls `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, NULL)` on client and server, but `src/x509_str.c` gates certificate date validation under `#ifndef NO_ASN_TIME`. - impact: expired or not-yet-valid certificates are accepted in sample-derived builds, weakening TLS peer authentication and making stale test certs look valid in production. - recommendation: remove `NO_ASN_TIME` from the shipped IoT-Safe settings, require a real time source for peer auth, or fence no-time builds behind an explicit test-only configuration that also disables normal certificate validation claims. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - `isr_usart1()` appends modem bytes into a fixed 256-byte buffer with no bounds check, so a chatty or malicious modem can corrupt adjacent memory from interrupt context - location: `IDE/iotsafe/devices.c:35-37,448-453`; consumer/reset logic `:132-136` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: BG96/UART input delivers more than 256 unread bytes before foreground code drains `modem_rx_buf` (for example unsolicited modem output, malformed AT/CSIM responses, or a compromised baseband). - evidence: the IRQ handler does `modem_rx_buf[modem_rx_idx++] = ...` into `static char modem_rx_buf[256];` with no cap check; indices are only reset later when parser consumption catches up. - impact: UART input can smash neighboring static state, causing crashes, misparsed APDUs, or unpredictable control-flow corruption in the IoT-Safe transport path. - recommendation: convert this to a bounded ring buffer with overflow handling/drop accounting, or disable RX interrupts until space is available; never increment past `sizeof(modem_rx_buf)`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - DTLS benchmark timeout state is process-global, so one DTLS connection can disable timeouts for every other thread pair - location: `examples/benchmark/tls_bench.c:409,654-660,741,837,1091` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `DoneHandShake` is a global `int`; the client handshake callback sets it to `1`, `ReceiveFrom()` uses it to force `dtls_timeout = 0`, and `CloseAndCleanupSocket()` resets it to `0` on any socket close. The benchmark also supports multiple thread pairs via `-T`. - impact: concurrent DTLS runs share timeout state, so one session finishing its handshake can make unrelated sessions block indefinitely or skew retry/latency behavior; later closes can also re-enable timeouts for active peers. - recommendation: move handshake-done state into per-connection `info_t`/SSL user context instead of a global, and keep DTLS timeout behavior local to each socket. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Medium - DTLS server setup creates a UDP socket but skips `bind()` and still calls `listen()`, making `tls_bench -u` server mode non-portable or outright broken - location: `examples/benchmark/tls_bench.c:1265-1317` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: in `SetupSocketAndListen()`, the `doDTLS` branch only does `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)`; the `bind()` call exists only in the TCP branch, but the function then unconditionally executes `listen(*listenFd, 5)`. - impact: on many platforms `listen()` on UDP fails, and even where it does not, the socket was never bound to `argPort`, so DTLS benchmark startup fails or receives no traffic. - recommendation: for DTLS, `bind()` the datagram socket to the requested port and skip `listen()` entirely; keep TCP and UDP setup paths separate. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Reviewed - `examples/benchmark/tls_bench.h`, `examples/benchmark/include.am`, `IDE/iotsafe/{README.md,Makefile,target.ld,include.am,startup.c,devices.h,ca-cert.c,main.c}` were otherwise mostly example/build/linker scaffolding in this pass; the chunk-local issues promoted were the benchmark verification/DTLS state bugs and the IoT-Safe no-time/USART-overflow hazards above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks outside the promoted findings were mainly packaging, static demo certificates, and board bring-up glue without a stronger additional exploit path in this chunk.

### Low - `gen-ext-certs.sh` uses a fixed world-writable `/tmp` pathname for OpenSSL output, so a local symlink planted before `renewcerts.sh` runs can clobber arbitrary files writable by the build user - location: `certs/test/gen-ext-certs.sh:3,7-20`; entrypoint `certs/renewcerts.sh:837` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker has local access on a shared build/CI host and can pre-create `/tmp/gen-ext-certs.sh` as a symlink before a more privileged user or job regenerates cert fixtures. - evidence: the script sets `TMP="/tmp/\`basename $0\`"`, redirects OpenSSL output with `>$TMP 2>&1`, later `cat $TMP`, and finally `rm $TMP`; there is no `mktemp`, `trap`, or ownership check. Running `certs/renewcerts.sh` invokes this script directly. - impact: the redirection truncates the symlink target, enabling arbitrary file clobber/DoS within the invoking user's permissions and making concurrent fixture regeneration non-deterministic. - recommendation: replace the fixed `/tmp` name with `mktemp` (or a repo-private temp dir), quote it consistently, and `trap` cleanup on exit. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - the remaining files in this chunk are mostly static certificate test vectors (`cert-over-max-*`, `cert-ext-*`) plus small fixture-generation helpers; this pass did not confirm a stronger runtime security issue from the X.509 extension configs themselves beyond the local temp-file clobber in `gen-ext-certs.sh` - location: `certs/test/{cert-over-max-altnames.cfg,cert-over-max-nc.cfg,cert-ext-multiple.cfg,cert-ext-ndir-exc.cfg,cert-ext-ndir.cfg,cert-ext-nc.cfg,cert-ext-joi.cfg,cert-ext-nct.cfg,cert-ext-ia.cfg,gen-testcerts.sh,gen-badsig.sh}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: repo search shows these fixtures are consumed by parser/validation tests (`tests/api/test_ossl_x509*.c`, `tests/api/test_certman.c`, `wolfcrypt/test/test.c`) and are intentionally exercising oversized SAN/name-constraint and extension-handling cases rather than exposing a stronger new production-path flaw in this chunk.

### Medium - Espressif crt-bundle generator still advertises the old `{subject,key}` wire format even though the only supported wolfSSL bundle format is full-DER records - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/gen_crt_bundle.py:24-33,275-281`; consumer `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/esp_crt_bundle.c:151-164,1415`; public header `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle.h:50-51` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the generator header comment says it `stores just the subject name and public key` with `subject name length` and `public key length`, but `create_bundle()` actually emits only `struct.pack('>H', len(self.certificates))`, then for each cert `cert_der = crt.public_bytes(serialization.Encoding.DER)` plus a 2-byte length. The consumer side unconditionally enables `IS_WOLFSSL_CERT_BUNDLE_FORMAT`, documents that no other format is supported, sets `CRT_HEADER_OFFSET 2`, and advances records as `cur_crt = cur_crt + (CRT_HEADER_OFFSET + cert_len)`. - impact: maintainers/integrators reading the shipped generator are pointed at a nonexistent bundle layout and adjacent dead-format logic, making bundle debugging and modifications audit-hostile; any update based on the file header rather than the real parser contract can generate bundles that current wolfSSL rejects or misparses at startup. - recommendation: rewrite the generator’s top-level format contract to match the DER-only format actually consumed today, and fence or delete the stale subject/key-format commentary and dead compatibility cues. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Low - `gen_crt_bundle.py` unconditionally creates and appends `cert_bundle.log` while sorting every bundle, adding avoidable filesystem I/O and a stray build artifact to normal runs - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/gen_crt_bundle.py:217-245,269-272` - category: performance - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `create_bundle()` always opens `cert_bundle.log` with `"w"` before sorting; the active sort path `sort_certificates_by_as_is()` calls `extract_dn_components_as_is()`, which reopens `cert_bundle.log` in append mode and writes `oid_name=`, `attribute_value=`, `dn_string=`, `cleaned_string=`, and `result_string=` for each certificate subject processed. There is no debug flag guarding this path. - impact: every bundle build rewrites a sidecar log in the working directory and performs unnecessary disk writes in the hot path for certificate sorting, slowing the utility and dirtying source/build trees and CI artifacts for no functional gain. - recommendation: remove the logging from the default sort path or gate it behind an explicit verbose/debug option; keep sort-key generation pure and in-memory. - reference: `grugbrain: small sharp tools`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Reviewed - this Espressif bundle-generator chunk did not expose a stronger direct runtime security bug than the format drift and always-on debug-I/O above - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/gen_crt_bundle.py` with consumer/header spot checks in `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/{esp_crt_bundle.c,README.md}` and `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle.h` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the sharper repo-local problems here were mismatched generator/parser contracts and shipped unnecessary logging. The remaining issues in this chunk were lower-signal robustness nits (for example PEM parsing tied to exact line markers and the filter regex’s rigid input shape) without a clearer exploit path or production-impacting failure mode on this pass.

### Medium - default hostname verification also accepts partial-wildcard common names like `CN=*localhost`, enabling wrong-host matches when SAN is absent - location: wildcard fixture `certs/test/server-goodcnwild.pem`; positive test wiring `tests/test.conf:1871-1878`; CN fallback `src/internal.c:13330,13402-13403`; wildcard matcher `src/internal.c:13194-13364`; stricter wildcard flags exposed but not enforced by default in `src/x509.c:15507-15516` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: client relies on wolfSSL default hostname verification and the presented cert has no SAN, so verification falls back to CN matching. - evidence: `server-goodcnwild.pem` contains `Subject ... CN=*localhost` and no SAN extension; `tests/test.conf` treats that cert as a success case; `CheckForAltNames()` sets `checkCN = 1` when no alt name exists, then `MatchDomainName(dCert->subjectCN, ...)` runs the same wildcard matcher used for SANs. - impact: a certificate for `*localhost` can satisfy verification for `localhost` and similarly-shaped hosts, weakening identity binding and allowing wrong-host acceptance when a CA issues or an app trusts such partial-wildcard certs. - recommendation: reject partial wildcards in CN fallback by default, prefer SAN-only hostname binding per RFC 6125, and make `WOLFSSL_NO_PARTIAL_WILDCARDS`/left-most-only behavior actually effective in the default verifier. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - OpenSSL-compat `TRUSTED CERTIFICATE` import strips and ignores auxiliary trust/reject purposes, so restricted trust anchors become unrestricted CAs - location: trusted fixture `certs/test/ossl-trusted-cert.pem`; test coverage `tests/api.c:11673-11694,13351`; parser/import path `src/x509.c:6089-6104,13238-13411`; verify-policy gap `src/x509.c:10351`, `src/x509_str.c:839-844` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application imports OpenSSL `-----BEGIN TRUSTED CERTIFICATE-----` bundles or `X509_AUX` roots expecting `addtrust`/`addreject` purpose restrictions to be enforced. - evidence: `ossl-trusted-cert.pem` is a `TRUSTED CERTIFICATE`; tests only assert that `wc_PemToDer(..., TRUSTED_CERT_TYPE, ...)` and `PEM_read_bio_X509_AUX(...)` parse it. In `src/x509.c`, `TRUSTED_CERT_TYPE` explicitly truncates the DER buffer to exclude the auxiliary trust data before certificate parsing, and `WOLFSSL_X509_VERIFY_PARAM` notes purpose/trust are not recorded while `wolfSSL_X509_STORE_CTX_set_purpose()` is a stub. - impact: trust anchors carrying purpose restrictions or rejects are silently widened to plain root CAs, so chains for unintended usages can validate where OpenSSL-style consumers expected them to be denied. - recommendation: parse and retain X509_AUX trust/reject OIDs and enforce them during path validation, or fail closed/reject `TRUSTED CERTIFICATE` inputs when auxiliary semantics cannot be honored instead of silently discarding them. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - this cert-fixture chunk was otherwise mostly negative-test data for bad signatures, name constraints, S/MIME canonicalization, weak DH rejection, and extension parsing; no stronger chunk-local issue was promoted from `certs/test/{digsigku.pem,cert-ext-multiple.pem,server-cert-ecc-badsig.pem,cert-ext-joi.pem,cert-ext-ndir-exc.pem,cert-ext-ndir.pem,cert-ext-ncmulti.pem,cert-ext-nc-combined.pem,cert-ext-ncdns.pem,cert-ext-nct.pem,cert-ext-nc.pem,cert-ext-ia.pem,cert-ext-ncip.pem,crit-cert.pem,dh1024.pem,crit-key.pem,dh512.pem,smime-test-multipart-badsig.p7s,smime-test-multipart.p7s,smime-test-canon.p7s,smime-test.p7s,include.am,catalog.txt,client-ecc-cert-ski.hex}` beyond the CN wildcard acceptance and trusted-cert auxiliary-policy loss above - location: listed files with adjacent checks in `tests/{test.conf,api.c,api/test_ossl_p7p12.c}` and `src/{internal.c,x509.c,x509_str.c}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining files were primarily fixtures intentionally exercising parser/verification edge cases or catalog/distribution metadata; this pass avoided filing low-signal notes about test-only weak keys/expired certs without a broader repo behavior gap.

### Medium - shipped ESP-IDF/PlatformIO wolfSSL example configs enable cert-date bypass, and the default ESP bundle still includes expired DST Root CA X3, so example-derived builds can accept expired/not-yet-valid chains - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/{cacrt_local.pem,README.md}` with adjacent enablement in `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/*/components/wolfssl/include/user_settings.h:883-885`, default bundle wiring in `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/*/components/wolfssl/CMakeLists.txt:950-959`, and verify override in `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/esp_crt_bundle.c:479-503,1107-1112` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator reuses the shipped Espressif/PlatformIO `user_settings.h` or example component config; attacker presents a certificate chain that is otherwise valid except for notBefore/notAfter (including chains anchored on the retained `DST Root CA X3`). - evidence: `cacrt_local.pem` still ships `DST Root CA X3` even though the file says `This will be removed once the cross-signed certificate expires in Sep 2024`; the default Espressif bundle build appends `cacrt_local.pem` for both full/common bundles; shipped example configs define both `WOLFSSL_DEBUG_IGNORE_ASN_TIME` and `WOLFSSL_DEBUG_CERT_BUNDLE`; and `esp_crt_bundle.c` explicitly overrides `ASN_BEFORE_DATE_E` / `ASN_AFTER_DATE_E` in the verify callback when those macros are set. - impact: expired or not-yet-valid certificates can be accepted in sample-derived ESP builds, weakening TLS peer authentication and keeping a sunset compatibility root trusted past its stated removal window. - recommendation: remove `WOLFSSL_DEBUG_IGNORE_ASN_TIME` from shipped example/platform configs, gate the override code behind an explicit local test-only option that is off by default, and drop `DST Root CA X3` from the default `cacrt_local.pem` now that the Sep 2024 compatibility window has passed. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Reviewed - this Espressif cert-bundle chunk's main concrete issue was the date-bypass + stale local-root combination above; `pio_install_cryptography.py` appears to be a standalone helper and repo search did not find a tree-local invocation path from the checked-in ESP examples/CMake files on this pass - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/{pio_install_cryptography.py,cacrt_deprecated.pem,README.md}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `pio_install_cryptography.py` does auto-run `pip install cryptography`, but search found no repo-local caller beyond file listing; `cacrt_deprecated.pem` is only appended behind `CONFIG_WOLFSSL_CERTIFICATE_BUNDLE_DEPRECATED_LIST` in the ESP example Kconfig/CMake flow, so this pass did not promote a stronger default-path exploit from the deprecated bundle alone.

### Medium - installed `wolfssl-config` helper generated by `AX_CREATE_GENERIC_CONFIG` executes attacker-supplied shell syntax via `eval` - location: `m4/ax_create_generic_config.m4:150-159`; shipped entrypoint `configure.ac:11579`, `Makefile.am:327-334` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller runs the installed `wolfssl-config` script with untrusted arguments (for example from a build wrapper, package script, or copied command line). - evidence: the macro emits a script that matches arguments like `-I//...`, rewrites `//` to `$` with `sed`, then runs `v=\`eval "echo $v"\``, `o=\`eval "echo $o"\``, and finally `eval "echo $o"`. With the shipped `wolfssl-config`, an argument such as `'-I//(id>&2)'` becomes `-I$(id>&2)` before `eval`, causing command execution as the invoking user. - impact: local arbitrary command execution in any workflow that invokes `wolfssl-config` on attacker-controlled input; this is especially risky in build/CI environments that treat config-helper output as trusted. - recommendation: remove `eval` entirely, treat options as data, and if variable expansion is still needed restrict it to a fixed whitelist without shell re-parsing; preferably retire this helper in favor of `pkg-config`. - reference: `v5.0.0-V1`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - `AX_HARDEN_LINKER_FLAGS` computes RELRO/NOW/PIE options but writes them into an unconsumed `LIB` variable, so configured builds silently miss the intended linker hardening - location: `m4/ax_harden_compiler_flags.m4:75-95`; enabled from `configure.ac:11191`; no `$(LIB)`/`@LIB@` consumer found in `Makefile.am` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build relies on the repo’s hardening macro and assumes it enabled `-z relro -z now -pie`. - evidence: the macro accumulates hardening flags in `AX_HARDEN_LIB` then ends with `LIB="$LIB $AX_HARDEN_LIB"`; repo search found the hardening macro invoked from `configure.ac`, but no substitution or make-variable consumer for `LIB`, while other dependency plumbing uses `LIBS`/`LDFLAGS`. - impact: binaries can ship without RELRO/NOW/PIE despite the configure logic claiming to add hardening, reducing mitigation against memory-corruption exploits and creating a false sense of hardened builds. - recommendation: append linker hardening flags to `LDFLAGS` (or `LIBS` where appropriate), and add a configure/build assertion that final link lines actually contain RELRO/NOW/PIE when the macro reports success. - reference: `v5.0.0-V13`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - Renesas RA6M4 TLS client loads a CA but never enables peer or hostname verification, so any reachable TLS server can impersonate the target - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/test/src/wolf_client.c:110-128,160,236`; default ctx behavior `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the device’s network path or operate any server at the configured `SERVER_IP`. - evidence: `wolfSSL_TLS_client_init()` creates a new client context and loads a CA with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations()` / `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer()`, but the sample never calls `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)` or `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`. wolfSSL’s context setup defaults to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c:512`. The client then connects to `SERVER_IP` and calls `wolfSSL_connect()`. - impact: the sample authenticates nobody; a wrong server or on-path attacker can complete the handshake and read/modify the client’s application traffic. - recommendation: enable `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER`, bind the expected DNS name/IP with the hostname-verification API before connect, and fail closed when no expected identity is configured. - reference: `v5.0.0-V12`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - Renesas RA6M4 TLS client writes one byte past a 256-byte stack buffer when the peer returns a full-length record - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/test/src/wolf_client.c:152,155,251-256` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: connected peer sends exactly 256 bytes in the first application read; the same sample currently accepts unauthenticated peers. - evidence: the client allocates `char rcvBuff[BUFF_SIZE]` with `#define BUFF_SIZE 256`, calls `ret = wolfSSL_read(ssl, rcvBuff, BUFF_SIZE)`, then unconditionally terminates with `rcvBuff[ret] = '\0';`. When `wolfSSL_read()` returns `256`, the NUL write lands one byte past the array. - impact: attacker-controlled response length can corrupt adjacent stack state or crash the embedded client after handshake completion. - recommendation: reserve space for the terminator (`wolfSSL_read(..., BUFF_SIZE - 1)`) or guard `ret >= BUFF_SIZE` before writing a NUL; treat the buffer as binary data when length is explicit. - reference: `v5.0.0-V1`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Reviewed - this chunk’s remaining autoconf helpers and Renesas SCE unit-test scaffolding were mostly upstream probe/build macros or test harness code; this pass did not promote a stronger repo-specific issue from `m4/{ax_compiler_version,ax_pthread,ax_linuxkm,visibility,ax_count_cpus,ax_atomic,ax_tls,ax_check_library,lib_socket_nsl,ax_vcs_checkout,ax_am_jobserver,ax_check_compile_flag,ax_check_link_flag,ax_debug,ax_append_flag,ax_am_macros,ax_bsdkm,ax_append_compile_flags,ax_append_link_flags,ax_file_escapes,hexversion,ax_require_defined,ax_add_am_macro,ax_append_to_file,ax_print_to_file,require_canonical}.m4` or `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/test/src/{wolfssl_sce_unit_test.c,test_main.c}` beyond the generated-config `eval`, dead linker-hardening plumbing, and RA6M4 client verification/bounds issues above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: review concentrated on code paths that crossed trust boundaries or altered shipped security posture; the rest of this chunk was dominated by compile-time feature detection and embedded self-test orchestration without a clearer new exploit path or material production failure mode on this pass.

### High - STM32Cube-generated config unconditionally defines `WOLFSSL_NO_CLIENT_AUTH`, silently compiling out mutual-TLS client-certificate authentication for all algorithms, not just Ed25519/Ed448 - location: `IDE/STM32Cube/default_conf.ftl:771`, generated example `examples/configs/user_settings_stm32.h:782`, client-auth gates `src/internal.c:18185,25413,34325,41053,41760`, handshake caller `src/ssl.c:7012,7061`, algorithm-specific knobs `src/ssl_load.c:691,773` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator uses the STM32Cube-generated config for a server expecting mTLS / client-certificate auth. - evidence: the template always emits `#define WOLFSSL_NO_CLIENT_AUTH /* disable client auth for Ed25519/Ed448 */`, but wolfSSL's CertificateRequest and client-cert loading paths are compiled only under `!defined(WOLFSSL_NO_CLIENT_AUTH)`. The library already has narrower `NO_ED25519_CLIENT_AUTH` / `NO_ED448_CLIENT_AUTH` guards in `ssl_load.c`, so this template knob disables far more than the comment claims. - impact: STM32 server builds never request or verify client certificates, so deployments that rely on mTLS silently lose client authentication and can accept unauthenticated peers. - recommendation: remove the unconditional `WOLFSSL_NO_CLIENT_AUTH` from the template; if Ed25519/Ed448 client-auth needs to be disabled, use the algorithm-specific macros instead and gate any broad client-auth disable behind an explicit opt-in. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V6 Authentication`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - STM32 example's peer-cert pretty-printer overflows a fixed 80-byte stack buffer on long but valid certificate serial numbers - location: `IDE/STM32Cube/wolfssl_example.c:405-416,472,868,1774`, serial-size contract `src/x509.c:4373-4392` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `KEEP_PEER_CERT` or `KEEP_OUR_CERT`, then connects to a peer presenting a certificate with a serial longer than ~21 bytes (wolfSSL allows up to 32 bytes here). - evidence: `ShowX509()` fetches up to 32 serial bytes into `serial[32]`, then formats each byte with `sprintf(serialMsg + strLen + (i*3), ":%02x ", serial[i]);` into `char serialMsg[80];`. With the API's documented `EXTERNAL_SERIAL_SIZE (32)` maximum, later iterations write well past the 80-byte buffer. - impact: attacker-controlled certificate metadata can smash the example task's stack during peer-info logging, crashing the demo and potentially corrupting adjacent state. - recommendation: size the buffer from `sz` (`strLen + sz*4 + 1`), use `snprintf`, or print serial bytes directly without incremental `sprintf` into a fixed stack array. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - TLS 1.3 over UART client disables all certificate verification, so any device on the UART link can impersonate the expected peer - location: `IDE/STM32Cube/wolfssl_example.c:1733-1744,1764-1774`, docs `IDE/STM32Cube/README.md:169-193` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: operator uses the shipped UART TLS example or copies it into a real board-to-host / board-to-board deployment where the UART path is attacker-accessible or bridged by untrusted hardware/software. - evidence: `tls13_uart_client()` creates a TLS 1.3 client context and then calls `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, SSL_VERIFY_NONE, NULL);` before `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`; the README presents this as the TLS-over-UART example and links host-side peers, but there is no trust anchor or pinning step. - impact: the client authenticates encryption only, not peer identity; a rogue host/peripheral can terminate the TLS handshake and read/modify application data as the accepted server. - recommendation: load/pin the server certificate or public key and use `SSL_VERIFY_PEER`; if the intent is a lab-only demo, gate the insecure mode behind an explicit test macro and document it as unauthenticated. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - UART TLS receive path can hang forever on a stalled peer and also returns fabricated zero-padded bytes when DMA delivers less than the requested length - location: `IDE/STM32Cube/wolfssl_example.c:1521-1579`, docs `IDE/STM32Cube/README.md:171-193` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `HAL_UARTEx_RxEventCallback()` only sets global `msg_length`; `uartIORx()` starts `HAL_UARTEx_ReceiveToIdle_DMA(...)`, then spins in `while (msg_length == 0) { HAL_Delay(10); }` with no timeout/abort. After any idle event it blindly does `XMEMCPY(buf, tb->buf, sz); tb->data_len = msg_length; tb->curr_index = sz; return sz;`, even when the callback reported fewer than `sz` bytes. The comment explicitly warns that if it gets stuck, users should reset the board. - impact: a peer that stops transmitting can wedge the demo indefinitely, and fragmented/short UART bursts can feed zero-filled tail bytes into the TLS parser instead of the actual received length, causing hard-to-debug handshake/data corruption and repeated reconnect failures. - recommendation: make the interrupt-shared length/state robust, add a real timeout/error path, and return only the bytes actually received while preserving partial-record state across calls instead of copying `sz` unconditionally. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Reviewed - `IDE/STM32Cube/{main.c,wolfssl_example.h,STM32_Benchmarks.md,README.md,include.am}` were otherwise mostly sample wiring, benchmark output, or packaging metadata in this pass; the concrete issues promoted here were the global client-auth disable in the Cube template plus the UART/example logging bugs above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `main.c` is wrapped in `#if 0` as a sample scaffold, the benchmarks file is measurement output, and `include.am` just ships the example assets.

### Medium - default-enabled MDK shell parser overruns its argv table and can walk past the input buffer on short or overlong commands - location: `IDE/MDK-ARM/MDK-ARM/wolfSSL/shell.c:309-341`; enabled by default in `IDE/MDK-ARM/MDK-ARM/wolfSSL/config-{RTX-TCP-FS,FS,BARE-METAL}.h:47-53,51-57,53-59` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: shipped MDK projects build the shell, and an attacker can send serial/console input to it. - evidence: `MAXARGS` is 10 but `getline()` writes `args->argv[args->argc] = &(line[i]); ... args->argc++;` with no bound check; delimiter detection ignores `\0`, so a `fgets()` line that fills `LINESIZE` without a newline makes `while (!IF_DELIMITER(line[i])) i++` run off the end of `line`; and a blank line hits `line[strlen(line)-2]`. - impact: malformed shell input can corrupt adjacent memory or crash the MDK control shell from its command parser before any command-specific validation runs. - recommendation: cap `argc`, treat `\0` as an end delimiter, guard short lines before `strlen()-2`, and reject/truncate commands that do not fit the buffer. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - MDK shell task launch passes pointers to mutable stack/global command state into RTX tasks, so background commands can run with overwritten argv/function data - location: `IDE/MDK-ARM/MDK-ARM/wolfSSL/shell.c:316-318,478,511,523,544-565` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `shell_main()` keeps `func_args args` on its own stack and a global `static char line[LINESIZE]`; it stores the function pointer in `args.argv[0]`, then hands `&args` to `os_tsk_create_user_ex(...)` for both foreground and background paths. The worker later dereferences `((func_args*)args)->argv[0]`, while the shell loop can immediately overwrite `args`/`line` on the next `getline()`. The background path has no synchronization at all. - impact: command execution depends on scheduler timing rather than local reasoning: a quick follow-up command can change the argv/function seen by the spawned task, leading to wrong-command execution, corrupted arguments, or sporadic crashes in the sample shell. - recommendation: deep-copy the parsed command line into task-owned storage before spawning, and keep function pointers out of reused text buffers. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad`

### Medium - the shipped RTX-TCP-FS MDK config enables NULL ciphers and PSK by default, and adjacent sample client/server code then auto-select `PSK-NULL-SHA256`, yielding cleartext “TLS” - location: `IDE/MDK-ARM/MDK-ARM/wolfSSL/config-RTX-TCP-FS.h:34,75-84,246`; cipher selection in adjacent sample code `examples/server/server.c:2956-2989`, `examples/client/client.c:3479-3486` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator builds the shipped RTX-TCP-FS project with the sample/simple client-server PSK path instead of overriding the cipher list. - evidence: the MDK config hard-enables `HAVE_NULL_CIPHER` and `MDK_CONF_PSK 1`; in the example server/client, when PSK is in use and `defaultCipherList == NULL`, the `#elif defined(HAVE_NULL_CIPHER)` branch sets `defaultCipherList = "PSK-NULL-SHA256"`. - impact: sample-derived deployments can negotiate authentication without encryption, so passive network observers can read all application data even though the session presents as TLS. - recommendation: remove `HAVE_NULL_CIPHER` from shipped networked MDK configs, gate null ciphers behind an explicit test-only macro, and prefer an encrypted PSK suite by default. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - Nitrox AES-GCM offload uses uninitialized stack bytes as part of short IVs, so common 12-byte GCM nonces produce nondeterministic hardware IVs - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/cavium/cavium_nitrox.c:814-830,838-854` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Cavium Nitrox offload is enabled and callers use AES-GCM with an IV shorter than 16 bytes (for example the common 12-byte nonce). - evidence: both `NitroxAesGcmEncrypt()` and `NitroxAesGcmDecrypt()` allocate `byte ivLcl[AES_BLOCK_SIZE];`, then on `if (ivSz < AES_BLOCK_SIZE)` only do `XMEMCPY(ivLcl, iv, ivSz);` before passing `ivLcl` to the hardware path; the remaining bytes are never initialized. - impact: AES-GCM computations depend on stale stack data instead of a deterministic nonce expansion, causing tag/interop failures and making cryptographic behavior hinge on unrelated memory contents. - recommendation: zero-initialize the whole temporary IV buffer and construct the hardware-required IV representation explicitly for each supported nonce length, rejecting unsupported lengths instead of padding with garbage. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - `IDE/MDK-ARM/MDK-ARM/wolfSSL/{main.c,wolfssl_MDK_ARM.c,wolfssl_MDK_ARM.h,cert_data.[ch],time-CortexM3-4.c,time-dummy.c,config-WOLFLIB.h}` and `wolfcrypt/src/port/cavium/{cavium_octeon_sync.c,README.md,README_Octeon.md}` did not surface a stronger chunk-local issue than the MDK shell parser/task-state hazards, the null-cipher build default, and the Nitrox GCM IV bug above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining code was mainly board/runtime glue, embedded cert includes, timing stubs, or vendor setup documentation; no sharper exploit path was confirmed from those files on this pass.

### Medium - `wolfSSL_set_psa_ctx()` stores a borrowed `psa_ssl_ctx*` in session callback slots, so stack/early-freed PSA contexts become use-after-free during handshake crypto - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/psa.h:42-47,76-87`; implementation `wolfcrypt/src/port/psa/psa_pkcbs.c:528,549-552`; callback use `src/internal.c:5608-5611,5667,5735,5816`; sinks `wolfcrypt/src/port/psa/psa_pkcbs.c:235,309` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application follows the public PSA API with caller-owned `struct psa_ssl_ctx` storage and lets that storage go out of scope or be freed before the `WOLFSSL*` is finished; the docs even show stack-local `struct psa_ssl_ctx psa_ctx = { 0 };` examples. - evidence: `wolfSSL_set_psa_ctx()` does not copy the context; it just wires `ctx` into `wolfSSL_SetEccSignCtx`, `wolfSSL_SetEccKeyGenCtx`, `wolfSSL_SetEccSharedSecretCtx`, and `wolfSSL_SetEccVerifyCtx`. The handshake path later fetches those pointers from `ssl` and PSA callbacks dereference `psa_ctx->private_key` / `psa_ctx->dh_key`. - impact: normal caller lifetime mistakes become handshake-time UAF/crash or wrong-key PSA operations inside certificate signing/verification and ECDH. - recommendation: make `wolfSSL_set_psa_ctx()` copy the small context into `ssl`-owned storage (and free/detach it from `wolfSSL_free`), or make the API explicitly borrowed/unsafe and fix the docs/examples to require storage that outlives the whole session. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - `wc_CheckCertOcspResponse()` accepts signed `int responseSz` and feeds negative lengths into the OCSP ASN.1 parser as huge unsigned bounds - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/ocsp.h:41-48`; entry `src/ocsp.c:76-99`; cast/bounds sink `src/ocsp.c:381-382`; parser state `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c:40977-40978,41140-41141,41215-41216` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: caller passes a negative `responseSz` (for example via FFI misuse, signed underflow, or unchecked external length) while validating attacker-controlled OCSP response bytes. - evidence: the public API takes `int responseSz` and `wc_CheckCertOcspResponse()` forwards it to `CheckOcspResponse(...)`; that path calls `InitOcspResponse(..., (word32)responseSz, ...)`, which stores the cast value in `resp->maxIdx`, and later parsing uses `resp->source` with `word32 size = resp->maxIdx` as the upper bound. - impact: negative lengths can become near-4GiB parser bounds, enabling out-of-bounds reads/crashes while checking OCSP responses. - recommendation: reject `response == NULL || responseSz <= 0` before parsing, use an unsigned length type at the public boundary, and audit adjacent OCSP/CRL buffer APIs for the same signed-length pattern. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - this Japanese header-doc chunk mostly mirrored existing public APIs; the only new chunk-local issues promoted were the PSA borrowed-context lifetime hazard and the OCSP signed-length parser hazard above - location: `doc/dox_comments/header_files-ja/{pkcs11.h,random.h,camellia.h,coding.h,pwdbased.h,sha256.h,siphash.h,poly1305.h,sha512.h,md5.h,sha.h,psa.h,types.h,chacha.h,md2.h,pem.h,blake2.h,ripemd.h,md4.h,logging.h,ocsp.h,cryptocb.h,compress.h,arc4.h,error-crypt.h,bn.h,wc_port.h,tfm.h,doxygen_groups.h,doxygen_pages.h,asn.h}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks against adjacent code did not surface a stronger new exploit path than those two issues; remaining material in this chunk mainly duplicated already-tracked legacy-crypto/doc-drift/global-state sharp edges from earlier passes.

### Medium - Espressif cert-bundle cleanup frees a borrowed `store->current_cert`, so bundle-assisted TLS handshakes can double-free the active X509 on teardown - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/esp_crt_bundle.c:204,641,654,996-999,1566-1603`; ownership source `src/x509_str.c:754,821`; normal store cleanup `src/internal.c:14677` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: do not persist `current_cert` in file-global state unless you take ownership with an explicit duplicate/refcount; otherwise keep it callback-local and never free it in `wolfSSL_bundle_cleanup()`. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: ESP-IDF build uses `esp_crt_bundle_attach()` / `wolfSSL_bundle_cleanup()`, and a peer certificate path reaches the no-signer bundle callback before connection cleanup. - evidence: `store_cert` is declared as `will point to existing param values`, assigned from `wolfSSL_X509_STORE_CTX_get_current_cert(store)` or `store->current_cert`, and commented as `just a pointer into the actual ctx store cert`; nevertheless `wolfSSL_bundle_cleanup()` later does `wolfSSL_X509_free(store_cert)`. The getter returns `ctx->current_cert`, and core cleanup separately frees `store->current_cert`. - impact: a remote certificate chain that triggers bundle lookup can drive heap corruption or a crash during connection shutdown on ESP targets.

### Medium - Espressif no-signer cleanup frees `WOLFSSL_X509_NAME*` subobjects after freeing the owning `bundle_cert`, so an unmatched chain can hit UAF/double-free in verification cleanup - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/esp_crt_bundle.c:778,788,856-862,986-993` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: treat `this_subject` / `this_issuer` as borrowed views into `bundle_cert`; clear them when `bundle_cert` is freed and remove the explicit `wolfSSL_X509_NAME_free()` calls on that path. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: peer presents a chain that reaches `wolfssl_ssl_conf_verify_cb_no_signer()` and no matching CA subject is found in the bundle. - evidence: the callback saves `this_issuer = wolfSSL_X509_get_issuer_name(bundle_cert)` and `this_subject = wolfSSL_X509_get_subject_name(bundle_cert)`; the loop comment states `this_issuer and this_subject are parts of this bundle_cert`; but the `_crt_found == 0` cleanup path first frees `bundle_cert` and then calls `wolfSSL_X509_NAME_free(this_subject)` and `wolfSSL_X509_NAME_free(this_issuer)`. - impact: attacker-controlled unmatched server chains can turn a routine verify failure into a client crash or allocator corruption.

### Low - malformed custom ESP cert bundles leak the allocated pointer table because init only frees `crts` when it is already `NULL` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/esp_crt_bundle.c:1346-1351,1427-1434,1560-1563` - category: performance - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: invert the cleanup guard to `if (crts != NULL)` and add a regression that repeatedly calls `esp_crt_bundle_set()` with an invalid bundle after allocation has succeeded. - status: candidate - severity: low - evidence: `wolfssl_esp_crt_bundle_init()` allocates `crts = calloc(num_certs, sizeof(x509_bundle));`; on later validation failure it enters `if (_esp_crt_bundle_is_valid == ESP_FAIL) { if (crts == NULL) { free(crts); ... } }`, so the non-NULL allocation is never released. `esp_crt_bundle_set()` exposes this path as a public reinit API. - impact: repeated invalid bundle loads leak heap on memory-constrained ESP devices, increasing the chance of avoidable reconnect/init failures.

### Reviewed - `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/esp_crt_bundle.c` mainly surfaced ownership/cleanup bugs in the runtime bundle path; its cert-date override logic under `WOLFSSL_DEBUG_IGNORE_ASN_TIME` was already covered by the earlier Espressif example-config finding - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/esp_crt_bundle.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass promoted the concrete borrowed-pointer frees and malformed-bundle leak above; the remaining notable risk in this file is the already-tracked debug time-bypass callback guarded by `WOLFSSL_DEBUG_CERT_BUNDLE && WOLFSSL_DEBUG_IGNORE_ASN_TIME`.

### Reviewed - `autogen.sh` and `commit-tests.sh` did not surface a stronger chunk-local security issue than low-signal build-script hygiene; the main concrete concern is that `commit-tests.sh` reconfigures and re-tests multiple build variants in one tree without an intervening clean, which can hide config-specific regressions if stale objects survive dependency tracking - location: `autogen.sh`, `commit-tests.sh:7-30` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the script does `make clean; make -j 8 test;`, then `./configure --disable-fastmath; make -j 8 test;`, then another `./configure ...; make -j 8 test;` with no `make clean`/`distclean` between the later config changes. `autogen.sh` itself mainly creates placeholder files/dirs and runs `autoreconf --install --force`; repo search in this pass found no consumer for the WSL-only `no_links` flag it sets. - impact: CI or pre-commit runs can report success for the "basic" or "full" configs while still using artifacts from an earlier configuration, reducing the value of the script as a regression gate and making config-specific failures harder to reproduce. - recommendation: run each configuration in a fresh build directory or `make distclean` between `./configure` variants; if the WSL `no_links` probe is meant to matter, either wire it into the bootstrap flow or remove the dead state. - reference: `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - ESP-IDF `wolfssl_client` enables certificate validation but never binds the expected hostname/IP, so any certificate chaining to the loaded CA can impersonate the target - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_client/main/client-tls.c:231-247,315-347,360-365,519-527`; verification gate `src/internal.c:16777`, hostname API `src/ssl.c:7437`, `wolfssl/ssl.h:3358` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or control another server certificate issued by the CA loaded into `CTX_CA_CERT`. - evidence: the sample resolves `TLS_SMP_TARGET_HOST` with `gethostbyname()` / `inet_pton()`, loads a CA, and sets `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, 0)`, but never calls `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` (or equivalent IP binding) before `wolfSSL_connect()`. wolfSSL only performs hostname checks when `ssl->buffers.domainName.buffer` is set. - impact: the example authenticates the chain but not the endpoint identity, so a wrong-host certificate from the trusted CA can complete the TLS handshake and read/modify client traffic. - recommendation: call `wolfSSL_check_domain_name(ssl, TLS_SMP_TARGET_HOST)` (or the appropriate expected IP/identity API) before `wolfSSL_connect()`, and fail closed when no expected peer identity is configured. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - ESP-IDF `wolfssl_client` logs a TLS response as a C string after reading a full 256-byte record, so a peer-controlled 256-byte reply causes an out-of-bounds stack read - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_client/main/client-tls.c:185,539-555` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: connected server (or MITM accepted via the missing hostname check above) sends exactly 256 application bytes in one read. - evidence: the sample uses `char buff[256];`, clears it with `memset(buff, 0, sizeof(buff));`, then calls `ret_i = wolfSSL_read(ssl, buff, sizeof(buff));` and immediately logs `ESP_LOGI(TAG, "Server response: \n\n%s\n", buff);`. When `wolfSSL_read()` returns 256, the buffer contains no NUL terminator and `%s` reads past `buff`. - impact: attacker-controlled response length can leak adjacent stack bytes into logs or crash the task. - recommendation: reserve space for a terminator (`sizeof(buff) - 1`), track the returned length, and log with a length-bounded format (`%.*s`) or binary-safe dump routine. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - IoT-Safe CSIM parsing treats callback data as NUL-terminated text even though the public callback contract only returns a byte count, so modem input can drive out-of-bounds reads and reply misparsing - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/iotsafe/iotsafe.c:124-172`; callback API `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/iotsafe/iotsafe.h:41-45`; docs `doc/dox_comments/header_files/iotsafe.h:123-164` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: platform read callback returns modem bytes without an added terminator, or a malformed/full-length modem reply fills the buffer. - evidence: `wolfIoTSafe_SetCSIM_read_cb()` exposes `int (*wolfSSL_IOTSafe_CSIM_read_cb)(char*, int)` and documents only a returned length. `expect_tok()` does `ret = csim_read(csim_read_buf, MAXBUF);` and then immediately calls `XSTRSTR(csim_read_buf, tok)`, `XSTRNCPY(parser_line, r_found, ...)`, and `XSTRLEN(reply)` without appending a NUL or constraining searches to `ret`. - impact: untrusted modem/APDU traffic can make the parser scan past `csim_read_buf`, causing crashes, stale-data matches, or corrupted APDU state in certificate/key operations. - recommendation: make the parser length-delimited end-to-end, explicitly NUL-terminate only when space remains, clear buffers between reads, and replace raw `XSTR*` scans with bounded `mem*`/`strn*` logic. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Renesas callback-context registration has an off-by-one/global-capacity bug, so the sixth synced crypto context writes past `gCbCtx[]` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_common.c:67-75,394-397,456-478,495-510`; entrypoints reached from `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_fspsm_util.c:1259-1296` and `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_tsip_util.c:2244-2286`; feature declaration `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/Renesas/renesas_sync.h:25-31` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: Renesas sync build (`HAVE_RENESAS_SYNC`) creates more than five callback contexts over process lifetime or concurrently via `wc_sce_set_callback_ctx()` / `tsip_set_callback_ctx()`. - evidence: `INITIAL_DEVID` starts at 7890, `MAX_FSPSM_CBINDEX` is 5, and `FSPSM_ST *gCbCtx[MAX_FSPSM_CBINDEX];` allocates only five slots. Registration does `cbInfo->devId = gdevId++;` with no upper-bound check and stores `gCbCtx[cbInfo->devId - INITIAL_DEVID] = cbInfo;`; lookup/cleanup also accept `<= (MAX_FSPSM_CBINDEX + INITIAL_DEVID)`, so devId `7895` indexes slot `5` out of bounds. - impact: the sixth registration corrupts adjacent global state, causing crashes or wrong callback-to-session routing in Renesas hardware-crypto TLS flows; on connection-driven servers this can become externally triggerable memory corruption under load. - recommendation: either allocate `MAX_FSPSM_CBINDEX + 1` slots or, better, tighten every bound check to reject indexes `>= MAX_FSPSM_CBINDEX`; add a regression that opens six contexts and expects a clean failure instead of an OOB write. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - the remaining chunk files were mostly declarations or sample scaffolding once the concrete issues above were accounted for: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/Renesas/{renesas-tsip-crypt.h,renesas-fspsm-types.h,renesas_fspsm_internal.h,renesas_tsip_internal.h,renesas_cmn.h,renesas-fspsm-crypt.h,renesas_tsip_types.h,renesas-rx64-hw-crypt.h,renesas_sync.h}` are largely type/macro headers; `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_client/main/{wifi_connect.c,time_helper.c,main.c,CMakeLists.txt,Kconfig.projbuild,component.mk}` are primarily example wiring/build config - location: listed files with adjacent implementation checks in `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/renesas_common.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/iotsafe/iotsafe.c`, `src/{internal.c,ssl.c}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass did not confirm a stronger additional exploit path in those files beyond the missing endpoint identity binding, fixed-size response logging, IoT-Safe parser misuse of unbounded strings, and Renesas callback-table overflow above.

### Medium - `gencertbuf.pl` generates and the build installs public headers containing reusable private test keys (including a CA key), so sample-derived deployments can ship attacker-known TLS identities - location: `gencertbuf.pl:24-32,64-89,116-141,827-848`; installed via `wolfssl/include.am:20-21`; consumed by shipped configs such as `examples/configs/user_settings_{arduino,espressif,platformio}.h:573-608,1037-1076,734-773` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator reuses the installed `wolfssl/certs_test*.h` material or the shipped example `CTX_*` mappings instead of provisioning unique credentials. - evidence: the generator embeds private materials like `client-key.der`, `server-key.der`, `ca-key.der`, `server-ed25519-priv.der`, and `ca-sm2-key.pem` into `wolfssl/certs_test.h` / `wolfssl/certs_test_sm.h`; those headers are installed as public headers by `wolfssl/include.am`; example configs then `#include <wolfssl/certs_test*.h>` and map `CTX_CA_CERT`, `CTX_SERVER_KEY`, and `CTX_CLIENT_KEY` to the fixed embedded credentials. - impact: any product built from these sample defaults can be impersonated with the published server/client private keys, and the shipped CA private key lets an attacker mint certificates that sample-derived clients will trust, breaking TLS authentication and mTLS trust boundaries. - recommendation: stop installing test-key headers in the public include set, never ship CA/private keys outside test fixtures, and make example configs require user-provided cert/key material (or fail closed with placeholders) instead of defaulting to shared credentials. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Reviewed - `gencertbuf.pl` is otherwise mostly a one-shot cert-to-C generator; this pass did not promote a stronger chunk-local performance/complexity issue than the installed shared-key material above - location: `gencertbuf.pl` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the remaining sharp edges were low-signal generator concerns (single-byte reads, duplicated lists, large static blobs) without a clearer exploit path or production impact than the shipped/private-key issue above.

### Medium - `fips-check.sh`'s documented switches and selected build tool do not match the code path actually run, so FIPS compatibility checks can execute against stale configuration or the wrong `make`
- location: `fips-check.sh:32,50,61,104,681`
- category: complexity
- severity: medium
- status: candidate
- evidence: the usage text says `noautogen` only means `don't run autogen`, but the parser handles it as `DOCONFIGURE='no'; DOAUTOGEN='no'`, skipping configure too. Separately, the `solaris` flavor switches `MAKE='gmake'`, but after rewriting `wolfcrypt/src/fips_test.c` the script hard-codes `make clean` instead of `$MAKE clean`.
- impact: release/FIPS verification runs can silently reuse stale configure state or fail only on hosts where `make` is not GNU make, producing false pass/fail signals from the repo's canonical FIPS compatibility helper.
- recommendation: keep `noautogen` scoped to `DOAUTOGEN`, use `$MAKE clean` consistently, and add a small regression check for the `noautogen` and `solaris` paths.
- reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - `Makefile.am` in this chunk was mainly build/distribution scaffolding; no stronger new issue was promoted beyond the already-filed installed `wolfssl-config` helper risk and the `fips-check.sh` workflow bug above
- location: `Makefile.am:273,327-346`
- category: security/complexity review
- severity: none
- status: reviewed
- evidence: this file mostly aggregates subdir includes, test lists, install hooks, and cleanup targets. The notable security-relevant surface here is the install hook for `@GENERIC_CONFIG@` already traced to the vulnerable generated helper from `m4/ax_create_generic_config.m4`; the remaining `merge-clean`/cleanup logic looked like lower-signal local maintenance debt rather than a sharper trust-boundary break or material production failure mode.

### Reviewed - root `CMakeSettings.json` is just a Visual Studio placeholder and did not expose a chunk-local security, complexity, or material performance issue - location: `CMakeSettings.json` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the file contains a single `"No-CMake"` Ninja configuration whose only `buildCommandArgs` is `echo 'No build command'`; adjacent repo docs (`cmake/README.md`) describe `CMakeSettings.json` as a Visual Studio GUI override file, and repo search found real non-placeholder CMake settings only in the Azure Sphere IDE subtree.

### Medium - Vagrant provisioning downloads and executes an unverified libevent release tarball as root, so a tampered upstream asset or MITM can get code execution during `vagrant up` and modify the shared checkout - location: `Vagrantfile:11-17,22` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: developer runs the shipped Vagrant environment; attacker can tamper with the fetched release artifact or the account serving it. - evidence: the provisioner does `wget -q $URL-$VER/$LIB-$VER.tar.gz && tar -zxf ...`, then immediately runs `./autogen.sh && ./configure -q && make -s` before `sudo make install`; the same VM session also copies `/vagrant/` into `wolfssl/`, so the guest has write access to the synced repo tree. - impact: arbitrary code from the downloaded tarball runs with provisioning privileges inside the VM and can backdoor the working tree or steal build/test material from the synced checkout. - recommendation: stop building unauthenticated third-party tarballs in provisioning; prefer distro packages or pin and verify a checksum/signature, and build as an unprivileged user before any privileged install step. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Shipped Vagrant box is `ubuntu/trusty64`, an Ubuntu 14.04 image long past end-of-life, so the default dev VM starts from an unsupported and unpatched base - location: `Vagrantfile:39` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: user brings up the provided Vagrant VM and uses it for networked build/test work. - evidence: the only configured base box is `config.vm.box = "ubuntu/trusty64"`; Trusty is EOL and no longer receives normal security updates. - impact: developers and CI reproductions using this file inherit known-issue OS packages and outdated toolchain components by default, increasing compromise risk in a VM that mounts the project checkout and exposes forwarded ports. - recommendation: replace the box with a supported LTS image and refresh the provisioning steps against maintained package sources. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Reviewed - `resource.h` is generated Visual C++ resource boilerplate and this chunk did not show a stronger repo-specific issue there beyond the Vagrant provisioning risks above - location: `resource.h`, `Vagrantfile` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `resource.h` only contains default resource-ID macros for `wolfssl.rc`; the concrete trust-boundary issues in this chunk came from the Vagrant bootstrap script and base image selection.

### Medium - Renovate advertises GitHub Action digest pinning, but the repo still checks in 210 mutable tag-based workflow actions, leaving CI supply-chain execution mutable today - location: `renovate.json:2` with adjacent evidence in `.github/workflows/*` (for example `ada.yml:19`, `bind.yml:25`, `docker-OpenWrt.yml:63`, `coverity-scan-fixes.yml:5,15,39-40,51-52`) - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can retag or compromise an upstream action repo referenced by a mutable tag such as `actions/checkout@v4`, `docker/build-push-action@v5`, or `wolfSSL/actions-build-autotools-project@v1`; any scheduled or maintainer-triggered workflow run then executes the attacker-controlled action code. - evidence: `renovate.json` only extends `config:recommended` and `helpers:pinGitHubActionDigests`, but repo search still finds 210 `uses:` entries pinned to tags rather than immutable SHAs.
