The scheduled `coverity-scan-fixes.yml` workflow uses `actions/checkout@v4` and passes `secrets.COVERITY_SCAN_TOKEN` / `secrets.COVERITY_SCAN_EMAIL`, so compromise is not just theoretical build drift. - impact: CI compromise, secret exfiltration, and malicious artifact or release generation via mutable third-party/self-hosted action refs. - recommendation: check in full commit-SHA pins for all external and org actions now and use Renovate only to keep those SHA pins updated; do not rely on the preset alone to secure current workflows. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - `renovate.json` is otherwise a 2-line preset wrapper; this pass did not confirm a stronger chunk-local complexity or performance issue beyond the still-unpinned workflow action refs above - location: `renovate.json` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the file contains only `config:recommended` plus `helpers:pinGitHubActionDigests`; adjacent repo search was needed to assess whether that preset was actually reflected in checked-in workflow refs.

### Medium - `valgrind-error.sh` treats any stderr from the wrapped command as a Valgrind finding, so `USE_VALGRIND` can fail clean tests and discourage the memory-check path it is supposed to enforce - location: `valgrind-error.sh:6-18`, wired from `Makefile.am:266-268` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the wrapper runs `valgrind ... "$@" 2> $TMP`, then `cat`s that file and forces `result=1` whenever it is non-empty. The wrapped test process inherits the same stderr redirection, so normal child stderr is indistinguishable from Valgrind diagnostics. `SCRIPTS-LIST:41` even labels the helper as deprecated, but `TESTS_ENVIRONMENT` still uses it when `USE_VALGRIND` is enabled. - impact: tests/examples that legitimately write to stderr produce false Valgrind failures, making the valgrind lane noisy and less likely to be trusted or run, which in turn weakens regression detection for real memory bugs. - recommendation: use Valgrind's own error exit code handling and/or separate child stderr from tool diagnostics; if this wrapper is obsolete, remove the `TESTS_ENVIRONMENT` hook. - reference: `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `pull_to_vagrant.sh` reuses `.gitignore` as an rsync transport filter, so tracked files outside its tiny manual allowlist can silently stop updating in the guest - location: `pull_to_vagrant.sh:5-14`, `Vagrantfile:25-30`, `.gitignore:13-15,50` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the helper does `rsync --exclude-from .gitignore -rvt /$SRC/ ~/$DST/` and then only re-syncs `.git`, `IDE`, `mcapi`, `mplabx`, `certs`, and `configure.ac`. The repo ignore file excludes names like `configure`, `config.*`, and `fips_test.c`, while also relying on gitignore-specific negation for tracked exceptions such as `!cmake/config.in`; this script is auto-added to the Vagrant shell startup flow. - impact: guest builds/debug sessions can drift from the host checkout after edits to tracked files not covered by the manual re-copy list, causing stale configuration or code in the VM and hard-to-reproduce build/test results. - recommendation: stop using `.gitignore` as sync policy; drive the copy from git-aware file lists (`git ls-files`, `git archive`) or a dedicated rsync filter that explicitly models what the VM needs. - reference: `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `fips-hash.sh`, `CMakePresets.json`, and `.editorconfig` did not expose a stronger chunk-local security issue than the valgrind-wrapper false-positive path and the Vagrant sync drift above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `fips-hash.sh` is a narrow build helper around the FIPS hash update flow already wired into distro packaging, while `CMakePresets.json` and `.editorconfig` are small build/editor metadata with no clearer trust-boundary break or material production-impacting behavior in this pass.

### Medium - `examples/async/async_client` disables certificate and hostname verification by default, so any TLS-speaking host can impersonate the requested server (including the README’s external `ecc256.badssl.com` example) - location: `examples/async/async_client.c:350-387,393-404,423-448`, `examples/async/README.md:39-42` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: user runs the sample client as documented against any non-local host or through an active network attacker. - evidence: unless `--mutual` is passed, the client does `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE, NULL)` and never calls `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`. It still sends SNI for the attacker-chosen `host` and the README explicitly demonstrates `./examples/async/async_client --x25519 ecc256.badssl.com 443`. - impact: the sample authenticates no server identity at all, so a MITM or wrong endpoint can complete the handshake and read/modify application data while the client prints a successful negotiated cipher/group. - recommendation: load a trust store and enable `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` by default for server authentication independent of client-auth mode, then bind the expected host with `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` (or IP verification equivalent); keep `--mutual` only for optional client-certificate auth. - reference: `v5.0.0-V12`, `v5.0.0-V15` (ASVS 5.0 lookup)

### Medium - `examples/async/async_server` exposes an unauthenticated network shutdown command, so any client that can open a TLS session can terminate the server with a single message - location: `examples/async/async_server.c:409-413,542-544` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can reach the listening port; default sample mode leaves client auth disabled. - evidence: when `--mutual` is not set the server config is `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE, NULL)`, and after `wolfSSL_read()` it does `if (strncmp(buff, "shutdown", 8) == 0) { ... mShutdown = 1; }`. - impact: the demo protocol contains a trivial remote DoS path; any unauthenticated network client can stop the process immediately after handshake/data exchange. - recommendation: remove the message-triggered shutdown behavior from the network path, or gate it behind an explicit local/admin control that is not reachable from normal client traffic. - reference: `v5.0.0-V2`, `v5.0.0-V15` (ASVS 5.0 lookup)

### Medium - KCAPI TLS hashing is documented as requiring `WOLFSSL_KCAPI_HASH_KEEP`, but that mode buffers the entire transcript and duplicates it on every hash copy, turning TLS handshakes into O(transcript) memory plus repeated full-buffer copies - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/kcapi/README.md:69-77,144-149`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/kcapi/kcapi_hash.c:92-132,157-177,180-214,219-249`, TLS callers `src/internal.c:7490-7500,10611-10631,24076-24088`, `src/tls13.c:431-449,1385-1406,12144-12217`, `src/ssl_crypto.c:1447-1494` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the README says `WOLFSSL_KCAPI_HASH_KEEP` is `required for TLS`. In that mode `KcapiHashUpdate()` reallocates and appends every byte into `hash->msg`, `KcapiHashGet()` replays the full buffered message into a fresh KCAPI hash each time, and `KcapiHashCopy()` allocates `src->len` bytes and clones the entire transcript buffer. TLS 1.2/1.3 codepaths repeatedly call `GetHash()` and `Copy()` around transcript hashing. - impact: large certificate chains/handshakes and concurrent connections pay avoidable heap growth and repeated whole-transcript memcpy/replay costs, making the KCAPI TLS path materially slower and more memory-hungry than the streaming software path. - recommendation: avoid the transcript-buffering fallback for TLS by implementing stateful KCAPI hash clone/snapshot support, or fence the KCAPI hash backend away from TLS until partial-hash/copy can be done without retaining and duplicating the whole message. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - KCAPI DH silently ignores the public API’s `priv`/`privSz` inputs and outputs, so the generated private key is never written to the caller buffer and later agreement depends on opaque handle state instead of the documented secret key bytes - location: `wolfcrypt/src/dh.c:1977-1999,2283-2296`, KCAPI backend `wolfcrypt/src/port/kcapi/kcapi_dh.c:70-92,113-137`, public API docs `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/dh.h:148-149`, `doc/dox_comments/header_files/dh.h:71-95` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the KCAPI path in `wc_DhGenerateKeyPair()` explicitly discards `priv` and `privSz` with `(void)priv; (void)privSz;` and only calls `KcapiDh_MakeKey(key, pub, pubSz)`. The KCAPI path in `wc_DhAgree()` again discards `priv`/`privSz` and calls `KcapiDh_SharedSecret(key, otherPub, pubSz, agree, agreeSz)`. The public header/docs still advertise `priv`/`privSz` as the generated private key material for later use. - impact: callers get a successful return without a usable exported private key, so key persistence/import/export and any workflow that expects `wc_DhGenerateKeyPair()` to populate `priv` breaks or becomes tied to the lifetime of the in-memory KCAPI handle. - recommendation: either preserve the documented API contract by exporting/importing the private key bytes through the KCAPI path, or fail closed / expose a separate handle-owned API instead of pretending the caller buffer was populated. - reference: `v5.0.0-V15` (ASVS 5.0 lookup), `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Medium - KCAPI AES-GCM mis-handles `posix_memalign()` failures as success, so allocation errors can fall through into NULL/invalid-buffer use instead of a clean `MEMORY_E` - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/kcapi/kcapi_aes.c:272-280,295-297,385-393,405-407` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: KCAPI AES-GCM build without `KCAPI_USE_XMALLOC`; allocation fails (for example under memory pressure). - evidence: both encrypt and decrypt do `ret = posix_memalign((void*)&data, pageSz, dataSz); if (ret < 0) ret = MEMORY_E;`, but POSIX `posix_memalign()` returns non-zero positive error codes on failure. The code therefore treats ENOMEM/EINVAL as success, proceeds into `kcapi_aead_setkey(...)`, and then unconditionally copies into `data` with `XMEMCPY(data, authIn, authInSz)`. - impact: low-memory or invalid-alignment conditions can crash the process or corrupt control flow in the AES-GCM path instead of returning a handled error, turning resource exhaustion into a harder failure mode. - recommendation: treat any non-zero `posix_memalign()` result as failure (`ret != 0`), set `data = NULL` defensively on error, and add a regression that forces allocation failure on the KCAPI AES-GCM path. - reference: `v5.0.0-V15` (ASVS 5.0 lookup)

### Reviewed - `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/SK-S7G2/wolfssl_lib/configuration.xml`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/kcapi/kcapi_{ecc,hmac,rsa}.c`, and `examples/async/{Makefile,async_tls.[ch],user_settings.h,include.am}` were otherwise mostly board/build glue or thin backend wrappers in this pass; the promoted issues were the async example trust/DoS problems and the KCAPI hash/DH/AES failure-mode issues above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining chunk-local concerns were mostly sample/build drift or lower-signal wrapper quirks without a clearer exploit path or production-impacting failure mode than the findings appended here.

### Reviewed - `ChangeLog.md` was historical release metadata only; spot checks of named symbols/CVEs mapped to already-tracked issues or fixed-version notes, and this chunk did not justify a new current-branch finding - location: `ChangeLog.md` with adjacent symbol checks in `src/internal.c`, `src/ssl_load.c`, `wolfcrypt/src/asn.c`, `IDE/STM32Cube/default_conf.ftl`, and `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_client/main/client-tls.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the changelog mostly records past vulnerabilities and feature flags. Targeted searches tied `CVE-2025-7395` to the Apple native-validation path already covered by existing Apple/system-CA review work, while `WOLFSSL_NO_CRL_DATE_CHECK` / `NO_ASN_TIME_CHECK`, `HAVE_NULL_CIPHER`, and `WOLFSSL_NO_CLIENT_AUTH` all still exist in code/config surfaces that are already represented in the inbox. No new exploit path or production failure mode was established from the release-note prose itself beyond those existing findings.

### High - devcrypto AES-GCM backend violates the public buffer contract and can write past caller buffers on both encrypt and decrypt - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/devcrypto/devcrypto_aes.c:309-335,342-364`, public API `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:621` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography` - recommendation: do not mutate or extend the caller's `in`/`authTag` buffers; allocate a scratch buffer for `ciphertext||tag` when the kernel API requires contiguous input, and preserve the caller-requested tag length on encrypt instead of forcing 16 bytes. - status: candidate - severity: high - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_DEVCRYPTO` AES-GCM build; decrypt path with a ciphertext buffer sized to exactly `sz`, or encrypt path with a valid short GCM tag buffer (for example 12-15 bytes). - evidence: decrypt does `XMEMCPY(in + sz, authTag, authTagSz);` before `CIOCAUTHCRYPT`, even though the API already passes `authTag` separately and the file comments `it is assumed that "in" buffer has enough room for cipher text + tag`. Encrypt accepts `authTagSz >= WOLFSSL_MIN_AUTH_TAG_SZ`, then overwrites `authTagSz = WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE` and copies 16 bytes out with `XMEMCPY(authTag, out + sz, authTagSz);`. - impact: heap/stack out-of-bounds writes during AES-GCM operations, with attacker-reachable memory corruption on decrypting network ciphertext and local memory corruption on shortened-tag encrypt callers.

### Medium - RZN2L TLS client loads a CA but never enables peer or hostname verification, so any reachable server can impersonate the target - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RZN2L/test/src/test/wolf_client.c:41-72,144`; default ctx behavior `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: call `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)` and bind the expected DNS name/IP with the hostname-verification API before `wolfSSL_connect()`. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or run a TLS server at `SERVER_IP`. - evidence: the sample only loads a CA via `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations()` / `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer()` and then calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`; there is no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(... WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER ...)` or `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` on this path, while wolfSSL context creation defaults to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE`. - impact: the client authenticates nobody, so a wrong server or on-path attacker can terminate TLS locally and read/modify application traffic.

### Medium - RZN2L TLS client writes one byte past its 256-byte stack buffer when the peer returns a full-sized read - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RZN2L/test/src/test/wolf_client.c:159-164` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: reserve space for the terminator (`wolfSSL_read(..., BUFF_SIZE - 1)`) or guard `ret >= BUFF_SIZE` before writing `rcvBuff[ret] = '\0'`; treat the buffer as binary when length is explicit. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: connected peer sends exactly 256 bytes in one application record; the same sample currently accepts unauthenticated peers by default. - evidence: `char rcvBuff[BUFF_SIZE]` with `BUFF_SIZE 256`; `wolfSSL_read(ssl, rcvBuff, BUFF_SIZE)` may return `256`; the next line unconditionally does `rcvBuff[ret] = '\0';`. - impact: attacker-controlled response length can corrupt adjacent stack state or crash the embedded client after handshake completion.

### Medium - FreeBSD kernel vector-register save path can return after `fpu_kern_enter()` without a matching leave, leaving the current thread in kernel-FPU mode with migration/preemption disabled - location: `bsdkm/x86_vecreg.c:164-175` with invariant comments at `:164-167` and `:45-46` - category: complexity - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: complexity very bad` - recommendation: on every error after `wolfkmod_fpu_kern_enter()` call `fpu_kern_leave()` before returning, and reset/check per-CPU bookkeeping atomically so stale state cannot strand the thread in a half-entered FPU section. - status: candidate - severity: medium - evidence: after `wolfkmod_fpu_kern_enter();`, the code comments say migration and soft preemption are disabled; if `fpu_states[PCPU_GET(cpuid)].nest != 0 || td_tid != 0`, the function logs and immediately `return (EINVAL);` without `wolfkmod_fpu_kern_leave()`. - impact: a stale bookkeeping state can pin the calling kernel thread in kernel-FPU mode and cause hangs, scheduler pathologies, or hard-to-debug crypto failures in AES-NI / benchmark paths.

### Medium - RZN2L demo server reply handling is length-confused: it computes `strlen` on a single character and later reuses client-controlled read length for a fixed reply string - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RZN2L/test/src/test/wolf_server.c:113-114,163-176`; macro contract `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/types.h:975` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: use a dedicated `replyLen = XSTRLEN(reply)` once, keep it separate from the inbound read length, and bound copies/writes by the actual reply size. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: startup hits the bad `XSTRLEN(*reply)` path immediately; if the sample is patched enough to accept connections, any client that completes TLS can choose `len` via `wolfSSL_read()`. - evidence: `reply` is a fixed string, but the code does `len = (word32)XSTRLEN(*reply);` even though `XSTRLEN(s1)` expands to `strlen((s1))`; later `len` is overwritten by `wolfSSL_read(...)`, then reused as `memcpy(buff, reply, len);` and `wolfSSL_write(ssl, buff, len)`. - impact: the sample can fault before serving traffic, and if the startup bug is corrected without fixing the second length mixup it will over-read past the reply literal and send garbage/adjacent memory when the client sends a longer message.

### Reviewed - `bsdkm/{wolfkmod.c,wolfkmod_aes.c,Makefile,README.md,include.am,bsdkm_wc_port.h}`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/devcrypto/{devcrypto_rsa.c,devcrypto_hash.c,devcrypto_hmac.c,devcrypto_x25519.c,README.md,wc_devcrypto.c,devcrypto_ecdsa.c}`, and `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RZN2L/test/src/test/wolfssl_rsip_unit_test.c` were otherwise mostly build glue, backend wrappers, or test scaffolding in this pass; the sharper chunk-local issues promoted were the devcrypto AES-GCM buffer-contract break, the RZN2L demo verification/bounds/reply bugs, and the bsdkm FPU-state unwind hazard above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining observations in this chunk were lower-signal test-harness or backend-consistency nits without a clearer exploit path or production-impacting failure mode than the findings appended here.

### Medium - CMake typo breaks encrypted-key dependency wiring, so `-DWOLFSSL_ENCKEYS=yes` still leaves password-based decryption support off and encrypted PEM/PKCS#8 loads fail at runtime - location: `CMakeLists.txt:1951-1964,1992-2003`; adjacent guards `src/ssl_load.c:1159-1223,1345-1356,2403`; parity reference `configure.ac:8832-8873`, CI workaround `.github/workflows/cmake.yml:49,62` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the CMake option is `WOLFSSL_ENCKEYS` and it adds `-DWOLFSSL_ENCRYPTED_KEYS`, but the later PWDBASED auto-enable check looks for nonexistent `WOLFSSL_ENC_KEYS`. The encrypted-key loader paths are compiled only under `defined(WOLFSSL_ENCRYPTED_KEYS) && !defined(NO_PWDBASED)`. Autotools wires `ENCKEYS` to `PWDBASED` correctly, and the repo's own CMake CI compensates by turning both knobs on separately. - impact: CMake builds can claim encrypted-key support yet reject encrypted private key files unless operators discover the hidden second flag, causing production startup failures and build-system parity drift. - recommendation: change the dependency check to `WOLFSSL_ENCKEYS`, add a regression that `-DWOLFSSL_ENCKEYS=yes` implies `!NO_PWDBASED`, and keep CMake/autotools option names aligned. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - CMake's OpenSSL-coexist gating is effectively broken because it has no supported coexist option and checks two different undeclared variable names - location: `CMakeLists.txt:580,2137`; adjacent parity/reference `configure.ac:2758-2767,9998`, `.github/workflows/opensslcoexist.yml:22,33` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the only CMake references are `WOLFSSL_OPENSSLCOEXIST` and `WOLFSSL_OPENSSL_COEXIST`, but this file defines no `add_option(...)` for either name. Autotools and CI do support `--enable-opensslcoexist` and map it to `-DOPENSSL_COEXIST`. As written, CMake consumers cannot enable coexist in a supported way, and manually setting one cache variable leaves the other gate false. - impact: CMake cannot faithfully reproduce the repo's tested OpenSSL-coexist build; consumers can end up with inconsistent `OPENSSL_EXTRA`/old-name behavior and symbol-compatibility drift when trying to coexist with system OpenSSL. - recommendation: add one supported CMake option mapped to `-DOPENSSL_COEXIST`, use that exact variable name in every CMake gate, and add a CMake CI job mirroring the autotools coexist workflow. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Reviewed - `CMakeLists.txt` mainly exposed build-parity/configuration bugs rather than a stronger default-path runtime security issue in this pass - location: `CMakeLists.txt` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the concrete chunk-local problems were the encrypted-key dependency typo and the broken OpenSSL-coexist knob above. Other sharp edges seen here (for example the explicit `WOLFSSL_KEYLOG_EXPORT` opt-in, duplicate LMS option declaration, and source-tree `options.h` cleanup logic) looked intentional or lower-signal maintainability debt without a clearer exploit path or material failure mode on this pass.

### Medium - Windows solution's shipped `echoclient` example loads CA files but then disables peer verification and never binds an expected hostname, so any reachable TLS endpoint can impersonate the server - location: `wolfssl64.sln:12`, `INSTALL:32-34`, `examples/echoclient/echoclient.c:143,151-161,215,231` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: user builds/runs the documented Windows Visual Studio solution; attacker can MITM the network path or operate a different server the client can reach. - evidence: `wolfssl64.sln` includes `examples\echoclient\echoclient.vcxproj`, and `INSTALL` tells Windows users to use `wolfssl64.sln`. The sample loads CA cert files with `SSL_CTX_load_verify_locations(...)`, but then explicitly calls `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE, 0);` before `SSL_connect(ssl)`, and repo search found no hostname-binding call such as `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` in this client. The same sample also hardcodes `SSLv3_client_method()`. - impact: the sample authenticates no server identity, so a MITM or wrong endpoint can terminate the session and read/modify traffic while appearing to complete a TLS connection successfully. - recommendation: default the sample to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER`, require/configure the expected DNS name or IP with the hostname-verification API before connect, and gate any insecure lab-only mode behind an explicit test macro/CLI switch instead of shipping it in the default Windows example path. - reference: `v5.0.0-V12`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - Windows solution's shipped `echoserver` example exposes an unauthenticated network `quit` command that terminates the server process - location: `wolfssl64.sln:10`, `INSTALL:32-34`, `examples/echoserver/echoserver.c:160,346,370` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can reach the sample listener; the sample does not configure client-certificate authentication. - evidence: `wolfssl64.sln` includes `examples\echoserver\echoserver.vcxproj`, and `INSTALL` directs Windows users to that solution. The server reads attacker-controlled bytes with `wolfSSL_read(ssl, command, sizeof(command)-1)` and then exits its loop on `if ( strncmp(command, "quit", 4) == 0) { ... break; }`. Repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(...)` / client-auth setup in this sample, so any client that can complete the handshake can send the shutdown token. - impact: any network client can trivially stop the demo server, turning the shipped sample into a one-packet remote DoS pattern that is easy to cargo-cult into derived code. - recommendation: remove message-triggered shutdown from the network protocol, or restrict it to an authenticated admin/debug path compiled only for tests; if remote control is required, require explicit application-layer authentication and authorization. - reference: `v5.0.0-V2`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Reviewed - this docs/build-metadata chunk was otherwise dominated by license/readme/project scaffolding; beyond the shipped Windows echo client/server issues above, this pass did not confirm a stronger repo-specific exploit or production-impacting failure mode in `LPCExpresso.{cproject,project}`, `README{,.md,-async}`, `COPYING`, `LICENSING`, `.gitignore`, `.codespellexcludelines`, `.cyignore`, `SCRIPTS-LIST`, `wnr-example.conf`, `valgrind-bash.supp`, `quit`, `AUTHORS`, or `stamp-h.in` - location: listed files with adjacent checks in `examples/{echoclient,echoserver}/*.c` and `src/ssl.c` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: most remaining files were documentation, license text, ignore lists, IDE metadata, or static sample inputs; the only chunk-local trust-boundary behaviors that promoted cleanly were the Visual Studio-shipped echo examples’ authentication/shutdown paths.

### Medium - `wc_Sha512_224Final()` masks finalize failures and can report success with an invalid digest - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/cypress/psoc6_crypto.c:617-635`; contrast `:581-586,669-687` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: PSoC6 SHA-512/224 build hits a crypto-mutex or hardware finalize failure. - evidence: unlike neighboring finals, `wc_Sha512_224Final()` calls `Cy_Crypto_Core_Sha_Finish(...)` and then unconditionally `return wc_InitSha512_224(sha);`; there is no `if (ret != 0) return ret;`. A lock/finalize error is overwritten by the reinit result. - impact: callers can treat a failed digest finalization as success and continue with stale/garbage hash output, breaking integrity checks and making auth failures hard to diagnose. - recommendation: preserve and return `ret` on lock/finalize failure before reinitializing, matching `wc_Sha512Final()` / `wc_Sha512_256Final()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - exported PSoC6 GCM decrypt helper trusts caller-sized tags and can overflow its fixed 16-byte stack buffer - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/cypress/psoc6_crypto.c:1942-2019`; exported in `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/cypress/psoc6_crypto.h:135` and installed via `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/include.am:127`; generic wrapper guard `wolfcrypt/src/aes.c:10700-10703,10771` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: application calls the installed port-specific `wc_Psoc6_Aes_GcmDecrypt()` directly instead of `wc_AesGcmDecrypt()`, and attacker can influence `authTagSz > 16`. - evidence: the function allocates `byte computedTag[AES_BLOCK_SIZE];`, then passes caller-controlled `authTagSz` to `Cy_Crypto_Core_Aes_GCM_Finish(..., computedTag, authTagSz, ...)` and `ConstantCompare(computedTag, authTag, authTagSz)` with no local bounds check. The standard `wc_AesGcmDecrypt()` wrapper validates `authTagSz <= WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE`, but direct users of the exported port API bypass that guard. - impact: oversized tag lengths can write and read past `computedTag`, causing stack corruption or crashes in the decrypt path. - recommendation: reject `authTagSz == 0 || authTagSz > AES_BLOCK_SIZE` and null pointers inside the port helper, or make the helper internal-only so callers must use the validated generic API. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - PSoC6 ECDSA verification races the shared hardware block because the ECC path skips the crypto mutex entirely - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/cypress/psoc6_crypto.c:86,2059-2169`; dispatch `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:9194-9200`; docs `wolfcrypt/src/port/cypress/README.md:141-142` - category: security/complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: multithreaded PSoC6 build performs ECDSA verify concurrently with AES/SHA or another ECC verify on the same accelerator. - evidence: the port uses one global `static CRYPTO_Type* crypto_base`; hash/AES paths repeatedly wrap hardware access with `wolfSSL_CryptHwMutexLock()` / `wolfSSL_CryptHwMutexUnLock()`, but `psoc6_ecc_verify_hash_ex()` calls `Cy_Crypto_Core_ECC_MakePublicKey()` and `Cy_Crypto_Core_ECC_VerifyHash()` without any lock. The README explicitly says hash/AES are mutex-protected while ECC is not. - impact: concurrent certificate/signature verification can stomp shared accelerator state, causing intermittent verify failures, corrupted results, or crashes in auth-critical paths such as TLS/X.509 verification. - recommendation: take the same hardware mutex around the full ECC hardware sequence, or provide a dedicated serialized ECC context instead of exposing unsynchronized shared hardware through the standard verify API. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Low - `psoc6_ecc_verify_hash_ex()` returns positive `173` instead of `BAD_FUNC_ARG`, breaking wolfCrypt's negative-error contract on invalid inputs - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/cypress/psoc6_crypto.c:2075,2087`; definition `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/error-crypt.h:144`; caller entry `wolfcrypt/src/ecc.c:9194-9200` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the function returns `-BAD_FUNC_ARG`, but `BAD_FUNC_ARG` is already defined as `-173`, so these invalid-argument paths return `+173` instead of the expected negative error code. - impact: callers that key off standard negative wolfCrypt error semantics can mis-handle invalid-input failures, and platform-specific error drift makes diagnostics/tests less reliable. - recommendation: return `BAD_FUNC_ARG` directly and add a PSoC6 regression for NULL and oversize-key error paths. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `wolfcrypt/src/port/cypress/README.md` mostly matched the implementation surface; the concrete chunk-local issues promoted here were the SHA-512/224 error-masking bug, the exported GCM tag-length overflow, and the unsynchronized ECC hardware path - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/cypress/{psoc6_crypto.c,README.md}` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining deltas in this chunk were mainly documentation quality or port-helper robustness nits without a stronger trust-boundary break or material production failure mode than the findings above.

### Low - shipped Win32 DLL Debug Visual Studio projects explicitly disable SafeSEH, weakening 32-bit exploit mitigations in debug DLL builds - location: `wolfssl.vcxproj:233`, `wolfssl-VS2022.vcxproj:234` with downstream consumers in `examples/{client,echoclient,echoserver,server}/*.vcxproj`, `testsuite/testsuite.vcxproj`, `wolfcrypt/{benchmark,test}/*VS2022*.sln` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: developer builds or ships the provided `DLL Debug|Win32` configuration and an attacker can trigger a memory-corruption bug in the hosting 32-bit Windows process. - evidence: both project files set `<ImageHasSafeExceptionHandlers>false</ImageHasSafeExceptionHandlers>` on the only Win32 DLL debug link configuration; repo solutions/examples reference these library projects directly. - impact: SEH-based exploitation on 32-bit Windows becomes easier because the linker omits SafeSEH metadata that would otherwise constrain exception-handler hijacking; this does not affect x64/ARM64 where SafeSEH is irrelevant. - recommendation: remove the explicit disable and keep SafeSEH enabled for Win32 DLL builds; if a specific object is incompatible, isolate that requirement to the minimal legacy target instead of the shared shipped project. - reference: `v5.0.0-V13`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Reviewed - Visual Studio project drift in this chunk was mostly feature/support metadata rather than a sharper trust-boundary break: both `.vcxproj` files force `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` with the small `IDE/WIN/user_settings.h`, and `.wolfssl_known_macro_extras` appears to be an unreferenced macro inventory file on this pass - location: `wolfssl.vcxproj`, `wolfssl-VS2022.vcxproj`, `.wolfssl_known_macro_extras` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: repo search found direct consumers for the two `.vcxproj` library projects, but no tree-local reference to `.wolfssl_known_macro_extras`; the main concrete issue promoted from this chunk was the explicit SafeSEH disable above, while other drift such as source-list differences looked more like build-maintenance debt than a clearer exploitable path in the reviewed configuration.

### Medium - Source distribution ships reusable PEM private keys (including a CA key) that examples, tests, and docs reference directly, so sample-derived deployments are trivially impersonable - location: `certs/{ca-key.pem,server-key.pem,server-keyPkcs8.pem,server-keyPkcs8Enc.pem,server-keyPkcs8Enc2.pem,server-keyPkcs8Enc12.pem,entity-no-ca-bool-key.pem}` with distribution wiring in `certs/include.am:5-78,129-142`; representative consumers `examples/sctp/sctp-server-dtls.c:46`, `sslSniffer/README.md:123`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RZN2L/README.md:435-448` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator, developer, or package reuses the shipped `certs/` material or follows the documented sample paths in anything reachable by other parties. - evidence: the repo commits raw private keys, including `certs/ca-key.pem` and `certs/server-key.pem`; `certs/include.am` adds them to `EXTRA_DIST`; adjacent examples/docs point at these exact files as ready-to-use defaults. Anyone with the public repo/source tarball therefore has the same server key and CA key material as the deployment. - impact: attackers can impersonate sample-derived servers directly with the known private key, and the published CA key lets them mint certificates trusted by sample-derived clients or mTLS peers. - recommendation: keep private keys only in non-distributed test fixtures, never ship CA/private keys in release/source dist payloads, and make examples require user-supplied cert/key paths instead of defaulting to shared credentials. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Reviewed - this cert-only chunk mostly contained test fixture certificates/keys; the only chunk-local issue worth promoting was the distributed shared PEM key material above, while the remaining public certs and encrypted-key variants did not show a sharper standalone exploit path on this pass - location: `certs/{client-ca-cert.pem,client-crl-dist.pem,server-ecc-rsa.pem,client-ecc-cert.pem,server-ecc-comp.pem,ca-ecc384-cert.pem,server-ecc-self.pem,ca-ecc-cert.pem,server-ecc.pem,client-ecc-ca-cert.pem,server-keyPkcs8Enc2.pem,server-keyPkcs8Enc12.pem,server-keyPkcs8Enc.pem,entity-no-ca-bool-key.pem,server-keyPkcs8.pem,server-key.pem,ca-key.pem}` - category: security review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks against `certs/include.am`, sample code, tests, and docs found broad reuse of the shipped PEM identities but no stronger additional chunk-local failure mode beyond that shared-key distribution risk.

### Medium - TI `wc_AesEncryptDirect()`/`wc_AesDecryptDirect()` are implemented as CBC, so repeated direct-block AES calls silently chain on the prior ciphertext and break CMAC / other direct-AES consumers - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/ti/ti-aes.c:282-290`, CBC IV mutation `wolfcrypt/src/port/ti/ti-aes.c:151-166`, public API contract `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:600-601`, doc example `doc/dox_comments/header_files/aes.h:243-249`, affected caller `wolfcrypt/src/cmac.c:167,174,244,346,351` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_TI_CRYPT` build uses `wc_AesEncryptDirect()` directly or indirectly via CMAC/direct-AES helpers. - evidence: the TI port’s direct APIs call `AesProcess(..., AES_CFG_MODE_CBC)` instead of ECB/direct mode. `AesAlign16()` updates `aes->reg` on every CBC call, so the second and later `wc_AesEncryptDirect()` invocations on the same `Aes` object are chained CBC operations, not stateless single-block AES as the public API/documentation promise. - impact: CMAC and any other code that relies on direct single-block AES produce wrong outputs after the first block, causing authentication/interoperability failures and making the TI crypto backend cryptographically incorrect for those paths. - recommendation: implement the TI direct path with ECB/no-IV semantics and add regression coverage that performs multiple direct encrypt/decrypt calls and CMAC test vectors on one context. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - TI hash `Copy()` drops the buffered message state, so `wc_ShaCopy()`/`wc_Sha256Copy()` clones digest an empty transcript and can break TLS/HMAC state copying - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/ti/ti-hash.h:39-42`, state update `wolfcrypt/src/port/ti/ti-hash.c:75-93`, hash sink `wolfcrypt/src/port/ti/ti-hash.c:100-115`, broken copy `wolfcrypt/src/port/ti/ti-hash.c:124-131,245-340`, TLS/HMAC users `src/internal.c:7481,7490,12450,24027`, `src/tls13.c:12144,12199`, `src/ssl_crypto.c:1433-1467` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_TI_HASH` build hits any code path that copies an in-progress SHA/SHA256 state, including TLS transcript/HMAC cloning. - evidence: TI hash state stores pending input in `msg/used/len`, and `hashGetHash()` hashes `hash->msg` with `hash->used`. But `hashCopy()` zeros `dst->msg`, `dst->used`, and `dst->len` and copies only `dst->hash`; this backend never populates `hash[]` anywhere else. Internal TLS code relies on `wc_ShaCopy()`/`wc_Sha256Copy()` to snapshot transcript state. - impact: copied TI hash states lose the accumulated message and later compute a digest for empty/truncated data, causing Finished/HMAC/transcript operations to fail on TI-hash builds. - recommendation: deep-copy `msg/used/len` (or clone hardware hash state) instead of copying the unused `hash[]` buffer; if that is not possible, fence the TI hash backend away from callers that require `Copy()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - TI CCM/HASH init marks the backend globally initialized before success, so a first-time peripheral or mutex init failure becomes a sticky false-success for all later crypto calls - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/ti/ti-ccm.c:38-70` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `wolfSSL_TI_CCMInit()` does `if (ccm_init) return true; ccm_init = true;` before checking `ROM_SysCtlPeripheralPresent(...)`, peripheral readiness, or `wc_InitMutex(&TI_CCM_Mutex)`. Any failure returns `false` but leaves `ccm_init` set, so later callers skip initialization and return success immediately. - impact: unsupported hardware, transient bring-up failure, or mutex init failure can leave later AES/HASH operations running against absent/uninitialized CCM state or an uninitialized mutex instead of failing closed, causing hangs/faults that are hard to reason about. - recommendation: only set `ccm_init` after all init steps succeed, clear it on failure, and use one-time/thread-safe initialization that cannot race into partial state. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - INtime `wolfExamples` TLS client loads a CA but never enables peer or hostname verification, so any reachable TLS server can impersonate the configured remote host - location: `IDE/INTIME-RTOS/wolfExamples.c:87-107`, default ctx behavior `src/ssl.c:512`, sample guidance `IDE/INTIME-RTOS/README.md:104-110` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: operator runs the shipped INtime client example against a non-local host or across an attacker-controlled network. - evidence: the client loads `ca_cert_der_2048` with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer()` and then immediately calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`, but never calls `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)` or `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`. wolfSSL context init defaults to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE`. - impact: a MITM or wrong endpoint can complete the handshake and read/modify application traffic while the sample appears to be using TLS. - recommendation: enable `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER`, bind the expected DNS name/IP before connect, and fail closed when no expected peer identity is configured. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - INtime `wolfExamples` client logs a full-size TLS read as a C string, so a 4096-byte peer reply causes an out-of-bounds read past the stack buffer - location: `IDE/INTIME-RTOS/wolfExamples.c:60,118-125` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: connected server (or MITM accepted via the missing verification above) returns exactly `TLS_MAXDATASIZE` bytes in one read. - evidence: the client allocates `char rcvBuff[TLS_MAXDATASIZE]`, calls `wolfSSL_read(ssl, rcvBuff, TLS_MAXDATASIZE)`, and then unconditionally prints `printf("Received: \t%s\n", rcvBuff);`. A full 4096-byte read leaves no terminating NUL inside the array. - impact: attacker-controlled reply length can leak adjacent stack memory into logs or crash the sample task. - recommendation: reserve space for a terminator (`TLS_MAXDATASIZE - 1`) or print using the returned length (`%.*s` / binary-safe logging). - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V1 Encoding and Sanitization`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - ESP-IDF `wolfssl_server` exposes an unauthenticated network shutdown command, so any client that can complete a normal TLS handshake can stop the server with one message - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_server/main/server-tls.c:314-396`, no client-auth setup in file, default verify mode `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can reach the listening port; the sample does not enable client-certificate auth. - evidence: inside the main accept loop the server accepts any TLS client, reads application data, and does `if (strncmp(buff, "shutdown", 8) == 0) shutdown = 1;`. This file never calls `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)`, so the command is not bound to an authenticated admin identity. - impact: any network client can terminate the example service on demand after a successful handshake, yielding a trivial remote DoS path. - recommendation: remove message-triggered shutdown from the network protocol, or gate it behind an explicit authenticated admin control that is disabled by default in shipped examples. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V2 Validation and Business Logic`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - remaining files in this chunk were mostly build/sample scaffolding once the concrete issues above were accounted for: `wolfcrypt/src/port/ti/ti-des3.c` is a thin DES/3DES wrapper with no stronger new chunk-local exploit confirmed on this pass; `IDE/INTIME-RTOS/{Makefile,libwolfssl.c,wolfssl-lib.vcxproj,libwolfssl.vcxproj,wolfExamples.vcxproj,wolfExamples.sln,wolfssl-lib.sln,include.am,wolfExamples.h,README.md,user_settings.h}` were primarily packaging/config/example glue beyond the INtime client issues above; and `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_server/main/{wifi_connect.c,time_helper.c,main.c,CMakeLists.txt,Kconfig.projbuild,component.mk}` were mostly WiFi/time/build wiring aside from the promoted remote-shutdown behavior (the default test-key material in this example is already covered by the broader installed `certs_test*.h` finding) - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass concentrated on trust-boundary crossings and correctness-critical crypto backend code; no stronger additional exploit path or material production failure mode was confirmed from the remaining chunk-local scaffolding.

### Medium - sample mTLS trust anchors are generated from CA-capable client leaf certs, so any holder of the sample client key can mint fresh client certs accepted by example-derived servers - location: `certs/renewcerts/wolfssl.cnf:125-130`, `certs/renewcerts.sh:208-214,632-636`, generated outputs `certs/client-cert.pem:43,47`, `certs/client-ca.pem:43,47,130,174`; example trust path `examples/server/server.c:1831,3033-3065`, `wolfssl/test.h:517,526` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator enables mutual-auth using the shipped example defaults (`verifyCert = cliCertFile`) or `client-ca.pem`, and reuses the sample client key/cert material. - evidence: the default `wolfssl_opts` profile used for `client-cert.pem` sets `basicConstraints=CA:true` and `extendedKeyUsage=serverAuth, clientAuth`; `client-ca.pem` is then just `cat client-cert.pem client-ecc-cert.pem`; the example server defaults `verifyCert` to `cliCertFile`, and `cliCertFile` resolves to `certs/client-cert.pem` before `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations_ex(...)`. - impact: the sample “client cert” is also a trust anchor, so a client that possesses that private key can sign additional client certificates/keys and keep authenticating under arbitrary new identities instead of being limited to one issued leaf. - recommendation: generate dedicated CA certificates for trust stores, mark sample client/server end-entity certs `CA:FALSE` with single-purpose EKUs, and have the examples trust only CA files rather than end-entity client certs. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - `certs/check_dates.sh` cannot return its intended pass/fail status and can exercise stale binaries after failed rebuilds - location: `certs/check_dates.sh:101-122,126` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the script is a standalone `/bin/sh` program but ends with `return $expired` outside any function, which is not a valid script return path in POSIX shells; in the optional future-date branch it runs `./configure --enable-all -q` and `make -s` but never checks either result before executing `faketime "${FUTURE}" ./tests/unit.test`, so a failed rebuild can still run an old test binary. - impact: certificate-expiry automation gets a non-portable/wrong exit code, and future-date validation can false-pass or mislead maintainers by testing stale artifacts instead of the just-requested build. - recommendation: replace the final `return` with `exit`, and fail fast on `configure`/`make` errors before invoking `./tests/unit.test`; a fresh build dir would make the result easier to trust. - reference: `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - `certs/{renewcerts.sh,gen_revoked.sh,wolfssl-website-ca.pem,server-cert.pem,server-revoked-cert.pem,entity-no-ca-bool-cert.pem,client-ca.pem,client-keyEnc.pem,server-keyEnc.pem,ecc-client-key.pem,client-cert.pem,client-cert-ext.pem,ca-cert.pem,client-relative-uri.pem,client-absolute-urn.pem,client-uri-cert.pem}` mainly surfaced sample PKI role confusion and the broken expiry-check helper above; remaining content was mostly test-fixture cert/key material and certificate-generation scaffolding without a sharper chunk-local exploit path on this pass.

### Medium - Release/source packaging still ships raw PEM private keys, including CA signing keys, under `certs/`, so downstream sample-derived builds can deploy attacker-known TLS identities - location: `certs/include.am:5-55,129-140`; raw key material in this chunk includes `certs/client-key.pem`, `certs/server-revoked-key.pem`, `certs/ca-ecc-key.pem`, `certs/ca-ecc384-key.pem` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration` - recommendation: remove private keys from `EXTRA_DIST`/release artifacts, generate test credentials at build/test time, and make examples require user-provided cert/key material instead of shipping reusable defaults. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: integrator/vendor copies the shipped `certs/` tree or reuses sample defaults in a deployed client/server/test harness. - evidence: `certs/include.am` adds raw private keys and CA keys to `EXTRA_DIST` (`certs/ca-key.pem`, `certs/client-key.pem`, `certs/server-key.pem`, `certs/server-revoked-key.pem`, `certs/ca-ecc-key.pem`, `certs/ca-ecc384-key.pem`). This chunk contains the actual PEM private key bodies for several of those files, and sample docs still point users at the same paths (`README-async.md:255-256`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_client/README.md:203-210`). - impact: anyone reusing the packaged material ships public, attacker-known private keys; reuse of the CA keys is worse because attackers can mint certificates that sample-derived clients will trust, breaking TLS/mTLS authentication.

### Reviewed - this cert-data-heavy chunk was otherwise mostly static test vectors and distribution metadata; beyond the continued shipping of reusable private key material above, no stronger chunk-local code-path bug was established from `certs/{*.pem,*.der}` and `certs/taoCert.txt` alone - location: `certs/server-revoked-key.pem`, `certs/client-key.pem`, `certs/dh2048.pem`, `certs/server-ecc384-cert.pem`, `certs/dsa2048.pem`, `certs/client-ecc384-cert.pem`, `certs/empty-issuer-cert.pem`, `certs/csr.dsa.pem`, `certs/x942dh2048.pem`, `certs/dh-priv-2048.pem`, `certs/dh-pub-2048.pem`, `certs/dh4096.pem`, `certs/dh3072.pem`, `certs/ecc-privOnlyCert.pem`, `certs/dsaparams.pem`, `certs/server-keyPub.pem`, `certs/rsa-pub-2048.pem`, `certs/client-keyPub.pem`, `certs/ecc-keyPkcs8Enc.pem`, `certs/client-ecc384-key.pem`, `certs/server-ecc384-key.pem`, `certs/ca-ecc384-key.pem`, `certs/ca-ecc-key.pem`, `certs/ecc-keyPkcs8.pem`, `certs/ecc-client-keyPub.pem`, `certs/ecc-keyPub.pem`, `certs/ecc-key-comp.pem`, `certs/ecc-privkeyPkcs8.pem`, `certs/ecc-privOnlyKey.pem`, `certs/ecc-privkey.pem`, `certs/ecc-params.pem`, `certs/include.am`, `certs/taoCert.txt`, `certs/ecc-privkey.der`, `certs/ecc-params.der` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the files are predominantly known test keys/certs, malformed-cert fixtures, parameter blobs, and package manifests/docs rather than executable logic; searches in adjacent packaging/docs only reinforced the distributed-test-key issue already promoted here.

### High - AF_ALG Xilinx AES-GCM backend writes past caller buffers and mutates `in` despite the public API taking separate `out` and `authTag` buffers - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/af_alg/afalg_aes.c:558,683,759,860,894`, public API `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/aes.h:616,621` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography` - recommendation: keep the public contract intact by using a scratch buffer for `ciphertext||tag`, never appending into caller `in`, and only writing `sz` bytes to `out` while copying the tag separately; also validate exact byte counts on the AF_ALG reads/writes. - status: candidate - severity: high - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_AFALG_XILINX_AES` build; caller sizes buffers per `wc_AesGcmEncrypt()` / `wc_AesGcmDecrypt()` contract; decrypt path is attacker-reachable anywhere untrusted AES-GCM records are fed into this backend. - evidence: the public API exposes separate `out` and `authTag` buffers, but the Xilinx encrypt path explicitly warns `out` must hold `sz | 16 bytes`, then does `read(aes->rdFd, out, sz + WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE)` and copies the tag from `out + sz`; the decrypt path warns `in` must hold ciphertext+tag, does `XMEMCPY((byte*)in + sz, tag, WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE)`, and again `read(aes->rdFd, out, sz + WC_AES_BLOCK_SIZE)`. - impact: heap/stack out-of-bounds writes on both encrypt and decrypt plus input-buffer corruption on decrypt; a hostile ciphertext can turn authenticated-decrypt calls into memory corruption or process crashes.

### Medium - CUDA AES backend does not compile as shipped because the kernel loop uses undefined `index` instead of the computed thread id - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/cuda/aes-cuda.cu:669-673`; docs claim tested at `wolfcrypt/src/port/cuda/README.md:1-7` - category: complexity - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell` - recommendation: change the loop to use `idx`, and add the documented `--enable-cuda` build to CI so this backend cannot silently rot. - status: candidate - severity: medium - evidence: `AesEncrypt_C_CUDA()` computes `int idx = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x;` and `int stride = blockDim.x * gridDim.x;` but then loops with `for (int i = index; i < sz; i += stride)`. Repo search in this file finds no definition of `index`. The same kernel is launched from both `AesEncrypt_C()` and `AesEncryptBlocks_C()`. - impact: the non-bitsliced CUDA AES path fails to build under `--enable-cuda`, leaving a documented accelerator path broken and effectively untested.

### Reviewed - `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/caam/{caam_driver.h,wolfcaam.h,wolfcaam_seco.h,wolfcaam_fsl_nxp.h,wolfcaam_sha.h,wolfcaam_qnx.h,caam_qnx.h,wolfcaam_hash.h,wolfcaam_aes.h,wolfcaam_ecdsa.h,wolfcaam_rsa.h,wolfcaam_x25519.h,wolfcaam_cmac.h,caam_error.h}` and `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/test/{component.mk,README.md,CMakeLists.txt}` were declaration/build scaffolding in this pass; the concrete chunk-local issues promoted were the AF_ALG Xilinx AES-GCM buffer-contract break and the CUDA backend build failure above - location: listed files with adjacent implementation checks in `wolfcrypt/src/port/af_alg/afalg_aes.c` and `wolfcrypt/src/port/cuda/aes-cuda.cu` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: this pass did not confirm a stronger exploit path or production-impacting failure mode in those headers/build files without matching implementation beyond the issues appended here.

### Medium - threaded `snifftest` mutates borrowed key-file strings in place, so the default threaded startup can write into string literals and multi-worker runs race on shared `-key` buffers - location: `sslSniffer/sslSnifferTest/snifftest.c:359-390,655,858,1212-1226,1276` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `load_key()` tokenizes with `XSTRTOK((char*)keyFiles, ",", &ptr)`, which writes NULs into the supplied buffer; `ssl_Init_SnifferWorker()` stores only a borrowed pointer (`worker->keyFilesSrc = (char*)keyFilesSrc`), and each worker thread later calls `load_key(..., worker->keyFilesSrc, ...)`. In the no-`-key` path `keyFilesSrc` is left as `DEFAULT_SERVER_EPH_KEY` / `DEFAULT_SERVER_KEY` string literals, so threaded startup writes into read-only storage; with a user-supplied comma-separated `-key` list, all workers tokenize the same shared buffer concurrently. - impact: threaded sniff-test startup can crash with the default key path, or corrupt/race the key list across workers, making packet decryption depend on undefined behavior rather than local reasoning. - recommendation: copy the key list into worker-owned mutable storage before tokenizing, and keep `load_key()` const-correct instead of casting away const. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Medium - threaded `snifftest` busy-spins when no packets are queued, so the default 5-worker mode burns CPU even while idle - location: `sslSniffer/sslSnifferTest/snifftest.c:613-628,863-937,1051-1053,1276-1384` - category: performance - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the custom `wm_Sem`/`pthread_cond_t` is only used as a mutex-like lock; producers never signal “queue not empty”. `snifferWorker()` loops `while (!worker->shutdown) { while (worker->head) { ... } ... }`, so once `worker->head == NULL` and `shutdown` is still false the thread immediately re-enters the outer loop without any wait/sleep. The threaded build defaults to `workerThreadCount = 5` when session tickets are off. - impact: an idle threaded sniffer can peg several CPU cores just waiting for work, which is a material operational cost for a capture tool that often sits idle between packets. - recommendation: replace the pseudo-semaphore with a real producer/consumer queue that blocks on empty and wakes on packet arrival or shutdown. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - installed CMake package silently falls back to any host `libwolfssl`, so `find_package(wolfssl)` can bind consumers to the wrong binary/version when the packaged library is missing - location: `cmake/wolfssl-targets.cmake.in:4-14` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: a consumer uses the generated installed CMake package and the configured `@WOLFSSL_LIBDIR_ABS@` no longer contains the expected library (partial install, stale package metadata, or hostile build environment with another `libwolfssl` on the search path). - evidence: the target file first searches the configured install dir with `find_library(... PATHS "${_wolfssl_libdir}" NO_DEFAULT_PATH)`, but if that fails it immediately retries plain `find_library(WOLFSSL_LIBRARY NAMES wolfssl)` and still exports `wolfssl::wolfssl` from whatever host library is found. - impact: downstream builds can silently link against an unrelated wolfSSL build with different version, ABI, FIPS state, or hardening options, undermining the trust boundary implied by the package config and potentially reintroducing patched vulnerabilities. - recommendation: fail closed when the configured install-path library is absent, or verify that any resolved fallback path/version still belongs to this package prefix before exporting the imported target. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - `sslSniffer/sslSnifferTest/{sslSniffTest.vcxproj,sslSniffTest.vcproj,README_WIN.md,include.am}` and `cmake/{options.h.in,config.in,README.md,wolfssl-config-version.cmake.in,Config.cmake.in,include.am}` were otherwise mostly sample/build metadata in this pass; the concrete chunk-local issues promoted here were the threaded snifftest shared-buffer/spin-loop hazards and the permissive CMake package fallback above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining content was primarily build templates, generated config headers, or packaging docs without a sharper confirmed exploit path or production failure mode than those findings.

### Medium - Rowley CrossWorks Kinetis image hard-codes the MCU flash security byte to `UNSECURE`, so devices flashed from the shipped project boot with readout/debug protections disabled - location: `IDE/ROWLEY-CROSSWORKS-ARM/kinetis_hw.c:320-339`, linked placement `IDE/ROWLEY-CROSSWORKS-ARM/Kinetis_FlashPlacement.xml:5` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator flashes the provided Rowley image layout onto real hardware; attacker gains physical debug/programming access to the board. - evidence: the project emits a `.flashconf` section, and `flash_conf.fsec` is set to `NV_FSEC_SEC(FSEC_UNSECURE) | NV_FSEC_FSLACC(FSEC_FSLACC_GRANTED) | NV_FSEC_MEEN(FSEC_MASS_ERASE_ENABLE)`. On Kinetis this leaves the device unsecured and Freescale access enabled instead of fusing a protected production state. - impact: physical attackers can read or rewrite firmware and any embedded secrets on sample-derived deployments using this stock flash configuration. - recommendation: do not ship an unsecured flash configuration as the default project state; require an explicit lab-only macro for `UNSECURE`, or move `flash_conf` to a board-local example file with prominent production-hardening instructions. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - ATECC encrypted-ECDH support falls back to a published all-`0xFF` transport key, so sample-derived builds can protect secure-element I2C traffic with a known shared secret - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/atmel/atmel.c:344-350,359-391,419-434,563-568`, default macro in `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/atmel/atmel.h:112-115` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_ATECC_ECDH_ENC` or `WOLFSSL_ATECC_ECDH_IOENC` and the integrator does not override `ATECC_GET_ENC_KEY`; attacker can observe or inject traffic on the host↔ATECC I2C bus or clone the published default configuration. - evidence: `ATECC_GET_ENC_KEY` defaults to `atmel_get_enc_key_default()`, which does `XMEMSET(enckey, 0xFF, keysize)`. `atmel_init_enc_key()` writes that key into the device encryption slot when unlocked, and `atmel_ecc_create_pms()` reuses the same key for `atcab_ecdh_enc()` / `atcab_ecdh_ioenc()`. - impact: the bus-encryption layer meant to protect ECDH commands/results is reduced to a repo-known constant, so attackers with bus access can decrypt or spoof the “protected” secure-element exchange in sample-derived deployments. - recommendation: fail closed unless `ATECC_GET_ENC_KEY` is overridden with a device-unique provisioned secret, and never auto-seed the hardware slot with a fixed constant default. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Medium - Espressif's SRP stack-safety check is dead code because it compares against an undefined macro name, so low-stack SRP builds miss the only warning and are more likely to crash under load - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/Espressif/esp32-crypt.h:1094-1103` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the header computes `ESP_SRP_MINIMUM_STACK_8K` from `FP_MAX_BITS`, but the later guard is `#if (CONFIG_ESP_MAIN_TASK_STACK_SIZE < ESP_SRP_MINIMUM_STACK)`. `ESP_SRP_MINIMUM_STACK` is never defined anywhere in the repo, so the preprocessor treats it as `0` and the warning path never triggers. - impact: the port silently loses its intended compile-time protection against undersized ESP main-task stacks for SRP, increasing the chance of hard-to-diagnose stack overflows/resets in SRP-enabled builds. - recommendation: use one consistent macro name (`ESP_SRP_MINIMUM_STACK_8K` or renamed equivalent) and add a tiny preprocessor regression test so the warning actually fires for undersized configs. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - remaining files in this chunk were mostly project scaffolding, board glue, or helper headers once the concrete issues above were accounted for; this pass did not promote a stronger chunk-local exploit or material performance issue from `IDE/ROWLEY-CROSSWORKS-ARM/{arm_startup.c,retarget.c,benchmark_main.c,test_main.c,wolfssl*.hzp,README.md,include.am,hw.h}`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/Espressif/{esp-sdk-lib.h,esp_crt_bundle.h}`, `IDE/MCUEXPRESSO/RT1170/{fsl_caam_*.patch,user_settings.h}`, or the rest of `wolfcrypt/src/port/atmel/atmel.c` beyond the unsecured Kinetis flash config, the ATECC default transport key, and the broken ESP SRP stack guard above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the CAAM patch additions mainly exposed API-contract/documentation rough edges but not a sharper in-repo exploit path from this chunk, while the remaining Rowley/Espressif files were primarily sample/build metadata or helper declarations without a clearer trust-boundary break than the findings appended here.

### High - ARIA GCM public API violates its own separate-buffer contract and can write past caller buffers on both encrypt and decrypt - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/aria/aria-crypt.c:203-246,254-296`, public API `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/aria/aria-crypt.h:69-74`, internal users `src/internal.c:20356,20831`, `wolfcrypt/src/evp.c:1453-1458` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `HAVE_ARIA` build; caller sizes `out` to ciphertext length and passes `authTag` separately as the exported API signature implies; decrypt is attacker-reachable wherever untrusted ARIA-GCM records are processed. - evidence: the exported signatures take separate `out`/`authTag` and `in`/`authTag` buffers, but encrypt copies the tag from `out + inSz` (`XMEMCPY(authTag, out + inSz, authTagSz)`) and decrypt appends the supplied tag into the caller input buffer (`XMEMCPY((byte*)in + inSz, authTag, authTagSz)`). The file comments admit hidden extra-space requirements (`'out' buffer is expected to be 'inSz + authTagSz'`, `'in' buffer is expected to be 'inSz + authTagSz'`). - impact: direct callers that follow the public header contract can hit heap/stack out-of-bounds writes or input-buffer corruption; decrypting attacker-controlled ciphertext can turn authenticated-decrypt calls into crashes or memory corruption. - recommendation: preserve the public API contract by using an internal scratch buffer for `ciphertext||tag`, never appending into caller `in`, and writing only `inSz` bytes to `out` while copying the tag exclusively via `authTag`. - reference: `v5.0.0-V11`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - MDK shell command parser reads before the buffer on empty input and can overrun its fixed `argv[10]` table with long token lists from the debug console - location: `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/wolfSSL-Full/shell.c:355-381,607` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: operator or attacker with access to the board's debug/printf shell can submit blank lines or more than 10 space-delimited tokens in a <=100-byte command. - evidence: `getline()` does `if(line[strlen(line)-2] == '&')` with no length check, so a one-character line such as just `"\n"` dereferences `line[-1]`. The same function fixes `MAXARGS` at 10 but unconditionally writes `args->argv[args->argc] = &(line[i]); ... args->argc++;` for every parsed token with no bounds check. - impact: malformed console input can crash the embedded shell or corrupt adjacent static state via out-of-bounds reads/writes. - recommendation: reject lines shorter than 2 before the `strlen()-2` access, cap tokenization at `MAXARGS-1` with an explicit error, and stop parsing on consecutive delimiters instead of blindly advancing `argc`. - reference: `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - ESP-IDF `compileAllExamples.sh` can false-pass broken installs and builds because it checks `$?` after `popd`/assignment instead of the saved command status - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/compileAllExamples.sh:31-35,48-52,68-72,87-94` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: after each `idf.py`/`setup.sh` run the script saves `THIS_ERR=$?`, but several branches then test `if [ $? -ne 0 ];` only after `popd` or after the assignment itself. That examines the exit code of `popd`/`THIS_ERR=$?`, not the build/setup command. - impact: local validation can report success even when `setup.sh` or an example build actually failed, letting broken ESP-IDF packaging and examples ship or regress unnoticed. - recommendation: test `if [ $THIS_ERR -ne 0 ]; then` immediately after the command of interest (before `popd`), or enable `set -euo pipefail` and use a small helper to run/check each example build. - reference: `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Renesas `Projects/test` server/client API drift leaves TLS client-auth policy dependent on undefined call ABI rather than an explicit argument - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/Projects/test/src/wolf_server.c:68,136-145`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/Projects/test/src/test_main.c:209`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/Projects/test/src/wolfssl_demo.h:45` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: implementation is `void wolfSSL_TLS_server_init(byte doClientCheck)`, but the public header declares `void wolfSSL_TLS_server_init();` and `test_main.c` calls `wolfSSL_TLS_server_init();` with no argument. The callee then branches on `if (doCliCertCheck) { wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(... WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER | WOLFSSL_VERIFY_FAIL_IF_NO_PEER_CERT ...) }`. - impact: whether the demo server requires client certificates can depend on whatever garbage value lands in the missing parameter slot/register, producing non-deterministic auth behavior and making the sample hard to reason about or safely reuse. - recommendation: make the prototype and call sites consistent, pass an explicit boolean for client-auth policy, and default the sample to one documented mode instead of relying on unspecified C calling behavior. - reference: `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`), `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - remaining files in this chunk were otherwise mostly sample/build glue or stale project metadata once the promoted issues above were accounted for: `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/wolfSSL-Full/{main.c,time-CortexM3-4.c,wolfsslFull.uvprojx,wolfsslFull.uvoptx,Abstract.txt}`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/{setup_win.bat,setup.sh,README.md,README_32se.md,UPDATE.md,dummy_test_paths.h,dummy_config_h,user_settings.h}`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/Projects/test/src/{key_data.c,key_data.h,wolfssl_demo.h}`, and `wolfcrypt/src/port/aria/aria-cryptocb.c` did not show a stronger chunk-local exploit or production failure mode in this pass beyond the ARIA buffer-contract break, MDK shell parser bug, ESP-IDF false-green test script, and Renesas server-init ABI drift noted above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the remaining content was dominated by example configuration, project metadata, dummy key placeholders, and documentation; adjacent spot checks mostly reinforced already-tracked sample trust/test-fixture themes rather than exposing a sharper new chunk-local issue.

### Medium - DEOS custom `XREALLOC` over-reads old heap blocks on buffer growth, because `realloc_deos()` copies the new requested size instead of the old allocation size - location: `IDE/ECLIPSE/DEOS/deos_malloc.c:30-40`, enabled by `IDE/ECLIPSE/DEOS/user_settings.h:161-169`; representative growth call sites include `src/x509.c:13185,13305` and `src/internal.c:9591,36006,36082,39713` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: DEOS build with `WOLFSSL_DEOS` hits any `XREALLOC` growth path, including attacker-influenced parsing/buffer-growth flows. - evidence: `user_settings.h` globally maps `XREALLOC(p, n, h, t)` to `realloc_deos(p, n)`. `realloc_deos()` allocates `size` bytes and then does `XMEMCPY((char*)newptr, (const char*)ptr, size);` even though it has no knowledge of the old allocation length. Core wolfSSL paths use `XREALLOC` specifically to grow buffers. - impact: normal growth reallocations read past the old heap object, causing crashes or copying adjacent stale heap data into live protocol/parser buffers. - recommendation: replace this shim with a real `realloc` or track allocation sizes and copy only `min(old_size, new_size)`; add a DEOS regression that grows an allocation and verifies no over-read. - reference: `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - DEOS and Micrium example configs hard-code certificate-validation time to 2022/2018, so X.509 validity checks drift permanently away from real wall-clock time - location: `IDE/ECLIPSE/DEOS/user_settings.h:64-66`, `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:3832-3834`, `IDE/ECLIPSE/MICRIUM/user_settings.h:37-40`, `IDE/ECLIPSE/MICRIUM/wolfsslRunTests.c:56-61` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: builds reuse the shipped settings and perform certificate validation. - evidence: DEOS defines `CURRENT_UNIX_TIMESTAMP 1663020069`, and `wc_port.c` returns `system ticks + CURRENT_UNIX_TIMESTAMP` as current time when that macro is set. Micrium defines `CURRENT_UNIX_TS 1542605837`, and `wolfsslRunTests()` calls `Clk_SetTS_Unix(CURRENT_UNIX_TS)` when the macro is present. - impact: certificate not-before/not-after checks are pinned near the build example’s timestamp, so expired certs can remain accepted (or valid current certs can fail) long after deployment, causing stale trust decisions and hard-to-diagnose outages. - recommendation: remove fixed timestamps from shipped configs, use a real RTC/NTP-backed time source, and keep any static timestamp override test-only and opt-in. - reference: `v5.0.0-V12`, `v5.0.0-V13`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - DEOS TLS client validates only the chain, not the expected host, so any publicly trusted certificate can impersonate the hard-coded Google endpoint - location: `IDE/ECLIPSE/DEOS/tls_wolfssl.c:67,146-176,196-242` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the client’s network path or steer it to another reachable TLS server. - evidence: the client connects to literal IP `172.217.3.174`, enables `SSL_VERIFY_PEER`, and loads Google CA certs, but never calls `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` / IP verification. `TCP_SERVER_DOMAIN_NAME` is defined as `"www.google.com"` and then unused. - impact: certificate-chain validation alone does not authenticate the intended peer identity, so a wrong endpoint with any CA-valid cert can complete TLS and read/modify traffic. - recommendation: bind the expected identity before `wolfSSL_connect()` with `wolfSSL_check_domain_name(TCP_SERVER_DOMAIN_NAME)` or the IP-verification equivalent; keep trust-store loading and hostname verification coupled in the sample. - reference: `v5.0.0-V12`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - PIC32 hash-state copies alias the same mutable update buffer, so updating one copy mutates the other and breaks hash/HMAC/transcript correctness - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/pic32/pic32mz-crypt.c:469-475,480-530`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/pic32/pic32mz-crypt.h:115-123`, copy entry points `wolfcrypt/src/md5.c:534-550`, `wolfcrypt/src/sha.c:1156-1193`, `wolfcrypt/src/sha256.c:2708-2760` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: `WOLFSSL_PIC32MZ_HASH` build hits `wc_Md5Copy()` / `wc_ShaCopy()` / `wc_Sha256Copy()` on an in-progress hash state. - evidence: the generic copy functions first `XMEMCPY(dst, src, sizeof(...))`, so `dst->cache.buf` points at the same buffered message as `src`. `wc_Pic32HashCopy()` then only sets `dst->isCopy = 1`. Later `wc_Pic32HashUpdate()` reuses `cache->buf` when there is spare capacity (`newLenPad <= cache->bufLen`) and appends directly into it with `XMEMCPY(newBuf + cache->updLen, data, len)`, mutating both the original and the copy. - impact: copied hash states are not independent, so HMAC/transcript snapshots can corrupt each other and produce wrong digests or intermittent TLS/authentication failures on PIC32MZ hash builds. - recommendation: deep-copy the buffered state, or implement copy-on-write when `isCopy` is set; do not let copied hash objects share a writable `cache->buf`. - reference: `v5.0.0-V11`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Reviewed - remaining files in this chunk were mostly sample/build/test scaffolding once the concrete issues above were accounted for: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfCrypt-Test/*` is a wrapper test app without a sharper new trust-boundary bug on this pass; `IDE/iotsafe-raspberrypi/{README.md,Makefile,client-tls13.c}` mainly exposed demo/debug surfaces but no stronger default-path issue than the promoted findings here; `.github/renode-test/stm32h753/*` is CI/emulation glue and did not show a production-path security bug beyond test harness fragility; and `IDE/ECLIPSE/MICRIUM/{client_wolfssl.c,server_wolfssl.c,README.md,include.am}` largely mirrored the DEOS sample patterns already captured above. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found some additional sample rough edges (for example legacy demo flow and test-only wiring), but they were either duplicative of already-tracked sample-auth/time-source issues or lower-signal than the findings appended here.

### High - XilinxSDK non-Versal example seeds wolfCrypt from a fixed counter, so every boot starts the DRBG from predictable bytes and makes RNG-dependent crypto reproducible - location: `IDE/XilinxSDK/wolfssl_example.c:67-73`, `IDE/XilinxSDK/user_settings.h:87-89`, `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:2217-2240`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/random.h:61-63,68-70`, `IDE/XilinxSDK/README.md:90,140-142` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: remove the shipped `CUSTOM_RAND_GENERATE my_rng_seed_gen` fallback, require a board entropy source that fills a seed buffer (`CUSTOM_RAND_GENERATE_SEED`/`_BLOCK` or hardware TRNG), and fail closed until real entropy is wired. - status: candidate - severity: high - exploitability/preconditions: non-Versal XilinxSDK build uses the shipped `user_settings.h`; application performs RNG-backed operations such as key generation or randomized private-key ops. - evidence: the README says XilinxSDK configuration comes from `IDE/XilinxSDK/user_settings.h` and this is a bare-metal wolfCrypt example.
