On ESP-IDF, `benchmark.c` imports `extern char* __argv[22]` and calls `construct_argv()` before `wolfcrypt_benchmark_main()`. - impact: selecting supported benchmark names can corrupt the stack or crash the example before tests run, producing unstable or misleading benchmark results. - recommendation: allocate each token by measured length (or reject oversize tokens), change the bound to reserve space for `NUL`, and add a regression for the longest documented arguments. - reference: `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`), `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - SCTP sample clients and servers write one byte past their 80-byte stack buffers on full-size reads - location: `examples/sctp/sctp-client.c:66-71`, `examples/sctp/sctp-server.c:70-74`, `examples/sctp/sctp-client-dtls.c:76-107`, `examples/sctp/sctp-server-dtls.c:83-117` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: peer sends exactly 80 bytes in one `recv()`/`wolfSSL_read()`; DTLS variants are reachable once the sample handshake completes. - evidence: each sample uses `char buffer[80]`, reads up to `sizeof(buffer)`, then unconditionally does `buffer[got] = 0;`. When `got == 80`, the terminator write is out of bounds. - impact: a remote peer can crash or corrupt stack state in these SCTP samples with a full-sized message/record. - recommendation: read at most `sizeof(buffer) - 1`, or guard `got >= sizeof(buffer)` before writing a terminator and treat data as length-delimited. - reference: `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Reviewed - the remaining files in this chunk were mostly headers, project metadata, or already-covered shipped test material once the concrete issues above were accounted for: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/riscv/riscv-64-asm.h`, `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/{build.rs,headers.h,Cargo.toml,Makefile,Cargo.lock,README.md,CHANGELOG.md}`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/nxp/{se050_port.h,ksdk_port.h,dcp_port.h}`, `mcapi/wolfssl.X/nbproject/*`, `mplabx/wolfcrypt_benchmark.X/nbproject/*`, and `certs/sm2/*` did not show a sharper chunk-local exploit or material production failure mode in this pass beyond the NuttX test-seed default, ESP-IDF benchmark parser overflow, and SCTP sample buffer overrun noted here; the SM2 PEMs mainly reinforced the already-tracked shipped-private-key/test-credential theme rather than adding a distinct new defect. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks were dominated by macro definitions, build glue, stale IDE metadata, and certificate fixtures/scripts, with no stronger trust-boundary break than the findings appended above.

### Medium - CS+ `t4_demo` client loads a CA but never enables certificate or endpoint verification, so any TLS server can impersonate the target - location: `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/t4_demo/wolf_client.c:69-113,127-165`; default verify mode `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or convince the operator to connect to an attacker-controlled IP/port via the documented `c <IP addr> <Port>` command. - evidence: `wolfSSL_TLS_client_init()` loads a CA with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations()` / `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer()` and `wolfSSL_TLS_client()` calls `wolfSSL_connect()`, but repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(...WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER...)`, `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`, or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` anywhere in `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/t4_demo`; new contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c:512`. - impact: the demo authenticates no server chain or peer identity, so a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic. - recommendation: set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` on the context and bind the expected DNS name/IP before `wolfSSL_connect()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - CS+ `t4_demo` command parsing writes through a NULL `argv` table, so the documented `c <IP> <Port>` path can fault before TLS starts - location: `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/t4_demo/wolf_main.c:48-65,86,107-109`, `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/t4_demo/wolf_demo.h:3-8` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: operator or attacker with console access enters any command containing a space; the README's client syntax does this by design. - evidence: `func_args` only provides `char **argv;`; `wolfSSL_main()` initializes `func_args args = {0};`, leaving `args.argv == NULL`; `get_arg()` then stores tokens with `args->argv[++args->argc] = arg;`, and the client path later dereferences `args.argv[1]` / `args.argv[2]` in `printf()` and `wolfSSL_TLS_client()`. - impact: ordinary console input can trigger a NULL-pointer write or later NULL/garbage dereference, crashing the board or corrupting memory on the command path. - recommendation: back `argv` with a fixed local array (for example `char* argv[8]; args.argv = argv;`), initialize it before parsing, and reject overlong arg counts explicitly. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - Renesas sample clients write one byte past their 256-byte receive buffers when a peer returns a full-sized read - location: `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/t4_demo/wolf_client.c:134-180`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/src/client/simple_tls_tsip_client.c:206-345`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/src/client/simple_tcp_client.c:89-123` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: connected peer sends exactly 256 application bytes in one `wolfSSL_read()` / `tcp_rcv_dat()` result. - evidence: each client allocates `char rcvBuff[BUFF_SIZE]` with `BUFF_SIZE 256`, reads up to `BUFF_SIZE` bytes (`wolfSSL_read(..., BUFF_SIZE)` or `my_IORecv(..., BUFF_SIZE, ...)`), then unconditionally does `rcvBuff[ret] = '\0';`. - impact: a full-sized read turns normal peer-controlled input length into a 1-byte stack overflow, causing crashes or adjacent stack corruption after connection setup. - recommendation: read at most `BUFF_SIZE - 1`, or guard `ret >= BUFF_SIZE` before writing a terminator and treat the data as length-delimited. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RX72N EnvisionKit simple TLS/TSIP client loads a CA but never enables peer or hostname verification, so any reachable TLS server can impersonate the hard-coded endpoint - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/src/client/simple_tls_tsip_client.c:92-188,214-335`; default ctx behavior `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or answer at `192.168.11.6:11111`. - evidence: `wolfSSL_TLS_client_init()` loads a CA with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations()` / `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer()` and `wolfSSL_TLS_client()` calls `wolfSSL_connect()`, but repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(...WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER...)`, `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`, or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` anywhere under `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/src/client`; new contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE`. - impact: the sample does not authenticate the server chain or expected identity, so an on-path attacker or wrong server can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic. - recommendation: set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` and bind the expected hostname/IP before `wolfSSL_connect()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - `WOLF_STSAFE_DEVID` has a stray trailing semicolon, so the documented callback-registration example fails to compile when used as a function argument - location: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/st/stsafe.h:197`; documented use `wolfcrypt/src/port/st/README.md:161-172` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the public header defines `#define WOLF_STSAFE_DEVID 0x53545341;` with a trailing `;`, while the README shows `wc_CryptoCb_RegisterDevice(WOLF_STSAFE_DEVID, wolfSSL_STSAFE_CryptoDevCb, &stsafeCtx);`; that expansion injects a semicolon into the argument list. - impact: integrators following the published STSAFE setup snippet hit an avoidable compile-time failure on a public macro/API path. - recommendation: drop the semicolon from the macro and add a tiny compile test or doc snippet that uses the macro in an expression/function-call context. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Low - CS+ server's `NO_FILESYSTEM` + `USE_ECC_CERT` branch does not compile because the embedded key buffer is declared as a second `cert` variable instead of `key` - location: `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/t4_demo/wolf_server.c:68-71` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: under the `#else` / `#ifdef USE_ECC_CERT` path, the file declares `char *cert = serv_ecc_der_256;` and then immediately declares `char *cert = serv_ecc_key_der_256;` again instead of a `key` variable, while later code unconditionally calls `wolfSSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey_buffer(ctx, key, sizeof_key, ...)`. - impact: the embedded ECC/no-filesystem server path fails at compile time, so the sample cannot be built in that configuration. - recommendation: rename the second buffer to `key` (ideally `const unsigned char*`) and keep this no-filesystem ECC path in CI. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - QNX example client loads a CA and client cert/key but never enables peer verification or binds the expected server identity, so any reachable TLS server can impersonate the target IP - location: `IDE/QNX/example-client/client-tls.c:119,149-201,224`; default context behavior `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or run a TLS server at the IPv4 address passed in `argv[2]`. - evidence: the sample parses the target IP from `argv[2]`, loads a CA from `argv[3]` with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer(ctx, pem, pemSz, ...)`, then calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`; repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)` or hostname/IP binding call in this file, and new contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE`. - impact: the client authenticates neither the certificate chain nor endpoint identity, so a wrong server or on-path attacker can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic. - recommendation: set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` on the context and bind the expected DNS name/IP before `wolfSSL_connect()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Renesas DK-S7G2 benchmark template measures 100 TLS handshakes against a hard-coded server IP without ever enabling peer or hostname verification, so a local-network attacker can spoof the benchmark peer - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/DK-S7G2/benchmark-template/src/app_entry.c:40-41,90-100,175`; default context behavior `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the benchmark network or answer on `192.168.3.10:11112`. - evidence: `benchmark_TLS()` loads embedded RSA/ECC CA buffers, connects to hard-coded `SERVER_IP`/`TLS_PORT`, and loops over `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)` 100 times; repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)` or hostname/IP binding in this template, and wolfSSL contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE`. - impact: the benchmark can silently handshake with an attacker-controlled endpoint, invalidating security assumptions and benchmark results while exposing any exchanged application data. - recommendation: enable peer verification by default and bind the expected server name/IP before connecting; keep any insecure benchmark mode behind an explicit lab-only switch. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Azure Sphere sample clients trust a CA buffer but never enable peer verification or bind the expected server identity, so any certificate chaining to that CA can impersonate `SERVER_IP`/`www.wolfssl.com` - location: `IDE/VS-AZURE-SPHERE/client/client.c:65,83,102`, `IDE/VS-AZURE-SPHERE/user_settings.h:8-25`, `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/client/client.c:70,88,107`, `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/user_settings.h:31-49`; default context behavior `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or present any cert chaining to the loaded root when these samples are reused. - evidence: both clients connect to `SERVER_IP`/`DEFAULT_PORT`, load `CERT`/`SIZEOF_CERT` into the trust store with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer(...)`, and call `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`; repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)` or `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`/SNI binding in either client. - impact: sample-derived Azure Sphere clients do not authenticate the remote endpoint, so a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker can terminate TLS and modify traffic while the sample appears secure. - recommendation: default these samples to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER`, bind the expected DNS name/IP before connect, and make insecure demo mode an explicit opt-in only. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mainly example build glue, SDK metadata, static config headers, or legacy port helpers after the concrete sample-authentication issues above; this pass did not confirm a stronger chunk-local exploit or material performance failure in `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_test/*`, `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/*`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/src/server/{simple_tls_server.c,simple_tcp_server.c}`, `IDE/MDK-ARM/STM32F2xx_StdPeriph_Lib/time-STM32F2xx.c`, `IDE/LINUX-SGX/*`, `mplabx/*`, `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL-TLS-Server/*`, or the Cavium headers beyond lower-signal sample/test limitations and build-script rough edges. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks mostly found duplicated sample scaffolding, hard-coded lab settings, or non-executable metadata; no sharper trust-boundary break was confirmed from those files in this pass.

### Medium - release packaging ships the RSA-PSS root, CA, server, and client private keys verbatim, and the bundled RSA-PSS test configs use those repo-public identities as live trust anchors and endpoints - location: `certs/rsapss/include.am:5-55`, key files `certs/rsapss/*-rsapss-priv.pem`, consumers `scripts/rsapss.test:112-119` and `tests/test-rsapss.conf:4-43,49-87` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: keep RSA-PSS private keys out of `EXTRA_DIST`, generate them during CI/lab setup, and mark any remaining fixtures as non-reusable test material; sample/test configs should refuse these identities outside explicit test mode. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: an integrator, test harness, or sample-derived deployment reuses the shipped `certs/rsapss/*.pem` identities or trust anchors outside an isolated lab. - evidence: `include.am` distributes `root-rsapss-priv.pem`, `ca-rsapss-priv.pem`, `server-rsapss-priv.pem`, `client-rsapss-priv.pem` and all 3072-bit counterparts; `scripts/rsapss.test` launches a live server with `server-rsapss.pem`/`server-rsapss-priv.pem` and trusts `root-rsapss.pem`; `tests/test-rsapss.conf` likewise uses these bundled certs and keys for both server and client runs. - impact: anyone with the public repo can clone the exact sample server/client identities or mint new certificates under the shipped RSA-PSS trust hierarchy wherever these fixtures leak into real environments.

### Medium - RSA-PSS mTLS fixtures collapse the trust anchor and presented client identity by generating `client-*.pem` as self-signed `CA:true` certs and then using the same file as both `-A` trust store input and `-c` peer cert - location: generator `certs/rsapss/renew-rsapss-certs.sh:106,198`, extension profile `certs/renewcerts/wolfssl.cnf:125-130`, shipped certs `certs/rsapss/{client-rsapss.pem,client-3072-rsapss.pem}`, consumers `tests/test-rsapss.conf:19,25-27,35,64,70-72,80,86-87` - category: complexity - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: testing security boundaries`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: split RSA-PSS mTLS fixtures into a real client CA plus separate end-entity client certs, and stop reusing the same self-signed CA certificate as the presented peer credential. - status: candidate - severity: medium - evidence: `renew-rsapss-certs.sh` signs `client-rsapss.pem` and `client-3072-rsapss.pem` with `[wolfssl_opts]`; that profile sets `basicConstraints=CA:true`, `subjectAltName=DNS:example.com, IP:127.0.0.1`, and `extendedKeyUsage=serverAuth, clientAuth`. `tests/test-rsapss.conf` then both presents `client-rsapss.pem`/`client-rsapss-priv.pem` as the client cert and trusts `client-rsapss.pem` / `client-3072-rsapss.pem` via `-A`. - impact: client-auth/path-building coverage is unrealistically permissive and can false-pass bugs in EKU, CA/leaf separation, or trust-anchor handling because the tests accept a trust-anchor-as-leaf pattern instead of a normal client chain.

### Low - the mixed RSA key / RSA-PSS signature regression is wired to a nonexistent certificate name, so the chunk’s only mixed-mode test input is effectively dead - location: consumer `tests/test-rsapss.conf:78`, produced/distributed file `certs/rsapss/{renew-rsapss-certs.sh:64-70,include.am:56}` - category: complexity - reference: `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: rename the test input to the actual `server-mix-rsapss-cert.pem` filename (or rename the produced file consistently) and add a CI existence check so mixed-mode RSA/RSA-PSS coverage cannot silently rot. - status: candidate - severity: low - evidence: `tests/test-rsapss.conf` points `-c` at `./certs/rsapss/server-mixed-rsapss-cert.pem`, but this chunk only generates and ships `server-mix-rsapss-cert.pem`; repo search found no matching `server-mixed-rsapss-cert.pem` file anywhere else. - impact: the intended mixed RSA/RSA-PSS regression path will fail to load its certificate or be skipped, leaving an avoidable coverage gap around the mixed-mode fixture.

### Reviewed - the remainder of this chunk was mostly static RSA-PSS fixture PEMs/public keys and straightforward regeneration glue; the concrete chunk-local issues were the shipped private keys, the CA-as-leaf client fixture design, and the mixed-file-name test drift above - location: `certs/rsapss/*`, `certs/rsapss/include.am` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: aside from those items, the remaining PEM bodies primarily reflected generated fixture data (including the expected 2048-bit restricted-PSS vs 3072-bit no-parameter cases already exercised elsewhere) rather than a sharper new exploit path or material performance problem in this chunk.

### Medium - Apple multiplatform `simple_client_example` loads a CA file but never enables certificate or hostname verification, so any MITM can impersonate `www.wolfssl.com` - location: `IDE/apple-universal/wolfssl-multiplatform/wolfssl-multiplatform/simple_client_example.c:82-115`; default verify mode `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: user runs or reuses the shipped sample on an attacker-controlled or intercepted network. - evidence: the sample calls `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations(ctx, CERT_PATH, NULL)`, `wolfSSL_set_fd(ssl, sockfd)`, and `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`, but repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)` or `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` in this file. New contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c:512`. - impact: a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker can terminate TLS with any certificate and read/modify traffic while the sample appears to connect securely. - recommendation: set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` explicitly and bind `SERVER_HOST` with `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` or the IP equivalent before `wolfSSL_connect()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - ESP-IDF benchmark component claims to auto-install `user_settings.h` into `components/wolfssl/include` but actually copies it into a different top-level `wolfssl/include` directory - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_benchmark/components/wolfssl/CMakeLists.txt:650-654,741-748` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `WOLFSSL_PROJECT_DIR` is set to `${CMAKE_HOME_DIRECTORY}/components/wolfssl`, `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS_DIR` is defined as `${WOLFSSL_PROJECT_DIR}/include/user_settings.h`, and the status message says `Installing wolfSSL user_settings.h to ${WOLFSSL_PROJECT_DIR}/include/user_settings.h`; but the actual copy destination is `file(COPY ... DESTINATION "${CMAKE_HOME_DIRECTORY}/wolfssl/include/")`, missing `/components`. - impact: first-time developer-repo setup can silently populate an unused path while the real component include directory stays missing or stale, leading to confusing build failures or builds against the wrong settings header. - recommendation: copy to `${WOLFSSL_PROJECT_DIR}/include/`, then assert the expected file exists before continuing. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - C# custom I/O callback example drops short socket writes by always reporting success for the full TLS record length - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL-Example-IOCallbacks/wolfSSL-Example-IOCallbacks.cs:86-105` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `wolfSSLCbIOSend()` copies `sz` bytes into `msg`, calls `con.Send(msg, 0, msg.Length, SocketFlags.None);`, ignores the return value, logs `sz`, and returns `sz`. Stream sockets can legally send fewer bytes than requested. - impact: under network backpressure the sample tells wolfSSL that a whole record was sent when only a prefix left the process, corrupting the TLS byte stream and causing intermittent handshake or application-data failures in code copied from this example. - recommendation: loop until all bytes are written, or return the actual count / WANT_WRITE condition consistently; add a regression with a throttled socket that forces short writes. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - The `WIN-SRTP-KDF-140-3` FIPS project is internally version-skewed: headers build for FIPS v6 while the test project, asm custom-build commands, and README still hard-code FIPS v5.1 - location: `IDE/WIN-SRTP-KDF-140-3/user_settings.h:14-31,108-124`, `IDE/WIN-SRTP-KDF-140-3/test.vcxproj:114-249`, `IDE/WIN-SRTP-KDF-140-3/wolfssl-fips.vcxproj:326-384`, `IDE/WIN-SRTP-KDF-140-3/README.txt:52-53` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `user_settings.h` sets `HAVE_FIPS_VERSION 6` plus v6-only feature macros such as `HAVE_ED25519`, `HAVE_CURVE25519`, `WOLFSSL_AES_XTS`, and `WC_SRTP_KDF`, but every `test.vcxproj` configuration still defines `HAVE_FIPS_VERSION=5;HAVE_FIPS_VERSION_MINOR=1`, the x64 asm custom build steps pass `/DHAVE_FIPS_VERSION=5 /DHAVE_FIPS_VERSION_MINOR=1`, and the README still documents v5.1 as the default build. - impact: integrators cannot rely on one source of truth for the validated build profile; mixed-version translation units and stale instructions make FIPS reproduction, support, and hash/debugging much harder and risk building a binary that does not match the intended certification profile. - recommendation: derive all project/asm/README version settings from one shared source (for example `user_settings.h` or a common props file) and add CI that fails when the project metadata and shipped header disagree. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mainly declarations, build metadata, or sample/test scaffolding once the concrete issues above were accounted for: `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/{pic32/pic32mz-crypt.h,iotsafe/iotsafe.h}` are declaration-only headers, the MCUEXPRESSO files are test-harness setup around board/TRNG initialization, and the remaining Apple/Espressif headers (`wifi_connect.h`, `client-tls.h`, `time_helper.h`, `main.h`) were mostly lightweight configuration/declaration surfaces without a sharper chunk-local exploit or material performance failure than the promoted sample-auth and CMake-path issues above. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks mostly found sample defaults, include-path scaffolding, and packaging metadata rather than stronger standalone trust-boundary breaks in this chunk.

### Medium - release packaging ships the Ed25519 test PKI private keys, so anyone can forge the repo’s Ed25519 identities if these fixtures leak into demos or sample-derived deployments - location: `certs/ed25519/{root-ed25519-priv.pem,ca-ed25519-priv.pem,server-ed25519-priv.pem,client-ed25519-priv.pem,eddsa-ed25519.pem}`, distribution manifest `certs/ed25519/include.am:6-31`, active consumers `tests/test-ed25519.conf:4-11,30-41,61-72` and `scripts/sniffer-gen.sh:57-63` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: an integrator, test harness, or sample deployment reuses the bundled `certs/ed25519/*` identities outside an isolated lab. - evidence: this chunk includes plaintext private keys for the Ed25519 root, CA, server, and client, and `include.am` places both `*-priv.pem` and `*-priv.der` in `EXTRA_DIST`. The shipped tests/scripts then use the same files as live `-c/-k/-A` identities and trust anchors. - impact: anyone with repo access can mint certificates under the shipped Ed25519 root/CA or clone the same server/client identities, enabling straightforward impersonation wherever these fixtures are trusted. - recommendation: stop shipping Ed25519 private keys in release artifacts; generate them during tests or quarantine them behind a non-distributed fixture step, and ship only public certs by default. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - the ESP-IDF wolfSSL component embeds git remote/branch/hash metadata into firmware and the sample prints it at boot, leaking source-control details to any UART/log observer - location: build-time source `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_test/components/wolfssl/CMakeLists.txt:1172-1198,1243-1244`, runtime sink `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_util.c:371-411,660-664,725-736`, caller `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_test/main/main.c:219` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build runs from a git checkout and the device exposes boot logs over UART, console, or telemetry. - evidence: CMake runs `git config --get remote.origin.url`, `git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD`, and `git rev-parse HEAD` and saves them as compile definitions; `ShowExtendedSystemInfo_git()` logs `LIBWOLFSSL_VERSION_GIT_ORIGIN`, `...GIT_BRANCH`, and `...GIT_HASH`; the example app calls `esp_ShowExtendedSystemInfo()` unconditionally. - impact: development or internal builds can disclose repository origin URLs, branch names, and exact revision identifiers from device logs; credential-bearing remotes or private repo names would leak verbatim. - recommendation: do not compile `remote.origin.url` into firmware, and gate all verbose provenance logging behind an explicit debug-only option that defaults off; if provenance is needed, prefer a sanitized version string or short hash only. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V16 Security Logging and Error Handling`

### Medium - ESP-IDF auto-discovery silently walks parent directories and common clone names (`wolfssl-$USER`, `wolfssl-master`, `wolfssl`) until `/`, so builds can pick up an unintended wolfSSL checkout based only on filesystem layout - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_test/components/wolfssl/CMakeLists.txt:354-493,578-607`, documented in `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_test/components/wolfssl/README.md:20-32` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: when `WOLFSSL_ROOT` and `CONFIG_CUSTOM_SETTING_WOLFSSL_ROOT` are unset or invalid, `FIND_WOLFSSL_DIRECTORY()` recursively ascends to the filesystem root and returns the first directory containing `wolfcrypt/src`, trying user-suffixed and generic clone names at each level. The README documents this search order as normal behavior. - impact: identical project sources can compile against different wolfSSL trees on different developer or CI machines, producing non-reproducible crypto builds and silent provenance drift that is hard to review or incident-trace. - recommendation: require an explicit `WOLFSSL_ROOT`/managed component for non-repo builds, or fail when multiple candidates exist instead of auto-selecting the first ancestor match. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mainly sample build metadata, static certificate fixtures, and project scaffolding once the concrete issues above were accounted for; this pass did not confirm a sharper exploit or material performance issue from `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/SimpleServer/{SimpleServer.uvprojx,SimpleServer.uvoptx,wolfssl-link.sct,Abstract.txt}`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_test/components/wolfssl/{component.mk,Kconfig,README.md}` beyond the promoted provenance/logging issues, or the remaining Ed25519 public-cert bodies beyond the shipped-private-key exposure already noted here. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining observations were mostly sample-only defaults, IDE metadata, and PEM fixture structure without a cleaner chunk-local trust-boundary break than the entries appended above.

### Medium - Azure Sphere sample TLS servers expose an unauthenticated network `shutdown` command that stops the service - location: `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/server/server.c:169-184`, `IDE/VS-AZURE-SPHERE/server/server.c:164-179`, externally reachable via `IDE/VS-AZURE-SPHERE/server/app_manifest.json:8` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can reach TCP port `11111`; the samples do not enable client-certificate auth or any application auth before processing commands. - evidence: both servers accept arbitrary TLS clients, read attacker-controlled bytes into `buff`, and set `shutdown = 1` on `strncmp(buff, "shutdown", 8) == 0`; the Azure Sphere manifest explicitly opens `AllowedTcpServerPorts: [11111]`. - impact: any client that can complete a normal handshake can remotely terminate the sample service, a trivial DoS pattern that is easy to cargo-cult into deployed Azure Sphere code. - recommendation: remove message-triggered shutdown from the wire protocol, or gate it behind an authenticated admin/debug path compiled only for tests. - reference: `v5.0.0-V2`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - C# TLS/DTLS PSK server wrappers hard-code the shared secret and ignore client identity, so anyone with the sample source or binary can authenticate as a valid PSK peer - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL-TLS-PSK-Server/wolfSSL-TLS-PSK-Server.cs:48-60,149-165`, `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL-DTLS-PSK-Server/wolfSSL-DTLS-PSK-Server.cs:49-62,135-160` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: sample-derived server is exposed on the network; attacker knows the repo-public sample or can recover the same constant from the binary. - evidence: `my_psk_server_cb()` logs but does not validate `identity`, then always copies the same 4-byte key `{26, 43, 60, 77}` into the PSK buffer; both samples bind to `0.0.0.0:11111`/`11111` and enable the PSK callback for the live listener. - impact: PSK mode provides no real peer authentication in these samples; any attacker who knows the public constant can complete the handshake and read/modify supposedly authenticated traffic. - recommendation: require a deployment-supplied high-entropy PSK, validate the claimed identity against per-peer policy, and keep fixed demo PSKs behind an explicit lab-only build flag. - reference: `v5.0.0-V11`, `v5.0.0-V12`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Low - `IDE/WIN10/test.vcxproj` uses the obsolete `CYASSL_DLL` macro only in `DLL Debug|x64`, so that one configuration compiles with different DLL import assumptions than every other WIN10 DLL build - location: `IDE/WIN10/test.vcxproj:211`, contrasted with `IDE/WIN10/test.vcxproj:191,236,256` and DLL visibility guards in `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/visibility.h:37-69` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the `DLL Debug|x64` preprocessor definitions use `CYASSL_DLL`, while the other WIN10 DLL configs use `WOLFSSL_DLL`; repo search finds no code checking `CYASSL_DLL`, but `visibility.h` gates Windows DLL import/export behavior on `WOLFSSL_DLL`. - impact: one solution configuration can drift into unresolved/duplicate symbol issues or config-specific ABI behavior that only reproduces in `DLL Debug|x64`, making Windows DLL failures harder to reason about. - recommendation: replace `CYASSL_DLL` with `WOLFSSL_DLL` and add a smoke build for every solution configuration so stale legacy macros cannot survive unnoticed. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mainly project metadata, platform config headers, doc-generation config, and benchmark scaffolding once the concrete issues above were accounted for; this pass did not confirm a stronger chunk-local exploit or material production performance failure in `IDE/WIN10/{user_settings.h,wolfssl-fips.vcxproj,wolfssl-fips.sln,README.txt,wolfssl-fips.rc,include.am,resource.h}`, `IDE/QNX/wolfssl/{user_settings.h,Makefile}`, `IDE/M68K/{Makefile,user_settings.h,README.md,include.am}`, `tirtos/packages/ti/net/wolfssl/tests/EK_TM4C1294XL/wolfcrypt/benchmark/*`, or `doc/formats/pdf/{header.tex,Doxyfile}` beyond the sample-server auth/DoS and Windows config-drift findings appended here. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks were dominated by feature toggles, packaging manifests, and documentation build knobs; no sharper in-repo trust-boundary break or clearly material runtime hot path issue was established from those files on this pass.

### Medium - MQX example client verifies the chain but never binds the expected server identity, so any cert chaining to the loaded CA can impersonate the hard-coded endpoint - location: `mqx/wolfssl_client/Sources/main.c:202-224,261`, declarations in `mqx/wolfssl_client/Sources/main.h:20-22` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: after loading the CA, bind the expected peer name/IP with `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` / `wolfSSL_check_ip()` before `wolfSSL_connect()`, or make the expected identity an explicit configuration input rather than only `wolfsslIP`. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or operate another TLS server with a certificate chaining to `a:\certs\ca-cert.pem`. - evidence: the sample hard-codes `wolfsslIP = IPADDR(192,168,1,125)`, enables `SSL_VERIFY_PEER`, loads `caCert`, and calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`; repo search found no `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`, `wolfSSL_check_ip()`, or SNI/hostname-binding call in this client. - impact: the sample authenticates the CA chain but not the specific peer, so a wrong server with any CA-valid certificate can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic.

### Medium - MQX example trusts unauthenticated SNTP to set the clock immediately before TLS verification, making certificate validity checks spoofable by network time injection - location: `mqx/wolfssl_client/Sources/main.c:80-88,145-172,214-224,261` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: use a trusted RTC or authenticated/sanity-checked time source, keep a monotonic floor from provisioned time, and fail closed if trustworthy time is unavailable before certificate validation. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can spoof or tamper with SNTP traffic to the hard-coded server IP `216.229.0.179` on the network path. - evidence: `Main_task()` calls `setup_clock()` before `client_test()`. `setup_clock()` performs `SNTP_oneshot(ipaddr, 5000)` against a fixed NTP server and updates device time; `client_test()` then enables peer verification, loads a CA, and calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`. - impact: a spoofed time reply can shift the device clock so expired/not-yet-valid certificates are accepted or legitimate connections are rejected, directly changing TLS trust decisions.

### Medium - Both shipped GCC-ARM linker scripts map `.rodata` from `*(.text*)` instead of `*(.rodata*)`, leaving constant-data placement to orphan-section heuristics and making flash/FIPS layout non-deterministic - location: `IDE/GCC-ARM/linker.ld:14`, `IDE/GCC-ARM/linker_fips.ld:76` - category: complexity - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell` - recommendation: change these rules to collect `*(.rodata .rodata*)`, then add a map-file regression/smoke test that asserts non-empty `.rodata` placement for the example targets. - status: candidate - severity: medium - evidence: both scripts define `.text : { *(.text*) } > FLASH` and then later define `.rodata : { *(.text*) } > FLASH`; no explicit catch-all for generic `.rodata*` remains. - impact: read-only constants become linker orphans whose placement depends on linker/version defaults, which makes memory accounting and, in the FIPS variant, section-stability reasoning harder and can break reproducible/example memory maps.

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mostly build metadata and sample scaffolding once the MQX trust-boundary issues and GCC-ARM linker typo were accounted for; no sharper chunk-local exploit was confirmed in `mcapi/*/nbproject/*`, `IDE/MPLABX16/{main.c,README.md,include.am}`, `IDE/QNX/example-server/{server-tls.c,Makefile}`, or `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/src/{test_main.c,wolfssl_simple_demo.h}` beyond already-tracked shipped test-key / sample-config patterns elsewhere in the repo. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: MCAPI entries were NetBeans/MPLAB project metadata; MPLABX16 was a wolfCrypt test harness with a platform config but no stronger chunk-local network sink; the QNX server was primarily a CAAM callback demo around already-known test cert material; the Renesas files were feature toggles and harness glue without a clearer new trust-boundary failure than the findings above.

### High - RT-Thread port ships deterministic test seeding in its default `user_settings.h`, so sample-derived TLS/keygen builds start from a public byte stream instead of real entropy - location: `IDE/ECLIPSE/RTTHREAD/user_settings.h:34-37`, seed fallback `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:3621-3628,3864-3874` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: remove `USE_TEST_GENSEED`/`NO_DEV_RANDOM` from the shipped RT-Thread defaults and require a board entropy source/TRNG before enabling client/server/TLS examples. - status: candidate - severity: high - exploitability/preconditions: integrator reuses the shipped RT-Thread profile for any RNG-backed operation (TLS handshakes, key generation, nonces, session secrets). - evidence: the default profile enables `WOLFSSL_CLIENT_TEST`, `WOLFSSL_SERVER_TEST`, `USE_TEST_GENSEED`, and `NO_DEV_RANDOM`; in `random.c`, that build path defines `USE_TEST_GENSEED` and `wc_GenerateSeed()` fills the seed with `output[i] = (byte)i`. - impact: RT-Thread sample-derived deployments can generate predictable keys, IVs, and session secrets, breaking confidentiality/integrity.

### Medium - ESP-IDF `wolfssl_client` authenticates only the CA chain and never binds the expected server name/IP, so any certificate under the trusted CA can impersonate the configured target host - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_client/main/client-tls.c:187,231,309-347,355-377,495`, documented target config `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_client/README.md:18,59` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: keep `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER`, but also call `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` / `wolfSSL_check_ip()` for `TLS_SMP_TARGET_HOST` before `wolfSSL_connect()`, and document hostname/IP binding as required. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network or present a different certificate chaining to the CA loaded by the example. - evidence: the client resolves and connects to `TLS_SMP_TARGET_HOST`, loads a CA with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer(...)`, and sets `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, 0)`, but repo search found no `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` anywhere in the example path. The README describes the configured target as an IP address. - impact: a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker with any cert under the same trust anchor can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic while the example appears to verify peers.

### Medium - liboqs port cleanup is not symmetrical with init: `wolfCrypt_Cleanup()` frees the default RNG but leaves `liboqs_init` and the mutex live, so a later re-init can reuse freed RNG state and abort PQC operations - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/liboqs/liboqs.c:42,72-89,95-117`, init/cleanup hooks `wolfcrypt/src/wc_port.c:452,585` - category: complexity - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell` - recommendation: make `wolfSSL_liboqsClose()` reset `liboqs_init`, clear `liboqsCurrentRNG`, free/reinitialize the mutex, and re-register the RNG callback only after a successful fresh init; add an init-cleanup-init regression. - status: candidate - severity: medium - evidence: `wolfSSL_liboqsInit()` sets `liboqs_init = 1`, initializes/locks the mutex, initializes `liboqsDefaultRNG`, and points `liboqsCurrentRNG` at it. `wolfSSL_liboqsClose()` only calls `wc_FreeRng(&liboqsDefaultRNG)`; it never clears `liboqs_init` or frees the mutex. A later `wolfCrypt_Init()` therefore skips the init path, while `wolfSSL_liboqsRngMutexUnlock()` still resets `liboqsCurrentRNG = &liboqsDefaultRNG`, and `wolfSSL_liboqsGetRandomData()` calls `abort()` on RNG failure. - impact: processes that legitimately cycle `wolfCrypt_Init()`/`wolfCrypt_Cleanup()` can leave liboqs-backed Dilithium/Falcon/ML-KEM/SPHINCS operations in a crash-prone state after reinit, causing hard-to-diagnose production aborts instead of clean recovery.

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was primarily sample/build metadata, docs, partition defaults, and thin platform glue; no sharper chunk-local exploit or material performance failure was confirmed from `IDE/HEXAGON/DSP/*`, `IDE/IAR-EWARM/Projects/benchmark/*`, `IDE/M68K/*`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/{ti,tropicsquare}/headers`, `IDE/VS-AZURE-SPHERE/{README.md,wolfssl.sln,wolfssl.vcxproj,include.am}`, `zephyr/samples/wolfssl_benchmark/*`, `zephyr/samples/wolfssl_tls_sock/*`, or `.github/workflows/hostap-files/configs/hostap_2_10/*` beyond the RT-Thread deterministic seeding, ESP-IDF client identity-binding gap, and liboqs init/cleanup asymmetry noted above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks were dominated by project files, Kconfig/CMake glue, sample configuration, and static docs/test lists; the promoted findings above were the clearest trust-boundary or operationally material issues confirmed in this pass.

### High - Renesas common sample profiles ship deterministic test seeding by default, so sample-derived builds can generate predictable keys and nonces - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/common/user_settings.h:23-27`, `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/common/user_settings.h:58`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/Projects/common/user_settings.h:74`, seed fallback `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:3621-3628,3864-3874` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: an integrator reuses one of these shipped Renesas `user_settings.h` profiles without replacing the entropy source. - evidence: each profile defines `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST`; `random.c` maps that build to `USE_TEST_GENSEED` and fills the seed buffer deterministically with `output[i] = (byte)i`. - impact: DRBG state becomes predictable across boots/devices using these defaults, undermining generated keys, nonces, and session secrets. - recommendation: remove `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST` from shipped Renesas defaults, require a real board TRNG hook before enabling TLS/keygen/benchmarks, and fail closed if no entropy source is present. - reference: `v5.0.0-V11`, `v5.0.0-V13`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### Medium - Renesas e2studio common `strncasecmp()` compares pointer values instead of bytes, breaking case-insensitive X.509 and HTTP parser paths - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/Projects/common/wolfssl_dummy.c:35-39`, mapping `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/types.h:1029`, consumers `src/x509.c:13110`, `src/wolfio.c:1915-1965` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the shim implements `if(toupper(s1++) != toupper(s2++))`, passing incremented pointers to `toupper` instead of `*s1`/`*s2`; wolfSSL maps `XSTRNCASECMP` to `strncasecmp` and uses it in certificate-name and HTTP-header parsing. - impact: sample-derived builds can spuriously fail case-insensitive SAN/CN comparisons and HTTP header recognition (for example OCSP/CRL responder headers), creating hard-to-debug verification and interoperability failures on network input. - recommendation: compare bytes (`toupper((unsigned char)*s1)` / `*s2`), return proper ordering, and add a regression that exercises `XSTRNCASECMP` through the Renesas dummy-libc shim. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - C# wrapper build profile explicitly disables Curve25519 blinding, undoing current default hardening for private-key operations - location: `wrapper/CSharp/user_settings.h:64-65`, default hardening gate `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/settings.h:4115-4119`, wrapper sinks `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/wolfCrypt.cs:361-363,400-402`, exercised by `wrapper/CSharp/wolfCrypt-Test/wolfCrypt-Test.cs:583-652` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: the wrapper is built on the C Curve25519 path and an attacker can mount local timing/cache/power/rowhammer-style observation against private-key operations. - evidence: the wrapper forces `#define NO_CURVE25519_BLINDING`; `settings.h` otherwise enables Curve25519 blinding by default unless that macro is set; the wrapper exposes `wc_curve25519_make_key` and `wc_curve25519_shared_secret`, and the shipped test code uses those private-key operations. - impact: wrapper users who copy this build profile lose the library’s default side-channel hardening for Curve25519, increasing private-key leakage risk under local side-channel conditions. - recommendation: stop defining `NO_CURVE25519_BLINDING`; plumb an RNG into wrapper Curve25519 operations or reject configurations that cannot support blinding. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - the remaining files in this chunk were mainly IDE metadata, README/build glue, debugger scripts, or declaration-only headers once the concrete issues above were accounted for; this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material performance failure from `wrapper/CSharp/{wolfSSL_CSharp.sln,wolfssl.vcxproj,include.am,README.md}`, `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/CryptTest/*`, `mqx/wolfcrypt_benchmark/Debugger/*`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/{psa/psa.h,aria/aria-crypt.h,aria/aria-cryptocb.h}`, `IDE/XCODE/*`, `IDE/apple-universal/*`, `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/{include.am,README.md,user_settings.h}`, `zephyr/samples/wolfssl_test/{sample.yaml,CMakeLists.txt,prj*.conf,README,zephyr_*.conf}`, or `IDE/MCUEXPRESSO/benchmark/source/run_benchmark.c` beyond the deterministic-seed defaults, broken Renesas libc shim, and Curve25519 blinding regression noted here. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks were dominated by project manifests, sample build instructions, benchmark harness setup, and non-executable headers/scripts without a stronger trust-boundary break than the findings appended above.

### Medium - `certs/ecc/genecc.sh` masks ECC fixture regeneration failures, and its custom-curve key generation logic is inverted so missing keys are never regenerated - location: `certs/ecc/genecc.sh:112-140,168`, caller `certs/renewcerts.sh:68-69` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the secp256k1 and brainpool branches use `if [ -f ./certs/ecc/secp256k1-key.pem ]; then openssl ecparam ... -out ./certs/ecc/secp256k1-key.pem` and the same pattern for `bp256r1-key.pem`, so generation runs only when the file already exists; if the file is missing, the script still proceeds into `openssl req -key ./certs/ecc/{secp256k1,bp256r1}-key.pem ...`. The script has no `set -e`/status checks and ends with unconditional `exit 0`, while `renewcerts.sh` Step 0 trusts only that exit code. - impact: clean rebuilds or deleted-key regeneration can false-pass with stale/missing custom-curve fixtures, while normal runs silently overwrite existing keys and churn the generated cert set. - recommendation: invert the conditions to `if [ ! -f ... ]`, fail closed with `set -euo pipefail`/checked `openssl` calls, and write regenerated files via temp + atomic rename. - reference: `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Medium - release packaging ships the ECC custom-curve lab private keys, including a signing CA key, so anyone can clone or forge those identities if the fixtures escape test-only use - location: `certs/ecc/{secp256k1-key.pem,bp256r1-key.pem,secp256k1-privkey.pem,ca-secp256k1-key.pem}:1`, distribution manifest `certs/ecc/include.am:12-24,28-33`, active consumers `tests/test-ecc-cust-curves.conf:5-240` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator, CI fixture, or sample-derived deployment reuses the bundled custom-curve certs/CA outside an isolated lab. - evidence: this chunk includes plaintext `-----BEGIN EC PRIVATE KEY-----` / `-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----` material for the secp256k1, brainpool, and secp256k1 CA identities; `certs/ecc/include.am` places those keys in `EXTRA_DIST`; `tests/test-ecc-cust-curves.conf` then uses the same files as live `-k` identities and trusts `certs/ecc/ca-secp256k1-cert.pem`. - impact: anyone with repo access can impersonate the bundled custom-curve server/client fixtures or mint additional `server2-secp256k1`-style certificates under the shipped secp256k1 CA. - recommendation: stop distributing these private keys, generate them during test setup, and keep release/sample consumers public-cert-only unless a developer injects local private material explicitly. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - MDK5-ARM `SimpleClient` pins wolfSSL certificate time checks to a compile-time 2023 date instead of a real clock - location: `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Conf/user_settings.h:33`, `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/SimpleClient/main.c:71-108,216-218` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator reuses the shipped MDK5-ARM sample without replacing the time hook with a trusted RTC/NTP source. - evidence: the profile enables `WOLFSSL_USER_CURRTIME`; `main.c` overrides `time(time_t *t)` to return a static `epochTime`; startup then sets that value from fixed macros `RTC_YEAR 2023`, `RTC_MONTH 1`, `RTC_DAY 1` via `setTime((time_t)((RTC_YEAR-1970)*365*24*60*60) + ...)`. - impact: TLS/X.509 validation in sample-derived clients is anchored to a stale fake date, so expired certificates that were valid in 2023 can be accepted and currently valid certificates can fail as not-yet-valid. - recommendation: require a real RTC or synchronized network time before certificate validation, or fail closed until time is trustworthy; keep the fixed-date hook behind an explicit test-only macro. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - the C# TLS client sample explicitly overrides certificate not-before failures and never binds the expected hostname, weakening server authentication even with `SSL_VERIFY_PEER` enabled - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL-TLS-Client/wolfSSL-TLS-Client.cs:56-65,179,191` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: developer reuses the shipped sample callback/client logic; attacker can MITM or operate a server with any certificate chaining to the configured CA. - evidence: `myVerify()` calls `wolfssl.X509_STORE_CTX_get_error(x509_ctx)` and forces success when `error == wolfcrypt.ASN_BEFORE_DATE_E`; the client installs that callback with `wolfssl.CTX_set_verify(ctx, wolfssl.SSL_VERIFY_PEER, myVerify)`. Repo search found no `check_domain_name`/`CheckDomainName` call in this sample before `tcp.Connect("localhost", 11111)` / `wolfssl.connect(ssl)`. - impact: sample-derived clients can accept future-dated certificates and do not authenticate the peer name, so a wrong-host but CA-signed certificate can still terminate TLS. - recommendation: do not override date-validation failures in the verify callback, and require explicit hostname/IP verification before `connect()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mainly declarative headers, IDE metadata, or sample scaffolding once the concrete issues above were accounted for; this pass did not promote a stronger chunk-local bug from `IDE/WIN/{user_settings.h,user_settings_dtls.h,wolfssl-fips.vcxproj,test.vcxproj,wolfssl-fips.sln,README.txt}`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_server/main/include/{wifi_connect.h,server-tls.h,time_helper.h,main.h}`, `mqx/util_lib/Sources/{util.c,util.h}`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/silabs/*.h`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/nrf51.c`, or `wrapper/Ada/tests/src/support/*` beyond the promoted fixture-packaging, time-validation, and sample-authentication findings above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the Windows FIPS project’s ASLR/DEP tradeoffs are explicitly documented in `IDE/WIN/README.txt`, the Silabs/Ada files are declaration-only in this chunk, and the remaining embedded helper/sample files were mostly glue without a sharper exploit path or material production failure mode on this pass.

### High - `Docker/run.sh` mounts the host user's entire `~/.ssh` into a container that immediately runs repo-controlled build scripts, so a malicious tree or compromised dependency can exfiltrate developer SSH keys - location: `Docker/run.sh:8-10`, related trust bootstrap `Docker/Dockerfile:25` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: developer runs `Docker/run.sh` on an untrusted fork/tree or on a compromised dependency chain with outbound network access from the container. - evidence: `docker run ... -v ${HOME}/.ssh:/home/docker/.ssh:ro ... /bin/bash -c "./autogen.sh && ./configure ${*@Q} && make"` gives the build container read access to host private keys and known_hosts before executing repository-controlled shell scripts. Read-only still permits exfiltration. - impact: attacker-controlled build steps can steal developer SSH private keys or use mounted SSH material to pivot into private repos/infrastructure from the containerized build. - recommendation: do not mount `~/.ssh` by default; use `--mount type=ssh`/agent forwarding for the rare fetch step that needs it, scope credentials to specific commands, and keep build/test containers credential-free by default. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Mynewt file-port shims misimplement `fseek`/`ftell`: `SEEK_SET` uses an uninitialized offset, and `ftell` moves the file pointer to EOF instead of reporting the current position - location: `wolfcrypt/src/port/mynewt/mynewt_port.c:78-115`, mapping in `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/wc_port.h:1012-1014`, affected consumers include `src/ocsp.c:1191-1199`, `src/ssl_misc.c:231-256`, `src/x509.c:13526-13537` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `mynewt_fseek()` declares `uint32_t fs_offset;` then in `SEEK_SET` does `fs_offset += offset;` without initialization; `mynewt_ftell()` calls `fs_filelen(stream, &fs_offset); fs_seek(stream, fs_offset); return fs_offset;`, so a tell operation both returns file length and mutates stream position to EOF. wolfSSL maps `XFSEEK`/`XFTELL` to these shims on Mynewt. - impact: filesystem-backed cert/CRL/OCSP and PEM parsing on Mynewt can seek to unpredictable offsets or silently move to EOF during size probes, causing intermittent load failures and hard-to-reproduce parsing bugs. - recommendation: initialize `fs_offset` correctly for each whence case, implement `ftell` as a pure current-position query with no seek side effect, reject negative/overflowing seeks, and add regression coverage for save-position/seek-end/restore patterns used by X.509 and OCSP loaders. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - MQX `wolfcrypt_test` passes an uninitialized `func_args` struct into the test harness, which then dereferences garbage `argc`/`argv` in `mygetopt()` - location: `mqx/wolfcrypt_test/Sources/main.c:68-81`, sink `wolfcrypt/test/test.c:1936-1938` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the sample declares `func_args args;` with no zero-init and immediately calls `wolfcrypt_test(&args);`; `wolfcrypt_test()` then sets `((func_args*)args)->return_code = -1` and executes `mygetopt(((func_args*)args)->argc, ((func_args*)args)->argv, "s:m:a:h")`. - impact: the MQX test app can crash, walk arbitrary stack garbage as `argv`, or behave nondeterministically before any actual crypto test runs, undermining bring-up and CI confidence on that target. - recommendation: initialize the structure (`func_args args = {0};`) or provide a real argv array before calling the harness, and keep this platform wrapper in CI so argument-plumbing regressions fail fast. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Medium - RA6M3 sample TLS client loads a CA but never enables certificate or endpoint verification, so any reachable TLS server can impersonate `SERVER_IP` - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/client-wolfssl/src/wolfssl_thread_entry.c:95-131`, configured peer `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/client-wolfssl/wolfssl_thread_entry.h:35-38`, default context behavior `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or answer on the configured `SERVER_IP:DEFAULT_PORT`. - evidence: the client loads a CA with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer(ctx, CERT, SIZEOF_CERT, ...)` and calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`, but repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)`, `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`, or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` in the RA6M3 client path; new contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c`. - impact: sample-derived RA6M3 clients authenticate neither the certificate chain nor the expected peer identity, enabling straightforward MITM or wrong-endpoint impersonation. - recommendation: set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` on the context and bind the expected DNS name/IP before `wolfSSL_connect()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RA6M3 sample server exposes an unauthenticated remote shutdown command: any TLS client that sends `shutdown` breaks the accept loop and leaves the thread in an infinite cleanup loop - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/server-wolfssl/src/wolfssl_thread_entry.c:101-174`, default port `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/server-wolfssl/wolfssl_thread_entry.h:40`, cleanup sink `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/common/util.h:40-44` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: network access to the sample server port; no client authentication is configured in the sample. - evidence: after `wolfSSL_accept(ssl)` the server does `wolfSSL_read(ssl, buff, sizeof(buff) - 1); if (strncmp(buff, "shutdown", 8) == 0) break;`; the post-loop path calls `util_inf_loop(...)`, which cleans up and then spins forever in `while (1)`. - impact: a single unauthenticated client can remotely stop the sample service until reboot, turning the documented demo endpoint into a trivial network DoS target. - recommendation: remove the network-triggered shutdown path from shipped samples, or gate it behind explicit debug-only/local-console authorization and close the connection instead of terminating the server loop. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`

### Low - the new Azure Sphere sample package launches the server app but ships no inbound networking capability in its manifest, so the sample cannot accept connections as packaged - location: `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/wolfssl_new_azsphere/main.c:81`, manifest `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/wolfssl_new_azsphere/app_manifest.json:7-10`, working comparison `IDE/VS-AZURE-SPHERE/server/app_manifest.json:8` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: `main.c` calls `azsphere_server_app();`, but the bundled manifest contains only `Gpio` and empty `AllowedApplicationConnections`; it lacks `AllowedTcpServerPorts`, whereas the repo's other Azure Sphere server sample declares `"AllowedTcpServerPorts": [ 11111 ]`. - impact: the sample is packaged in a self-contradictory state and will fail at runtime when trying to expose its server socket, creating avoidable platform bring-up/debug churn. - recommendation: add the required Azure Sphere networking capability for the sample's listen port, or split client-only and server-capable manifests so the packaged app matches its runtime behavior. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Reviewed - remaining files in this chunk were mostly port headers, Doxygen templates/config, simple helper scripts, or sample/build metadata; apart from the promoted issues above, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material performance fault from `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/{atmel,kcapi,devcrypto,arm}/*.h`, `doc/formats/html/*`, `IDE/QNX/example-cmac/*`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/Projects/tools/*`, `cmake/modules/{FindARIA,FindOQS}.cmake`, `IDE/mynewt/*` packaging metadata, `mqx/wolfcrypt_benchmark/*`, or `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/wolfssl_new_azsphere/{CMakeLists.txt,CMakeSettings.json,launch.vs.json,applibs_versions.h}` beyond the Docker credential exposure, Mynewt file-IO shim bugs, MQX args bug, RA6M3 sample authn/shutdown flaws, and Azure Sphere manifest mismatch noted here. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks mostly found declarations, docs, and straightforward build glue without a more direct trust-boundary break or production-impacting hot path than the findings appended above.

### High - release packaging ships the legacy 1024-bit demo PKI private keys and multiple embedded/sample builds wire those same repo-public identities in as live CA/server material, making sample-derived deployments trivially forgeable - location: `certs/1024/include.am:5-24`, plaintext keys `certs/1024/{ca-key.pem,client-key.pem,server-key.pem}:1`, 1024-bit certs `certs/1024/{ca-cert.pem,client-cert.pem,server-cert.pem}:13-34`, embedded exports `wolfssl/certs_test.h:418-700`, active consumers `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/*/components/wolfssl/include/user_settings.h:1078-1093` and `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/{client-wolfssl,server-wolfssl}/wolfssl_thread_entry.h:34-38` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator or sample deployment reuses the shipped `certs/1024` identities or any build embedding `certs_test.h`’s 1024-bit CA/server material outside an isolated lab. - evidence: `include.am` distributes `ca-key.pem`, `client-key.pem`, and `server-key.pem`; those files are unencrypted private keys; the paired certs are only 1024-bit RSA; `certs_test.h` embeds `ca_cert_der_1024`, `server_key_der_1024`, and `server_cert_der_1024`; ESP-IDF example configs and the RA6M3 sample map their active `CTX_CA_CERT` / `CTX_SERVER_CERT` / `CTX_SERVER_KEY` macros to those same 1024-bit fixtures. - impact: anyone with the repo can clone the exact server identity, sign new chains under the shipped CA, or impersonate sample-derived endpoints that trust these defaults; the 1024-bit strength also falls below modern security expectations. - recommendation: stop shipping private keys in `EXTRA_DIST` and `certs_test.h`, replace 1024-bit fixtures with generated lab-only material of modern size, and require examples to inject local credentials explicitly instead of compiling in repo-public keys. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RA6M3 sample client loads a CA buffer but never enables peer verification or binds the expected server identity, so any reachable TLS server can impersonate `192.168.11.40:11111` - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/client-wolfssl/src/wolfssl_thread_entry.c:74-75,98-127`, config `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/client-wolfssl/wolfssl_thread_entry.h:35-38`, default ctx behavior `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the LAN path or answer on the configured IP/port. - evidence: the client connects to literal `SERVER_IP`/`DEFAULT_PORT`, creates a context with `wolfSSL_CTX_new(...)`, loads `ca_cert_der_1024` via `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer(ctx, CERT, SIZEOF_CERT, ...)`, then calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`; repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)`, `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`, or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` anywhere under `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3`, while new contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c:512`. - impact: the sample authenticates neither the certificate chain nor the intended peer, so a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic. - recommendation: set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` explicitly and bind the expected DNS name/IP before `wolfSSL_connect()`, keeping any insecure demo mode behind an explicit lab-only switch. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### High - `Docker/wolfCLU` builds pull both `wolfssl` and `wolfCLU` over plaintext HTTP and execute them as root during image build, so any network/MITM attacker can inject arbitrary code into the produced container - location: `Docker/wolfCLU/Dockerfile:14,17` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: image is built on an untrusted network path, behind a tampering proxy, or against a compromised mirror. - evidence: the Dockerfile runs `git clone ... http://github.com/wolfssl/wolfssl` and `git clone ... http://github.com/wolfssl/wolfCLU`, then immediately executes `./autogen.sh && ./configure && make && make install` as root in the builder stage. - impact: a build-time attacker can replace the source tree with malicious code, yielding arbitrary code execution during build and a trojaned `libwolfssl.so` / `wolfssl` binary copied into the final image. - recommendation: switch to `https://` (or SSH), pin an immutable commit/tag with checksum/signature verification, and avoid running unauthenticated network-fetched code as root. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mostly static certificates/CRLs, IDE metadata, linker/startup scaffolding, or packaging manifests; no sharper chunk-local exploit or material performance issue was confirmed from `certs/external/*`, `certs/crl/extra-crls/*`, `IDE/{IAR-EWARM,VS-ARM,CSBENCH,TRUESTUDIO}/*`, `wrapper/{Ada,rust,CSharp}/*/Properties/AssemblyInfo.cs`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/{liboqs/liboqs.h,nrf51.h}`, `wolfcrypt/src/port/rpi_pico/{pico.c,README.md}`, or `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/libs/*` beyond the promoted shipped-test-key, sample-authentication, and Docker supply-chain issues above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks were dominated by static trust stores, sample build glue, declarations, and project metadata; the remaining code paths did not show a stronger exploit sink or production hot path than the findings appended here.

### Medium - LPCXpresso RNG port reads a fifth 32-bit word past its documented 128-bit OTP random block and feeds that out-of-range value directly into DRBG seeding - location: `IDE/LPCXPRESSO/lib_wolfssl/lpc_18xx_port.c:84-106`, config `IDE/LPCXPRESSO/lib_wolfssl/user_settings.h:68-72`, seed sink `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:2223-2241` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator uses the shipped LPCXpresso port/profile for RNG-backed operations. - evidence: the port comments the RNG MMIO window as "total of 128 bits" at `mRandData`, but `rand_gen()` returns `mRandData[mRandIndex]` and only wraps after `if (++mRandIndex > 4)`, so calls consume indices `0..4` before reset. `CUSTOM_RAND_GENERATE rand_gen` is wired into `wc_GenerateSeed()`, which copies each returned word/byte straight into the seed buffer. - impact: every fifth seed word comes from outside the intended 128-bit RNG output window, which can inject stale/predictable register contents or fault on some parts, weakening entropy and destabilizing crypto initialization. - recommendation: wrap after four words (`>= 4` after increment or `> 3` before use), regenerate before any out-of-range read, and add a regression asserting one `Chip_OTP_GenRand()` yields exactly four 32-bit outputs. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - release packaging ships full P-521 and Ed448 private test PKIs, so anyone with repo access can clone or forge those TLS identities if the bundles leak beyond isolated tests - location: `certs/p521/include.am:6-30`, private keys `certs/p521/{root-p521-priv.pem,ca-p521-priv.pem,server-p521-priv.pem,client-p521-priv.pem}`, `certs/ed448/include.am:6-30`, private keys `certs/ed448/{root-ed448-priv.pem,ca-ed448-priv.pem,server-ed448-priv.pem,client-ed448-priv.pem}`, active consumers `tests/test-p521.conf:4-59`, `tests/test-ed448.conf:4-59` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: an integrator, demo, or test environment reuses these shipped roots/CAs/leaf certs outside lab-only isolation. - evidence: both `include.am` files place root, CA, server, and client private keys in `EXTRA_DIST`; the committed PEMs contain plaintext private-key material; shipped test configs then start peers with `-k ...-priv.pem` and trust the matching `root-*.pem` / `client-*.pem`. - impact: attackers can mint arbitrary certificates under the shipped roots/CAs or impersonate the exact published server/client identities, making any sample-derived trust setup trivially clonable. - recommendation: stop distributing these private keys in release artifacts, generate ephemeral keys during test setup, and keep shipped bundles public-cert-only unless a developer injects local private material explicitly. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - C# PSK client hard-codes a repo-public 4-byte shared secret and fixed identity, so sample-derived PSK deployments are trivially impersonable - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL-TLS-PSK-Client/wolfSSL-TLS-PSK-Client.cs:50-64,108-133` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: developer reuses the sample's PSK callback or ships the sample credential unchanged. - evidence: `my_psk_client_cb()` always copies identity bytes for `"C# client"` and key bytes `{ 26, 43, 60, 77 }`; the client then forces a PSK cipher suite (`DHE-PSK-AES128-CBC-SHA256`) and connects using that static secret. - impact: anyone who has the repo can stand up a rogue PSK server the client will authenticate to, or authenticate as the sample client against any server derived from the same callback; all trust collapses to a published 32-bit secret. - recommendation: remove the built-in PSK, require deployment-specific PSKs from secure configuration/provisioning, and make any demo secret opt-in and loudly non-production. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - C# threaded TLS server spawns an unbounded OS thread per connection and blocks indefinitely in handshake/read, enabling trivial remote thread exhaustion - location: `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL-TLS-ServerThreaded/wolfSSL-TLS-ServerThreaded.cs:65-87,186-191` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can open TCP connections to the sample listener on port `11111`. - evidence: the accept loop runs `AcceptSocket()` inside `while (true)` and starts `new Thread(new ThreadStart(thread.start_client))` for every client; each worker then blocks in `wolfssl.accept(ssl)` and an infinite `wolfssl.read()` loop. The file sets no socket timeouts and no concurrency cap. - impact: many slow or idle connections can exhaust threads and memory, denying service to legitimate clients. - recommendation: use a bounded thread pool or async I/O, set handshake/read idle timeouts, and cap concurrent clients before accepting more work. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V4 API and Web Service`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mainly sample config headers, docs, cert text bodies, and IDE metadata; this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material performance issue in `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RZN2L/common/{user_settings.h,wolfssl_demo.h}`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/DK-S7G2/example_server-template/src/app_entry.c`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/cypress/psoc6_crypto.h`, `doc/{generate_documentation.sh,check_api.sh,QUIC.md,README_DOXYGEN,include.am,README.txt}`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/template/main/{Kconfig.projbuild,main.c,component.mk,CMakeLists.txt}`, or `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/EchoServer/{main.c,EchoServer.uvprojx,EchoServer.uvoptx,wolfssl-link.sct,Abstract.txt}` beyond the promoted RNG, shipped-key, PSK, and threaded-DoS findings above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found a few low-signal sample rough edges (for example stale/manual time setup and doc-helper shell brittleness), but no stronger chunk-local exploit path or production-impacting failure mode than the items appended here.

### Medium - ESP-IDF starter template disables watchdog recovery and halts on panic by default, so sample-derived devices can hang indefinitely instead of self-recovering - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/template/README.md:1-3`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/template/Makefile:7-10`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/template/CMakeLists.txt:22-27`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/template/sdkconfig.defaults:37-54`, `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/template/sdkconfig.defaults.esp8266:1-4,24-30` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator starts from the shipped `wolfssl_template` for a real device/app and does not undo the defaults; any crash, deadlock, long-running path, or attacker-triggered fault then loses watchdog/panic recovery. - evidence: the README presents this as a template "to get started with your own project"; both build systems force `WOLFSSL_ESP_NO_WATCHDOG=1`; the default configs disable task and interrupt watchdogs (`CONFIG_ESP_TASK_WDT_EN=n`, `CONFIG_ESP_INT_WDT=n`, `CONFIG_TASK_WDT=n`) and change panic handling to halt instead of reboot (`CONFIG_ESP_PANIC_PRINT_REBOOT=n`, `CONFIG_ESP_PANIC_PRINT_HALT=y`). - impact: a sample-derived production build can turn otherwise recoverable faults into persistent device DoS until manual reset/power-cycle, which is especially risky for unattended networked firmware. - recommendation: keep watchdogs and panic reboot enabled in the starter template; move watchdog suppression behind an explicit benchmark/test-only option that defaults off. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`
