### Medium - packaging Dockerfiles use floating distro bases and unpinned package upgrades, making release/package builds non-reproducible over time - location: `Docker/packaging/debian/Dockerfile:1,3-5`, `Docker/packaging/fedora/Dockerfile:1,3` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the Debian image builds from `FROM debian:latest`, runs `apt-get -y upgrade`, then installs unversioned packages; the Fedora image builds from `FROM fedora:latest` and installs unversioned toolchain packages. - impact: the same packaging build can pull materially different toolchains and dependency sets on different days, causing hard-to-reproduce packaging failures, output drift, and difficult support/security triage for release artifacts. - recommendation: pin base images by version/digest, pin package versions where practical, and avoid blanket `upgrade` in release-packaging images unless the resulting environment is also locked and tracked. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`

### Reviewed - the remaining files in this chunk were mostly thin headers, board mapping stubs, shell glue, and certificate fixtures; the 3072 client PEMs and expired test certs mainly reinforced already-tracked shipped-private-key / trust-anchor-as-leaf test-material themes rather than adding a sharper new exploit path in this pass - location: `certs/3072/*`, `certs/test/expired/*`, `IDE/MYSQL/*`, `IDE/PlatformIO/examples/*/include/*`, `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/wolfssl_new_azsphere/HardwareDefinitions/*/template_appliance.h`, `IDE/apple-universal/wolfssl-multiplatform/wolfssl-multiplatform/Assets.xcassets/Contents.json` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found distributed 3072 test credentials and self-signed `CA:TRUE` fixture usage in `tests/test.conf`, but those matched the repo’s existing shipped-test-key/fixture-design pattern already captured elsewhere; the rest were declarations, README text, or packaging metadata without a stronger chunk-local trust-boundary break.

### Medium - ESP-IDF `wolfssl_server` loads a client CA but never enables client-certificate verification, so any TLS client can reach the sample protocol and trigger the built-in network `shutdown` command - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_server/main/server-tls.c:248,291-322,374,388-396,403`, implied mTLS docs `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_server/README_server_sm.md:23,84-87`, default verify mode `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can connect to the sample server port on the local network; no client-certificate auth or application auth is required. - evidence: the server loads `client_sm2` with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer(ctx, client_sm2, ...)`, then `bind()`/`listen()`/`accept()` and `wolfSSL_accept(ssl)` before processing attacker-controlled `buff`; `strncmp(buff, "shutdown", 8) == 0` sets `shutdown = 1`. Repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER...)` or `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_FAIL_IF_NO_PEER_CERT` anywhere under `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_server`, and new contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE`. - impact: the example looks like mTLS because it loads a client CA, but any client that completes an ordinary handshake can use the service and remotely stop it. - recommendation: set `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER | WOLFSSL_VERIFY_FAIL_IF_NO_PEER_CERT, NULL)` (or an explicit verify callback), bind/authorize expected client identities, and remove or gate wire-level `shutdown` behind an authenticated debug path. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - release packaging ships the 4096-bit self-signed client CA private key and embeds it into test/benchmark code, so anyone can clone the same repo-public identity wherever these fixtures are reused - location: distributed files `certs/4096/{client-cert.pem,client-key.pem}`, manifest `certs/4096/include.am:6-9`, generator `certs/renewcerts.sh:267-280`, extension profile `certs/renewcerts/wolfssl.cnf:125-130`, embeddings `gencertbuf.pl:107,109` and `wolfssl/certs_test.h:2905-3146,3211-3397`, consumers `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.c:10152-10153` and `wolfcrypt/test/test.c:25231-25523,29891-29892,64109-64110` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator, test harness, or sample-derived build reuses the bundled 4096-bit fixture or ships binaries containing the generated cert buffers. - evidence: `certs/4096/client-key.pem` contains plaintext `-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----`; `include.am` distributes both PEM and DER key/cert; `renewcerts.sh` signs `./4096/client-cert.pem` from `./4096/client-key.pem` using `[wolfssl_opts]`, and that profile sets `basicConstraints=CA:true`; `gencertbuf.pl`/`wolfssl/certs_test.h` then materialize `client_key_der_4096` and `client_cert_der_4096` that the benchmark/test code consumes directly. - impact: anyone with repo access can impersonate this shipped 4096-bit identity or reuse the same trust anchor in sample-derived environments, and test binaries built from these fixtures also carry the private key material. - recommendation: stop distributing or embedding private keys by default; generate 4096-bit fixtures during CI/lab setup and ship only public certs unless an explicit non-production fixture mode is enabled. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - remaining files in this chunk were mostly project/workspace metadata, static attribute/AIA cert fixtures, or straightforward example scaffolding; this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material performance issue in `IDE/PlatformIO/examples/*`, `.github/workflows/hostap-files/*`, `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/wolfssl_new_azsphere/HardwareDefinitions/*`, `IDE/HEXIWEAR/*`, `cmake/consumer/*`, `wrapper/rust/wolfssl-wolfcrypt/tests/common/mod.rs`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/rpi_pico/pico.h`, or `certs/acert/*` beyond the ESP-IDF server auth gap and shipped 4096 private-key fixture above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found mostly build glue, docs, and static parser fixtures; the AIA certs are only referenced from `tests/api.c:20413,20444,20489`, and `IDE/MDK5-ARM/README.md:44` frames `Src/ssl-dummy.c` as a wolfCrypt-only link shim rather than a production integration path.

### High - MDK5-ARM sample profile enables deterministic test seeding, so `EchoClient` and `CryptBenchmark` builds can run with predictable wolfCrypt RNG output - location: `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Conf/user_settings.h:25-26,201,366`, consumers `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/EchoClient/EchoClient.uvprojx:337,416,883`, `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/CryptBenchmark/CryptBenchmark.uvprojx:337,386,794`, sample call path `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/EchoClient/main.c:189`, seed fallback `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:3621-3628,3864-3874` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator builds either MDK5-ARM project as shipped, or reuses the shared `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Conf/user_settings.h` in a real product build. - evidence: the shared MDK5 profile defines `NO_DEV_RANDOM`, `HAVE_HASHDRBG`, and `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST`; wolfCrypt maps that build to `USE_TEST_GENSEED` and fills the seed with deterministic bytes (`output[i] = (byte)i`). Both project files import that shared header via `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS`, and `EchoClient` immediately runs `echoclient_test(&args)` from the shipped build. - impact: RNG-backed operations in sample-derived builds (ephemeral TLS secrets, key generation, nonces, session secrets) become predictable across boots/devices, breaking confidentiality and integrity assumptions. - recommendation: remove `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST` from shipped MDK5 defaults, wire a real board entropy source before enabling TLS/crypto, and fail closed when no trusted seed source is present. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - MDK5-ARM `EchoClient` imports the generic `echoclient` example that loads CA material but then disables peer verification and never binds the expected server identity - location: `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/EchoClient/main.c:156,189`, project wiring `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/EchoClient/EchoClient.uvprojx:337,416`, implementation `examples/echoclient/echoclient.c:151-166,215,231` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or operate the endpoint the sample connects to. - evidence: the MDK project calls `echoclient_test(&args)` directly; `echoclient_test()` loads CA files/buffers with `SSL_CTX_load_verify_locations()` / `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer()`, then explicitly does `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE, 0);` before `SSL_connect(ssl)`. Repo search found no `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()` or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` in that client path. - impact: a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic while the sample appears to be using CA-backed TLS. - recommendation: switch the sample to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER`, bind the expected DNS name/IP before connect, and avoid reusing the insecure `echoclient` defaults in packaged board examples. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - `scripts/bench/bench_functions.sh` creates its control FIFO with `mktemp -u`, leaving a race where another local user/process can pre-create the path and corrupt benchmark control flow or clobber a writable file - location: `scripts/bench/bench_functions.sh:25,35,37,86` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker has local access on the same host and can win the race between `mktemp -u` and `mkfifo`. - evidence: the script does `fifo="$(mktemp -u)"`, then `mkfifo $fifo` without checking success, and later writes/reads that path with `echo "finished" > $fifo`, `read ... < $fifo`, and `rm $fifo`. If the path is pre-created as a regular file or symlink, the script can write to the attacker-chosen target or consume spoofed completion data. - impact: local attackers can hang or falsely complete runs, poison benchmark outputs, or clobber files writable by the invoking user. - recommendation: create a private temp directory/file atomically (`mktemp -d` or `mktemp`), create the FIFO inside it, quote path uses, and abort if `mkfifo` fails. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - remaining files in this chunk were primarily C# sample glue/project metadata, TI/Xilinx declarations, Infineon config docs, Renesas wrapped-key test material, and negative X.509 fixture assets; this pass did not confirm a stronger chunk-local exploit or material performance failure in `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL-DTLS-Server/*`, `certs/intermediate/ca_false_intermediate/*`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/test/key_data/*`, `IDE/Infineon/*`, `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/CryptBenchmark/*`, `tirtos/packages/ti/net/wolfssl/tests/EK_TM4C1294XL/wolfcrypt/test/*`, or `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/xilinx/*` beyond the promoted MDK5-ARM deterministic-seed/authentication issues and the host-side benchmark FIFO race above - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks mostly found sample scaffolding, packaged test fixtures, hardware-wrapped key blobs, or declaration-only headers; the `ca_false_intermediate` fixture bundle continues the already-recurring shipped-test-PKI/private-key theme, but this pass did not promote a sharper distinct issue from those files over the findings appended here.

### High - IAR EWARM and KDS shipped user settings enable wolfCrypt's deterministic test seeding, making RNG-backed crypto predictable - location: `IDE/IAR-EWARM/Projects/user_settings.h:17`, `IDE/KDS/config/user_settings.h:16`, seed fallback `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:3621-3628,3864-3874` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator reuses either shipped `user_settings.h` in any build that performs TLS, key generation, nonce generation, or other RNG-backed operations. - evidence: both chunk headers define `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST`; `random.c` maps that build to `USE_TEST_GENSEED` and fills the seed with the fixed public byte sequence `output[i] = (byte)i`. - impact: sample-derived builds start their DRBG from deterministic seed material, so keys, nonces, and session secrets become predictable across devices/boots using the default profile. - recommendation: remove `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST` from shipped defaults and fail closed until a real board entropy source/TRNG is wired. - reference: `v5.0.0-V11`, `v5.0.0-V13`, `v5.0.0-V15` (`ASVS 5.0`)

### High - Yocto image build pulls core sources over unauthenticated transport and floating branches, then the companion script publishes the resulting images to Docker Hub - location: `Docker/yocto/Dockerfile:16-29`, `Docker/yocto/buildAndPush.sh:13-20` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: CI or a developer runs the Yocto build on a hostile or intercepted network, or upstream `master` changes unexpectedly before build. - evidence: the Dockerfile clones `poky` via `git://git.yoctoproject.org/poky` (no TLS/integrity) and clones `https://github.com/wolfssl/meta-wolfssl.git` at floating `--branch=master`; `buildAndPush.sh` then pushes `wolfssl/yocto:${ver}-${CUR_DATE}` and `${ver}-latest` on successful builds. - impact: a network MITM or compromised/moved upstream branch can inject attacker-controlled build recipes into published wolfSSL Yocto images, creating a direct supply-chain compromise path. - recommendation: fetch over authenticated HTTPS only, pin exact commits/tags (or digests) for both `poky` and `meta-wolfssl`, and verify provenance before publishing. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RA6M3 FreeRTOS+TCP port uses libc `rand()` for TCP initial sequence numbers, making connection spoofing/reset materially easier - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/common/src/freertos_tcp_port.c:36-64` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: sample-derived RA6M3 build uses this callback for FreeRTOS+TCP and an attacker can spoof or inject traffic on the reachable network path; no `srand()` was found under `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3`. - evidence: `ulApplicationGetNextSequenceNumber()` returns `ulRand()`, and `ulRand()` is just `(uint32_t) rand();`. The file itself warns the implementations are "NOT SUITABLE FOR PRODUCTION" and "are not secure." - impact: predictable TCP ISNs weaken off-path/adjacent-path resistance to forged segments and connection resets, especially after reboot or with default libc PRNG state. - recommendation: derive ISNs from a per-device secret/CSPRNG or hardware RNG as required by the stack, and remove the `rand()` fallback from shipped common sources. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - release packaging ships private static-ephemeral TLS keys, and the sample server can load them directly, so anyone with repo access can decrypt traffic from deployments that reuse these test keys - location: `certs/statickeys/include.am:8-26`, private keys `certs/statickeys/{ecc-secp256r1.pem,dh-ffdhe2048.pem,x25519.pem}:1`, sample consumer `examples/server/server.c:2804-2833`, test consumer `scripts/sniffer-testsuite.test:132-192` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build enables `WOLFSSL_STATIC_EPHEMERAL`/sniffer-style static key support and an integrator reuses the bundled statickeys in a real listener or capture workflow. - evidence: `EXTRA_DIST` ships the private PEMs; `examples/server/server.c` calls `wolfSSL_CTX_set_ephemeral_key()` with those exact files for ECDH, DH, and X25519; `scripts/sniffer-testsuite.test` uses the same repo-public private keys to decrypt captured TLS 1.3 pcaps. - impact: any deployment that reuses these fixed static-ephemeral keys loses confidentiality/forward-secrecy for captured sessions because attackers can derive the same handshake secrets from the public repo keys. - recommendation: do not ship reusable private statickeys in release artifacts; generate them per test run or require explicit local injection, and keep static-ephemeral support fenced behind test-only configuration. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - custom embOS benchmark template calls the test suite instead of the benchmark harness, so the advertised benchmark target never measures performance - location: `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/custom_port/wolfcrypt_benchmark_custom_port/Application/runBenchmarks.c:10-12`, contrasted with correct call in `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_benchmark_SAMV71_XULT/Application/runBenchmarks.c:10-12` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the custom-port file prints `"Begin benchmark tests"` and then calls `wolfcrypt_test(NULL)`; the parallel SAMV71 benchmark sample calls `benchmark_test(NULL)` as expected. - impact: developers selecting the custom benchmark target get functional tests instead of benchmark numbers, producing false performance results and wasting bring-up/debug time. - recommendation: replace `wolfcrypt_test(NULL)` with `benchmark_test(NULL)` and add a smoke check that benchmark targets execute the benchmark entry point. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - remaining files in this chunk were mostly IDE metadata, packaging manifests, sample scaffolding, or declaration-only headers after the issues above were accounted for; spot checks did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material production performance failure in `zephyr/samples/wolfssl_tls_thread/*` beyond test/sample config churn, `IDE/QNX/*`, `IDE/MCUEXPRESSO/wolfssl/liblinks.xml`, `IDE/MSVS-2019-AZSPHERE/shared/util.h`, `IDE/VS-AZURE-SPHERE/shared/util.h`, `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/port/af_alg/*`, `wrapper/CSharp/wolfSSL_CSharp/Properties/*`, `Docker/OpenWrt/*`, `tirtos/*`, `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/test/*`, `IDE/PlatformIO/examples/*`, or the remaining embOS/MPLABX project files in this pass. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the remaining content was dominated by project files, docs, static metadata, or already-understood sample harness code; the Zephyr TLS-thread sample at least does set peer verification in `src/tls_threaded.c`, so no stronger new TLS-auth finding from that subdir was promoted here.

### Medium - the IAR embOS SAMV71 benchmark ships a half-empty `Release` configuration that no longer matches the documented/debug board profile, so `Rebuild All` can fail or produce meaningless release benchmark artifacts - location: `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_benchmark_SAMV71_XULT/wolfcrypt_benchmark.ewp:185-186,315-319,734,782-783,1149-1150,1278-1279,1629-1742`, `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_benchmark_SAMV71_XULT/README_wolfcrypt_benchmark:45-52` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the working Debug config defines `__SAMV71Q21__` and `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS`, pulls in the board/user-settings include paths, and links the custom `samv71q21_wolfssl.icf`, `wolfcrypt_lib.a`, and embOS library. The only Release define in the project is `NDEBUG` (`1150`); `CCIncludePath2` is empty (`1278-1279`); `IlinkOutputFile` is `###Uninitialized###` (`1630`); `IlinkIcfOverride` is disabled and falls back to `lnk0t.icf` (`1689-1694`); and `IlinkAdditionalLibs` is empty (`1741-1742`). The README still tells users to open the project and `Rebuild All`. - impact: release benchmarking on this sample is not reproducible and may fail outright; if a user hand-fixes only part of it, the resulting binary no longer reflects the documented SAMV71 wolfSSL layout/settings, so benchmark numbers and memory behavior become misleading. - recommendation: delete the broken Release configuration or make it mirror Debug’s include paths, defines, linker script, and libraries, then add a smoke build for both IAR configurations. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - this chunk was mostly IAR project/debugger metadata and a setup README; beyond the broken `Release` benchmark configuration above, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local security issue or material runtime hot-path problem in `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_benchmark_SAMV71_XULT/{wolfcrypt_benchmark.ewd,wolfcrypt_benchmark.ewp,README_wolfcrypt_benchmark}`. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the remaining content was probe settings, placeholder debugger transport values, repo-relative embOS trial paths documented for manual setup, and straightforward benchmark task scaffolding around `benchmark_test(NULL)` without a clearer trust-boundary break.

### Medium - Renesas cs+ `wolfssl_lib` default project builds a legacy-TLS/legacy-cipher profile, so sample-derived RX firmware can still offer 3DES and old MD5-based handshake paths unless integrators harden it themselves - location: `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/wolfssl_lib/wolfssl_lib.mtpj:109-111,193-195,319-321,876-996`, active settings `IDE/Renesas/cs+/Projects/common/user_settings.h:58-65`, suite gates `wolfssl/internal.h:277-280,397-398,434-435`, runtime use `src/internal.c:4121-4124,4203-4206,4474-4477` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator ships the provided cs+ library project with its default `..\common\user_settings.h` and does not further restrict protocol/cipher negotiation at runtime. - evidence: the project compiles `des3.c`, `md5.c`, `md4.c`, `md2.c`, and passes `-define=WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` with `..\common`; that header disables only RC4 (`NO_RC4`) but repo search found no `NO_DES3`, `NO_MD5`, or `NO_OLD_TLS` there. With those guards absent, `wolfssl/internal.h` defines `BUILD_SSL_RSA_WITH_3DES_EDE_CBC_SHA`, `BUILD_TLS_DHE_RSA_WITH_3DES_EDE_CBC_SHA`, and `BUILD_TLS_RSA_WITH_NULL_MD5`, and `src/internal.c` adds those suites/MD5 old-TLS paths to the compiled library. - impact: the shipped Renesas library defaults keep obsolete protocol/cipher code reachable, increasing downgrade surface and making it easy for sample-derived embedded deployments to negotiate weak 3DES or legacy MD5/old-TLS handshake behavior that modern profiles should exclude. - recommendation: ship a hardened Renesas `user_settings.h` for this library that sets `NO_OLD_TLS`, `NO_DES3`, `NO_MD5`, `NO_MD4`, and `NO_MD2` unless explicitly needed, and drop the legacy modules from the default project to keep the RX build TLS-1.2+/AEAD-oriented by default. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - MQX CodeWarrior debug metadata is internally inconsistent: the shipped `*_Debug_PnE_U-MultiLink.launch` launchers actually select J-Link, and `wolfssl_client` reuses `wolfcrypt_test` RSE host IDs/names - location: `mqx/wolfcrypt_test/wolfcrypt_test_twrk70f120m_Int_Flash_SramData_Debug_{PnE_U-MultiLink,JTrace}.launch:80-86`, `mqx/wolfssl_client/wolfssl_client_twrk70f120m_Int_Flash_SramData_Debug_{PnE_U-MultiLink,JTrace}.launch:80-86`, `mqx/wolfssl_client/ReferencedRSESystems.xml:13,43,82,112,152,195,234,264`, compare `mqx/wolfcrypt_test/ReferencedRSESystems.xml:25,94,176,246` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: both `...Debug_PnE_U-MultiLink.launch` files set `DebuggerCommonData.Connection Type` to `com.freescale.mcu.debug.connections.jlink.arm` and point at the same `rseSystemId ...140905160549-0600.2` used by the JTrace launch, while the filename and `wizardSystemNameHint` still say `PnE U-MultiLink`; `wolfssl_client/ReferencedRSESystems.xml` names every imported host `wolfcrypt_test_*` and reuses the same `.140905160549-0600[.1-.3]` IDs as the separate `wolfcrypt_test` project. - impact: selecting the advertised PnE launcher actually drives J-Link settings, and importing both MQX samples into one workspace can alias stale shared debugger objects across projects, making board bring-up/debugging nondeterministic. - recommendation: regenerate the `.launch` and `ReferencedRSESystems.xml` files from clean per-project configs, give each project unique RSE IDs/host names, and align each launcher's filename/hint with its actual connection plugin. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code`

### Reviewed - this chunk was almost entirely CodeWarrior/J-Link/PnE workspace metadata; beyond the promoted debugger-metadata mismatch above, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local security issue or material performance failure in `mqx/wolfcrypt_test/{ReferencedRSESystems.xml,*.launch,*.jlink}` or `mqx/wolfssl_client/{ReferencedRSESystems.xml,*.launch,*.jlink}`. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found stale copied host names, shared RSE ids, and mislabeled transport metadata, but no direct runtime crypto/data-path bug or exploitable trust-boundary break in these declarative debugger project files.

### Medium - Apple Xcode library targets install `certs_test.h` as a public header while the shared Xcode defaults enable certificate buffers, putting repo-public private keys directly in the Apple SDK surface - location: `IDE/XCODE/wolfssl.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj:66,192,481,543,709,794,1989-1991,2017,2043-2045,2071`, `IDE/XCODE/user_settings.h:75-76`, `wolfssl/certs_test.h:707,1510,1880` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: an iOS/macOS/tvOS consumer reuses the shipped Xcode headers/defaults for sample or test TLS identities. - evidence: all three Apple targets (`wolfssl_ios`, `wolfssl_osx`, `wolfssl_tvos`) copy `certs_test.h` into `include/wolfssl`; the same Xcode configs build with `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS`, and the shared `user_settings.h` enables `USE_CERT_BUFFERS_2048` and `USE_CERT_BUFFERS_256`. `wolfssl/certs_test.h` contains embedded private-key arrays such as `client_key_der_2048`, `ca_key_der_2048`, and `server_key_der_2048`. - impact: sample-derived Apple apps can easily ship cloneable test CA/server/client identities from the public repo, letting anyone who knows the bundled material impersonate those endpoints or mint trusted certs where the test CA is reused. - recommendation: stop publishing `certs_test.h` in Apple library `CopyFiles` phases, keep test key buffers in test/example-only targets, and disable `USE_CERT_BUFFERS_*` in shipped Xcode defaults unless an explicit lab build enables them. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - `IDE/XCODE/wolfssl.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` was otherwise source-list/build-metadata heavy; beyond the public `certs_test.h` packaging issue above, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material performance failure from the remaining target/source duplication and Xcode build settings in this file. - location: `IDE/XCODE/wolfssl.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining content was dominated by file references, copy phases, and per-target build settings without a clearer trust-boundary break than the packaged test-key surface noted above.

### Medium - RX72N TSIP client sample loads a CA but never enables peer or peer-identity verification, so any reachable TLS server can impersonate the hard-coded endpoint - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/src/client/simple_tls_tsip_client.c:35-36,122-138,215-219,330`, default verify mode `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the same LAN or answer on `192.168.11.6:11111`. - evidence: the client hard-codes `SIMPLE_TLSSEVER_IP` / `SIMPLE_TLSSERVER_PORT`, creates a context with `wolfSSL_CTX_new(...)`, loads a CA via `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations()` / `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer()`, then calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`; repo search under `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/src` found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)`, `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`, or `wolfSSL_check_ip()`. New contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c:512`. - impact: a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic while the sample appears to trust a CA. - recommendation: call `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)` and bind the expected peer with `wolfSSL_check_ip("192.168.11.6")` (or a configurable DNS name) before `wolfSSL_connect()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RX72N EnvisionKit sample BSP/debug configs keep the Renesas device ID code at all-`F` and leave serial programming enabled, so sample-derived boards remain readable and reflashable over the debug/programming header - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/test.scfg:1144-1148`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/test_HardwareDebug.launch:25,57,86,117` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker has physical access to the FINE/JTAG/programming interface or a field-service path exposing it. - evidence: `test.scfg` sets `BSP_CFG_ID_CODE_LONG_{1..4}` to `0xFFFFFFFF` and `BSP_CFG_SERIAL_PROGRAMMER_CONECT_ENABLE` to `1`; the committed launch config passes `-uIdCode="FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF"` and stores the same all-`F` `id_code` for E1/EZ/E2 Lite. - impact: devices built from this profile have no meaningful Renesas debug/readout lock, enabling firmware extraction, key/material disclosure, and arbitrary reflashing by anyone with probe access. - recommendation: set non-default production ID codes and disable serial programming/debug for release images; keep all-`F` unlock settings only in explicit lab/debug-only configs. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RX72N `HardwareDebug` links a `Debug` wolfSSL library from a different build profile, so the active sample depends on stale/mismatched crypto objects instead of a config-matched library build - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/test.rcpc:257-336`, linked library `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/test.rcpc:334`, companion library config `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/wolfssl/wolfssl.rcpc:93-109` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the test project’s active `HardwareDebug` mode compiles with `-nodpfpu` and links `../wolfssl/Debug/wolfssl.lib`, while the companion `Simple/wolfssl` project in this tree only exposes a `Debug` build mode and compiles that library with `-dpfpu`. - impact: the sample can silently link a stale or option-skewed `wolfssl.lib`, making config-specific TLS/runtime failures hard to reproduce and review. - recommendation: add a matching `HardwareDebug` configuration (or project-reference build) for `wolfssl.lib`, and fail the app build when the selected configuration does not have a correspondingly built library artifact. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - this chunk was otherwise mostly Renesas SMC/project/debug metadata; the only sharper chunk-local issues confirmed were the TSIP client authentication gap, permissive device-debug defaults, and the app-vs-library build skew above. The same `test.rcpc` also routes builds through `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/user_settings.h`, which still carries the already-tracked Renesas `NO_DEV_RANDOM` + `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST` test-seed pattern (`wolfssl_demo/user_settings.h:98,280`; `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:3874`), so no separate duplicate seed finding was promoted from this overlap. - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/*`, related config `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/user_settings.h` - category: security/complexity review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: remaining content was generated clock/pin/interrupt setup, file lists, and debugger metadata without a sharper exploit path or material performance hot path than the entries above.

### Medium - IAR embOS SAMV71 `Release` silently drops the custom wolfSSL build configuration used by `Debug`, so the shipped release profile does not build the same crypto library that the project evaluates - location: `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_lib_SAMV71_XULT/wolfcrypt_lib.ewp:184,315-317,1147,1275`, config hook `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/settings.h:355-356`, intended settings `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_SAMV71_XULT_user_settings/user_settings.h:5-65` - category: complexity - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: define `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` and the same include paths in both Debug and Release, then add a CI/preprocess check that both configurations consume the same `user_settings.h` and expose the same intended feature macros. - status: candidate - severity: medium - evidence: Debug sets `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` and points `CCIncludePath2` at the repo root plus `..\embOS_SAMV71_XULT_user_settings`; Release defines only `NDEBUG` and leaves `CCIncludePath2` empty. `settings.h` includes `user_settings.h` only when `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` is defined. The skipped target config carries this build's crypto/memory knobs such as `WOLFCRYPT_ONLY`, `USE_FAST_MATH`, `TFM_TIMING_RESISTANT`, `HAVE_AESGCM`, `WOLFSSL_SMALL_STACK`, and `USE_WOLFSSL_MEMORY`. - impact: integrators cannot reason locally about what a Release artifact contains; Debug and Release can differ in feature set, hardening, memory behavior, and footprint, turning validation on one profile into false assurance for another.

### Low - the shipped IAR project is not portable or release-ready: Debug archives to a developer-specific absolute Windows path, while Release output/archive names are left uninitialized - location: `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_lib_SAMV71_XULT/wolfcrypt_lib.ewp:958,1627,1917`, expected artifact docs `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_lib_SAMV71_XULT/README_wolfcrypt_lib:24-34` - category: complexity - reference: `grugbrain: boring code`, `grugbrain: local reasoning` - recommendation: replace absolute host paths and `###Uninitialized###` outputs with project-relative `Debug/Release` archive locations, and smoke-test the shipped project on a clean machine/CI runner. - status: candidate - severity: low - evidence: Debug `IarchiveOutput` is hard-coded to `C:\Users\boz_windows\Desktop\embos-wolfssl\...\Debug\Bin\wolfcrypt_lib.a`; Release leaves `IlinkOutputFile` and `IarchiveOutput` as `###Uninitialized###`; the README only documents the Debug artifact path. - impact: other developers and CI cannot reliably reproduce the sample build, and the nominal Release configuration is effectively broken until manually repaired.

### Reviewed - this chunk was otherwise mostly IAR debugger/project metadata and a short build README; beyond the debug/release config drift and broken output paths above, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material runtime performance failure in `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_lib_SAMV71_XULT/wolfcrypt_lib.ewd`, `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_lib_SAMV71_XULT/wolfcrypt_lib.ewp`, or `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_lib_SAMV71_XULT/README_wolfcrypt_lib`. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `wolfcrypt_lib.ewd` is probe/C-SPY configuration with placeholder transport addresses and disabled logs; the README is limited to local build steps and does not add a stronger trust-boundary or production sink.

### Medium - IAR embOS `wolfcrypt_test` ships a drifted/broken Release profile, so the validated Debug build graph and the nominal production build are materially different - location: `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_test_SAMV71_XULT/wolfcrypt_test.ewp:183-186,314-319,729-734,781-783,1149-1150,1278-1280,1629-1694,1741-1742` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the Debug config defines `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS`, includes `..\embOS_SAMV71_XULT_user_settings`, overrides the linker script with `samv71q21_wolfssl.icf`, and links `..\embOS_wolfcrypt_lib_SAMV71_XULT\Debug\Bin\wolfcrypt_lib.a`; the Release config instead contains only `NDEBUG`, has an empty `CCIncludePath2` block, leaves `IlinkOutputFile` as `###Uninitialized###`, disables the custom linker script override and falls back to `lnk0t.icf`, and has no additional libraries listed. - impact: this sample has two incompatible build graphs; Release either fails outright or gets hand-patched into a binary with different wolfSSL settings, memory layout, and linked objects than the Debug configuration actually exercised, which makes embedded bring-up and security/perf triage configuration-dependent and hard to reproduce. - recommendation: either delete the unusable Release profile or clone the Debug profile’s required defines/include paths/linker script/libs into Release and add CI smoke builds for both IAR configurations. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - this chunk was otherwise IAR project/debugger metadata plus setup docs for an embOS evaluation harness; aside from the broken Release-vs-Debug config drift above, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit, trust-boundary break, or material performance fault in `IDE/IAR-EWARM/embOS/SAMV71_XULT/embOS_wolfcrypt_test_SAMV71_XULT/{wolfcrypt_test.ewd,README_wolfcrypt_test}`. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: `runWolfcryptTests.c` is a local test task that calls `wolfcrypt_test(NULL)`; repo search shows the test harness only dereferences `func_args` behind `if (args)` guards (`wolfcrypt/test/test.c:1932,1936,1938`), so this sample callsite did not yield a concrete null-deref or trust-boundary issue on this pass.

### High - GR-ROSE RX65N common settings enable deterministic test seeding, so the shipped `wolfssl` library and linked `test` app build with predictable wolfCrypt RNG output - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/common/user_settings.h:257`, build wiring `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/wolfssl/wolfssl.rcpc:98-104,210-216`, consumer/link `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/test/test.rcpc:280,314,394`, seed sink `wolfcrypt/src/random.c:128,3621-3628,3864-3874` - category: security - severity: high - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator builds the shipped GR-ROSE `wolfssl`/`test` projects or reuses `common/user_settings.h` in any real TLS/crypto firmware. - evidence: `common/user_settings.h` defines `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST`; both `wolfssl.rcpc` and `test.rcpc` compile with `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` and the library project includes `..\common`; `test.rcpc` then links `../wolfssl/Debug/wolfssl.lib`. In `random.c`, that build maps to `USE_TEST_GENSEED` and seeds with fixed bytes `output[i] = (byte)i`. - impact: RNG-backed operations in sample-derived RX65N builds (TLS ephemeral secrets, keygen, nonces, session secrets) become predictable across devices/boots, breaking confidentiality and integrity assumptions. - recommendation: remove `WOLFSSL_GENSEED_FORTEST` from the shared GR-ROSE profile, move it to an explicit lab-only header, and fail closed until a real entropy source/TRNG is wired. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - GR-ROSE RX65N sample TLS client loads a CA but never enables peer or endpoint verification before connecting to a hard-coded server IP, so any reachable host can impersonate `192.168.1.12:11111` - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/test/src/wolf_client.c:34-35,171,223-227,359`, default ctx behavior `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or answer on the configured IP/port. - evidence: `wolf_client.c` hard-codes `SIMPLE_TLSSEVER_IP "192.168.1.12"` and `SIMPLE_TLSSERVER_PORT "11111"`, loads CA material with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer(client_ctx, cert, SIZEOF_CERT, ...)`, and calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`. Repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(...)`, `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`, or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` in this client path, while new contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c`. - impact: a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic while the sample appears to be CA-backed. - recommendation: set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` explicitly and bind the expected IP/DNS name before `wolfSSL_connect()`, keeping any insecure demo mode behind a separate lab-only switch. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - the MDK5 `wolfSSL-Lib` project advertises Keil and wolfSSL pack feeds over plaintext HTTP, enabling MITM tampering of IDE-fetched build packages - location: `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/wolfSSL-Lib/wolfSSL-Lib.uvprojx:20,427-457,789-837` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: developer opens/imports the project or updates packs on an untrusted/intercepted network and the IDE honors the embedded pack URLs. - evidence: the project sets `<PackURL>http://www.keil.com/pack</PackURL>` and embeds numerous package URLs such as `url="http://www.keil.com/pack/"` and `url="http://www.wolfSSL.com/files/ide"`. Those feeds control the CMSIS/MDK/wolfSSL packs the project references for code, headers, startup files, and middleware. - impact: a network attacker can replace or tamper with pack contents during developer setup, yielding a trojaned embedded build environment and malicious firmware outputs. - recommendation: switch all pack/package URLs to HTTPS, pin exact pack versions with integrity verification where available, and avoid shipping IDE metadata that directs users to plaintext package feeds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - remaining files in this chunk were mostly VisualGDB/solution metadata, Keil debugger settings, and Renesas launch/project scaffolding; apart from the promoted RX65N deterministic-seed/client-auth gaps and MDK plaintext-pack feed issue above, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material runtime hot-path problem in the listed `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_{benchmark,test}/VisualGDB/*.{vgdbproj,sln}`, `IDE/MDK5-ARM/Projects/wolfSSL-Lib/{wolfSSL-Lib.uvoptx,Abstract.txt}`, or `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/test/test_HardwareDebug.launch` files. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found machine-specific COM ports/interface serials, absolute local debugger paths, and one ESP32C6 VisualGDB project pinned to `esp-idf/master`, but those were lower-signal/reproducibility issues compared with the concrete build-seeding, TLS-authentication, and plaintext package-fetch findings appended here.

### Medium - IAR EWARM `wolfSSL-Lib` Release configuration is internally inconsistent: it drops `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS`, clears include paths, and leaves release outputs uninitialized, so the shipped Release profile cannot reproduce the intended ported library - location: `IDE/IAR-EWARM/Projects/lib/wolfSSL-Lib.ewp:170-172,300-303,939,1115-1116,1244-1245,1595-1596,1881-1882`, guard in `wolfssl/wolfcrypt/settings.h:355-356` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: Debug defines `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` and `ewarm` and points include paths into the repo, but Release defines only `NDEBUG`, has an empty `CCIncludePath2`, and leaves both `IlinkOutputFile` and `IarchiveOutput` as `###Uninitialized###`. `settings.h` only includes `user_settings.h` when `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` is defined. - impact: developers selecting the shipped Release profile can get a non-buildable archive target or a library built with materially different defaults than the Debug-tested IAR port, making security/configuration review and reproduction unreliable. - recommendation: make Release share the same `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS`/include-path block as Debug, set concrete archive outputs, and CI-build both IAR configurations so port-profile drift fails fast. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: boring code`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Low - MQX benchmark launch metadata conflates J-Link and P&E probe profiles, so the checked-in `PnE U-MultiLink` debug launch can actually drive the J-Link backend and import user-specific workspace state - location: `mqx/wolfcrypt_benchmark/wolfcrypt_benchmark_twrk70f120m_Int_Flash_SramData_Debug_PnE_U-MultiLink.launch:80,86,166`, `mqx/wolfcrypt_benchmark/wolfcrypt_benchmark_twrk70f120m_Int_Flash_SramData_Debug_JTrace.launch:80,86,166`, `mqx/wolfcrypt_benchmark/ReferencedRSESystems.xml:11-24,150-179` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the `*_Debug_PnE_U-MultiLink.launch` file sets `DebuggerCommonData.Connection Type` to `com.freescale.mcu.debug.connections.jlink.arm` while the hint still says `PnE U-MultiLink`; the companion RSE export stores both JTrace and PnE launch applicators against the same J-Link-backed host block and also bakes in `host.defaultUser="Christopher Conlon"`. - impact: engineers can select the wrong checked-in launcher and spend bring-up time chasing probe/connection failures caused by stale workspace metadata instead of the benchmark itself. - recommendation: regenerate and commit separate launchers per actual probe backend, remove workspace-personalized RSE state from version control, and keep only minimal reproducible debugger settings under source control. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Reviewed - the remaining files in this chunk were predominantly IAR/CodeWarrior debugger and project metadata (`IDE/IAR-EWARM/Projects/lib/wolfSSL-Lib.ewd`, the remaining `mqx/wolfcrypt_benchmark/*.launch`, and `mqx/wolfcrypt_benchmark/wolfcrypt_benchmark_twrk70f120m_Int_Flash_SramData_Debug_JTrace.jlink`); beyond the promoted Release-profile drift and launcher-metadata mismatch above, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local security exploit or material production performance fault from these files. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks were limited to declarative debugger settings, board IDs, launch wiring, and workspace exports without a more direct trust-boundary break than the findings appended here.

### Medium - RX65N GR-ROSE sample BSP leaves the Renesas device ID code unset (`0xFFFFFFFF`), so sample-derived boards remain readable/reflashable over physical debug or programming access - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/smc/smc.scfg:711-718` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker has physical access to a deployed board’s programming/debug interface or equivalent service access and the integrator ships this sample-derived BSP unchanged. - evidence: `BSP_CFG_ID_CODE_LONG_1..4` are all `0xFFFFFFFF` in the shipped SMC config; adjacent protection-related fields like `BSP_CFG_FAW_REG_VALUE` and `BSP_CFG_ROMCODE_REG_VALUE` are also left at `0xFFFFFFFF`. - impact: devices flashed from the sample can be dumped, cloned, or reflashed instead of enforcing board-level readout/debug lock settings. - recommendation: provision non-default per-device ID codes and production flash/debug protection values in release builds, and keep the all-`FF` sample defaults clearly lab-only. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - `wolfBench` builds the full wolfCrypt self-test and benchmark harness into the iOS app target, dragging in test-only fixture code and embedded cert buffers that are unrelated to mobile benchmarking - location: `IDE/XCODE/Benchmark/wolfBench.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj:99,102,506,540`, source sinks `wolfcrypt/test/test.c:506`, `wolfcrypt/benchmark/benchmark.c:2011` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: the Xcode app target lists both `test.c in Sources` and `benchmark.c in Sources`; both source files include `<wolfssl/certs_test.h>`, so the mobile app compiles the large self-test harness and fixture bundle directly into the app binary. - impact: the benchmark app carries unnecessary code/data, increasing build time, binary size, reverse-engineering surface, and audit difficulty without improving benchmark fidelity. - recommendation: keep self-tests and benchmarks in separate non-app targets, link the benchmark app against a slim library build, and exclude `test.c`/`certs_test.h` from release mobile targets. - reference: `grugbrain: complexity very bad`, `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - the hostap snapshot in this chunk is clearly CI/test-oriented and this pass did not promote a sharper direct vulnerability beyond existing workflow trust-boundary findings - location: `.github/workflows/hostap-vm.yml:153,273,283,337`, `.github/workflows/hostap-files/configs/07c9f183ea744ac04585fb6dd10220c75a5e2e74/{hostapd.config,wpa_supplicant.config,tests}` - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the workflow pins `hostap_ref` to `07c9f183ea744ac04585fb6dd10220c75a5e2e74`, copies the local config snapshots into the checked-out hostap tree, and runs the listed tests; the configs intentionally enable broad legacy/test coverage (`CONFIG_TESTING_OPTIONS`, `CONFIG_MODULE_TESTS`, `CONFIG_WEP`, `CONFIG_TLSV11`, etc.), but in this chunk that looked CI-scoped rather than a stronger shipped exploit path.

### Medium - Xcode `wolfssl-FIPS` exports `certs_test.h` as a public SDK header, propagating repo-public private keys into built iOS/macOS packages - location: `IDE/XCODE/wolfssl-FIPS.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj:50,217,368,386,492`, embedded keys in `wolfssl/certs_test.h:528-592,2905-3146` - category: security - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture` - recommendation: remove `certs_test.h` from the Xcode targets' `CopyFiles` install phases, keep test cert/key buffers in non-installed test-only targets, and require Apple app consumers to inject their own credentials instead of shipping repo-public key material. - status: candidate - severity: medium - exploitability/preconditions: an integrator builds/distributes the Xcode FIPS SDK or reuses its installed headers in an app/sample. - evidence: both iOS and macOS targets copy `certs_test.h` into `include/wolfssl`; that header contains embedded private keys including `server_key_der_1024` and `client_key_der_4096`. - impact: the Apple SDK packaging itself redistributes compile-ready test private keys, making sample-derived identities trivially clonable or forgeable wherever consumers reuse the installed header material.

### Medium - ESP-IDF `wolfssl_client`, `wolfssl_server`, `wolfssl_test`, and `wolfssl_benchmark` all ship with watchdogs disabled and panic-halt defaults; benchmark/test also turn the watchdogs off at runtime, so sample-derived devices can hang indefinitely after faults - location: build flags `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/{wolfssl_client,wolfssl_server,wolfssl_test,wolfssl_benchmark}/CMakeLists.txt:25-27`, headers `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/{wolfssl_client,wolfssl_server,wolfssl_test,wolfssl_benchmark}/components/wolfssl/include/user_settings.h:25-26`, defaults `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/{wolfssl_client,wolfssl_server,wolfssl_test,wolfssl_benchmark}/sdkconfig.defaults:43-55`, runtime calls `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_{test,benchmark}/main/main.c:193,254`, sink `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_util.c:759-788` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator ships or starts from any of these example profiles; any crash, deadlock, long-running path, or attacker-triggered fault then relies on manual reset/power-cycle instead of watchdog recovery. - evidence: all four examples force `WOLFSSL_ESP_NO_WATCHDOG=1` in both CMake and `user_settings.h`; all four `sdkconfig.defaults` disable task and interrupt watchdogs (`CONFIG_ESP_TASK_WDT_EN=n`, `CONFIG_ESP_INT_WDT=n`, `CONFIG_TASK_WDT=n`) and switch panic handling from reboot to halt (`CONFIG_ESP_PANIC_PRINT_REBOOT=n`, `CONFIG_ESP_PANIC_PRINT_HALT=y`); `wolfssl_test` and `wolfssl_benchmark` then call `esp_DisableWatchdog()`, whose implementation disables the RTC watchdog and deinitializes the task watchdog before logging `"Watchdog disabled."`. - impact: packaged networked examples stop self-recovering from hangs/faults, turning otherwise recoverable failures into persistent device DoS until manual intervention. - recommendation: keep watchdogs and reboot-on-panic enabled by default in all example profiles except explicit benchmark-only lab configs; remove unconditional `WOLFSSL_ESP_NO_WATCHDOG` from client/server/test examples and gate `esp_DisableWatchdog()` behind an opt-in debug/benchmark switch. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - `sslSniffer/README.md`, `sslSniffer/sslSniffer.vcxproj`, and `sslSniffer/sslSniffer.vcproj` did not expose a sharper chunk-local sink than already-tracked shipped-key/test-material themes; this pass mainly found docs reiterating existing risky fixtures/compatibility knobs (`../../certs/server-key.pem`, `../../certs/ecc-key.pem`, `--enable-arc4`, `--enable-des3`, `-DWOLFSSL_MIN_RSA_BITS=512`) rather than a new code-path exploit or material hot-path regression. - location: `sslSniffer/README.md`, `sslSniffer/sslSniffer.vcxproj`, `sslSniffer/sslSniffer.vcproj` - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the Windows project files are mostly declarative build metadata for `../src/sniffer.c`; the README mostly documents passive-sniffer operation and options that overlap existing shipped-key and weak-compatibility findings already in the inbox.

### Medium - RA6M4 TLS client sample loads a CA but never enables peer verification or binds the expected server identity, so any reachable host can impersonate the configured TLS server - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/test/src/wolf_client.c:110-126,160,192-236`, defaults `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/common/wolfssl_demo.h:39-40,52`, default verify mode `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the LAN path or answer on the configured `SERVER_IP`/port in a sample-derived RA6M4 build. - evidence: the sample creates a context with `wolfSSL_CTX_new()`, loads CA material with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations()` / `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer()`, connects to `SERVER_IP` via `FreeRTOS_connect()`, then calls `wolfSSL_connect()`; repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)`, `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`, or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` in this client path, and new contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c:512`. - impact: a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic while the example appears to use CA-based authentication. - recommendation: set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` explicitly and bind the expected DNS name/IP before `wolfSSL_connect()`, keeping any insecure demo mode behind an explicit lab-only switch. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - Apple universal multiplatform sample app loads a CA file for `www.wolfssl.com` but never turns on certificate or hostname verification, so DNS/on-path attackers can impersonate the server - location: app wiring `IDE/apple-universal/wolfssl-multiplatform/wolfssl-multiplatform.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj:17,175,305,348`, client `IDE/apple-universal/wolfssl-multiplatform/wolfssl-multiplatform/simple_client_example.c:35,50,67,89-95,114,124`, default verify mode `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: user runs the shipped sample app on an untrusted network or against attacker-controlled DNS/routing. - evidence: the Xcode target compiles `simple_client_example.c`; that client resolves `SERVER_HOST "www.wolfssl.com"`, loads `CERT_PATH` with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations()`, opens the socket with `connect()`, and calls `wolfSSL_connect()`. Repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)`, `wolfSSL_check_domain_name("www.wolfssl.com")`, or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` in the sample, while `wolfSSL_CTX_new()` contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE`. - impact: the sample accepts attacker-presented certificates and does not bind the certificate to `www.wolfssl.com`, so confidential request/response data can be intercepted or modified. - recommendation: call `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, NULL)` and `wolfSSL_check_domain_name(ssl, "www.wolfssl.com")` (or equivalent) before `wolfSSL_connect()`. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RA6M4 packaging ships an example RSA private key used by the provided SignedCA tooling, so any sample-derived signed-CA workflow that reuses it is forgeable by anyone with repo access - location: manifest `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/include.am:21-23`, docs `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/tools/README.md:8-19,32-37`, key `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/tools/example_keys/rsa_private.pem:1`, generator `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/tools/example_keys/generate_SignedCA.sh:4-5` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator uses the shipped `example_keys/rsa_private.pem` with the documented SignedCA process instead of generating a deployment-unique signing key. - evidence: `include.am` distributes `generate_SignedCA.sh`, `rsa_private.pem`, and `rsa_public.pem`; the committed `rsa_private.pem` is plaintext `-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----`; the README explicitly says it is "an example 2048-bit rsa private key for signing CA cert" and shows `openssl dgst -sha256 -sign rsa_private.pem ...` to produce `Signed-CA.sign`. - impact: anyone who has the repo can create the same CA-signing output as the sample tooling, collapsing trust for any device or workflow that reuses the bundled example key. - recommendation: stop shipping the private key in release artifacts, generate a fresh signing key per lab/deployment, and require explicit local key injection before SignedCA generation. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - RX72N EnvisionKit and RX65N e2studio wolfSSL library projects compile legacy 3DES/MD2/MD5 code by default while their shipped user settings do not disable old TLS/MD5/3DES paths, keeping weak suites reachable in sample-derived builds - location: project files `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl/wolfssl.rcpc:43,59-61`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/RSK/wolfssl/wolfssl.rcpc:43,59-61`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/wolfssl/wolfssl.rcpc:44,60-62`; settings `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/user_settings.h:98-99,280`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/RSK/wolfssl_demo/user_settings.h:85-86,260`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/common/user_settings.h:83-84,257`; suite gates `wolfssl/internal.h:280,398,435`; runtime additions `src/internal.c:4121-4124,4203-4206,4474-4477` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator ships one of these board projects with the default `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS` profile and does not further restrict protocol/cipher negotiation. - evidence: all three `.rcpc` files compile `des3.c`, `md2.c`, `md4.c`, and `md5.c`; the referenced user settings disable only `NO_MD4` plus test-seed/dev-random knobs, with no `NO_DES3`, `NO_MD5`, or `NO_OLD_TLS`. With those guards absent, `wolfssl/internal.h` defines legacy suite macros such as `BUILD_SSL_RSA_WITH_3DES_EDE_CBC_SHA`, `BUILD_TLS_DHE_RSA_WITH_3DES_EDE_CBC_SHA`, and `BUILD_TLS_RSA_WITH_NULL_MD5`, and `src/internal.c` appends those suites to the compiled list. - impact: sample-derived RX firmware keeps obsolete TLS and digest code reachable, increasing downgrade surface and making it easy to ship weak 3DES or NULL-MD5/old-TLS capability by default. - recommendation: ship hardened board defaults that set `NO_OLD_TLS`, `NO_DES3`, `NO_MD5`, and `NO_MD2`, and drop the corresponding legacy source files from the default project builds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mainly IDE metadata, README/setup text, packaging manifests, linker scripts, and board project wiring; beyond the promoted RA6M4 / Apple client-authentication gaps, shipped RA6M4 SignedCA example key, and RX72N/RX65N legacy-crypto defaults above, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material production performance issue in `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/*/VisualGDB/*`, `IDE/XCODE/wolfssl_testsuite.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj`, `debian/*`, `IDE/RISCV/SIFIVE-UNLEASHED/*`, `IDE/ARDUINO/sketches/wolfssl_server/README.md`, `IDE/WORKBENCH/*`, or `IDE/XilinxSDK/2018_2/lscript.ld`. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks were dominated by project/solution metadata, package/install manifests, or usage docs; the remaining files did not expose a stronger trust-boundary break or meaningful hot-path inefficiency than the issues appended here.

### Medium - GR-ROSE source distribution ships a repo-public RSA private key for the TSIP `SignedCA` workflow, so any sample-derived build that reuses the bundled example keypair can be tricked into trusting attacker-signed roots - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/include.am:28-30`, private key `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/tools/example_keys/rsa_private.pem:1`, tooling docs `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/tools/README.md:8-19,32-37`, helper `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/tools/example_keys/generate_SignedCA.sh:4-5`, verification sink `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/test/src/key_data.h:42`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX65N/GR-ROSE/test/src/wolf_client.c:326-328` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator follows the bundled GR-ROSE SignedCA flow with the shipped `example_keys` material, or ships the sample’s wrapped RSA verification key without rotating it. - evidence: `include.am` distributes `rsa_private.pem` and `rsa_public.pem`; the committed file is a plaintext `-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----`; the board tools explicitly use `rsa_private.pem` to sign `Signed-CA.sign` for a CA cert and describe the bundled keypair as example keys for CA signing/verification; the sample client uses a TSIP RSA public-key verification path for RootCA-sign verification. - impact: anyone with repo access can mint a new SignedCA blob for an attacker-chosen CA and undermine sample-derived trust bootstrapping that reuses the published keypair. - recommendation: stop shipping the example private key, generate a per-board/per-project signing key locally during provisioning, and make lab-only sample keys opt-in and fail-closed in release builds. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - the shipped SGX Visual Studio project is internally broken and non-reproducible: duplicate configuration blocks conflict on toolset selection, x64 intermediate paths are malformed, and several configs still depend on a sibling `wolfssl-3.9.6` tree - location: `IDE/WIN-SGX/wolfSSL_SGX.vcxproj:52-58,69-92,146,159,171,184,193,206,245,259,287,307,309-310` - category: complexity - severity: medium - status: candidate - evidence: `Prerelease|Win32` and `Simulation|Win32` each appear twice with different `PlatformToolset` values (`v120` and `Intel C++ Compiler 16.0`); multiple x64 `IntDir` entries are malformed as `$(Configuration)\$(Platform\obj\`; and Debug/Release/Prerelease include paths hard-code `$(SolutionDir)wolfssl-3.9.6` in addition to the repo-relative tree. - impact: a clean checkout cannot be built or reasoned about locally without ad-hoc fixes or a very specific side-by-side directory layout, so enclave consumers are likely to rely on stale/manual project edits that drift from the reviewed source. - recommendation: regenerate the `.vcxproj`, remove duplicate configuration groups, fix all `IntDir` macros, drop the stale `wolfssl-3.9.6` include path, and add a smoke build on a clean SGX toolchain image. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: testing complexity smell`

### Reviewed - the rest of this chunk was mostly board setup READMEs, Automake distribution manifests, SGX metadata, and a hostap CI/test snapshot; beyond the promoted GR-ROSE SignedCA key-shipping issue and the broken SGX project file, this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material production performance problem in `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M3/{README.md,README_APRA6M_en.md,README_APRA6M_jp.md,include.am}`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RZN2L/{README.md,include.am}`, `.github/workflows/hostap-files/configs/b607d2723e927a3446d89aed813f1aa6068186bb/{tests,hostapd.config,wpa_supplicant.config}`, `certs/renewcerts/wolfssl.cnf`, `IDE/Espressif/{README.md,include.am}`, or `IDE/WIN-SGX/{wolfSSL_SGX.sln,ReadMe.txt,wolfSSL_SGX.edl,include.am}`. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: the hostap files are clearly CI/test-oriented feature matrices rather than shipped runtime defaults, `wolfssl.cnf` mainly reinforced already-tracked CA:true fixture-generation patterns, and the remaining content was documentation or packaging metadata without a stronger trust-boundary break than the issues appended here.

### Medium - Renesas RA6M4 and RX72N EnvisionKit signed-CA examples ship the private RSA key that authorizes hardware Root-CA updates, so sample-derived devices can trust attacker-signed CA bundles - location: `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/include.am:21-23`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/tools/example_keys/rsa_private.pem:1`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/tools/README.md:15-19,32-37`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RA6M4/test/src/test_main.c:68-76,81-85`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/include.am:20-22`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/tools/example_keys/rsa_private.pem:1`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/tools/README.md:15-19,32-37`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/Simple/test/src/test_main.c:143-163`, `IDE/Renesas/e2studio/RX72N/EnvisionKit/wolfssl_demo/key_data.h:42-43`, supporting port docs `wolfcrypt/src/port/Renesas/README.md:172,185` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: integrator reuses the shipped `example_keys/rsa_private.pem` for the Renesas SCE/TSIP signed-CA flow, and the device/build consumes signed CA bundles using the corresponding built-in verification key. - evidence: both example packs distribute plaintext `rsa_private.pem`; the READMEs tell users to sign the CA file with that key for the example program; runtime code then calls `wc_sce_inform_cert_sign()` / `tsip_inform_cert_sign()` before loading CA material; the Renesas port README says TSIP TLS-linked APIs require a Root CA certificate bundle signature, and RX72N `key_data.h` labels `encrypted_user_rsa2048_ne_key` as the `RSA2048 public key for RootCA sign verification`. - impact: anyone with repo access can generate a valid signature over a malicious CA bundle for sample-derived hardware that still trusts the published key, letting attackers replace the device trust anchor and then mint server certificates that pass hardware-backed verification. - recommendation: stop shipping the signing private key; generate a unique signing key per product/build or provisioning domain, keep the private half out of source/distributions, and gate the sample key behind an explicit non-production lab path only. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V11 Cryptography`, `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Medium - QNX sample TLS client loads a CA but never enables peer verification or binds the expected server identity, so any reachable host can impersonate the endpoint - location: `IDE/QNX/example-client/client-tls.c:142,156,173,201,224`, default verify mode `src/ssl.c:512` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker can MITM the network path or answer on the configured server address used by the sample. - evidence: `client-tls.c` creates a context with `wolfSSL_CTX_new(wolfSSLv23_client_method())`, loads a client cert/key and CA buffer with `wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_buffer()`, then calls `wolfSSL_connect(ssl)`; repo search found no `wolfSSL_CTX_set_verify(..., WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...)`, `wolfSSL_check_domain_name()`, or `wolfSSL_check_ip()` in that client path, while new contexts default to `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_NONE` in `src/ssl.c:512`. - impact: a wrong endpoint or on-path attacker can terminate TLS and read/modify traffic while the sample appears to be CA-backed and mutually authenticated. - recommendation: set `WOLFSSL_VERIFY_PEER` explicitly, fail if no valid peer cert is presented, and bind the expected DNS name/IP before connect. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

### Reviewed - the remaining files in this chunk were mostly linker scripts, IDE/workspace metadata, packaging manifests, docs, and static sample assets; this pass did not confirm a sharper chunk-local exploit or material runtime performance problem in `IDE/XilinxSDK/*`, `mqx/*/Debugger/*`, `IDE/XCODE/Benchmark/*`, `IDE/MPLABX16/*`, `mcapi/*`, `mplabx/*`, `support/*`, `examples/{README.md,include.am,crypto_policies/*}`, `tirtos/packages/ti/net/wolfssl/*`, `IDE/PlatformIO/*`, `zephyr/samples/*/boards/*.conf`, or the assorted README/.gitignore/include.am files beyond the Renesas signed-CA key exposure and QNX client-auth gap above. - location: listed files - category: security/complexity/performance review - severity: none - status: reviewed - evidence: spot checks found mostly declarative build/debug settings (including some low-signal plaintext URLs and default debug IDs) but not a stronger concrete sink or production-impacting hot path in this chunk.

### Medium - ESP-IDF client/server samples bake local git remote/path metadata into the firmware and print it at startup, leaking internal repo URLs and filesystem paths to anyone who can read device logs - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_{client,server}/components/wolfssl/CMakeLists.txt:653-654,1172-1198,1243-1244`, sinks `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp32_util.c:384-410,638-664`, callers `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_{client,server}/main/main.c:188-190` - category: security - severity: medium - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: attacker or low-privilege operator can read UART/serial logs, remote log aggregation, or support dumps from a sample-derived device. - evidence: the component CMake saves `remote.origin.url`, branch/hash/date, `WOLFSSL_USER_SETTINGS_DIR`, and accumulated `LIBWOLFSSL_CMAKE_OUTPUT` into compile definitions via `LIBWOLFSSL_SAVE_INFO(...)`; `esp32_util.c` then logs `LIBWOLFSSL_VERSION_GIT_ORIGIN = %s`, `WOLFSSL User Settings file = %s`, and `LIBWOLFSSL_CMAKE_OUTPUT:%s`; both sample mains call `esp_ShowExtendedSystemInfo();` during startup. - impact: sample-derived firmware exposes internal repository hosts/URLs, branch state, usernames/absolute paths, and custom certificate-bundle paths in runtime logs, which materially aids environment reconnaissance and can disclose private repo endpoints or token-bearing HTTPS remotes. - recommendation: gate `esp_ShowExtendedSystemInfo()` and the git/path macros behind an explicit debug-only option, redact remote URLs and absolute paths before embedding/logging, and keep release/sample defaults log-minimal. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V14 Data Protection`, `ASVS 5.0 V16 Security Logging and Error Handling`, `ASVS 5.0 V13 Configuration`

### Low - ESP-IDF developer-repo bootstrap copies `user_settings.h` to the wrong directory, so the sample can write stray config outside the active component tree and still build against stale or missing settings - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_{client,server}/components/wolfssl/CMakeLists.txt:650,745-748` - category: complexity - severity: low - status: candidate - evidence: the script defines `WOLFSSL_PROJECT_DIR` as `${CMAKE_HOME_DIRECTORY}/components/wolfssl` and logs that it is installing `user_settings.h` there, but the actual `file(COPY ...)` destination is `${CMAKE_HOME_DIRECTORY}/wolfssl/include/` instead. - impact: first-run/bootstrap behavior becomes non-local and confusing: the generated settings file lands outside `components/wolfssl`, so the active component can keep using missing/stale headers while an unrelated sibling directory is mutated. - recommendation: copy into `${WOLFSSL_PROJECT_DIR}/include/` consistently, fail if that directory is absent, and avoid hidden build-time file writes outside the component root. - reference: `grugbrain: local reasoning`, `grugbrain: avoid wrong abstraction`

### Low - ESP-IDF wolfSSL component defaults certificate-bundle verification to accept zero-serial X.509 certificates, weakening strict chain validation in sample-derived clients unless integrators opt back out - location: `IDE/Espressif/ESP-IDF/examples/wolfssl_{client,server}/components/wolfssl/Kconfig:252-259`, enforcement path `wolfcrypt/src/port/Espressif/esp_crt_bundle/esp_crt_bundle.c:267-278,1389-1399` - category: security - severity: low - status: candidate - exploitability/preconditions: build uses the default wolfSSL certificate bundle settings, and the attacker can present an otherwise trusted chain containing a serial-0 certificate (for example via a misissued cert or a private/custom CA bundle). - evidence: Kconfig sets `config WOLFSSL_ASN_ALLOW_0_SERIAL` to `default y`; the bundle verifier treats `CONFIG_WOLFSSL_ASN_ALLOW_0_SERIAL` as relaxed serial-number checking and logs `WOLFSSL_ASN_ALLOW_0_SERIAL enabled` instead of rejecting that parse path. - impact: sample-derived bundle clients accept a class of non-compliant certificates that stricter X.509 validation would reject, reducing defense-in-depth around certificate parsing and trust-store hygiene. - recommendation: change the default to `n`, document it as a compatibility escape hatch for broken PKI only, and require explicit opt-in when a deployment truly needs serial-0 tolerance. - reference: `ASVS 5.0 V12 Secure Communication`, `ASVS 5.0 V15 Secure Coding and Architecture`

## Short audit log

- Phase1 dependency assessment: inspected newest relevant Renovate report `.tmp/renovate-2026-04-18.json`: no clear dependency or GitHub Actions risk beyond routine maintenance.
